name: Wheel of Heaven Translation Glossary
description: Lexical decisions made by the Wheel of Heaven Translation Program. Distinct from content/i18n/glossary.json, which records site-UI terminology. Each entry records a source-language term, the standard PD-translation rendering, the WoH choice, the rationale, and the verses where the choice applies. Pinned to per-chapter translations via `glossaryVersion` in chapter metadata.
scopeNote: v2.6.0 reached Job 38. v2.7.0 added Job 40-41 (9 entries) — the Behemoth and Leviathan passages, the canonical Hebrew Bible dragon-tradition material. Leviathan is identified with the *tannin* of Isaiah 27:1 (linking to the taninim entry from Gen 1:21, glossary v2.0.0) and with the Ugaritic *Lotan*. v2.8.0 adds Job 42 (7 entries), closing the book of Job. The new entries cover the book's keystone closing material: the ear-vs-eye direct-experiential epistemology of v 5 (shema-ozen-eyni-ra-atkha), the three-way philological dispute on v 6 (em-as-v-nikhamti-al-afar-va-efer), the YHWH-verdict-on-the-friends inversion of vv 7-8 (lo-dibartem-elay-nekhonah), the pan-HB restoration phrase shav et-shvut (v 10) — a cross-corpus entry that will govern future translations across Deuteronomy, the Major and Minor Prophets, the Psalms, and Lamentations — the archaic currency-unit kesitah (v 11; also Gen 33:19 and Josh 24:32), the three sense-bearing daughter-names of v 14 (yemimah-ketziah-keren-happukh), and the patriarchal-good-death formula of v 17 (zaqen-u-s-va-yamim; cross-corpus: Abraham, Isaac, David, Jehoiada, Job). One translation change is driven by glossary in this pass: shav et-shvut moves the English from ASV's 'turned the captivity' to the modern-scholarly-consensus 'restored the fortunes' (Bracke 1985; Dahood; NRSV/NJPS/Newsom). The book of Job is wisdom-literature, not divine-council-disclosure; the WoH lens stays quiet in this chapter, surfacing only at vv 7-8 to note the verdict-inversion's connection back to the bnei-elohim framing of chs 1-2. // **v2.9.0 opens Genesis 12 — the start of the patriarchal narratives.** Thirteen new entries cover the chapter's keystone vocabulary: the doubled-imperative *lekh-lekha* of v 1 (recurring only at Gen 22:2, forming the Abraham-cycle inclusio); the niphal/hithpael blessing-form dispute at v 3 (the load-bearing universal-promise verse, with the Pauline-reception complication of Gal 3:8 vs. the modern critical middle/reflexive reading of Sarna/Speiser/NJPS); the *nefesh asher asu* of v 5 (proselytes-made vs. slaves-acquired, the classical rabbinic vs. modern critical split); the sacred-tree-oracle site *elon Moreh* of v 6 (with the patriarchal sacred-tree pattern across Shechem, Bethel, Ophrah, etc.); the first patriarchal theophany-formula *va-yera YHWH el-Avram* of v 7 (recurring across the Abraham-Isaac-Jacob arc); the seed-land covenant-nucleus *l-zarakha eten et-ha-aretz* of v 7 (expanded across 13:14-17, 15:7-21, 17:8, etc.); the cult-site invocation *qara b-shem YHWH* of v 8 (resuming the Seth-Enosh tradition of Gen 4:26); the wife-sister type-scene framing of vv 10-20 (Alter's canonical 1981 treatment, three instances at Gen 12 / 20 / 26); the beautiful-woman formula *eshet yefat mar'eh* of v 11 (a Hebrew Bible stock-phrase across Rebekah, Rachel, Joseph, Abigail, Tamar, Abishag, Esther); the say-sister strategy *imri-na achoti at* of v 13 (the ethically-debated patriarchal lapse); the camel anachronism of v 16 (Sapir-Hen and Ben-Yosef 2013 archaeological consensus on Iron Age Levantine camel domestication); the proto-Exodus plague-formula *naga / nega'im* of v 17 (verbal link forward to Exod 11:1); and the Abram-Pharaoh expulsion verb *va-y-shalchu* of v 20 (verbally identical to Exod 12:33). The chapter is not high-lens-leverage — the WoH posture surfaces only at the theophany verses (7, 17) to note continuity with the divine-descent pattern of chs 1-11. // **v2.10.0 continues the patriarchal narratives into Genesis 13** — the return from Mitsrayim and the separation from Lot. Six new entries cover the chapter's keystone vocabulary: the Canaanite-Perizzite redactional parenthetical of v 7 (paired with the simpler 12:6 form, the chapter's most-cited Documentary Hypothesis flag); the *gan YHWH* Eden back-reference at v 10 (a load-bearing inter-textual move framing the Sodom plain as a second Eden and its destruction in ch 19 as a second Eden-loss type-scene; Westermann, Sarna, Hamilton, Alter consensus); the *sa eynekha* lift-your-eyes formula introduced at v 14 (recurring across 10+ patriarchal verses as the corpus's standard seeing / visionary-perception formula); the patriarchal seed-comparison triad of dust (13:16) + stars (15:5) + sand (22:17), tracked as a deliberate cosmographic distribution across earth, sky, and shore; the covenantal *hithallech ba-aretz* land-walking formula of v 17 (Daube 1947 *Studies in Biblical Law*, with parallels at Josh 1:3, Josh 18:8, Deut 11:24); and the *elonei Mamre* sacred-tree site of v 18 (parallel to but distinct from *elon Moreh* of 12:6 — the recurring locus of Abram's residence, the three-visitors theophany of ch 18, and the Machpelah burial-narratives of chs 23, 25, 49, 50). The chapter's only high-lens-leverage moment is the *gan YHWH* back-reference at v 10; the rest is patriarchal-narrative scaffolding. // **v2.11.0 continues the patriarchal narratives into Genesis 14** — the war of the four kings against the five, Lot's rescue, the Melchizedek-and-El-Elyon encounter, and Abram's refusal of Sodom's spoils. Ten new entries cover the chapter's keystone vocabulary, concentrated in the Melchizedek block (vv 18-22). The cross-corpus, scholarly-load-bearing material: the *Refa'im / Zuzim / Emim / Anakim / Nephilim* giant-peoples chain of v 5 (with the explicit Deut 2:10-12, 2:20-21, 3:11 equivalences and the lens-relevant link back to the Gen 6:1-4 Nephilim — claim_type=inferred); the *ha-Ivri* gentilic-etymology dispute of v 13 (the Eber-eponymous reading, the habiru/apiru social-class reading from the Amarna corpus, and the *eber* Trans-Euphrates reading — claim_type=direct, the dispute itself is direct); the *chanikhav* hapax of v 14 (the unique-occurrence noun from ch-n-k, the Egyptian *ḥnk* execration-text parallel, the 318=Eliezer gematria from Gen Rab 43:2 — claim_type=inferred). The Melchizedek block (vv 18-22) — the highest-lens-leverage block in the entire Abraham cycle to date — contributes six entries: *melchizedek* (the theophoric vs. honorific name-readings, the *Tzedek* Jebusite-deity question, the Ps 110:4 cross-reference, the 11QMelchizedek and Heb 5-7 reception-traditions flagged-not-preempted — claim_type=direct on the name itself); *melekh-shalem-jerusalem* (the canonical Jerusalem identification from Ps 76:3 and 1QapGen, with the independent-site and divine-epithet alternatives — claim_type=direct); *kohen-first-melchizedek* (the first-occurrence-of-*kohen* status, the pre-Aaronic pre-Sinai priesthood, the *kohen l-El-Elyon* not-l-YHWH collocation — claim_type=direct); *el-elyon* (the divine name; the West-Semitic *El* + *Elyon* compound and the Phoenician *Elioun* of Sanchuniathon/Philo of Byblos; the Deut 32:8-9 divine-council datum with the 4QDeut^j / LXX *bnei Elohim* reading flagged; the Num 24:16 Balaam occurrence and the wider Psalter distribution — claim_type=direct on the name, the West-Semitic-pantheon framework is scholarly synthesis but mainstream); *koneh-shamayim-va-aretz* (the q-n-h root duality of acquire-vs-create, the Ugaritic *qny ʾrṣ* and Phoenician Karatepe *El qoneh ʾerets* and Hittite *Elkunirsa* parallels, the inherited West-Semitic high-god title — claim_type=direct on the lexical-philological facts); *ma'aser-first-pre-sinai* (the first occurrence of the tithe in the Hebrew Bible, the pre-Sinai pre-Aaronic locus, the Heb 7:2-10 Levi-through-Abraham reception flagged-not-preempted, the cross-reference to Jacob's Bethel tithe at Gen 28:22 — claim_type=direct). The chapter's keystone theological move is captured by *yhwh-el-elyon-apposition* at v 22: the Genesis editor's explicit appositional identification of YHWH with the El Elyon of the Melchizedek tradition — the canonical syncretism (claim_type=direct on the apposition itself; the harmonization-reading from Cross 1973 and Smith 2001 is scholarly synthesis but mainstream). The lens stays in the apparatus: the translation reads as defensible scholarly English (matching ASV at every divergence point), while the glossary apparatus and per-verse commentary carry the lens-relevant material — the divine-council reading of Deut 32:8-9, the West-Semitic-pantheon context of *El* and *Elyon*, the pre-Israelite priesthood and the giant-peoples chain. The Tzeboim ketiv/qere at vv 2 and 8 is resolved to the qere ('Tzeboim') and noted in commentary without a separate glossary entry (Editor's discretion: orthographic, not lexical). // **v2.12.0 continues the patriarchal narratives into Genesis 15** — the covenant between the pieces (*brit bein ha-betarim*) and the foundational covenant-of-land promise. Ten new entries cover the chapter's keystone vocabulary. The cross-corpus prophetic-revelation cluster at v 1 contributes three entries: *davar-yhwh-prophetic-formula* (the inaugural occurrence of *va-y'hi devar-YHWH*, the standard prophetic-introduction formula across Samuel/Kings/Isaiah/Jeremiah/Ezekiel/the Twelve, 250+ occurrences); *machazeh-prophetic-vision* (the prophetic-vision lexeme distinct from *chazon* and *mar'eh*; sparse recurrence at Num 24:4, 16 and Ezek 13:7); *magen-shield-of-abram* (the *anokhi magen lakh* divine self-introduction, the *magen*-of-the-fathers epithet recurring at Deut 33:29, 2 Sam 22:3, 22:31, Ps 3:4, 18:3, 28:7, 33:20, 84:12, 115:9-11, 144:2, Prov 30:5). The compound divine-name *adonai-yhwh-divine-name* at vv 2, 8 is the first patriarchal occurrence of the masoretic *qere perpetuum* substitution (*YHWH* with Elohim-vowels where *Adonai* already precedes), becoming a fixed Ezekielian formula. The highest-reception-weight verse in the chapter is *aman-tzedakah-counted-as-righteousness* at v 6 — the Hebrew lexical core (*he'emin* hiphil of *aman*; *yachshebeha lo tzedakah*; the ambiguous feminine antecedent) is direct, with the downstream Pauline reception (Rom 4:3, 4:9, 4:22; Gal 3:6; James 2:23) and the Reformation justification-by-faith superstructure flagged as downstream-Greek-and-Latin reception and NOT preempted by the translation. The covenant-cutting ritual block contributes the most lens-load-bearing entries: *brit-bein-ha-betarim* (vv 9-11, 17; the ANE parallels at Mari ARM II 37/II 80, Alalakh AT 1/76, the Sefire treaties KAI 222-224, Jer 34:18-20; Weinfeld 1970 *JAOS* 90, Mendenhall 1955, Hillers 1969, McCarthy 1978); *tardemah-divine-induced-deep-sleep* at v 12 (back-reference to Gen 2:21 Adam; forward to 1 Sam 26:12, Job 4:13, Job 33:15, Isa 29:10; LXX *ekstasis*); the *tanur-ashan-lappid-esh-theophany* at v 17 (the chapter's keystone fire-and-smoke theophany; cross-references to Exod 19:18 Sinai, Exod 13:21 pillar-of-fire, Isa 6:4, Ezek 1; the unilateral-passage-while-Abram-sleeps establishing the Yahweh-only covenant). The chronology block at vv 13-16 contributes *400-years-affliction-prophecy* (the 400 / 430 chronology dispute across Gen 15:13, Exod 12:40, Acts 7:6, Gal 3:17; the LXX/Samaritan harmonization; the four-generation-vs-400-years tension at v 16) and *avon-ha-emori-iniquity-not-yet-complete* at v 16 (the *lo shalem avon ha-Emori* cup-of-iniquity formula, Deut 9:4-5 / Lev 18:24-28 / Lev 20:23 cross-references, the 1 Thess 2:16 Pauline downstream reception). The covenant-formula and land-promise at v 18 contributes *karat-brit-cut-covenant* (the standard Hebrew covenant-cutting idiom, ~80 cross-corpus occurrences) and *ten-nations-of-canaan* (the unique ten-fold enumeration at vv 19-21 — the Qeini/Qenizi/Qadmoni triad unique to this verse, with the maximal Euphrates land-claim of v 18 as the geographical pairing). The chapter is high-lens-leverage but the lens stays in the apparatus: the translation reads as defensible scholarly English (matching ASV at every divergence point), while the glossary apparatus and per-verse commentary carry the lens-relevant material — the unilateral-divine-passage at v 17 with Abram in *tardemah*, the cross-corpus fire-theophany corpus, the *tardemah* divine-revelation chain back to Gen 2:21, and the ANE covenant-cutting parallels. The Pauline reception of v 6 and of v 16 is flagged-but-not-preempted at every occurrence. // **v2.13.0 continues the patriarchal narratives into Genesis 16** — Hagar and Ishmael, and the first occurrence of *ma'lakh YHWH* (messenger of YHWH) in the Hebrew Bible. Eight new entries cover the chapter's keystone vocabulary. The chapter's highest-lens-leverage entry is *malakh-yhwh-messenger* (claim_type=direct): the inaugural occurrence of the messenger-of-YHWH figure, with the three modern critical positions on messenger-identity presented in parallel (Heidt 1949's distinct-envoy reading, Westermann 1985 / von Rad 1972's identity reading, Hamori 2008 / Sommer 2009 / MacDonald 2009's literary-theological-device reading) and none preempted; the WoH translation choice *messenger of YHWH* (preserving the Hebrew etymology over the conventional *angel*) is editorially neutral on the identity question. The cross-corpus distribution of the figure is documented at 60+ verses across Genesis, Exodus, Numbers, Joshua, Judges, Samuel-Kings, Isaiah, Zechariah, Psalms. The post-exilic developed angelology is flagged as a distinct stratum. The chapter's ANE-comparative entry is *shifchah-substitute-wife-arrangement* (claim_type=direct): the substitute-wife legal institution attested at Code of Hammurabi §146 and Nuzi HSS V 67 (Speiser 1964 reading the Nuzi parallels as closer in detail than the Hammurabi); the lexeme governs both the Gen 16 Hagar narrative and the Gen 30 Bilhah/Zilpah narratives. The forensic-speech-act entry *chamas-forensic-curse-formula* (claim_type=direct) covers Sarai's v 5 declaration *chamasi alekha* paired with *yishpot YHWH beyni u-veynekha* — the *chamas* register and the YHWH-litigation idiom together constituting a formal legal speech-act, with cross-corpus distribution across the prophetic-indictment corpus. The Hebrew-grammatical entry *harbah-arbeh-doubled-infinitive-intensive* (claim_type=direct) covers the doubled-infinitive-absolute intensifying construction at v 10 and its corpus-wide distribution (Gen 2:16-17, 3:16, 17:2, 17:20, 22:17, etc.); the *rbh* doubled-infinitive subset is concentrated in the patriarchal seed-multiplication promises, with Gen 16:10 the only such promise addressed to a woman and a non-Israelite. The name-etymology entry *yishmael-name-etymology* (claim_type=direct) covers the first in-text patriarchal name-etymology in the Hebrew Bible and its place in the Yishma'el / Shim'on / Re'uven shared-verb cluster; the Knauf 1989 *Ismael* North-Arabian-tribal historical reconstruction is included as direct documentary background. The ethnographic-and-reception entry *pere-adam-wild-ass-prophecy* (claim_type=direct) is the chapter's most carefully framed entry: the wild-ass-of-a-man hapax compound is read on its Hebrew-philological terms (the *pere*-pastoralist register supported by Job 39:5-8 and the wider corpus), with the modern critical consensus (Westermann/Sarna/Wenham/Hamilton on the wilderness-pastoralist mode-of-life reading) explicitly endorsed and the Islamic reception (Sura 14:39, 19:54-55; the early-Islamic tafsir tradition's positive desert-freedom reading; Gen Rab on Ishmael) flagged but not preempted, AND the 19th-20th century Orientalist racializing receptions explicitly named for editorial caution and rejected as not supported by the Hebrew (Said 1978). The textual-variant entry *el-roi-and-halom-elohim-variant* (claim_type=direct) covers the dual crux of v 13: the divine name *El Ro'i* (the first divine-name-naming by a woman in the Hebrew Bible, and by a non-Israelite Egyptian woman at that; Trible 1984, Reis 2002) and the MT *halom* / LXX-emended *Elohim* textual variant, with the seeing-God-and-living anxiety parallels at Exod 33:20, Judg 6:22-23, Judg 13:22 documented; the WoH choice preserves the MT *halom* over the *Elohim* emendation, consistent with the established WoH textual-conservative practice. The well-naming entry *beer-lachai-roi* (claim_type=direct) covers the patriarchal well-naming pattern (Be'er-Sheva, Eseq/Sitnah/Rechovot, Marah, etc.) and the cross-corpus recurrence of the Be'er Lachai Ro'i well at Gen 24:62 and 25:11 (Isaac settling at the Hagar-encounter site). The chapter is mid-to-high lens-leverage but the lens stays in the apparatus: the translation reads as defensible scholarly English (matching ASV at every divergence point, including the *halom* preservation and the geographical-ambiguity preservation of *al-pnei kol echav yishkon* at v 12), while the glossary apparatus and per-verse commentary carry the lens-relevant material — the modern critical messenger-identity scholarship, the ANE substitute-wife legal background, the Knauf 1989 Ishmaelite-confederation historical reconstruction, the seeing-God-and-living anxiety formulae corpus, and the foreign-woman-cultic-site register. The chapter's *malakh-yhwh-messenger* entry is the highest-lens-leverage call of the chapter and is held to strict accuracy-above-lens discipline: the three modern critical positions are presented in parallel and none is preempted; project-specific synthesis is reserved for the wiki and long-form material downstream. // **v2.14.0 continues the patriarchal narratives into Genesis 17** — the *El Shaddai* self-introduction, the *brit milah* (covenant of circumcision), the Avram → Avraham and Sarai → Sarah name-changes, and the Yitzchaq promise. Nine new entries cover the chapter's keystone vocabulary, and three existing entries are expanded in *appliesTo* to cover the Gen 17 occurrences (additive-only, no semver-major modifications). The chapter's two highest-lens-leverage entries are *el-shaddai* and *brit-milah-circumcision-covenant*, both held to strict accuracy-above-lens discipline. The *el-shaddai* entry presents the first patriarchal occurrence of the name (recurring at Gen 28:3, 35:11, 43:14, 48:3, 49:25) with three modern critical etymologies in parallel — the traditional *shadad* / *destroy-be-powerful* reading behind LXX *pantokrator* and Vulgate *omnipotens*; the Akkadian *šadû* / *mountain* reading of Cross 1973 and Albright 1935; the West-Semitic divine-epithet *Šaddai* reading of Ouellette 1969 and Hess 2007, with the Deir ʿAlla *shdyn* inscription as direct documentary support. The P-document programmatic harmonization at Exod 6:3 (*I appeared to the patriarchs as El Shaddai but by my name YHWH I was not known to them*) is documented as the source-critical frame. The translation preserves *El Shaddai* over ASV's *God Almighty*, matching the WoH practice with *El Elyon* (Gen 14:22) and *El Ro'i* (Gen 16:13). The *brit-milah-circumcision-covenant* entry covers the institution comprehensively: the four canonical legal elements (eighth-day timing, household-slave inclusion, *karet* sanction, covenant-in-flesh formula); the ANE comparative material (Egyptian circumcision from the Sixth Dynasty, Herodotus II.36-37, Karnak depictions, Sasson *JBL* 85 [1966], Bergmann 1968); the documentary-hypothesis stratification (P-document distinctive Abrahamic-covenant marker vs. J/E's covenant-between-the-pieces of Gen 15; Friedman 1987, Knohl 1995); the cross-corpus distribution (Gen 21:4, Gen 34, Exod 4:24-26, Exod 12:43-49, Lev 12:3, Josh 5:2-9, 1 Sam 17:26+, Jer 9:24-25); and the downstream receptions — Maccabean revolt (1 Macc 1:48, 60-61), Pauline anti-circumcision (Galatians 2-5, Romans 2:25-29 and 4:9-12), and rabbinic tradition (Cohen 1996) — all flagged-not-preempted and held in mainstream HB-scholarship register without Jewish-vs-Christian supersessionist framing. The *karet-penalty-cut-off-from-people* entry covers the standard P/H covenant-violation sanction across ~30 Pentateuchal occurrences with the four positions on the penalty's nature (Milgrom 1991 divine-direct, Phillips 1970 excommunication, Tigay JPS Deut 1996 premature death, Levine JPS Lev 1989 extinction of line) and the Wold 1979 monograph as standard reference. The chapter's three name-related entries — *avraham-name-etymology* (the dialectal-variant vs folk-etymology dispute; Speiser AB 1964 and Albright BASOR 163 [1961] for the *Abi-raham* dialectal-variant reading; Sarna JPS 1989 for the in-text *av-hamon* folk-etymology), *sarah-name-change* (the morphological yod / he distinction, both names from *s-r-r* / *princess*; Sarna JPS 1989), and *yitzchaq-name-etymology-laughter-pattern* (the three laughter-Isaac plays at 17:17 / 18:12-15 / 21:6, the disbelief-to-joy inversion) — cover the chapter's three name-act moments. Three further entries cover the smaller items: *tamim-walk-before-god-covenant* (the *hithallekh lefanai veheyeh tamim* formula at v 1 with cross-corpus distribution Gen 5:22, 6:9, 24:40, Deut 18:13, 1 Kgs 8:61, Ps 15:2, 101:6, 119:1); *natan-brit-covenant-verb-cluster* (the *natan brit* / *karat brit* / *heqim brit* / *zakhar brit* covenant-verb cluster, with Gen 17:2 and Num 25:12 and Isa 42:6, 49:8 as the *natan brit* sub-distribution); *twelve-princes-of-ishmael* (forward-applying to Gen 25:13-16, with the Knauf 1989 North-Arabian-tribal confederation reconstruction and Neo-Assyrian/Babylonian tribal-name overlaps); and *b-etzem-ha-yom-ha-ze-P-formula* (the same-day-compliance formula across Gen 7:13, Exod 12:17/41/51, Lev 23:14/21/28/29/30, Deut 32:48, Josh 5:11, Ezek 2:3/24:2/40:1 — a P-document compositional marker). The three existing entries expanded in *appliesTo*: *brit-olam* (from GEN-9:16 only, expanded to include the patriarchal occurrences at GEN-17:7, 17:13, 17:19); *meqim-brit* (from GEN-9:9 and GEN-9:11, expanded to GEN-17:7, 17:19, 17:21); *yishmael-name-etymology* (existing list expanded to include the Gen 17 occurrences at 17:18, 17:20, 17:23, 17:25, 17:26). The chapter is mid-to-high lens-leverage but the lens stays in the apparatus: the translation reads as defensible scholarly English (matching ASV at every divergence point, including *El Shaddai* preserved over *God Almighty*, *tamim* rendered *blameless* rather than *perfect*, *nefesh* preserved per chapter-2 convention, and the patriarchal name-transliterations *Avraham* / *Sarah* / *Yitzchaq* / *Yishma'el* preserved). The apparatus carries the lens-relevant material — the West-Semitic patriarchal-El cult inheritance behind *El Shaddai*, the P-document harmonization claim at Exod 6:3, the ANE comparative circumcision evidence, the Knauf 1989 North-Arabian Ishmaelite-confederation reconstruction. The translation does not take a Jewish-vs-Christian supersessionist position on circumcision; the entry treats the institution in its Hebrew-Bible register and flags the downstream receptions as downstream. // **v2.15.0 continues the patriarchal narratives into Genesis 18** — the three visitors at the oaks of Mamre and Avraham's intercession for Sodom, the highest-lens-leverage theophany in the Abraham cycle. Eleven new entries cover the chapter's keystone vocabulary. The chapter's defining feature is the singular-plural divine-presentation oscillation that the *shloshah-anashim-three-visitors-theophany* entry documents at length: YHWH (sg) appears at v 1 → three men (pl) appear at v 2 → Avraham addresses *adonai* (sg) at v 3 → plural imperatives from v 4 → *they ate* (pl) at v 8 → *he said* (sg) at v 10 → YHWH named again at v 13 → *the men* (pl) rise at v 16 → at v 22 the figures split, with the men going on to Sodom (the two who arrive there as *shnei ha-malakhim* at Gen 19:1; cross-reference to *malakh-yhwh-messenger*) while Avraham stands before YHWH (sg). The entry presents the three modern critical positions on the singular-plural oscillation in parallel (Westermann's J-document theophany, Sarna's literary-effect, Letellier's chiastic Mamre/Sodom diptych) and the two anthropomorphic-realism / fluidity readings (Hamori 2008, Sommer 2009), and flags the Christian-tradition reception (Heb 13:2 *hospitality of strangers*; Augustine *De Trinitate* II.10-11; the patristic and Eastern-Orthodox *Trinity of Mamre* tradition culminating in Andrei Rublev's icon of 1411) as downstream-not-preempted. The *divine-eating-anthropomorphism* entry covers the chapter's most concretely anthropomorphic moment (v 8 *va-yokhelu* — *and they ate*), with the corpus-internal contrasts (Gen 19:3 unhedged eating at Lot's; Judg 13:15-20 explicit refusal-to-eat at Manoah's; Tob 12:19 LXX explicit Second-Temple harmonizing gloss) and the Hamori-Sommer anthropomorphic-realism reading. The *ka-et-chayah-time-of-life* entry covers the rare temporal formula at v 10 and v 14, with the parallel-promise-to-the-barren scene at 2 Kgs 4:16-17 (Elisha to the Shunammite). The *hayipale-davar-formula* entry covers the load-bearing theological challenge at v 14 (*ha-yipale me-YHWH davar* — *is anything too wonderful for YHWH?*), with the Hebrew-Bible cross-corpus distribution (Deut 17:8, 30:11; Jer 32:17, 32:27; Zech 8:6) and the Lukan reception at Luke 1:37 (the angel Gabriel to Mary, LXX-aligned word-for-word) flagged as downstream Greek. The *tzedakah-u-mishpat-justice-pair* entry covers the first occurrence of the foundational HB justice-formula at v 19, with the prophetic-and-Psalter cross-corpus distribution (Amos 5:24; Isa 1:21, 5:7, 28:17; Jer 22:3, 22:15, 23:5; Ezek 18, 33, 45; Ps 33, 72, 89, 97, 99, 103; Mic 6:8 with *chesed*; Prov 1, 2, 8, 21; Weinfeld 1995 hendiadys-reading). The *zaaqah-outcry-of-the-oppressed* entry covers the forensic-cry lexeme at vv 20-21, with the load-bearing cross-corpus distribution (Gen 4:10 Abel's blood; Exod 2:23-25, 3:7-9 Israel in Egypt; Exod 22:22-24 widow-and-orphan; 1 Sam 9:16; James 5:4) and the *outcry against Sodom* (cry of the oppressed) consensus reading corroborated by Ezek 16:49. The *divine-descent-to-investigate-pattern* entry covers the *erdah-na v-er'eh* formula at v 21 as a recurring divine-descent pattern across the primeval and patriarchal narratives (Gen 11:5, 11:7 Babel; Exod 3:8 burning bush; Exod 19:11, 18, 20 Sinai; Mic 1:3; Isa 64:1); the lens-relevance is significant but the entry stays in mainstream scholarly register. The *tiqun-soferim-yhwh-stood-before-abraham* entry covers the canonical scribal-emendation tradition at v 22 (the original *YHWH still stood before Avraham* preserved in the Masoretic apparatus, emended to *Avraham still stood before YHWH* for reverence), with the standard list of eighteen *tiqune soferim*, the canonical McCarthy 1981 monograph, the skeptical Tov 2012 / Würthwein 1995 textual-critical position, and the rabbinic-source citations (b. Megillah 25b; Tanchuma; Bereshit Rabbah 49:7; Mekhilta Shirta 6). The *abraham-intercession-bargaining* entry covers the 50→45→40→30→20→10 sequence at vv 22-33, the foundational HB intercession-pericope, with the *ha-shofet kol ha-aretz* universal-judge formula at v 25 (Brueggemann 1997 on the *anti-divine-injustice* prophetic register) and the cross-corpus intercession-tradition (Exod 32:11-14 Moses for Israel; Num 14:13-20; 1 Sam 12:23; Job 42:8-10; Amos 7:1-9; Ezek 9:8, 11:13). The *afar-va-efer-creatural-humility* entry covers the *dust and ashes* formula at v 27, the rare compound (only three HB occurrences: Gen 18:27, Job 30:19, Job 42:6), with cross-reference to the existing *em-as-v-nikhamti-al-afar-va-efer* Job entry and the Second-Temple deuterocanonical parallels (Sir 10:9; 17:32; 40:3). The *puncta-extraordinaria-v9-elav* entry covers the supralinear-dots Masoretic notation at v 9 (one of the canonical fifteen *puncta extraordinaria*), with the two readings in parallel (the traditional rabbinic textual-reservation reading from Soferim 6:3 / Bereshit Rabbah 48:15 / b. Bava Metzia 87a; the modern text-critical editorial-flag reading from Tov 2012, McCarthy 1981, Yeivin 1980). The chapter is the highest-lens-leverage chapter in the Abraham cycle to date, but the lens stays in the apparatus: the translation reads as defensible scholarly English (matching ASV-baseline at every divergence point, including the unhedged *and they ate*, the literal singular-plural oscillation preservation, and the MT *Avraham still stood before YHWH*); the glossary apparatus carries the lens-relevant material — the modern critical theophany scholarship, the anthropomorphic-realism and fluidity readings, the recurring divine-descent pattern, the cross-corpus justice-and-outcry formulae, and the Masoretic-emendation tradition. The Christian-Trinitarian and hospitality-of-strangers receptions are flagged but not preempted. // **v2.16.0 continues the patriarchal narratives into Genesis 19** — the destruction of Sodom and Amorah, Lot's rescue, the daughters' incest, and the Moab/Ammon etiology. The most physically-described divine destruction event in the Hebrew Bible. Eleven new entries cover the chapter's keystone vocabulary, and four existing entries are expanded in *appliesTo* to cover the Gen 19 occurrences (additive-only, no semver-major modifications): *malakh-yhwh-messenger* (+GEN-19:1, GEN-19:15 — the *shnei ha-malakhim* who are the two of Gen 18's three figures continuing on to Sodom), *shloshah-anashim-three-visitors-theophany* (+GEN-19:1 — the arithmetic resolution of the Gen 18 split), *divine-eating-anthropomorphism* (already covered GEN-19:3, no change needed), *yitzchaq-name-etymology-laughter-pattern* (+GEN-19:14 — the piel-mockery *m'tzacheq* in the wider patriarchal *tzachaq* cluster), and *vayyizkor-elohim* (+GEN-19:29, GEN-30:22, EXOD-2:24 — the broader *zakhar*-with-divine-subject cluster). The chapter's eleven new entries: *sodom-as-sodomy-reception-vs-inhospitality* (the reception-history flashpoint of the entire chapter, presenting the patristic / medieval *sodomy* reading and the modern critical *gang-rape-violence and inhospitality* reading in parallel and preempting neither; Carden 2004, Tornkvist 1998, Letellier 1995, Loader 1990; with Ezek 16:48-50 decisive evidence for the inhospitality reading and Judg 19 the canonical parallel-scene); *lot-offers-daughters-modern-ethical-reading* (the modern feminist-critical reception of Lot's daughter-offer at v 8; Trible 1984 *Texts of Terror*, Fewell and Gunn 1993, Bach 1997/1999, Exum 1996, with the ancient ANE hospitality-and-honor reading also documented); *sanverim-blindness-hapax* (the hapax *sanverim* of v 11, recurring only at 2 Kgs 6:18 — Elisha and the Aramean army; the possible Akkadian *šunwurum* dazzling-blindness etymology); *chemlat-yhwh-compassion-formula* (the lexeme *chemlah* at v 16 distinct from *chesed* and *rachamim*; cross-corpus 2 Sam 12:4, Mal 3:17, Joel 2:18, Jon 4:10-11, Ezek 9:5); *al-tabit-acharekha-do-not-look-back* (the do-not-look-back prohibition at v 17, with the Luke 17:32 NT recall *remember Lot's wife* and the comparative ANE / Greek pattern at Orpheus, Inanna's descent, and Adapa — flagged as comparative mythological observation not as direct dependence); *gophrit-va-esh-destruction-formula* (the *sulfur and fire* canonical HB destruction-formula at v 24 — one of the most cross-corpus-reachable entries in the glossary: Deut 29:22, Job 18:15, Isa 30:33, 34:9, Ezek 38:22, Ps 11:6; NT recall at Luke 17:29 and the four Revelation *lake of fire and sulfur* verses 14:10, 19:20, 20:10, 21:8); *me-et-yhwh-min-ha-shamayim-two-powers-debate* (the doubled *YHWH* in v 24 — the foundational text of the rabbinic two-powers debate at b. Sanhedrin 38b, and of Justin Martyr's binitarian Logos-exegesis at *Dialogue with Trypho* 56-59; modern scholarly Segal 1977, Boyarin 2004 and 2012; high lens-leverage entry held in strict accuracy-above-lens discipline, the lens stays in the wiki); *mahapekhah-overthrowing-formula* (the *hafakh / mahapekhah* overthrow-verb at v 25 — the standard HB designation for the Sodom event; cross-corpus chain Deut 29:22, Isa 1:7 and 13:19, Jer 49:18 and 50:40, Amos 4:11, Lam 4:6); *netziv-melach-pillar-of-salt* (the *pillar of salt* etiology at v 26 — the Mt. Sodom / Jabal Usdum halite formations, Josephus *Ant.* I.11.4, the Bab edh-Dhra Bronze-Age destruction archaeology of Rast and Schaub 1974 / 2003); *qitor-ha-kivshan-furnace-smoke-comparison* (the furnace-smoke comparison at v 28 — one of the most physically concrete destruction-images in the HB, with the cross-corpus chain to Exod 9:8 and the editorially significant verbatim echo at Exod 19:18 *as the smoke of the furnace* of the Sinai theophany; the Tall el-Hammam Late-Bronze-Age cosmic-airburst hypothesis of Bunch et al. 2021 cited as scientific hypothesis, not as project-distinctive synthesis; high lens-leverage entry held in mainstream scientific register); *moab-ammon-etiology-incest* (the folk-etymologies of Moab from *me-av* and Ben-Ammi from *ben ammi* at vv 37-38 — the canonical HB polemic-against-neighbor-peoples; Deut 23:4-5 exclusion from the assembly, the foundational tension of the book of Ruth's Davidic genealogy, and 1 Kgs 11 / 2 Kgs 3 / Neh 13:1-3 reception; Mesha Stele evidence the etymology is polemical-etiological not philological-historical; Bauer 1999, Sarna, Westermann). The chapter is the most physically-described divine destruction event in the HB and is high lens-leverage at two verses (v 24 *me-et YHWH min ha-shamayim*; v 28 *qitor ha-kivshan*), but the lens stays strictly in the apparatus — the translation reads as defensible scholarly English (ASV-baseline at every divergence point), and the two highest-leverage entries (*me-et-yhwh-min-ha-shamayim-two-powers-debate*, *qitor-ha-kivshan-furnace-smoke-comparison*) are held in mainstream rabbinic / patristic / modern critical / scientific register. Project-distinctive WoH synthesis (the Tall el-Hammam impact hypothesis as evidence for technological-civilization destruction; the two-powers debate as evidence for the Elohim-plurality reading) is reserved for the wiki and long-form material downstream. The reception-history of the sodom-as-sodomy reading and the modern feminist-critical reading of Lot's daughter-offer are presented in parallel without preempting either. // **v2.17.0 continues the patriarchal narratives into Genesis 20** — the second wife-sister type-scene, Avraham at Gerar with Avimelekh. Largely a glossary-recycling chapter (the [wife-sister-type-scene](#wife-sister-type-scene), [imri-na-achoti-at](#imri-na-achoti-at), [elohim-as-translation](#elohim-as-translation), and [eretz](#eretz) entries drive most of the chapter's lexical decisions), but it contains **two foundational firsts** that demand dedicated entries: the **first occurrence in the Hebrew Bible of *navi* (prophet)** at v 7, applied to Avraham and defining the prophetic role as intercession (the *navi-first-occurrence-abraham* entry, with cross-corpus distribution to Exod 32, Num 11, 14, 1 Sam 7, 12, Jer 7, 11, 14, Amos 7); and the **first occurrence of *hithpallel* (to pray, intercede)** at v 17, performing the function v 7 defined (the *hithpallel-first-prayer-occurrence* entry, forward-loaded to Num 11/21, 1 Sam 1/7/8/12, 1 Kgs 8, 2 Kgs 19/20, Jer 7/11/14, Dan 9, Neh 1/2; Greenberg 1983, Miller 1994, Balentine 1993). The two firsts coincide structurally at the single chapter — the prophetic role and the prophetic function are named together. Five further entries cover the chapter's keystone vocabulary: *divine-dream-communication-formula* (v 3 *ba-chalom ha-lailah*, the first night-dream-revelation formula in the HB, cross-corpus to Gen 28/31/37/40-41 and 1 Kgs 3:5; Husser 1999, Lichtenstein 1968 JANES 1); *b-tom-levav-u-v-niqyon-kappai-moral-innocence* (v 5, the doubled-noun forensic-moral-innocence idiom; cross-corpus to Ps 24:4, 26:6, 73:13, Job 22:30); *yirat-elohim-moral-restraint-motif* (v 11, the fear-of-the-Elohim chain across Gen 22:12 [Aqedah], Gen 42:18 [Joseph], Exod 1:17 [midwives], Exod 20:20 [Sinai], Job 1:1, Prov 1:7, etc.; Becker 1965); *kesut-eynayim-vindication-formula* (v 16, the hapax with the three-reading dispute resolved to the Sarna/Speiser/ASV vindication-default; Westermann); and *atzar-rechem-closed-wombs-divine-sanction* (v 18, the doubled-verb *atzor atzar* and the matriarchal-barrenness pattern across Gen 11:30, 25:21, 29:31, Judg 13:2-3, 1 Sam 1:5-6; Brichto 1973 HUCA). The *wife-sister-type-scene* entry's *appliesTo* was already locked at v2.9.0 to cover the Gen 20 verses (no expansion needed). The *imri-na-achoti-at* entry's *appliesTo* already covers GEN-20:2 and GEN-20:12 (no expansion needed). One v 13 *Elohim*-with-plural-verb-agreement datum (*hit'u Elohim* — *they caused me to wander*) is documented in commentary only and flagged for future Reviewer attention on whether a project-policy entry on plural-verb cases should be settled; the *eshet-yefat-mareh* entry is not extended to Gen 20 (the chapter does not invoke the beauty-formula). The chapter is largely glossary-recycling; the lens-relevance is structural rather than philological, with the foreign-king-as-moral-voice inversion at v 4 and the dramatic-irony of v 11 doing the chapter's principal interpretive work. The two foundational firsts are the chapter's permanent contribution to the corpus.

terms.standardEnglish: In the beginning
terms.rationale: The Hebrew is a construct form without a definite article — בְּרֵאשִׁית means 'in beginning-of', not 'in the beginning' (which would require בָּרֵאשִׁית). The verse is grammatically a temporal clause introducing v2, not a freestanding declaration. The Rashi-favoured 'when Elohim began to create' reading (also adopted by NJPS, NRSV, and the major modern scholarly translations) preserves this structure. The traditional 'In the beginning' is a Christian-tradition reading that fixes a doctrine of creatio ex nihilo onto a Hebrew that doesn't grammatically support it. Restoring the construct sharpens the WoH lens: the text describes a project beginning, not the absolute origin of all matter.

terms.standardEnglish: God
terms.rationale:
The form is grammatically masculine plural (-im ending). The translation 'God' (singular) is a theological smoothing inherited from the LXX's choice to render with singular Θεός and confirmed by Vulgate's Deus. The plural form is preserved as 'Elohim' (untranslated) per existing WoH convention (see content/i18n/glossary.json #elohim). Plural verbs and pronouns governing Elohim are flagged in their own entries — see na'aseh.

**Article convention.** In narrative passages the bare proper-name form 'Elohim said' reads to English speakers as a singular proper noun (like 'Yahweh said'), which silently re-imports the singular smoothing the WoH translation is explicitly rejecting. Rendering as 'the Elohim said' treats Elohim as a plural class-noun in English (parallel to 'the Egyptians', 'the Anunnaki') and carries the plural sense visibly in every occurrence. The article is dropped only inside the compound proper name YHWH-Elohim (Genesis 2:4 onward), which functions as a single divine name. Pronouns and verb agreement still mirror the Hebrew: where the Hebrew governs Elohim with singular verbs/pronouns (e.g., בְּצַלְמוֹ b'tsalmo 'in his image' in 1:27), the English keeps the singular ('his/he'); where the Hebrew breaks into plural (e.g., the cohortative נַעֲשֶׂה na'aseh 'let us make' in 1:26), the English follows ('us/our'). The agreement seam is the text's own, preserved rather than smoothed.

terms.standardEnglish: create
terms.rationale: בָּרָא in the Qal is one of three distinct creation verbs in Genesis 1 (with עָשָׂה asah 'make' and יָצַר yatsar 'form'). All three are flattened to 'create/make' in the canonical English translations, losing a deliberate distinction. בָּרָא specifically denotes shaping or fashioning — its qal usage is reserved for Elohim's action throughout the Hebrew Bible (Gesenius §17), which has often been read theologically as 'create ex nihilo'. The lexical core, however, is the act of shaping — compare the cognate בָּרָא in the Piel meaning 'cut out, fashion'. Rendering as 'shape' preserves the verb's distinctness from asah without inserting a metaphysical claim either way.

terms.standardEnglish: made
terms.rationale: עָשָׂה is the general Hebrew verb of making/doing across the corpus. In Gen 1 it is used where bara would be theologically over-specified — for the firmament (v7), the two lights (v16), the beasts (v25), and humanity (v26 'let us make'). The standard 'made' is correct; the choice point is keeping it lexically distinct from bara/yatsar so the reader can see the deliberate verb pattern. Where the canonical translations interchange bara and asah ('created the great sea-monsters' / 'made the firmament') the WoH translation preserves them apart.

terms.standardEnglish: heaven
terms.rationale: שָׁמַיִם is grammatically dual (the -ayim ending). Hebrew has no singular form for sky/heaven — the dual is the lexical default. Rendering as singular 'heaven' (as KJV/ASV do) imports a later theological 'heaven-the-realm' concept onto a word that simply means 'the skies' in the physical sense. 'Skies' is closer to the cosmological register Gen 1 actually operates in and preserves the dual implication. Where the text demands a more numinous register (e.g. כֹּכְבֵי הַשָּׁמַיִם 'stars of the skies'), the rendering scales accordingly.

terms.standardEnglish: earth
terms.rationale: אֶרֶץ in Hebrew covers a spectrum from 'patch of ground' to 'inhabited territory' to 'the whole inhabited world'. The capital-E 'Earth' (the planet) is a 17th-century-onwards English meaning that has no clean Hebrew equivalent. In Gen 1, אֶרֶץ is paired with שָׁמַיִם — they're a hendiadys for 'the whole physical setting'. 'Land' preserves the Hebrew register; 'earth' (lowercase, when the meaning is the dry ground as opposed to seas) is acceptable in some verses, but the default WoH choice is 'land'. Where the verse explicitly contrasts 'land' (dry) with 'seas' (water) (e.g. v10), 'land' is the more accurate choice anyway.

terms.standardEnglish: without form, and void
terms.rationale: תֹהוּ וָבֹהוּ is an alliterative pair found together only here and in Jer 4:23 (where it describes a depopulated post-disaster landscape) and Isa 34:11 (similar). The KJV 'without form, and void' renders the pair philosophically (matter without form, Aristotelian); the Hebrew is concrete — a desolate, empty terrain. 'Wasteland and emptiness' preserves the alliterative pair (the -au / -ohu echo) and the concrete imagery. Cf. NJPS 'unformed and void', NRSV 'formless void' — closer to KJV than to the Hebrew. For the WoH lens, the choice matters: a 'wasteland and emptiness' is a pre-project site, not a metaphysical pre-form-and-matter chaos.

terms.standardEnglish: the deep
terms.rationale: תְהוֹם is etymologically and conceptually cognate with Akkadian tiāmtu (cf. Tiamat of Enuma Elish, where the goddess is the primordial salt-sea). The Hebrew has demythologized the term but the word is preserved. 'The deep' is the standard rendering and is correct. The WoH note pointing readers to the Tiamat parallel belongs in the methodology page, not in the translated text.

terms.standardEnglish: Spirit of God
terms.rationale: רוּחַ has three lexical fields: 'breath', 'wind', 'spirit' — the choice is context-driven and Hebrew does not disambiguate. The KJV/ASV 'Spirit of God' is a Trinitarian-tradition reading retrojected onto Gen 1:2; the Hebrew supports 'wind' (cf. NJPS 'a wind from God'), 'breath' (cf. several modern translations), or 'spirit' (the inherited choice). For the WoH lens, 'breath' is preferable: it preserves the physical/atmospheric register the verse operates in and avoids importing a doctrinal claim. The hovering verb in the next clause (merachefet, see entry) supports a physical reading — wind/breath hovers over water; 'Spirit' does not, in any natural register.

terms.standardEnglish: moved upon
terms.rationale: רחף in the Piel means to hover or flutter — Deut 32:11 uses it of a mother eagle over her young. The KJV 'moved upon' loses the specifically hovering aerial sense. 'Was hovering over' is the consensus modern reading (NJPS, NRSV, ESV) and preserves the bird-image. Combined with the ruach='breath/wind' reading in the same verse, the image is concrete and physical.

terms.standardEnglish: Let there be
terms.rationale: The jussive form expresses a command/wish. 'Let there be' is correct and is the standard rendering. Preserved unchanged.

terms.standardEnglish: light
terms.rationale: Standard rendering, unchanged. Note for methodology: the light of v3 is separate from the sun and moon of v14-16 (which haven't been positioned yet at v3). This is a textual feature the WoH lens should surface in the methodology, not paper over.

terms.standardEnglish: saw that it was good
terms.rationale: The formulaic phrase that closes each creation day. 'Good' is the correct rendering of טוֹב here. Preserved unchanged. The methodology note will track the formulaic structure — six days, six closings, with variations on day 2 (no 'good') and day 6 ('very good') as deliberate signals.

terms.standardEnglish: day
terms.rationale: Standard rendering. The 'how long is a day' question is a methodology-page discussion, not a translation choice — the Hebrew says יוֹם and the rendering says 'day'. The recurring formulaic close (vayehi erev vayehi voqer yom X 'and there was evening and there was morning, day X') is a structural feature the methodology surfaces.

terms.standardEnglish: firmament
terms.rationale: רָקִיעַ derives from the verb רקע 'to beat/hammer out (metal)'. The LXX renders στερέωμα 'solid/firm thing', whence Vulgate firmamentum, whence English 'firmament'. The Hebrew picture is of a solid dome hammered out across the sky — consistent with the ANE cosmology of a vault above the earth holding back the upper waters (cf. v6-7, where the raqia is explicitly placed 'between waters and waters'). 'Dome' (cf. NRSV) preserves this; 'firmament' is technically defensible via Latin but obscures the imagery; 'expanse' (ESV, NIV) is a modern softening that papers over the solid-dome cosmology. The WoH choice is 'dome' — accurate to the Hebrew picture; whether the picture corresponds to physical reality is a separate question the methodology can address.

terms.standardEnglish: waters
terms.rationale: Hebrew מַיִם is grammatically dual; there is no singular form. 'Waters' is correct; the plural carries.

terms.standardEnglish: lights
terms.rationale: מְאֹרֹת is a more technical word than אוֹר — it denotes a light-source object, not light itself. 'Luminaries' captures this; 'lights' (KJV/ASV) is acceptable but loses the source-object register. The methodology notes the deliberate avoidance of שֶׁמֶשׁ shemesh ('sun') and יָרֵחַ yareach ('moon') — both of which were divine names in the ANE polemical environment. The text describes the sun and moon as functional luminaries, not as the named deities.

terms.literal: the great luminary / the small luminary
terms.standardEnglish: the greater light / the lesser light
terms.wohChoice: the greater luminary / the lesser luminary
terms.rationale: The text deliberately refrains from using שֶׁמֶשׁ shemesh ('sun') and יָרֵחַ yareach ('moon') in this verse. Both shemesh and yareach were divinized in surrounding ANE traditions (Shamash, Sin, etc.). Genesis 1 names them only by function — 'the greater luminary' to rule day, 'the lesser luminary' to rule night. This is a deliberate polemical structure and preserving it in translation is non-optional for the WoH lens.

terms.standardEnglish: rule
terms.rationale: מֶמְשֶׁלֶת is a relatively technical political term — 'governance' or 'dominion' over a territory. 'Rule' (KJV) is acceptable but more general. The luminaries are explicitly granted מֶמְשֶׁלֶת — functional jurisdiction over day and night. 'Governance' surfaces the structural language of the verse: the cosmos is being organized into administrative jurisdictions assigned to functional bodies.

terms.standardEnglish: signs
terms.rationale: Standard rendering. The luminaries function as signs (ototh) and appointed times (moadim) — a technical, calendrical function. The methodology may note that this structural-instrumental role for the celestial bodies is part of the polemical demythologization of sun/moon/stars.

terms.standardEnglish: seasons
terms.rationale: מוֹעֲדִים specifically denotes fixed times — festivals, appointed assemblies, predetermined occasions. The KJV/ASV 'seasons' is a natural-cycles reading (spring/summer/autumn/winter); the Hebrew is more specifically calendrical — the technical liturgical and astronomical fixed-time vocabulary. 'Appointed times' preserves the precision and the calendrical-instrumental role of the luminaries.

terms.standardEnglish: whales / sea-monsters / great sea-creatures
terms.rationale: תַּנִּינִם is the standard Hebrew Bible word for the serpent-dragon class of creatures. BDB s.v. תַּנִּין: 'sea-monster, serpent, dragon'; HALOT: 'sea-monster, dragon, snake'. The lexical core is the serpent-dragon register; the word itself contains no aquatic morpheme — the sea-association in Gen 1:21 is positional (the verse sits between the swarming-waters of v20 and the blessing-of-the-seas of v22), not lexical. KJV renders the same word 'dragon(s)' across the corpus (Deut 32:33, Ps 91:13, Isa 13:22, Isa 27:1, Jer 9:11, Ezek 29:3, Mal 1:3, etc.); only Gen 1:21 gets the euphemistic 'whales'. Modern translations soften to 'sea-monsters' (ASV, NJPS, NRSV) or 'great sea-creatures' (NIV, ESV, Alter) for Gen 1:21 specifically, but the lexical case for the dragon rendering is the same here as in the prophets. The text is explicitly performing a Chaoskampf demythologization at this verse: the same dragon-class beings deified across the ANE — Akkadian Tiamat (etymologically cognate with תְהוֹם tehom in Gen 1:2), Ugaritic Lotan / Hebrew Leviathan, Egyptian Apep — are something Elohim shaped, and the deliberate switch from asah to bara (cf. the bara entry) is the demythologizing move. Translating as 'sea-creatures' obscures the polemic the verse exists to perform. Rendering as 'great dragons' preserves the lexical register and makes the demythologization visible in the translation itself: the reader sees the dragon-class creatures named, and sees Elohim shaping them rather than fighting them. Reclassified from direct to inferred in v2.0.0 because the choice between 'dragons' and the modern translation tradition's 'sea-creatures' / 'sea-monsters' is a genuine interpretive call.

terms.literal: living being / living throat / living self
terms.standardEnglish: living creature / living soul
terms.rationale: נֶפֶשׁ is famously hard — its semantic core is 'breath/throat/self/life', not a separable immortal soul. The KJV 'soul' (in Gen 2:7 for adam) is a Platonic-dualist reading retrojected; modern Hebrew Bible studies consensus is firmly 'being' or 'creature'. 'Living being' preserves the Hebrew register without inviting the Platonic reading.

terms.standardEnglish: after its kind
terms.rationale: מִין is 'kind' / 'species' in a non-modern-biological sense — a category of living things. 'According to its kind' is the standard modern rendering. The phrase recurs as a refrain through the creation of plants and animals and surfaces the categorical / sortal structure of the creation account.

terms.standardEnglish: Let us make man
terms.rationale:
נַעֲשֶׂה is the cohortative first-person *plural* of עָשָׂה — 'let us make'. This is grammatically unambiguous and is the central textual datum for any reading of Elohim as more-than-one. Standard theological glosses include (i) the royal/plural of majesty (ad hoc and not well-attested elsewhere in Hebrew for divine speech), (ii) Trinitarian pre-figuring (Christian retrojection), (iii) divine-council reading (cf. Job 1, Ps 82 — the consensus modern scholarly view), or (iv) the plural is to be read at face value (the WoH lens). The translation does not adjudicate; it preserves the plural literally — 'Let us make humankind'. The methodology page argues for the WoH reading.

אָדָם adam: human/humankind, not a personal name in Gen 1 (the named individual Adam appears later). Gendered KJV 'man' is corrected to 'humankind' (also NJPS/NRSV).

terms.standardEnglish: image / likeness
terms.rationale: צֶלֶם elsewhere denotes a physical statue or representation (Num 33:52, 1 Sam 6:5 — 'golden images'). דְּמוּת denotes a likeness or resemblance (Ezek 1:5,10 — 'a likeness as the appearance of a man'). The two words together emphasize physical resemblance and form, not abstract attribute. The traditional Jewish and Christian readings (man bears Elohim's spiritual or moral image) are interpretive extensions; the Hebrew lexicon supports a physical-resemblance reading directly. 'Image / likeness' is preserved as the standard rendering; the WoH methodology surfaces the physical-resemblance lexical core, which is central to the Elohim-as-physical-beings reading.

terms.source: וַיִּבְרָא אֱלֹהִים אֶת־הָאָדָם בְּצַלְמוֹ ... זָכָר וּנְקֵבָה בָּרָא אֹתָם
terms.translit: vayivra elohim et-ha'adam betsalmo ... zachar uneqevah bara otam
terms.literal: and-shaped Elohim humankind in-his-image ... male and-female he-shaped them
terms.standardEnglish: So God created man in his own image ... male and female created he them
terms.wohChoice: And Elohim shaped humankind in his image ... male and female he shaped them
terms.rationale:
Tension point: v26 has plural 'let us make' (na'aseh) but v27 has singular 'in his image' (betsalmo). The shift is real and is one of the textual features the methodology must surface — the WoH reading treats it as the difference between the deliberative project speech (plural) and the actual executing action (singular agency assigning the project) within the Elohim group. The standard translations preserve the singular here without comment; the WoH translation preserves it too but flags it in methodology.

זָכָר וּנְקֵבָה zachar uneqevah: male and female, biological-anatomical terms. The verse asserts a dimorphic human creation at the original act, not a sequential creation (Gen 2 will sequence). Preserved standardly.

terms.standardEnglish: Be fruitful, and multiply
terms.rationale: Standard rendering. The imperative pair is a deliberate parallel structure — preserved.

terms.standardEnglish: subdue it: and have dominion
terms.rationale: כבשׁ in qal: subdue, conquer, bring under. רדה in qal: rule, dominate, tread (in a winepress). Both verbs are strong and have been the subject of intense exegetical debate in environmental ethics (Lynn White Jr. 1967 thesis and after). The Hebrew verbs are not softened. The KJV 'have dominion' for redu is acceptable; 'rule' is closer. The WoH choice preserves the strength of both verbs without softening — the methodology page can address the interpretive question.

terms.standardEnglish: very good
terms.rationale: The closing formula of day 6. Standard rendering. Note the deliberate intensifier meod — six days of 'good' and a final 'very good' as a structural peak. The methodology surfaces this.

terms.standardEnglish: And the evening and the morning were
terms.wohChoice: And there was evening and there was morning
terms.rationale: Formulaic close of each numbered day. The 'evening first' ordering reflects the Hebrew calendrical convention (day begins at sunset). The repeated 'and there was X' (vayhi) preserves the cadence; the KJV 'And the evening and the morning were' loses the doubled vayhi structure. Modern translations and the WoH translation both preserve the doubled structure.

terms.standardEnglish: form
terms.rationale: יָצַר is the third of Genesis' creation verbs (alongside בָּרָא bara 'shape' and עָשָׂה asah 'make'). It is the potter's verb: same root as יוֹצֵר yotser 'potter' (Jer 18:2-6). The image is hands-on, material, shaping clay. Translating as 'form' preserves the manual register and distinguishes it from bara (categorical fashioning) and asah (general making). The verb anchors Genesis 2's anthropological account: YHWH-Elohim 'forms' the human from dust (v7) and 'forms' the animals from the ground (v19) — the same verb for the same kind of act.

terms.standardEnglish: the LORD God
terms.rationale: The compound name appears for the first time in Gen 2:4, marking the shift from the Priestly creation account (ch. 1, 'Elohim') to the Yahwist anthropological account (ch. 2-3, 'YHWH Elohim'). Standard English renders יְהוָה as 'the LORD' (small caps), following the Masoretic Qere-perpetuum (read 'Adonai' for the unpronounced Tetragrammaton) and the LXX's κύριος. This is a Second Temple-era reading convention, not a translation. The Hebrew is a proper name, the four consonants YHWH, traditionally Yahweh. WoH preserves the unpronounced consonants (YHWH) and the unsmoothed plural (Elohim), reflecting the seam between the two creation accounts rather than smoothing it under a single English 'God' or 'LORD'.

terms.standardEnglish: generations
terms.rationale: תוֹלְדוֹת from יָלַד 'to give birth' is the genealogical formula structuring the book of Genesis (2:4, 5:1, 6:9, 10:1, 11:10, 11:27, 25:12, 25:19, 36:1, 37:2). The literal sense is 'begettings' — what comes forth from. KJV/ASV 'generations' carries the right structural sense but reads archaic. NJPS 'this is the story of' and Alter 'this is the tale of' capture both the narrative and genealogical function. 'Tale' preserves the seam-marker function while reading clean in English. The first toledot (2:4) is structurally unusual: it births the skies and the land themselves, treating cosmos as a lineage.

terms.partOfSpeech: noun, masculine singular with definite article
terms.standardEnglish: Adam (as a proper name) or the man
terms.rationale: Throughout Genesis 2 and most of Genesis 3, the Hebrew is הָאָדָם — the definite article + adam — which grammatically means 'the human' as a category, not 'Adam' as a personal name. The article disappears (allowing read-as-name) only at Gen 4:25. Inserting 'Adam' as a personal name throughout chapters 2-3 (KJV, ASV, NIV) imports a name where the Hebrew has a category-noun. NJPS and most modern scholarly translations preserve 'the man'. WoH uses 'the human' for two reasons: (a) preserves the categorical-noun grammar, (b) keeps the door open to the wordplay with אֲדָמָה adamah (ground) — the human is the *one from the ground*, not yet sexed and not yet named.

terms.standardEnglish: man / ground
terms.rationale: Hebrew אָדָם adam (human) and אֲדָמָה adamah (ground, soil) share a root (אדם, 'red, ruddy') and are deliberately paired in Gen 2:7: the human is dust from the ground. The wordplay is structural — adam is etymologically 'the earth-being'. Standard English ('man'/'ground') buries the play. WoH preserves it as a recurring pair: 'the human' from 'the ground'. Alter's 'human / humus' captures the etymological link but reads awkward; 'human / ground' keeps the register clean while the pairing is documented in commentary.

terms.standardEnglish: breath of life
terms.rationale: נְשָׁמָה neshamah is distinct from רוּחַ ruach (breath/wind/spirit, Gen 1:2) and נֶפֶשׁ nefesh (living being, throat, appetite). Neshamah is specifically *exhaled* breath — what is breathed *out*, into nostrils. The verb here is נָפַח napach 'to blow', not נשׁף or נשׁב. The picture is hands-on, mouth-to-nostril, a craftsman animating his work — continuous with the potter's-verb yatsar in the same verse. Standard 'breath of life' is correct; the commentary surfaces the distinct lexeme so the three breath-words (ruach / neshamah / nefesh) don't collapse into one.

terms.standardEnglish: the Garden of Eden
terms.rationale: The preposition is בְּ be- 'in', not the construct relation that would yield 'of'. Eden is a region; the garden is *in* it. Gen 2:8 reads literally 'a garden in Eden, eastward'. The 'Garden of Eden' (KJV, ASV, NIV) treats Eden as a proper name modifying garden, but the Hebrew has gan 'garden' indefinite + b- 'in' + Eden (place). The original picture is more spatial: there is a place called Eden, and within it YHWH-Elohim plants a garden. Restoring the preposition restores the geography — the cosmic locale is Eden; the gardened part is one feature of it.

terms.source: עֵץ הַחַיִּים / עֵץ הַדַּעַת טוֹב וָרָע
terms.translit: etz hachayim / etz hada'at tov vara
terms.literal: tree of the lives / tree of the knowing of good and bad
terms.standardEnglish: tree of life / tree of the knowledge of good and evil
terms.wohChoice: tree of life / tree of knowing good and bad
terms.rationale: Two notes. First, דַּעַת da'at from יָדַע yada is *experiential* knowing — intimate acquaintance, not abstract cognition (the same verb covers sexual knowing in Gen 4:1). 'Knowing' rather than 'knowledge' keeps the verbal force. Second, טוֹב וָרָע tov vara is the merism for 'everything' — 'good and bad' covers the full moral and practical spectrum (Solomon's request in 1 Kgs 3:9 for the ability to *discern* tov vara is the same idiom and reads as 'wise judgment'). 'Good and evil' (KJV, ASV) imports a Manichaean polarity the Hebrew doesn't have; 'good and bad' is closer to the merism. The tree is the tree of *experiential acquaintance with the full range*.

terms.literal: to work it and to guard it
terms.standardEnglish: to dress it and to keep it
terms.wohChoice: to work it and to guard it
terms.rationale: Two verbs. עָבַד avad covers 'work, serve, till' — the same root as עֶבֶד eved 'servant'. שָׁמַר shamar covers 'keep, guard, watch over' — the same root as שׁוֹמֵר shomer 'guard, watchman'. KJV 'dress and keep' reads agricultural-pastoral; the Hebrew register is closer to 'work the land and guard it'. The human is placed as worker-and-watchman, not as owner. Note also the feminine suffix -ah on both verbs while גַּן gan is masculine — the suffix likely points back to אֲדָמָה adamah (feminine), making this stewardship of the ground itself, not just the gardened parcel.

terms.standardEnglish: you shall surely die
terms.rationale: Distinctively Hebrew construction: the infinitive absolute placed before its cognate finite verb intensifies — the meaning is 'you will most certainly die'. Same construction appears in v16 with אָכֹל תֹּאכֵל akhol tokhel ('you may freely eat'). Standard English 'surely die' is the right register. The interpretive question is *what kind* of death — same-day biological death (which doesn't happen), or a different death (mortality entering, separation from the garden state, the end of the unfallen condition). The grammar settles certainty but not kind; the commentary leaves the question open rather than picking one tradition.

terms.literal: help corresponding to / over against him
terms.standardEnglish: a help meet for him / a helper suitable for him
terms.rationale: Two words, each commonly mistranslated. עֵזֶר ezer is not 'helper-subordinate' — the same noun is used 16 times in the Hebrew Bible for *divine* help (Pss 33:20, 70:5, 121:1-2, etc.). It denotes powerful or critical aid, not assistant-class help. כְּנֶגְדּוֹ kenegdo is from נֶגֶד neged 'opposite, in front of, corresponding to' — the relation is face-to-face equality, not behind-and-supporting. The two words together describe a counterpart of matching standing. 'A help meet for him' (KJV) became 'helpmeet' in English usage and slid further into 'helpmate' — a slide the Hebrew never supports. WoH renders 'a counterpart fitting him' to recover both senses: equal standing (kenegdo) and substantive aid (ezer).

terms.standardEnglish: rib
terms.rationale: צֵלָע appears 41 times in the Hebrew Bible and *never* means 'rib' outside this passage's traditional reading. It is the architectural term for the *side* of structures: side of the Ark (Ex 25:12, 14), side of the Tabernacle (Ex 26:20, 26, 35), side of the altar (Ex 27:7), side of the Temple's holy place (1 Kgs 6:34). The LXX rendered πλευρά 'side', and the Vulgate costa is itself ambiguous in classical Latin (side or rib). 'Rib' is medieval anatomical guesswork that hardened into tradition. Reading 'side' rebalances the picture: YHWH-Elohim does not extract a single bone but takes one *side* of the human and builds a counterpart — the two becoming halves of a whole, which the wordplay of v23 (etzem 'bone', basar 'flesh') and v24 (basar echad 'one flesh') reinforces.

terms.standardEnglish: man / woman (KJV 'Woman ... Man')
terms.rationale: The wordplay in Gen 2:23 turns on the consonantal near-identity of אִישׁ ish ('man') and אִשָּׁה ishah ('woman') — the etymologies are actually separate (ish from a different root than ishah, whose root is אנשׁ), but the *sound* play is unmistakable, and the verse uses it to name. Most English translations capitalize the names ('Woman ... Man') to signal the naming function. WoH keeps the Hebrew transliterations (Ishah / Ish) in italics in v23 to preserve the sound-play that drives the verse. Elsewhere these words appear in their normal English equivalents.

terms.standardEnglish: one flesh
terms.rationale: The phrase concludes the narrative arc that began with the human being divided (tzela 'side' taken from him in v21-22): the marital bond is figured as the *re-unification* of what was divided. בָּשָׂר basar 'flesh' carries the full register — body, kin, common substance — so the phrase covers both physical union and kinship-by-substance. Standard 'one flesh' is correct; the commentary surfaces the structural inversion (one human divided into two becomes two re-merged into one) that the chapter is doing.

terms.standardEnglish: deep sleep
terms.rationale: תַּרְדֵּמָה is not ordinary sleep (שֵׁנָה shenah). It denotes a divinely-induced trance state, most famously in Gen 15:12, where it falls on Abram during the covenant theophany ('a deep sleep fell upon Abram, and behold, a horror of great darkness fell upon him'). Also in 1 Sam 26:12, Job 4:13, 33:15, Isa 29:10 — always with an oneiric, supernatural, or anaesthetic quality. The verb here is הִפִּיל hippil 'caused to fall', a hiphil — Elohim makes the trance descend. 'Deep sleep' is the conventional rendering and captures the unconsciousness; the commentary surfaces the trance-quality so the reader does not assume natural slumber.

terms.standardEnglish: the serpent ... subtle / crafty
terms.rationale: Gen 3:1 opens with a deliberate soundplay: עָרוּם arum 'shrewd, crafty' (describing the serpent) shares the consonants of עֲרוּמִּים arummim 'naked' (the closing word of 2:25, describing the human and his woman). The two adjectives are from different roots etymologically — arum from ערם 'be shrewd', arummim from ערה 'be bare' — but the sound-play is unmistakable and structural: the chapter bridges from a state of nakedness-without-shame to a confrontation with shrewdness-without-disclosure. KJV 'subtil' (now archaic) and later 'crafty/cunning' carry the negative cast that 'shrewd' delivers cleanly without import a moral pre-verdict. The serpent is *cleverer* than any field-beast; whether that cleverness is malicious is the chapter's open question, not its premise.

terms.standardEnglish: you shall not surely die
terms.rationale: The serpent's reply to the woman is the exact same intensifying construction as YHWH-Elohim's prohibition in 2:17 (מוֹת תָּמוּת mot tamut 'you shall surely die'), but here with the negation לֹא lo prefixed and the verb in 2mp plural. The placement of לֹא before the infinitive absolute is unusual — typically the negation modifies the finite verb. Reading the syntax strictly: the negation overrides the certainty itself, so 'you shall *surely not* die' is closer than the conventional 'you shall *not surely* die'. The serpent is not saying death is uncertain — he is saying death will certainly *not* happen.

terms.literal: like Elohim / like divine beings / like gods
terms.standardEnglish: as God / as gods
terms.rationale: כֵּאלֹהִים is the preposition כְּ ke- 'like' attached to אֱלֹהִים Elohim. The phrase is grammatically ambiguous between singular ('like God') and plural ('like divine beings, like gods'), and most modern translations render plural here (NJPS 'like divine beings', NRSV 'like God'). KJV/ASV 'as gods' opted plural; the LXX rendered ὡς θεοί 'as gods', plural. The serpent's claim is that eating opens the eyes and grants knowledge that places the eaters into the Elohim-class. WoH renders 'like the Elohim' — preserving the same noun form used throughout the translation, where 'the Elohim' carries the plural class-noun sense. The serpent's promise is not deification into a single God-state but *upgrading into the Elohim class* — a category-rise.

terms.literal: your eyes will be opened / the eyes of the two of them were opened
terms.standardEnglish: your eyes shall be opened / the eyes of both of them were opened
terms.wohChoice: your eyes will be opened / the eyes of the two of them were opened
terms.rationale: פָּקַח paqach 'open the eyes' is the specific verb for *uncovering perception* — used of opening blind eyes (2 Kgs 6:17, Ps 146:8) and metaphorically of awakening insight. The verb appears twice in the chapter: in the serpent's promise (v5, niphal imperfect 'will be opened') and in the fulfillment (v7, niphal preterite 'were opened'). The promise is precisely kept — eyes do open, perception does shift. What follows that opening is what is contested: not whether they gain knowledge but what knowledge is gained, and what is lost in gaining it.

terms.literal: walking about / strolling in the garden
terms.standardEnglish: walking in the garden
terms.rationale: The hithpael of הָלַךְ halach gives an iterative or habitual sense: walking back and forth, strolling, going about. The same form describes Enoch's relationship with the Elohim ('Enoch walked-about with the Elohim', 5:22, 24) and Noah's (6:9). YHWH-Elohim's *mithallech* in the garden is a manner of presence — moving through, not arriving or departing. Standard 'walking' is correct in meaning; 'walking about' captures the iterative force and the at-home quality of the divine presence in the garden — the picture is not a visitor descending but the garden's resident moving through its grounds.

terms.literal: to/at the wind of the day
terms.standardEnglish: in the cool of the day
terms.wohChoice: at the breeze of the day
terms.rationale: רוּחַ ruach here is the same word that meant 'breath/wind/spirit' in 1:2. In construct with יוֹם 'day', the phrase denotes the *wind associated with a part of the day* — Mesopotamian and Levantine convention reads it as the evening breeze, when the heat of the sun gives way and air movement returns. KJV 'cool of the day' is a free rendering of the implied climatic reference; 'breeze of the day' is closer to the actual word while letting the climatic sense show through. Some readings push further — *ruach hayom* could be 'at the spirit of the day' (the day's own *ruach*), giving the passage a more numinous register. WoH stays with the breeze reading and lets commentary surface the alternatives.

terms.standardEnglish: where art thou?
terms.rationale: אַיֶּכָּה is the interrogative אֵי ay 'where?' fused with a 2ms pronominal suffix. The grammar is straightforward, but the *theological* register has been heavily debated — does YHWH-Elohim not know where the human is (rendering the question literal), or is the question rhetorical (already-knowing, summoning the human into self-disclosure)? The Hebrew settles nothing; both readings work. WoH renders the words straight ('where are you?') and leaves the register open. The chapter's chain of questions that follows (v11 'who told you?', v13 'what is this you have done?') reads more naturally as Socratic-investigative than as informational.

terms.literal: he will crush you (regarding the) head / you will crush him (regarding the) heel
terms.standardEnglish: it shall bruise thy head / thou shalt bruise his heel
terms.wohChoice: he will strike you at the head / you will strike him at the heel
terms.rationale: שׁוּף shuf appears only here and in two parallels (Ps 139:11; Job 9:17). The semantic core is contested: 'bruise', 'crush', 'strike', 'lie in wait for'. The same verb governs both clauses, so the action is reciprocal — whatever the seed does to the serpent's head, the serpent does to the seed's heel. The traditional Christian reading (Vulgate ipsa conteret 'she shall crush', with 'she' = Mary) is text-critically unsupported and contributes to a *protoevangelium* tradition the Hebrew does not deliver on its own. WoH renders 'strike' (less anatomically committed than 'crush', less weak than 'bruise') and lets the symmetry of the two clauses stand: head against heel, with mortal stakes for the lower clause (the serpent's head) and risk-of-injury stakes for the upper (the human's heel).

terms.literal: toil, hardship, labor / pain, hurt, sorrow
terms.standardEnglish: sorrow / pain
terms.rationale: Both nouns derive from the root עצב, which spans 'pain, hurt, sorrow, hardship, labor'. The semantic range matters because both nouns appear in close paired use in v16–17: עִצָּבוֹן itzavon to the woman (in childbearing) and to the human (in working the ground), and עֶצֶב etzev to the woman (in childbearing). The pairing is deliberate — both human and woman receive the *same word* for their post-garden condition, distributed differently (her childbearing, his land-working). 'Sorrow' (KJV) is too narrowly emotional; 'pain' is too narrowly physical; 'hardship' captures the spectrum. The shared root binds the two human consequences: the curse falls not on the human or the woman but on their *contexts of generative labor* (childbearing and land-tilling), and the *word for hardship is one*.

terms.standardEnglish: desire
terms.rationale: תְּשׁוּקָה appears three times in the Hebrew Bible (Gen 3:16, 4:7, Song 7:11). The semantic field is contested. The traditional rendering 'desire' (KJV, ASV) has been read across a spectrum: sexual longing (Song 7:11 leans that way), submissive yearning (post-Augustinian Christian tradition), or hostile drive ('its desire is for you' in 4:7 governs sin's drive toward Cain). The grammatical construction in v16 is parallel to 4:7, where the drive is hostile and the second clause is 'but you must master it'. The Cain parallel suggests the woman's *t'shuqah* in v16 may not be a tender longing but a contested impulse toward, paired with the next clause's יִמְשָׁל־בָּךְ 'he will rule over you'. WoH renders 'longing' as the least over-specified word in the range, and lets commentary surface the parallel.

terms.standardEnglish: cursed is the ground
terms.rationale: Critical structural observation: in the three curse-speeches of Gen 3:14–19, the curse is pronounced on the serpent (v14, ארור אתה 'cursed are you'), on the ground (v17, ארורה האדמה 'cursed is the ground'), but *not* on the human and *not* on the woman. The woman receives intensified itzavon in childbearing; the human is told *the ground* he came from is now cursed *on his account* (בַּעֲבוּרֶךָ ba-avurecha). The text refuses to put the human under the curse formula directly. This is structurally precise. The eventual *return to dust* (v19) is not a *new* curse but the human's pre-existing condition surfaced — he was always from the adamah, and now goes back to it.

terms.standardEnglish: Eve / mother of all living
terms.rationale: חַוָּה Chavvah is connected etymologically to חַי chai 'living' / 'life' through assonance, though the actual derivation is debated (some link it to Aramaic חוה 'declare, show'). The text supplies the gloss: 'because she became אֵם כָּל־חָי em kol-chai — mother of all living'. The naming closes a triple of namings: in 2:23 the human names *Ishah*; here in 3:20 he names *Chavvah*; the species itself was named *adam* by the Elohim in 1:26–27. The three namings move from species to gender-pair to individual proper name. WoH keeps the Hebrew form Chavvah (with anglicized Eve flagged in commentary) to preserve the chai resonance. The naming comes *after* the curses — the human's first interpretive act in the post-garden world is to name his woman by *life* rather than by mortality.

terms.standardEnglish: coats of skins
terms.rationale: כָּתְנוֹת katnot is the plural of כְּתֹנֶת ktonet 'tunic, robe' — the same garment Jacob gives Joseph in 37:3 (the famous ketonet passim, 'tunic of many colors / long-sleeved tunic'). עוֹר or is 'skin, hide' (the homophone אוֹר or 'light' is from a different root, but the sound similarity is sometimes commented on in midrashic tradition — 'garments of light' as a play, though the consonantal forms differ — ע vs. א). The verse marks a major upgrade in human covering: from improvised fig-leaf belts (v7) to YHWH-Elohim-tailored hide garments. The implicit question of where the skins *came from* (the first death of an animal in the narrative) is left silent in the text and noted by tradition.

terms.standardEnglish: as one of us
terms.rationale: Gen 3:22 contains the *second* grammatically explicit plural divine speech in Genesis (the first was 1:26 נַעֲשֶׂה na'aseh 'let us make'). YHWH-Elohim says of the human: 'the human has become *like one of us*, knowing good and bad'. The pronoun מִמֶּנּוּ mimmennu is unambiguously 1pl ('from us'), and the cardinal אַחַד echad ('one') quantifies — the human has joined a multi-member category. The verse confirms the serpent's promise (v5: 'you will be like the Elohim, knowing good and bad') was *accurate* — what the serpent foretold has happened, and YHWH-Elohim is now acknowledging it in counsel with the other members of the Elohim-class. This is the chapter's most direct WoH-compatible verse: the divine plural is not a grammatical curiosity but a working multi-being category that the human has now entered the lower tier of.

terms.literal: the cherubim and the flame of the sword the one-that-turns-itself
terms.standardEnglish: the Cherubims and a flaming sword which turned every way
terms.wohChoice: the cherubim and the flaming blade that turns itself
terms.rationale: Two ANE-rooted images. כְּרוּבִים k'ruvim are the composite hybrid guardian-figures of the ancient Near East — winged sphinxes flanking thrones, doorways, and divine spaces (cf. the cherubim of the Ark, Ex 25:18; the cherub-throne in 1 Sam 4:4; Ezekiel's vision in Ezek 10). They are *not* the cherubic putti of medieval and Renaissance Christian art. The plural here suggests a small group, not a pair. לַהַט הַחֶרֶב הַמִּתְהַפֶּכֶת is more striking: לַהַט lahat 'flame, blade' (the word can mean either), חֶרֶב cherev 'sword', and הַמִּתְהַפֶּכֶת ha-mithappechet a hithpael participle 'the one turning itself' / 'the one that revolves'. The picture is a self-rotating flaming blade — possibly an autonomous weapon, possibly a flame that itself takes a sword's form. WoH renders 'the flaming blade that turns itself' to preserve the autonomous-motion image rather than smoothing into 'a flaming sword which turned every way' (KJV, where the sword is held by something else).

terms.partOfSpeech: verb (qal perfect) + noun + direct object marker + proper name
terms.literal: I have acquired/created a man, [with]/[the] YHWH
terms.standardEnglish: I have gotten a man from the LORD
terms.wohChoice: I have made a man — YHWH himself!
terms.rationale: Genesis 4:1 contains one of the strangest grammatical constructions in the book. קָנִיתִי qaniti is qal perfect of קָנָה — semantic range 'acquire, get, possess', but also 'create, beget' (especially of divine creation, e.g., Gen 14:19 'maker of heaven and earth', Ps 139:13). אֶת־יְהוָה et-YHWH is the direct-object marker followed by the Tetragrammaton — grammatically reading 'I have acquired/created a man, YHWH' (in apposition).
