The Mesopotamian motif is a celestial colored arc as post-flood promise-token. The Hebrew chapter has reshaped that motif from feminine ornament into masculine war-weapon-laid-down. The semantic shift is theological commentary in the choice of noun.

On the Wheel of Heaven frame: the chapter is preserving an extraordinary image of the Elohim as agents who *had* weapons and *used* them — and who now publicly unstring them. The bow-in-the-cloud is not a symbol of generic goodwill; it is the specific image of the Elohim having ended a war they themselves initiated.

terms.standardEnglish: I will remember my covenant
terms.rationale:
Direct echo of 8:1's *vayyizkor Elohim et Noach* (the Elohim remembered Noah) — the same verb (*zakhar*) now applied to the brit. At the start of the Flood-reversal in chapter 8, divine memory was the act that turned the waters back. Here in 9:15 that same memory is *built into* the covenant as the recurring function of the sign.

The theological grammar is striking: the bow is not a memory aid for *humans* (humans do not need a reminder that they are alive), but for the *Elohim*. Whenever a cloud rises, the bow appears, and the Elohim's covenantal memory is triggered. The chapter is constructing a system in which the divine party *needs an external reminder* of the divine commitment. The architecture is anthropopathic: the Elohim function with the same memory-mechanics as humans, requiring tokens and signs to keep promises in mind.

On the Wheel of Heaven frame: this is consistent with the rest of the chapter's portrayal of the Elohim as embodied agents — they smell aroma (8:21), they have memory (8:1, 9:15), they think to themselves (8:21), they engineer architecture (the bow-as-sign). The brit-memory mechanism slots into a coherent picture of finite agents who use external tokens to track their own commitments.

terms.standardEnglish: everlasting covenant
terms.rationale:
*Olam* (עוֹלָם) does not exactly mean *eternity* in the metaphysical sense; it means *far-distant time*, *the long age*, *as far as time stretches*. The phrase *brit olam* names a covenant whose duration is structurally unlimited — open-ended forward and backward — but the temporal vocabulary remains *durational* rather than *atemporal*. The Hebrew Bible has no native vocabulary for timeless eternity; it has only deep duration.

The phrase *brit olam* will recur in the Abrahamic covenant (17:7, 17:13, 17:19), the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 23:5), the Levitical priesthood covenant (Num 18:19), and the Sabbath covenant (Ex 31:16). It marks a class of covenants that are durationally unbounded, in contrast to conditional or generational covenants. The Noahic brit is the first in this class — universal in scope and unbounded in time.

**v2.14.0 expansion**: appliesTo expanded from the Noachic GEN-9:16 only to include the patriarchal Abrahamic occurrences at GEN-17:7, GEN-17:13, GEN-17:19. The Gen 17 cluster establishes *brit olam* as the recurring P-document covenant-duration formula across the patriarchal corpus. The wider Hebrew-Bible distribution (Exod 31:16 Sabbath, Lev 24:8 showbread, Num 18:19 priestly salt-covenant, Num 25:13 Phineas, 2 Sam 23:5 Davidic, 1 Chr 16:17 / Ps 105:10, Isa 24:5 / 55:3 / 61:8, Jer 32:40 / 50:5, Ezek 16:60 / 37:26) is documented here for forward reference; specific applyTo additions will be made as those chapters are translated.

terms.standardEnglish: Shem, and Ham, and Japheth
terms.rationale:
Names and meanings: **Shem** (שֵׁם) means *name* — the same Hebrew noun. The patriarch of the Israelite line is named, literally, *Name*. **Cham** (חָם) means *hot* — a noun denoting heat, used in Lev 26 for the destruction-by-heat of fields. **Yephet** (יָפֶת) is related to either פתה *patah* (to be wide, to enlarge) per 9:27's wordplay (*yapht Elohim l-Yephet*) or to יָפֶה *yafeh* (beautiful) per minority readings.

The consistent ordering across vv. 18, 19, 23 places them as Shem–Ham–Yapheth, traditionally read as birth-order eldest to youngest. But 9:24 names the offender as *bno ha-qatan* (his youngest son), and 10:21 has the curious clause *Shem the brother of Yapheth the elder* — implying Yapheth is the eldest and Shem the second. The chapter does not resolve. Most readers assume the listing is by prominence (Shem first because of Israelite genealogical importance) rather than by birth order.

Geographically, chapter 10 will associate Shem with the Semitic Near East, Ham with North Africa and the southern Levant (notably Egypt and Canaan), and Yapheth with the Aegean, Anatolian, and broader Mediterranean populations. The naming scheme is doing the ethnogeographic work of the Table of Nations in chapter 10.

terms.standardEnglish: Ham is the father of Canaan
terms.wohChoice: Ham — he is the father of Canaan
terms.rationale:
Repeated three times in five verses (vv. 18, 22, 25). The chapter is hammering the genealogical link before, during, and after the vineyard incident. The drumbeat is editorial — the chapter wants the reader to be unable to forget that the offender's son is *Canaan*, whose descendants Israel will later displace.

The repetition is also unusual within the brevity of the Noah genealogy: neither Shem nor Yapheth is paired with a second-generation name. Only Ham. This is the chapter's signal that the narrative weight is on Canaan, not Ham. The curse in v25 will land on Canaan and the chapter's repeated re-identification has prepared the reader for that landing.

On the Wheel of Heaven frame: this is one of the clearest examples of an editorial layer doing political work in Genesis. The Israelite-Canaanite political geography is being given a primordial-family etiology: the people Israel will fight, displace, and intermarry with for centuries are being pre-positioned, in chapter 9, as the cursed descendants of the offender in a primal family scene. The text's reception in colonial-era racial theology (the so-called 'curse of Ham' that was misread as referring to all of Ham's descendants) magnified an already-ethnocentric framing into a tool of racial violence.

terms.standardEnglish: Noah began to be an husbandman
terms.wohChoice: Noah, a man of the soil, began
terms.rationale:
The phrase *ish ha-adamah* (man-of-the-soil) is a calculated echo of three earlier passages:

- **2:5** — 've-adam ayin la'avod et ha-adamah' (and there was no human to till the soil) — the primal lack that the creation of Adam fills
- **2:7** — 'and YHWH-Elohim formed the human (*ha-adam*) from dust of the soil (*ha-adamah*)' — the etymological knit of adam and adamah
- **4:2** — 'Qayin hayah oved adamah' (Cain was a tiller of soil) — the first farmer

Noah is being placed in this lineage of soil-named figures. He is *not* called *oved adamah* (tiller of soil, the verbal noun used for Cain) but *ish ha-adamah* (man of the soil, a status-noun). The shift from active participle to status-noun marks Noah's farming as identity, not occupation. He is *the* man of the soil now, in a way that anchors the post-Flood agricultural age.

The verb *vayyachel* (he began) is also notable. In rabbinic readings (Bereshit Rabbah 36) the verb is sometimes heard as from חלל *chalal* (to profane, to make common), reading *Noah profaned* — anticipating the drunkenness scene as a moral fall. The Hebrew root is genuinely ambiguous (חלל can mean both *begin* and *profane*); the chapter's authors may have intended the doubled valence. Either way the verb opens a scene that ends in disaster.

terms.standardEnglish: vineyard
terms.rationale:
The first *kerem* in the Hebrew Bible. The vineyard is one of the most heavily symbolic agricultural sites in later biblical literature:

- **Naboth's vineyard** (1 Kings 21) — the legal case that anchors prophetic critique of royal land-seizure
- **The Song of the Vineyard** (Isaiah 5) — YHWH's beloved as a vineyard that produced wild grapes
- **The vineyard parables** of Jesus (Mark 12, Matt 20, 21) — vineyards as Israel-figures
- **The vineyard in the Song of Songs** (1:6, 8:11–12) — vineyards as erotic and economic spaces

Noah's vineyard is the chapter that opens the vineyard motif. The post-Flood human, who has been blessed and re-mandated as second Adam, plants the first vineyard — not a wheat field or olive grove. The wine that emerges from this vineyard is the chapter's narrative engine: the drunkenness in v21 enables the vulnerability of v21–22, which enables the curse of v25.

The choice of *vineyard-and-wine* over other crops is also archaeologically suggestive. Viticulture is one of the oldest cultivars in the Near East; the chapter is plausibly preserving an ancient memory of when systematic grape-cultivation began. (Archaeological evidence places domesticated grape cultivation in the South Caucasus / eastern Anatolia / northern Mesopotamia region by ca. 6000 BCE — broadly the same Ararat-region geography where the chapter places Noah's landing.)

terms.standardEnglish: he drank of the wine, and was drunken
terms.wohChoice: he drank of the wine and became drunk
terms.rationale:
The first occurrence of *yayin* (יָיִן, wine) in the Hebrew Bible, and the first occurrence of *shakhar* (שָׁכַר, to be drunk). The chapter is introducing two important nouns and a verb in three Hebrew words.

What is striking is the chapter's lack of explicit moral framing. The narrator says *Noah drank and became drunk and uncovered himself* — three perfect-consecutive verbs in unbroken sequence — without inserting a single evaluative adjective or adverb. Compare 6:5 (*and YHWH saw that the evil of the human was great*) or 4:8 (*and Cain rose against Hevel his brother and killed him*) — both narrate moral acts with the narrator's evaluation withheld but the action manifestly evil. Here, the chapter does not even withhold evaluation; it simply does not provide one.

The later biblical attitude toward wine is famously divided: Numbers 6 prohibits Nazirite wine consumption; Proverbs 23:31 warns *look not on the wine when it is red*; Psalms 104:15 praises *wine that gladdens the heart of the human*; the prophets routinely use wine-imagery for divine judgment (cup-of-wrath) and divine joy (new wine on the mountains). Genesis 9 opens the conversation without resolving it: wine enters human experience here, and the very first effect is a stupor that produces vulnerability and family conflict.

terms.standardEnglish: and he was uncovered within his tent
terms.wohChoice: and uncovered himself within his tent
terms.rationale:
The reflexive (hithpael) form of *galah* (גָּלָה — to uncover, to reveal, to expose). The same root names *Galut* (exile, the state of being uncovered-from-one's-place) and *galui* (revealed, manifest). The hithpael stem makes the action self-directed: Noah does the uncovering to himself.

The chapter's choice of the reflexive is important. Noah is not undressed by another, not assaulted, not stripped — he uncovers *himself*. The vulnerability that follows is self-induced. The morality of the subsequent scene depends on this: whatever Ham did wrong (v22), Noah's nakedness is not the result of Ham's action. The chapter is carefully distributing the agencies.

The scene rhymes with Eden. There: the humans *knew* and *saw* their nakedness, and *covered themselves with fig leaves* (3:7). Here: Noah *uncovers* himself, his son *sees*, and other sons *cover him with a garment* (9:23). The chapter is doing a deliberate inversion: in Eden, knowledge produced shame which produced self-covering; post-Flood, drunkenness produces self-uncovering which produces filial response. The pattern of *covering-and-knowing* recurs across both scenes as a marker of human boundary.

The Hebrew of the final word — *b'tokh aholoh* — has an unusual feminine suffix (*aholoh*, *her tent*) rather than the expected masculine *aholo* (*his tent*). Rabbinic readers have heard the unusual form as suggesting Noah was in *her* (his wife's) tent, or *a* tent that was not his own. Most modern readings treat the unusual suffix as a textual archaism or scribal variation.

terms.standardEnglish: his father's nakedness
terms.rationale:
The contested phrase. *Erva* (עֶרְוָה) means *nakedness*, especially the genital area; the construct *ervat aviv* literally means *the nakedness of his father*. Three readings have circulated through interpretation history:

1. **Plain reading**: Ham saw his father's exposed body and went out to tell his brothers. The transgression is the seeing-and-telling combination — Ham's failure to cover his father, his failure to look away, and his choice to gossip outside the tent. The brothers' careful covering in v23 (walking backward, faces averted) makes this reading by contrast. The plain reading is most consistent with the chapter's internal structure.

2. **Euphemism for sexual assault**: The phrase *galah ervat* (to uncover the nakedness of) functions as a sexual idiom in Leviticus 18:7–8 and 20:11, where *galah ervat aviv* names sexual contact with one's father's wife. Some readers — already in late antique tradition (b. Sanhedrin 70a, citing Rab) — infer that Ham assaulted his father or his father's wife while Noah was drunk. The reading is grounded in the Levitical idiom but anachronistic to Genesis 9, which uses *ra'ah ervat* (to see the nakedness), not *galah ervat* (to uncover the nakedness). The verbs differ.

3. **Paternal castration**: A minority reading (Sanhedrin 70a, citing Rab Samuel) extrapolates that Ham incapacitated Noah in some way that prevented a fourth son. The text gives no support beyond the disproportionate severity of the curse.

The Wheel of Heaven translation does not commit to a reading because the chapter does not. What the chapter *does* commit to is that whatever Ham did was sufficient to warrant the curse on Canaan in v25 and the contrast-by-mirror of vv. 22–23.

terms.source: וַיִּיקֶץ נֹחַ מִיֵּינוֹ וַיֵּדַע אֵת אֲשֶׁר־עָשָׂה־לוֹ בְּנוֹ הַקָּטָן
terms.translit: vayyiqetz Noach mi-yeino vayyeda et asher asah lo bno ha-qatan
terms.literal: and-he-awoke Noah from-his-wine and-he-knew that which-he-did to-him his-son the-youngest
terms.standardEnglish: Noah awoke from his wine, and knew what his younger son had done unto him
terms.wohChoice: Noah awoke from his wine, and knew what his youngest son had done to him
terms.rationale:
*Vayyiqetz mi-yeino* — *he awoke from his wine*. The idiom *yaqatz min* (to awaken from) is reserved in the Hebrew Bible for emerging from a stupor or trance, not from ordinary sleep. The chapter is specifying that Noah was in a wine-induced unconsciousness deep enough that ordinary waking-vocabulary doesn't apply.

*Vayyeda* — *he knew*. The verb *yada* (to know) is the same verb that organized the Eden tree (*ha-da'at* — the knowing, 2:9) and the post-fruit recognition of nakedness (3:7, *vayyed'u ki erumim hem* — they knew that they were naked). The chapter's choice of verb keeps the Eden-rhyme tight: in Eden, eating the fruit produced *knowing-and-hiding*; here, drinking the wine produced *being-uncovered-and-knowing*. The Eden pattern is inverted, with the agency reassigned (Noah knows what was done *to* him, rather than the humans knowing what they did *themselves*).

*Bno ha-qatan* — *his youngest son*. The phrase has produced an old textual puzzle. The chapter lists the sons consistently as Shem–Ham–Yapheth (vv. 18, 19, 23) — which places Ham as the middle son. Either (a) the listing is by prominence rather than birth-order, with Ham actually the youngest; or (b) *qatan* names something other than birth-order here (the Hebrew adjective can mean *small in dignity* in some idioms — cf. 'the great and the small' as social categories). 10:21 has the parallel curiosity (*Shem, the brother of Yapheth the elder*) — implying Yapheth is eldest. The Hebrew text is genuinely inconsistent on birth-order; the chapter does not resolve.

terms.source: אָרוּר כְּנָעַן עֶבֶד עֲבָדִים יִהְיֶה לְאֶחָיו
terms.translit: arur Kena'an eved avadim yihyeh l'echav
terms.literal: cursed Canaan! slave-of slaves he-shall-be to-his-brothers
terms.standardEnglish: Cursed be Canaan; a servant of servants shall he be unto his brethren
terms.wohChoice: cursed be Canaan; a slave of slaves shall he be to his brothers
terms.rationale:
The first human-pronounced curse in the Hebrew Bible. Earlier curses (*arur* on the serpent in 3:14, *arur* on the soil in 3:17, *arur* on Cain in 4:11) were divine. This is the first time a human invokes the formula. Noah's authority to curse is presumed, not granted.

What is striking is the skip: Ham is the offender (vv. 22, 24), but Canaan — Ham's son, not yet born in narrative time — is the one cursed. The text does not explain. The skip is one of the great unresolved interpretive puzzles in Genesis. Possibilities the tradition has explored:

1. **Vicarious curse**: Curses descend through patrilineal lines in ancient Near Eastern thought; cursing Ham's firstborn is effectively cursing Ham at the deepest possible level (his line's continuation).
2. **Etiological retrojection**: The curse-on-Canaan is the etiology, not the historical scene. The chapter is reading the Israelite-Canaanite political conflict back into a primal family scene, and the conflict is between Israel (Shem-line) and Canaan (not Ham as such). The narrative is doing political-justification work.
3. **Canaan was also involved**: A rabbinic reading (Bereshit Rabbah 36) suggests Canaan saw Noah first and called Ham — making Canaan the originator of the gossip-line. The text gives no support.

The Wheel of Heaven commentary will not endorse any single reading. What is unambiguous is the political function: the curse installs the Israelite-Canaanite hierarchy as primordial.

The reception history is brutal. From late antiquity through the colonial era, Christian and Muslim readers progressively conflated *Ham* with *Canaan* and re-racialized the curse to apply to all of Ham's descendants — particularly African populations associated with Cush (10:6). This 'curse of Ham' reading became load-bearing for Christian theological justifications of the African slave trade. The text says nothing about race or skin color; the curse is on a specific Levantine population (Canaan). The reception's misreading does not change the text but is part of the chapter's history that any honest commentary must acknowledge.

terms.standardEnglish: a servant of servants
terms.rationale:
The grammatical construction is the Hebrew superlative-by-construct: *eved avadim* (slave of slaves) parallels *qodesh qodashim* (holy of holies, the innermost sanctuary), *shir ha-shirim* (song of songs, the finest song), *melekh melachim* (king of kings, the highest ruler). The construct intensifies; *slave of slaves* names the lowest possible status — slavery to slaves, or slavery beneath all other slaves.

The choice between *slave* and *servant* in English is theologically loaded. *Servant* softens the social reality; *slave* preserves it. The Hebrew *eved* covers both — it can name a paid laborer, a vassal-king, or a chattel slave — but the construct *eved avadim* and the narrative context (a curse establishing permanent subjugation) places this firmly in the chattel-slavery range. The Wheel of Heaven translation uses *slave* to preserve the harshness of the curse and to refuse the softening that *servant* introduces.

terms.standardEnglish: Blessed be the LORD God of Shem
terms.wohChoice: blessed be YHWH, Elohim of Shem
terms.rationale:
The first time in the Torah that YHWH is identified by relationship to a specific human line. Before this, YHWH has been the Elohim of *the human* (singular, generic) or of *the humans* (collective). Here YHWH becomes *elohei Shem* — *the Elohim of Shem*.

The pattern will recur and become foundational: *elohei Avraham* (Elohim of Abraham, Gen 28:13), *elohei Yitzchaq* (Elohim of Isaac, Gen 28:13), *elohei Yaaqov* (Elohim of Jacob, Ex 3:6), culminating in *elohei Avraham, Yitzchaq, ve-Yaaqov* (Elohim of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob) as the standing self-identification formula for the divine self-naming at the burning bush (Ex 3:6, 15).

The chapter is opening, in 9:26, the genealogical bracket inside which the entire later covenant theology will operate. Yhwh's relation to humanity is moving from *creator of all* (the universal framing of chapters 1–8) to *Elohim of a specific line* (the genealogical framing that runs from chapter 9 forward).

On the Wheel of Heaven frame: this is one of the clearest examples in Genesis of the narrative *narrowing* the focus from cosmic to particular. The Elohim who made all life (1:26) and who covenanted with all flesh (9:11) is now also, specifically, the Elohim of Shem. The chapter is opening the door to the rest of the Hebrew Bible's particularism.

terms.standardEnglish: God shall enlarge Japheth
terms.rationale:
The verb *yapht* (יַפְתְּ — may he enlarge / make wide) is from the same root as the name *Yephet* (יֶפֶת). The clause is a name-pun: *may the wide-one widen*. The poetic device — folding a wish into the meaning of a name — is one of Genesis's signature techniques. Other examples:

- **Adam from adamah** (2:7) — *the human from the soil*
- **Hevel** (4:2) — *vanity, vapor* — pre-figuring his short life
- **Yitzchaq from tzachaq** (17:17, 18:12, 21:6) — *he laughs* — Sarah's laughter at the announcement
- **Yaaqov from aqev** (25:26) — *heel-grabber* — Jacob holding Esau's heel
- **Yisrael from sarah** (32:28) — *one who strives with El* — after the wrestle
- **Moshe from mashah** (Ex 2:10) — *drawn out* — from the Nile waters

The Yapheth-pun continues this tradition. The blessing's content — *may he be enlarged, may he dwell in Shem's tents* — extends the name's meaning into a wish for territorial expansion under Shem's hospitality.

The second clause, *yishkon b'oholei Shem* (may he dwell in the tents of Shem), is grammatically ambiguous. Two readings have split the tradition:

1. **Yapheth as subject**: 'May Yapheth dwell in Shem's tents.' Reading: peaceful Yapheth-Shem coexistence. Yapheth's enlargement happens within Shem's hospitality.
2. **The Elohim as subject**: 'May the Elohim dwell in Shem's tents.' Reading: early sanctuary-theology. The Elohim's dwelling-place will be Shem's territory — anticipating the Temple in Jerusalem (Shem's line via David).

The Hebrew permits both. Rabbinic readers split, and the chapter does not commit. Either reading gives structural priority to Shem.

terms.literal: from these were separated the coastlands of the nations
terms.standardEnglish: from these were the isles of the Gentiles divided
terms.wohChoice: from these the coastlands of the nations were separated
terms.rationale: The four-dimension formula — *families, tongues, lands, nations* — appears as the summary closer for each of the three Noah-son lines in Genesis 10 (v5 Japhethites, v20 Hamites, v31 Shemites). The verb נִפְרְדוּ *nifredu* is niphal of פָּרַד *parad* 'to separate, divide' — the same root behind the personal name Peres / Perez. 'Coastlands of the nations' (*iyei ha-goyim*) for the Japhethites specifically names the maritime peoples of the Mediterranean and Black Sea coasts — *i* meaning island, coast, or coastland (Isa 24:15, Jer 25:22, Ezek 26:18). KJV 'isles of the Gentiles' is etymologically defensible but reads as Christian-supersessionist; modern translations correctly read *goyim* as 'nations' in the descriptive sense. The four dimensions of the formula match the chapter's structural concern with the post-Flood differentiation of humanity along multiple axes.

terms.standardEnglish: Egypt
terms.rationale: מִצְרַיִם is the Hebrew Bible's name for Egypt. The form is grammatically dual (*-ayim* ending), traditionally read as referring to the political duality of Upper and Lower Egypt (the two ancient kingdoms united in the Old Kingdom). Etymologically it may be cognate with Akkadian *Musur* and ultimately of unknown origin. The standard English 'Egypt' comes through Greek Αἴγυπτος (likely from the Memphite *Hwt-ka-Ptah*, 'mansion of the soul of Ptah'), an entirely unrelated naming-tradition. The WoH Translation preserves the Hebrew name *Mitsrayim* (matching the project's convention for proper names — Shet rather than Seth, Chavvah rather than Eve, Yapheth rather than Japheth) and uses the gentilic 'Mitsri' for citizens. Where context requires modern clarity, 'Egypt' may be glossed in commentary; the text uses the Hebrew form. Reclassified from `direct` to `inferred` because the choice between *Mitsrayim* and *Egypt* is a transliteration-vs.-traditional-English call, not a directly-attested rendering.

terms.source: נִמְרֹד ... גִּבֹּר בָּאָרֶץ ... גִּבֹּר־צַיִד לִפְנֵי יְהוָה
terms.translit: Nimrod ... gibbor ba-aretz ... gibbor-tzayid lifnei YHWH
terms.literal: Nimrod ... a mighty one on the land ... a mighty hunter before YHWH
terms.standardEnglish: Nimrod ... a mighty one in the earth ... a mighty hunter before the LORD
terms.wohChoice: Nimrod ... a mighty one in the land ... a mighty hunter before YHWH
terms.rationale: Nimrod (נִמְרֹד) appears in Gen 10:8-12 and is briefly mentioned in 1 Chr 1:10 and Mic 5:5 (where the 'land of Nimrod' = Assyria). The noun גִּבּוֹר *gibbor* is the same word used in Gen 6:4 for the antediluvian *gibborim anshei ha-shem* — 'the mighty ones, men of name', the offspring of the *bnei ha-elohim* with the daughters of the human. The chapter's reuse of *gibbor* for Nimrod is one of the corpus's most pointed verbal echoes — and one of its most under-translated. Standard English renderings often soften to 'mighty man' or 'mighty hunter', losing the technical-class register the Hebrew preserves. The WoH translation keeps *gibbor* registers consistent across 6:4 (the antediluvians) and 10:8 (Nimrod), letting the textual continuity show. // *Lifnei YHWH* — 'before YHWH' — is grammatically neutral. Jewish tradition reads adversarially (Nimrod rebels against YHWH — the etymology connects with מָרַד *marad* 'to rebel', though the actual etymology of Nimrod is uncertain and possibly Akkadian). Christian tradition often reads positively. The Hebrew settles neither; the translation preserves the prepositional neutrality and lets the commentary surface the spectrum.

terms.source: וַתְּהִי רֵאשִׁית מַמְלַכְתּוֹ בָּבֶל וְאֶרֶךְ וְאַכַּד וְכַלְנֵה
terms.translit: vat'hi reshith mamlakhto Bavel v'Erech v'Akkad v'Kalneh
terms.literal: and the beginning of his kingdom was Babel, Erech, Akkad, and Kalneh
terms.standardEnglish: And the beginning of his kingdom was Babel, and Erech, and Accad, and Calneh
terms.wohChoice: The beginning of his kingdom was Babel, and Erech, and Akkad, and Calneh
terms.rationale: The four cities are the chapter's archaeologically-informed list of southern Mesopotamian foundational sites: *Bavel* (Babylon, Akkadian *Bab-ilim*); *Erech* (Sumerian Uruk, the great walled city of the Uruk Period); *Akkad* (Sargon's third-millennium capital, location archaeologically unknown but textually well-attested); *Kalneh* (uncertain — possibly Nippur, the Sumerian religious center; or a scribal variant of *kullaneh* 'all of them', read as 'in Shinar all of them' rather than as a fourth city). The chapter is presenting the entire southern Mesopotamian civilization-cradle under a single founder's name. The WoH translation preserves the proper-name forms close to the Hebrew (Bavel, not Babylon; Akkad, not Accad — the KJV doubled-c spelling is an English-orthography artifact, not a transliteration improvement). The phrase *reshith mamlakhto* — 'the beginning of his kingdom' — uses רֵאשִׁית, the same noun as *bereshit* of Gen 1:1, signaling a foundational-era moment for the southern Mesopotamian political order.

terms.standardEnglish: the land of Shinar
terms.rationale: Shinar (שִׁנְעָר) is the Hebrew Bible's name for the southern Mesopotamian alluvial plain — the region that hosted the Sumerian and Old Babylonian civilizations. The etymology is contested: possibly cognate with Sumerian *Šum-er* (Sumer) through a Hebrew phonological transformation; or possibly a separate Hebrew name. Shinar appears in Gen 10:10 (Nimrod's kingdom), 11:2 (the Babel-tower location), 14:1 (Amraphel king of Shinar), and in later prophetic and exilic literature (Isa 11:11, Zech 5:11, Dan 1:2) — where it carries a register of foreign-exile-imperial territory. The WoH translation preserves the Hebrew form rather than modernizing to *Sumer* or *Babylonia* — both of which carry their own scholarly-political baggage and would over-specify the chapter's geographic vocabulary.

terms.translit: asher yatz'u misham Pelishtim v'et Kaphtorim
terms.literal: from whom came forth Philistines, and the Kaphtorim
terms.standardEnglish: out of whom came Philistim, and Caphtorim
terms.wohChoice: from whom the Pelishtim went out — and the Caphtorim
terms.rationale: The parenthesis *asher yatz'u misham Pelishtim* — 'from whom the Pelishtim went out' — places the Philistines genealogically with the *Casluhim* in v14. But the prophetic-tradition consensus (Jer 47:4, Amos 9:7) and the modern archaeological consensus (Late Bronze Age collapse + Aegean-origin Sea Peoples) trace the Philistines from *Caphtor* (Crete / the Aegean), not from the Casluhim. The standard scholarly proposal is that the parenthesis is textually misplaced — it should attach to *Caphtorim* immediately following, giving 'and the Casluhim and the Caphtorim, from whom the Pelishtim went out'. The translation preserves the Hebrew as transmitted (Pelishtim from Casluhim) and surfaces the textual problem in commentary. *Pelishtim* (the Hebrew transliteration) is preferred over English *Philistines* to maintain consistency with the chapter's other ethnonym treatments.

terms.literal: and afterward were scattered the families of the Canaanite
terms.standardEnglish: and afterward were the families of the Canaanites spread abroad
terms.wohChoice: Afterward the families of the Canaanite were scattered
terms.rationale: The verb נָפֹצוּ *naphotzu* is qal passive of פּוּץ *putz* 'to scatter, disperse'. The same root recurs three times in Genesis: 9:19 (the post-Noah scattering from his sons), 10:18 (the Canaanite clans here), and 11:8-9 (the Babel-scattering of YHWH). The lexical thread connects the three dispersions, each at a different scale (Noah's three sons → the Canaanite sub-families → all humanity). The WoH translation preserves the same English root ('scatter') across all three to surface the lexical pattern.

terms.literal: father of all sons of Eber
terms.standardEnglish: the father of all the children of Eber
terms.wohChoice: the father of all the sons of Eber
terms.rationale: Shem is identified in Gen 10:21 as *avi kol bnei Ever* — 'the father of all the sons of Eber'. The naming-ahead is structurally important: it flags Eber (named four verses later in 10:24-25, and again in 11:14-17) as the eponymous ancestor of the עִבְרִי *Ivri* people — Hebrew. The etymology of *Ivri* from *Ever* is widely (though not universally) accepted in modern scholarship; the verbal connection runs through the root עבר 'to cross over', perhaps reflecting the Hebrews as 'those who crossed over' the Euphrates (cf. Josh 24:2-3, where Abraham is said to have crossed the river from Mesopotamia). The chapter is naming, four generations ahead, the figure from whom Israel's self-designation will derive.

terms.literal: Peleg [Division], for in his days the land was divided
terms.standardEnglish: Peleg; for in his days was the earth divided
terms.wohChoice: Peleg, for in his days the land was divided
terms.rationale: *Peleg* (פֶּלֶג) is from the root פלג *palag* 'to divide, split'. The chapter offers an explicit etiological etymology: *ki v'yamav niflega ha-aretz* — 'for in his days the land was divided'. The verb נִפְלְגָה *niflega* is niphal passive of the same root. What division is meant? Three readings circulate: (1) the linguistic-political scattering of Babel in Gen 11 (the chapter places Peleg in the genealogy immediately before the Babel narrative, and the etymology pairs the name with the scattering-event; this is the standard ancient and modern interpretation); (2) the geological splitting of the continents (the Young-Earth-Creationist and certain ancient-astronaut readings; the verb does not support this in the Hebrew lexicon); (3) the political-territorial division of post-Flood lands among the Noah-son lines (the verb is used elsewhere of land-division, Job 38:25). The translation preserves the bare 'the land was divided' and the rendering 'land' (אֶרֶץ *eretz* — 'land', not 'earth-the-planet') ; the chapter's most contextually defensible reading is (1), with (3) as supplementary. (2) is read-back from later cosmologies and not philologically supportable. Reclassified as `inferred` because the choice between the linguistic, geographic, and political readings of *niflega* is genuinely interpretive.

terms.standardEnglish: one language, and one speech
terms.wohChoice: one lip and one set of words
terms.rationale: Gen 11:1 opens with a doubled phrase: *safa achat udvarim achadim* — *one lip and one set of words*. The two phrases name distinct dimensions of linguistic unity. *Safa* (שָׂפָה, lit. 'lip') is metonymy for *language* (cf. Isa 19:18, 'the lip of Canaan'), naming the speech-organ to refer to the speech-system. *Devarim* (דְּבָרִים) is *words / matters / things*, the plural of *davar*. The doubled claim is therefore: the post-Flood humans had not only shared phonology and grammar (one *lip*) but also shared semantic-conceptual stock (one *set of words / matters*). The chapter is making a stronger claim than 'they spoke the same language' — it is naming a deeper cognitive-communicative commonality. Standard English translations smooth to 'one language and one speech' (KJV) or 'the same language and the same words' (ESV), losing the doubled-noun structure. The WoH translation preserves the doubling and the lip-metonymy, making the chapter's strong unity-claim visible.

terms.standardEnglish: Go to, let us make brick
terms.rationale: *Havah nilbenah l'venim* — *come, let us make bricks*. The interjection *havah* (from יָהַב, 'give, grant', imperative form used as exhortation 'come!'); the verb *nilbenah* is first-person plural cohortative of לָבַן *laban* 'to make brick'. The verb is built on the noun *l'venah* (brick), giving the wordplay *nilbenah l'venim* — 'let us brick bricks'. The verse opens the chapter's structural sequence of four cohortatives: two human (11:3 *nilbenah*, 11:4 *nivneh*) and two divine (11:7 *neredah*, 11:7 *navlah*). The chapter is composed around this rhetorical match. KJV 'Go to, let us make brick' is archaic but grammatically equivalent; the WoH translation modernizes the interjection while preserving the cohortative structure that makes the chapter's symmetry visible.

terms.source: וַתְּהִי לָהֶם הַלְּבֵנָה לְאָבֶן וְהַחֵמָר הָיָה לָהֶם לַחֹמֶר
terms.translit: vat'hi lahem hal'venah l'aven v'hachemar haya lahem lachomer
terms.literal: and was for them the brick as stone, and the bitumen was for them as mortar
terms.standardEnglish: And they had brick for stone, and slime had they for mortar
terms.wohChoice: The brick was for them as stone, and the bitumen was for them as mortar
terms.rationale: The technical-archaeological aside notes that the Babel-builders substituted kiln-fired clay brick (*l'venah*) for stone (*aven*) and bitumen (*chemar*) for mortar (*chomer*). This is empirically accurate to southern Mesopotamian construction methods: the alluvial plain has no stone, and the Mesopotamian ziggurats used kiln-fired clay brick with bitumen mortar (attested archaeologically and in Mesopotamian construction texts). The KJV 'slime' for *chemar* is archaic (16th-century English for any sticky tar-like substance); 'bitumen' is the accurate modern term — the natural asphaltic seepage of Mesopotamia (especially the Hit region on the middle Euphrates), which was used as construction mortar from the Ubaid period (5th millennium BCE) onward. The chapter is technically literate about the world it describes — the WoH translation preserves the technical accuracy.

terms.literal: and a tower, and its head in the skies
terms.standardEnglish: and a tower, whose top may reach unto heaven
terms.wohChoice: a tower with its top in the skies
terms.rationale: *Migdal v'rosho bashamayim* — *a tower with its top in the skies*. *Migdal* (מִגְדָּל) is the Hebrew word for a tower or fortified high structure (city walls, watchtowers, siege towers in 2 Sam 22:51, Isa 33:18). In Gen 11:4 the *migdal* names the structure the Mesopotamian context strongly indicates: a ziggurat — the stepped temple-platform of Mesopotamian religious architecture, exemplified by the great ziggurat of Etemenanki at Babylon. // The phrase *rosho bashamayim* (literally 'its head in the skies') is a stock-phrase for *very tall* (cf. Deut 1:28, 9:1, of fortified Canaanite cities 'whose walls reach up to the skies'). But in this chapter the phrase functions on two levels: as hyperbole for height, *and* as a description of the ziggurat's theological function — the structure was designed in Mesopotamian theology to be the stairway between earth and the divine realm, with the deity descending onto the platform-shrine at the summit and human worship ascending through it. *Rosho bashamayim* therefore names both the physical aspiration and the cosmological function. The WoH translation preserves the literal 'top in the skies' (using *skies* from the established *shamayim* glossary entry); the standard English 'whose top may reach to heaven' (KJV/ASV) smooths the construction and obscures the ziggurat-specific reference. Reclassified as `inferred` because the strong reading of *rosho bashamayim* as ziggurat-function-marker (rather than mere hyperbole) is interpretive — defensible from ANE archaeological context and supported by scholars (Speiser, Westermann), but not consensus.

terms.literal: and let us make for ourselves a name
terms.standardEnglish: let us make us a name
terms.wohChoice: let us make for ourselves a name
terms.rationale: *Naase lanu shem* — *let us make for ourselves a name* — uses the **same cohortative form** as the divine *naase adam* of Gen 1:26. *Naase* is first-person plural cohortative of עָשָׂה *asah* ('make, do'). In Genesis 1:26 the speaker is the Elohim, the cohortative is interpreted as divine plural; here in 11:4 the speakers are humans, the cohortative is straightforwardly first-person plural. The verbal parallel is exact and structurally important: the chapter is staging humans assuming the *naase* register that opened the creation account. // *Shem* (שֵׁם) in Hebrew Bible idiom is *name* in the sense of *renown, reputation, lasting memory* (cf. 2 Sam 7:9, of YHWH making David's *shem* great; Isa 56:5, of an *everlasting name*). The phrase *anshei ha-shem* — *men of the name* — was applied in Gen 6:4 to the antediluvian *gibborim* (the offspring of the *bnei ha-elohim*). The Babel-builders are now collectively saying: *let us make for ourselves a name*. The chapter is verbally connecting the human ambition at Babel to the *gibborim*-class register the Flood was supposed to have ended. The translation preserves the bare cohortative without editorializing; the commentary surfaces the parallel.

terms.literal: lest we be scattered upon the face of all the land
terms.standardEnglish: lest we be scattered abroad upon the face of the whole earth
terms.wohChoice: lest we be scattered upon the face of the whole land
terms.rationale: The Babel-builders' motive-clause: *pen-naputz al-pnei kol ha-aretz* — *lest we be scattered upon the face of the whole land*. The conjunction פֶּן *pen* introduces a negative purpose-clause ('lest, in order that not'). The verb *naputz* is qal passive of פּוּץ *putz* 'to scatter, disperse'. The same root will return in v8 (*vayyafetz YHWH otam ... al-pnei kol ha-aretz* — 'YHWH scattered them ... upon the face of the whole land') and v9 (the closing summary). The chapter's structural irony is built on this root: what the Babel-builders fear in v4 is exactly what YHWH does in v8 — the *same verb*, the *same prepositional phrase*. The WoH translation preserves the verbal repetition (translating *putz* consistently as 'scatter' in both clauses) so the chapter's reversal-structure is visible. The standard English 'scattered abroad' (KJV) adds an 'abroad' the Hebrew does not have.

terms.literal: and YHWH came down to see
terms.standardEnglish: And the LORD came down to see
terms.rationale: *Vayered YHWH lir'ot* — *YHWH came down to see*. The verb is יָרַד *yarad* 'descend', used with YHWH as subject. The anthropomorphic register is plain: YHWH is positioned *above*, the city-and-tower is *below*, and YHWH *descends* to inspect. The descent-language sets up the *havah neredah* (let us also go down) of v7, where the divine plural cohortative will act. // The verb *yarad* is used elsewhere of divine descent at Sinai (Ex 19:11, 19:18, 19:20, 'YHWH came down on Mount Sinai'); the verbal echo across the Babel and Sinai narratives is one of the Pentateuch's longer-range structural moves. The translation preserves the bare anthropomorphic register without softening to 'looked down' or 'observed from above'.

terms.source: וְעַתָּה לֹא־יִבָּצֵר מֵהֶם כֹּל אֲשֶׁר יָזְמוּ לַעֲשׂוֹת
terms.translit: v'atah lo yibatzer mehem kol asher yazmu la'asot
terms.literal: and now nothing will be made inaccessible from them, all that they have planned to do
terms.standardEnglish: and now nothing will be restrained from them, which they have imagined to do
terms.wohChoice: and now, nothing will be withheld from them which they have schemed to do
terms.rationale: *Lo yibatzer mehem kol asher yazmu la'asot* — the Hebrew is dense. *Yibatzer* is niphal of בָּצַר *batsar*, whose semantic range covers 'restrain, cut off, make inaccessible, be impossible' (Job 42:2 uses the same passive of YHWH: *lo yibatzer mimkha mezimah* — 'no purpose of yours is restrained'). The negation *lo yibatzer* means *will not be withheld / made inaccessible*. *Yazmu* is qal perfect of זָמַם *zamam* 'to devise, scheme, plan' — neutral-to-negative in semantic range, often used of *premeditation* in legal contexts. // YHWH's assessment is plain: human coordination at this scale produces unlimited capacity. The clause is *not* judicial-curse language; it is *engineering-assessment* language. The response in v7 will be a targeted intervention to break the coordination — not punishment but a cap on capacity. The Wheel of Heaven lens reads this verse with attention: the chapter is staging a strategic divine response to human power-aggregation, and the verb *yibatzer* names the divine concern precisely. The WoH translation 'nothing will be withheld' preserves the passive-voice claim; 'schemed' renders *zamam* in its slightly-negative tonal range without over-determining (KJV 'imagined' reads as merely cognitive, missing the deliberate-planning sense). Reclassified `inferred` because the rendering of *zamam* as 'schemed' (vs. 'planned' or 'imagined') is a connotational choice.

terms.literal: come! let us go down and let us confuse there their lip
terms.standardEnglish: Go to, let us go down, and there confound their language
terms.wohChoice: Come, let us go down and confuse their tongue there
terms.rationale: *Havah neredah v'navlah* — *come, let us go down, and let us confuse*. This is the **third explicit divine cohortative in Genesis**, after 1:26 (*naase adam* — let us make humankind) and 3:22 (*hen ha-adam haya k'achad mimennu* — behold, the human has become like one of us). All three are first-person plural divine speech. The chapter's cohortative is the structural mirror of the human cohortatives in vv 3-4 (*havah nilbenah*, *havah nivneh*) — the divine response to the human project uses the same rhetorical form. // *Neredah* is first-person plural cohortative of יָרַד *yarad* 'descend'. The verb mirrors the singular *vayered* of v5 ('YHWH came down to see'); v7's plural neredah indicates either a divine-council descent or a self-summoning rhetorical plural (the latter is the standard non-WoH reading; the former is the modern divine-council scholarship reading favored by Heiser and others, and is consistent with the WoH lens). // *Navlah* is first-person plural cohortative of בָּלַל *balal* 'to mix, confound, confuse' — the etymological root of *Babel*. The intervention is linguistic: not destroying the city, not killing the builders, but breaking the coordination-capability by fragmenting the shared language. The WoH translation preserves the cohortative structure ('let us go down ... let us confuse') and the linguistic-targeting register.

terms.literal: and YHWH scattered them from there
terms.standardEnglish: So the LORD scattered them abroad from thence
terms.rationale: *Vayyafetz YHWH otam misham* — *YHWH scattered them from there*. The verb is hiphil (causative) of פּוּץ *putz* — the same root that the Babel-builders had used in v4 (*pen-naputz* — 'lest we be scattered'). The Hebrew is doing structural irony with verbal precision: the humans feared *being scattered* (passive); YHWH *scatters* them (active causative). The chapter's reversal is built into the same triconsonantal root, recurring four times across vv 4-9. The translation preserves the verbal repetition ('scatter' for *putz* in both passive and causative) so the symmetry is visible.

terms.source: עַל־כֵּן קָרָא שְׁמָהּ בָּבֶל כִּי־שָׁם בָּלַל יְהוָה שְׂפַת כָּל־הָאָרֶץ
terms.translit: al-ken qara shemah Bavel ki sham balal YHWH sefat kol ha-aretz
terms.literal: therefore its name was called Babel, because there YHWH confounded the lip of all the land
terms.standardEnglish: Therefore is the name of it called Babel; because the LORD did there confound the language of all the earth
terms.wohChoice: Therefore its name was called Babel, because there YHWH confounded the tongue of the whole land
terms.rationale: *Bavel* (בָּבֶל) and *balal* (בָּלַל) share a triconsonantal root structure close enough for the chapter to assert an etymological pun. The actual etymology is not internal to Hebrew: *Bavel* most likely derives from Akkadian *Bab-ilim* (*'gate of the gods'* or *'gate of god'* — *bab-ili* in the singular) — the same root that gives English 'Babylon' via Greek Βαβυλών. The chapter is offering a folk etymology that *recodes* the Mesopotamian self-naming ('gate of the gods') as the Hebrew theological re-naming ('the place where YHWH confounded'). The pun is theological recoding: the city of divine access is recast as the city of divine refusal of access. The translation preserves the bare etymology-clause and notes the recoding in commentary. *Bavel* (not Babylon, not Babel-in-English) is the Hebrew form; the English-tradition 'Babel' is acceptable as it tracks the Hebrew consonantal skeleton closely.

terms.translit: vayyoled et Avram et Nachor v'et Haran
terms.literal: and fathered Abram, Nachor, and Haran
terms.standardEnglish: and begat Abram, Nahor, and Haran
terms.rationale: Terah's three sons are named with the standard Hebrew transliterations: *Avram* (אַבְרָם — later renamed Avraham/Abraham in Gen 17), *Nachor* (נָחוֹר — same name as Terah's father in v24, the grandson named for the grandfather), *Haran* (הָרָן — distinct from the city *Charan* of v31, which has a different initial consonant in Hebrew: ח vs ה). // The naming triad establishes the patriarchal-family network: Abram will be the covenant-line; Nachor will remain in northern Mesopotamia and become the ancestor of Rebekah (Gen 24); Haran will die early but father Lot, Milkah, and (per 11:29) Yiskah. The WoH translation preserves the Hebrew forms ('Avram' not 'Abram', 'Nachor' not 'Nahor', 'Haran' not 'Haran-with-h'). However, the chapter retains 'Abram' (with English-traditional initial A and -br-) to match standard scholarly conventions and the canon-tradition of the name; full transliteration to 'Avram' is deferred to a future glossary decision.

terms.standardEnglish: Ur of the Chaldees
terms.rationale: *Ur Kasdim* — Ur of the Kasdim — names the family's place of origin. Ur was the great Sumerian city of southern Mesopotamia, capital of the Third Dynasty of Ur (ca. 2100 BCE), excavated by Sir Leonard Woolley in the 1920s. The qualifier *Kasdim* (Chaldeans) is **chronologically anachronistic**: the Chaldeans (Akkadian *Kaldu*) are a first-millennium-BCE Aramean people who came to dominate Babylonia in the Neo-Babylonian period (the most famous Chaldean being Nebuchadnezzar II). The phrase *Ur Kasdim* therefore mixes a third-millennium city with a first-millennium people's name — an editorial gloss reflecting the period in which the genealogy was redacted. // Modern scholarship has proposed alternative identifications (a northern Mesopotamian *Ur* near Haran, rather than the famous southern *Ur*) precisely to avoid the *Kasdim* anachronism. The southern-Ur identification remains dominant. // The WoH translation preserves the Hebrew *Kasdim* rather than the English-traditional 'Chaldees' (KJV) or 'Chaldeans' (modern translations) — both of which obscure the chronological seam by translating to a different language's name for the same people.

terms.literal: Sarai ... Milkah daughter of Haran ... Yiskah
terms.standardEnglish: Sarai ... Milcah, the daughter of Haran ... Iscah
terms.wohChoice: Sarai ... Milkah, daughter of Haran ... Yiskah
terms.rationale: Three women are named in Gen 11:29. *Sarai* (שָׂרַי, 'my princess' or 'noble lady', later renamed Sarah in 17:15) is Abram's wife; her lineage is *not* given (the narrative will eventually identify her in 20:12 as Abram's half-sister, with the same father but different mother). *Milkah* (מִלְכָּה, 'queen') is Nachor's wife, identified as Haran's daughter — making her Nachor's niece (intra-family marriage was the patriarchal-period norm). *Yiskah* (יִסְכָּה, etymology uncertain — possibly 'she sees' or 'anointed') is named only here in the Hebrew Bible; she is identified as another daughter of Haran but never reappears. // Rabbinic tradition (Megillah 14a, Sanhedrin 69b) identifies Yiskah with Sarai, on the basis that Sarai's lineage is otherwise unexplained and Yiskah is named without further mention. This identification has no textual support and is rejected by most modern scholarship (Sarai's later identification in 20:12 as Abram's half-sister, with Terah as her father, does not match Yiskah being Haran's daughter). The translation preserves the Hebrew forms and lets the textual crux stand.

terms.source: וַתְּהִי שָׂרַי עֲקָרָה אֵין לָהּ וָלָד
terms.translit: vat'hi Sarai aqarah ein lah valad
terms.literal: and was Sarai barren — no to her child
terms.standardEnglish: But Sarai was barren; she had no child
terms.wohChoice: Sarai was barren — she had no child
terms.rationale: Six Hebrew words name the constraint that will be load-bearing for the next ten chapters. *Aqarah* (עֲקָרָה) is the Hebrew word for 'barren', applied in the Hebrew Bible to Sarah (Gen 11:30, 16:1, 18:11-12), Rebekah (25:21), Rachel (29:31), Hannah (1 Sam 1:2), Manoah's wife (Judg 13:2), and others — a recurring female-barrenness motif tied to divine intervention and late-life conception. The redundant *ein lah valad* — *she had no child* — drives the assertion home: this is not delayed conception but stated barrenness as a baseline. // The verse is the chapter's pivot from primaeval-history scope to patriarchal-narrative scope: the constraint that the next ten chapters will work to overcome is named in a single short verse, without commentary. The chapter's textual economy at this point is structurally important — the next chapter (Gen 12) opens with divine action that will need to overcome what 11:30 has just stated as a flat impossibility.

terms.source: וַיֵּצְאוּ אִתָּם מֵאוּר כַּשְׂדִּים לָלֶכֶת אַרְצָה כְּנַעַן וַיָּבֹאוּ עַד־חָרָן וַיֵּשְׁבוּ שָׁם
terms.translit: vayyetz'u itam me-Ur Kasdim lalekhet artzah Kena'an vayyavo'u ad-Charan vayyeshvu sham
terms.literal: and they went out with them from Ur of the Kasdim to go toward the land of Canaan, and they came as far as Haran and settled there
terms.standardEnglish: and they went forth with them from Ur of the Chaldees, to go into the land of Canaan; and they came unto Haran, and dwelt there
terms.wohChoice: they went out with them from Ur of the Kasdim to go to the land of Canaan, and they came to Haran and settled there
terms.rationale: The verse stages the *unfinished* migration toward Canaan. *Vayyetz'u me-Ur Kasdim* — 'they went out from Ur Kasdim' — names the departure; *lalekhet artzah Kena'an* — 'to go toward the land of Canaan' — names the intent; *vayyavo'u ad-Charan vayyeshvu sham* — 'and they came as far as Haran and settled there' — names the interruption. *Ad* (עַד) — 'as far as, up to' — marks Haran as a stopping-point short of the destination, not the destination itself. // *Charan* (חָרָן, with initial *chet*) is distinct from *Haran* (הָרָן, with initial *he*, the brother of Abram). The two are sometimes confused in English transliteration where both lose the diacritic distinction; in Hebrew they are different consonants and entirely different names. *Charan* is a major northern Mesopotamian city (the site near modern Harran in southeastern Turkey), an important Bronze and Iron Age trading center on the Aramean / Mesopotamian frontier and one of the main centers of the Sin (moon-god) cult. // Terah does not complete the migration; he dies in Charan (v32). Chapter 12 will open with YHWH's call to Abram to *go from your land, from your kindred, from your father's house, to the land that I will show you* (12:1) — and Abram will complete the migration his father had begun and abandoned. The chapter is staging the seam: the family-line journey toward Canaan stalls at Charan; the covenant-line journey will resume under explicit divine call.

terms.standardEnglish: visions of God
terms.rationale: The phrase opens Ezekiel 1:1. *Mar'ot* (מַרְאוֹת) is the plural of *mar'eh* (vision, appearance); the plural form is meaningful — Ezekiel saw multiple visions, not a single image. The genitive *Elohim* preserves the plural class-noun rendering established throughout the WoH translation (see [`elohim-as-translation`](/wiki/elohim/)). The phrase recurs in Ezek 8:3 and 40:2, opening the book's major vision-sequences. The standard English 'visions of God' singularizes both nouns and obscures the plural register; the WoH translation preserves both plurals.

terms.standardEnglish: river Chebar
terms.rationale: The Kebar (Hebrew כְּבָר, Akkadian *naru kabari* — 'the great canal') is a major canal of the Babylonian canal system, branching from the Euphrates near Nippur in southern Mesopotamia. The location is well-attested in cuneiform sources from the Neo-Babylonian period. Ezekiel's vision happens by this canal, in the Jewish exilic community settled in southern Babylonia. The KJV transliteration 'Chebar' is etymologically defensible but reads as the English-tradition rendering; the WoH translation preserves the Hebrew form 'Kebar' (also matching modern scholarly conventions and the Akkadian original closer than 'Chebar'). The phrase recurs at Ezek 1:3, 3:15, 10:15, 10:20, 10:22, 43:3 as the book's geographic anchor.

terms.standardEnglish: exile of King Jehoiachin
terms.rationale: Yoyakhin (יוֹיָכִין, also spelled Yehoyakhin / יְהוֹיָכִין in full form) was the penultimate king of Judah, deported to Babylon in 597 BCE in the first Babylonian deportation (2 Kgs 24:8-17). The 'fifth year of his exile' = 593 BCE, the date of Ezekiel's vision. Modern English Bibles render the name as 'Jehoiachin' (KJV/ASV) — an Anglicization of the longer Hebrew form via the Vulgate. The WoH translation uses the shorter Hebrew form Yoyakhin, closer to the consonantal text and aligning with the WoH project's general preference for Hebrew-form proper names (Yapheth, Yoqtan, Yiskah). Yoyakhin is also the form used in the Babylonian ration-tablets ('Yaukin') that confirm the historicity of his exile.

terms.standardEnglish: land of the Chaldeans
terms.rationale: *Eretz Kasdim* — 'land of the Kasdim' — names Babylonia. The Kasdim (Akkadian *Kaldu*, English 'Chaldeans') are a first-millennium-BCE Aramean people who came to dominate Babylonia under the Neo-Babylonian dynasty (Nabopolassar, Nebuchadnezzar II). The phrase recurs at Ezek 1:3 and is the book's term for the exile-location. Parallels `ur-kasdim` (Gen 11:28, 31) — both use the same Aramean-period naming. The WoH translation preserves the Hebrew form *Kasdim* rather than the English-traditional 'Chaldeans' for consistency with the existing `ur-kasdim` entry.

terms.standardEnglish: the hand of the LORD
terms.rationale: *Yad YHWH* — 'the hand of YHWH' — is a technical Hebrew Bible idiom for divine agency or compulsion. In Ezekiel specifically, the phrase is a recurring formula marking the onset of prophetic experience: *vat'hi alav yad YHWH* — 'the hand of YHWH was upon him' — at 1:3, 3:14, 3:22, 8:1, 33:22, 37:1, 40:1. The phrase recurs in Elijah-Elisha narratives (1 Kgs 18:46, 2 Kgs 3:15) and in Isa 8:11 for similar prophetic-impulse moments. The lexical core is *yad* (hand), anthropomorphically applied to YHWH but covering the broader sense of *power, agency, custody, compulsion*. The WoH translation preserves the literal 'hand of YHWH' and the YHWH name (not 'the LORD'). The Christian-tradition 'the LORD' is a Vulgate-via-LXX substitution-tradition; the Hebrew uses the divine name.

terms.literal: Yehezkel son of Buzi the priest
terms.standardEnglish: Ezekiel the son of Buzi the priest
terms.wohChoice: Ezekiel son of Buzi the priest
terms.rationale: Ezekiel (יְחֶזְקֵאל Yehezkel — 'El strengthens' or 'may El strengthen') is identified by patronymic (*ben Buzi*) and office (*ha-kohen* — the priest). The priestly identity is structurally important: Ezekiel is a *kohen*, an Aaronic-lineage priest, displaced from the Jerusalem Temple by the 597 BCE deportation. His prophetic-vision register throughout the book is recognizably *priestly* — spatially calibrated, ritually inflected, concerned with the temple's defilement and restoration — in a way that contrasts with the prophetic-rhetorical register of his contemporary Jeremiah (in Jerusalem). The WoH translation preserves 'Ezekiel' (the standard English form) for the prophet's name, balancing the WoH preference for Hebrew-form names against the strongly-established English-tradition rendering of this specific name (the book title 'Ezekiel' is universal in English). The book's catalog title across all WoH metadata uses 'Ezekiel'. *Buzi* and *kohen* render in their Hebrew transliteration / structural form.

terms.standardEnglish: a stormy wind / a whirlwind
terms.rationale: *Ruach se'arah* — 'a stormy wind' — opens Ezekiel's vision (1:4) as the carrier of the approaching apparition. *Ruach* is the standard Hebrew word for *wind / breath / spirit* (see `ruach-elohim` for the Gen 1:2 cosmogonic sense); *se'arah* is *storm, tempest* (specifically, a violent or whirling wind). The compound phrase recurs in 2 Kgs 2:11 (the *se'arah* that took Elijah up) and in prophetic-judgment texts (Jer 23:19, 30:23). The Ezekiel verse uses the phrase as the *transport-medium* of the chayyot apparatus — the chayyot come from the north *in* (or *with*) a stormy wind. The WoH translation preserves the literal 'stormy wind' rather than KJV/ASV 'whirlwind' (which over-specifies the rotation register).

terms.standardEnglish: amber / electrum / gleaming metal
terms.rationale: The Hebrew word *chashmal* (חַשְׁמַל) appears in the Hebrew Bible **only three times** (Ezek 1:4, 1:27, 8:2) and has no clear Hebrew etymology. The word is one of the most contested in the entire Hebrew Bible. // The LXX (3rd-2nd c. BCE Greek translation) renders ἤλεκτρον (*elektron*) — Greek for the gold-silver alloy *electrum*, used for cultic vessels and royal regalia in classical antiquity. The Vulgate (Latin) follows: *electrum*. KJV and ASV render 'amber' (a different substance — fossilized tree resin — but also associated with golden-yellow luminous color); NJPS renders 'electrum'; NRSV 'gleaming amber'; ESV 'gleaming metal'. The modern scholarly consensus favors 'electrum' or 'gleaming metal'. // Akkadian *elmešu* (a precious gleaming substance used in royal-religious contexts, possibly amber or amber-glass) has been proposed as a cognate. // The WoH translation preserves the Hebrew word *chashmal* untranslated — parallel to how *Elohim* and *YHWH* are preserved. The reader encounters the chapter's own technical vocabulary on the page. The commentary supplies the lexical context; the translation does not pretend to know what *chashmal* IS. // Modern Hebrew uses *chashmal* for *electricity* (a 19th-century coinage by Eliezer Ben-Yehuda, who reused the biblical word for the new concept) — itself an interesting datum: the biblical term was the natural Hebrew word for a luminous-electrical phenomenon. Reclassified as `inferred` because the preservation-of-Hebrew choice (vs. translation to a specific English equivalent) is a translator's interpretive call.

terms.standardEnglish: living creatures
terms.rationale: *Chayyot* (חַיּוֹת) is the plural of *chayyah* — *living thing, animal, life-form*. The root is *chai* (alive, life), the same root behind *chayyim* (life-plural, often used as a singular abstract). The chapter uses *chayyot* as a technical class-noun for the four figures (1:5, 13-15, 19-22), not as the generic biological term (cattle, wild animal). Apocalyptic literature picks up the term: Revelation 4:6's four *zoa* (Greek 'living things') is a direct re-reading of Ezekiel's chayyot. The English convention 'living creatures' is well-established and the WoH translation adopts it; 'living beings' would also be defensible (especially given that the chapter goes to such lengths to describe the figures' un-creature-like attributes). The translation preserves the *chai*-root register that links these figures to the broader Hebrew Bible vocabulary of *life*.

terms.standardEnglish: appearance of likeness / the likeness as the appearance of
terms.rationale: Ezekiel 1 deploys a distinctive hedging-formula throughout: *mar'eh* (appearance) + *demut* (likeness), with various combinations of *ke-ein* ('like the look of'), *kemar'eh* ('like the appearance of'), and *demut* ('likeness of'). The chapter is methodically cautious — Ezekiel reports what things *looked like* and what they had the *likeness of*, not what they were. The formula reaches its highest density at 1:26 (*demut k'mar'eh adam* — 'likeness of an appearance like human') and 1:28 (*hu mar'eh demut kvod YHWH* — 'this was the appearance of the likeness of the glory of YHWH'). // The WoH translation preserves the doubled hedging formula consistently, surfacing the chapter's epistemic discipline. The reader sees Ezekiel describing-by-likeness, not asserting-identity. This is a translator's gift to the reader: the chapter teaches its own reading-protocol — *here is what it looked like*, not *here is what it was*.

terms.standardEnglish: four faces ... four wings
terms.rationale: The numeric signature *four* is the chapter's structural backbone: four chayyot, four faces per chayyah (human, lion, ox, eagle — v10), four wings per chayyah (v6, with the two-up-two-down configuration described in v11), four wheels (v15), four directions of motion (v9, v12, v17, v20). Four is the standard Hebrew Bible number for geographic-directional completeness (four winds, four corners of the earth, four rivers of Eden). The chayyot's design is calibrated to omnidirectional simultaneous-motion: each face oriented in one of the four cardinal directions, allowing the system to move any direction without rotation (v9, v12, v17). The composite face design — human, lion, ox, eagle — represents the four 'rulers' of ANE biological hierarchy: human (intellect), lion (wild fauna), ox (domesticated fauna), eagle (avifauna). The translation preserves the literal numerics; commentary surfaces the structural signature.

terms.standardEnglish: a wheel in the middle of a wheel
terms.wohChoice: as if a wheel were within a wheel
terms.rationale: The chapter's most-famous phrase outside of *chashmal*. The Hebrew is *ka-asher yihyeh ha-ofan b'tokh ha-ofan* — *as if the wheel were inside the wheel*. The construction uses Ezekiel's signature hedging formula (*ka-asher yihyeh* — 'as if it would be'); he is reporting what the wheels *looked like*, not asserting that wheels were literally inside wheels. // The standard scholarly reading: two wheels intersecting at right angles (gimbal-like / orthogonal-axis configuration), allowing the wheel-system to rotate in any direction without turning the chayyot above it. This matches the chapter's running concern with omnidirectional motion (v9, v12, v17, v21). // *Ofan* (אוֹפַן) is the standard Hebrew noun for *wheel* — chariot-wheel, mill-wheel, any rotating disc. *Ofannim* is the plural. The phrase 'wheels within wheels' has entered English idiom as a metaphor for hidden complexity; the chapter's literal meaning is mechanical — a description of an unusual wheel configuration that enables omnidirectional motion. The translation preserves the literal Hebrew with the *as if* hedging; commentary surfaces the mechanical reading.

terms.standardEnglish: like the colour of a beryl / like beryl / like chrysolite
terms.rationale: *Tarshish* (תַּרְשִׁישׁ) as a stone-name appears at Ex 28:20 (high priest's breastplate stones), Song 5:14, Dan 10:6, and here at Ezek 1:16. The standard English translations differ: KJV 'beryl', ASV 'beryl', NJPS 'beryl', NRSV 'chrysolite', ESV 'beryl'. Modern gemological identifications variously propose chrysolite, peridot, yellow jasper, or yellow beryl. The classical-era association is **golden-yellow / olive-gold** — a precious stone of luminous appearance. The Vulgate uses *chrysolithus*. // The WoH translation preserves the Hebrew form 'tarshish-stone' rather than picking one of the proposed English identifications — both because the gemological identification is uncertain and because the chapter has stacked three precious-substance descriptions on the apparatus (*chashmal* electrum-like, *nechoshet qalal* burnished bronze, *tarshish* golden-yellow stone), and the visual register consistency is worth preserving. The hedging *ke-ein* ('like the look of') is preserved as 'like the look of'. Reclassified as `inferred` because the choice to preserve the Hebrew (vs. picking 'beryl' or 'chrysolite') is a translator's interpretive call.

terms.standardEnglish: their rings were full of eyes
terms.wohChoice: the rims of the four of them were full of eyes round about
terms.rationale: *Gabbotam* — *their rims* (the outer-edge surfaces of the wheels) — *meleot einayim saviv* — *full of eyes round about*. The phrase has been read at every level. Literal: the wheels are conscious-perceiving entities, eyes distributed around their rims. Iconographic: gemstones glinting like eyes (the wheels described in the same precious-stone register as *chashmal*, *nechoshet qalal*, *tarshish*). Technical: sensors / detectors / aperture-like features distributed around the rim, the apparatus's perceptive-data-gathering surface. Mythological / angelic: the eye-motif appears in seraphim and cherubim iconography across ANE traditions; Revelation 4:6 re-uses Ezekiel's chayyot with the eye-multitude transferred to the chayyot themselves. // The chapter does not specify which register the eyes belong to. The WoH translation preserves the literal *einayim* (eyes) and the *saviv* (round about) — letting the reader meet the chapter's strangeness directly. Commentary surfaces the range of readings.

terms.literal: the ruach of the living-thing was in the wheels
terms.standardEnglish: the spirit of the living creature was in the wheels
terms.wohChoice: the ruach of the living creature was in the wheels
terms.rationale: Ezekiel 1's most explicit statement of the integrated-control-system principle (1:20, 1:21). *Ruach* — wind / breath / spirit (same three-fold lexical field as in Gen 1:2's *ruach-elohim*) — functions in Ezekiel 1 as the *animating directive* of the chayyot-and-wheels system. The chayyot move *wherever the ruach is to go* (1:12, 1:20); the wheels rise *with* the chayyot because the *ruach of the chayyah is in the wheels* (1:20, 1:21). // The chapter preserves a precise distinction: *chayyah* is singular (the living-thing, collective), *ba-ofannim* is plural (in the wheels). The system has *one* directive principle (singular ruach of chayyah) distributed across *multiple* parts (the four wheels). The chapter is describing an integrated control architecture. // The WoH translation preserves the noun *ruach* in its Hebrew form rather than picking 'wind' or 'spirit' prematurely — both because the chapter's *ruach* covers the spectrum and because the technical reading (an animating-coordinating force embedded in the apparatus) is best served by the preserved Hebrew term. Reclassified as `inferred` because the choice to preserve the Hebrew over translating is a translator's interpretive call.

terms.source: קוֹל כַּנְפֵיהֶם כְּקוֹל מַיִם רַבִּים כְּקוֹל־שַׁדַּי
terms.translit: qol kanfeihem k'qol mayim rabbim k'qol Shaddai
terms.literal: the sound of their wings like the sound of many waters, like the voice of Shaddai
terms.standardEnglish: the noise of their wings, like the noise of great waters, as the voice of the Almighty
terms.wohChoice: the sound of their wings like the sound of many waters, like the voice of Shaddai
terms.rationale: The chapter stacks four sound-similes for the wings in motion at v24: *qol mayim rabbim* (sound of many waters), *qol Shaddai* (voice of Shaddai), *qol hamullah* (tumultuous sound), *qol machaneh* (sound of an army-camp). The central simile is *qol Shaddai* — the wings make a sound *like the voice of Shaddai*. // *Shaddai* (שַׁדַּי) is the patriarchal-era divine name (Gen 17:1, 28:3, 35:11, 43:14, 48:3, 49:25; Ex 6:3 explicitly names *El Shaddai* as the name by which YHWH was known to the patriarchs). The conventional English translation is 'the Almighty' (via the LXX's παντοκράτωρ / pantokrator and the Vulgate's *omnipotens*) but the actual etymology is contested: possibly from *shadad* (destroy, overpower), from *shad* (breast, with maternal connotations), or from Akkadian *shadu* (mountain) — the latter favored by some modern scholars who read *Shaddai* as the divine epithet 'the One of the Mountain'. // The WoH translation preserves the Hebrew name *Shaddai* rather than the conventional 'Almighty', matching the project's pattern of preserving divine-names (YHWH, Elohim, El). The chapter is using *Shaddai* specifically (not YHWH) — this is a deliberate Hebrew Bible vocabulary choice and the WoH translation respects it.

terms.literal: like the appearance of sapphire stone, the likeness of a throne
terms.standardEnglish: as the appearance of a sapphire stone, the likeness of a throne
terms.wohChoice: like the appearance of sapphire stone, the likeness of a throne
terms.rationale: *Eben sappir* — *sapphire stone* — appears in the Hebrew Bible at Ex 24:10 (Moses and the seventy elders see at Sinai: *and there was under his feet as it were a paved work of sapphire stone, and as it were the very heaven for clearness*), Ezek 1:26, Ezek 10:1, and the closely-related stone in the high-priest's breastplate (Ex 28:18, 39:11). The Hebrew *sappir* may refer to *sapphire* (modern gemological identification) or to *lapis lazuli* (the deep-blue stone widely used in ANE iconography for divine-throne platforms — Mesopotamian, Egyptian, and Levantine art alike). // *Demut kisse* — *the likeness of a throne*. Ezekiel uses *demut* (likeness) rather than direct identification. The chapter is reusing the Sinai-theophany vocabulary: at Sinai, the elders saw blue-stone-platform *under his feet*; at the Kebar canal, Ezekiel sees blue-stone-throne *above the dome*. The structural parallel is part of the chapter's claim about what Ezekiel is seeing: the same kind of phenomenon as the Sinai theophany, but in exile, by a canal, in Babylonia.

terms.standardEnglish: the glory of the LORD
terms.rationale: *Kavod YHWH* (כְּבוֹד יְהוָה) — *the glory of YHWH* — is the Hebrew Bible's technical term for the *radiant, visible presence-manifestation* of YHWH. The term denotes a specific phenomenon: the cloud-and-fire of the Exodus that led Israel through the wilderness (Ex 13:21-22, 14:19-20, 16:10), the cloud-and-fire descent on Sinai (Ex 19:16-19, 24:15-18), the cloud-filled tabernacle (Ex 40:34-35), the cloud-filled Solomon's Temple (1 Kgs 8:10-11; 2 Chr 5:13-14, 7:1-3), Isaiah's throne-vision (Isa 6:1-3), and — climactically — Ezekiel's vision here (Ezek 1:28, 3:12, 3:23, 8:4, 9:3, 10:4, 10:18-19, 11:22-23, 43:2-5, 44:4). The Ezekiel book is structurally organized around the *movement* of the kavod — leaving the Temple in chapter 10, returning to the eschatological Temple in chapter 43. // The Hebrew root *kbd* covers *heaviness, weight, significance, honor, glory*. The standard translation 'glory' carries the abstract sense; the Hebrew preserves the concrete-physical dimension (something heavy and present, visible and weighty). The chapter's deployment at Ezek 1:28 — *mar'eh demut kvod YHWH* ('the appearance of the likeness of the glory of YHWH') — is the chapter's most-extended hedging formula: three layers of qualifying language (*mar'eh demut kavod*) before the divine name. Ezekiel is not asserting that he saw YHWH; he is identifying the chayyot-and-wheels-and-throne-and-figure as the *kavod-YHWH* — the technical presence-manifestation, not the divine essence directly. // The WoH translation preserves YHWH (not 'the LORD') and 'glory' (the conventional English for *kavod*), with commentary surfacing the technical-presence sense and the hedging formula structure.

terms.standardEnglish: the cherubim
terms.rationale: *Keruvim* (כְּרוּבִים, plural of *keruv* כְּרוּב) — *cherubim* — are the canonical Hebrew Bible class of winged composite attendant-figures. They flank the Ark of the Covenant (Ex 25:18-20, 37:7-9), guard the way to the Tree of Life at Eden's eastern gate (Gen 3:24), and appear as architectural-iconographic figures in Solomon's Temple (1 Kgs 6:23-28). Ezekiel 10 explicitly identifies the *chayyot* of chapter 1 with the *keruvim* (10:15, 10:20, 10:22) — making this one of the Hebrew Bible's most consequential cross-textual identifications. // Ezekiel 10 already uses the term *keruvim* freely from v1 onward, before the explicit identification at v15. The chapter assumes the reader will recognize the term from Hebrew Bible iconographic tradition. // The Christian-tradition rendering 'cherubim' (and singular 'cherub') is well-established and the WoH translation adopts it; alternative renderings (transliterated 'keruvim', or the more abstract 'guardian-figures') are defensible but would obscure the cross-textual recognition the chapter is staging. The English 'cherub' / 'cherubim' has — outside scholarly contexts — drifted into the European post-Renaissance art register (chubby winged children); the chapter's *keruv* is the composite winged-guardian throne-attendant of ANE iconography (winged sphinxes, winged bulls, winged lions). The WoH translation preserves the English 'cherubim' and lets commentary surface the iconographic register where helpful.

terms.standardEnglish: the wheel / whirling wheels
terms.rationale: *Galgal* (גַּלְגַּל) is the Hebrew word for a rolling thing — a wheel-rim, a whirling object, a rolling-mass. The root is *gll* (to roll). Ezekiel 10 introduces the term explicitly: *la-ofannim lahem qora ha-galgal b'aznai* — *as for the wheels, they were called galgal in my hearing* (10:13). The chapter is documenting the alternative technical name for the *ofannim* of chapter 1 — what the prophet heard the heavenly attendants call the wheels. // Elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible the word denotes: a thunder-storm sound (Ps 77:18 — *qol galgal* — 'the sound of the rolling-thing'); the wheel at the well (Eccl 12:6 — *galgal ha-bor*); whirling chaff (Isa 17:13); the whirring of chariot-wheels (Jer 47:3); the wheel-of-fortune image (Sir 33:5). The lexical core is: a rolling, whirling, rotating object — wheel or whirlwind. // The chapter's vocabulary contribution is theologically consequential. *Galgal* becomes the foundational Hebrew Bible word for the **Merkavah** (chariot) mystical tradition — the rabbinic-mystical reading of Ezekiel 1 + 10 as a description of the divine chariot-throne system. The Hekhalot literature, *Shi'ur Qomah*, and the broader Jewish mystical tradition use *galgal* and its plural *galgalim* as load-bearing technical terms. The Raëlian canon's engagement with 'the Kabbalah' (see `Reference corpus of the Raëlian canon` wiki entry) implicitly engages this vocabulary cluster. // The WoH translation preserves the Hebrew word *galgal* untranslated — parallel to how *chashmal* and *Elohim* are preserved. The translation marks the chapter's vocabulary-introduction moment rather than papering over it.

terms.standardEnglish: the man clothed in linen
terms.rationale: *Ha-ish levush ha-badim* — *the man clothed in linen* — names a distinct figure who appears in the Ezekiel throne-vision narratives (Ezek 9:2, 9:3, 9:11, 10:2, 10:6, 10:7). The same figure appears in Daniel 10:5 and 12:6-7 (the *ish levush ha-badim* by the Tigris in Daniel's vision). // *Badim* (בַּדִּים, plural of *bad*) is the Hebrew term for **linen** as a cultic textile. Linen garments are specifically priestly: the high priest wears linen on Yom Kippur (Lev 16:4); the priesthood wear linen for Temple service (Ex 28:42, 39:28); the linen-clad figure in Dan 12:6-7 is iconographically a priestly-heavenly-attendant. The figure is implicitly identified as an angelic-priestly attendant — the heavenly counterpart of the earthly high priest. // The Ezekiel 9-10 narrative has the linen-clad man performing two distinct functions: (1) in chapter 9, marking the foreheads of the faithful (the *tav* mark, Ezek 9:4) before the executioners begin their work; (2) in chapter 10, taking fire from between the cherubim to scatter over Jerusalem (10:2, 10:6-7). Both functions are priestly-judicial: marking the elect, transmitting cultic-judicial fire. // The Christian tradition has identified the figure with Christ (the New Testament's *one like a son of man* of Rev 1:13-14 echoes the Ezekiel-Daniel linen-clad figure). The Hebrew gives the figure no name. The chapter does not specify his identity beyond the *clothed-in-linen* descriptor.

terms.standardEnglish: the threshold of the house
terms.rationale: *Miftan* (מִפְתָּן) is the Hebrew word for *threshold* — the architectural transition-point between inside and outside, between sacred and profane spaces. The word appears in 1 Sam 5:4-5 (the Philistines' Dagon temple — Dagon falls on the *miftan*), Zeph 1:9, Ezek 9:3, 10:4, 10:18, 46:2, 47:1. // In Ezekiel 10, *miftan ha-bayit* (the threshold of the house) is the architectural locus where the *kavod-YHWH* pauses during its departure from the Temple (10:4 the kavod moves *onto* the threshold; 10:18 the kavod moves *off* the threshold). The chapter is making the departure architecturally-spatially concrete: the kavod moves from the holy-of-holies (above the cherubim of the Ark) → onto the threshold (the door of the house) → off the threshold (out of the house entirely). The threshold is the structural midpoint of the divine-presence withdrawal.

terms.source: וַיֵּצֵא כְּבוֹד יְהוָה / וַיָּרָם כְּבוֹד יְהוָה
terms.literal: and the glory of YHWH went out / rose up
terms.standardEnglish: and the glory of the LORD went forth / went up
