terms.wohChoice: the glory of YHWH went out / rose up
terms.rationale: Ezekiel 10 stages the departure of the *kavod-YHWH* (see glossary entry `kavod-yhwh`) from the Jerusalem Temple. The chapter uses two distinct verbs of motion in sequence: // **v4**: *vayyaram kvod-YHWH me-al ha-keruv al miftan ha-bayit* — *the kavod-YHWH rose up from above the cherub onto the threshold of the house*. The verb is *ram* (to rise, be lifted up) — the kavod elevates from the holy-of-holies position toward the threshold. // **v18**: *vayyetze kvod-YHWH me-al miftan ha-bayit vayya'amod al ha-keruvim* — *the kavod-YHWH went out from above the threshold of the house and stood above the cherubim*. The verb is *yatza* (to go out, exit) — the kavod departs from the threshold and re-mounts the cherubim-apparatus. // **v19**: cherubim lift up, fly out the east gate; the kavod is *above them*. The chapter is staging a three-stage departure: holy-of-holies → threshold → above apparatus → exit. // Ezek 11:23 will note the next position: *vayya'al kvod-YHWH me-al tokh ha-ir vayya'amod al ha-har asher mi-qedem la-ir* — *the kavod-YHWH went up from the midst of the city and stood on the mountain that is east of the city* (the Mount of Olives). The full departure sequence: Temple → east gate → Mount of Olives east of Jerusalem. // The departure-vocabulary marks the chapter's theological-historical claim: the divine presence has *withdrawn* from the Temple ahead of the city's 587/586 BCE destruction. The chapter is the prophetic-textual record of the *divine abandonment* of Jerusalem. The WoH translation preserves the motion-verbs (*rose up*, *went out*) and the YHWH name (not 'the LORD').

terms.standardEnglish: the God of Israel
terms.rationale: *Elohei-Yisrael* (אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל) — *the Elohim of Israel* — is the patriarchal/covenantal compound divine name. The phrase first appears in Gen 33:20 (Jacob's altar at Shechem, *El-Elohei-Yisrael*), and recurs at Ex 5:1, Ex 24:10 (the elders see *Elohei-Yisrael* at Sinai), Josh 7:13, 1 Sam 23:10-11, and in liturgical-prophetic contexts throughout the Hebrew Bible. The compound is the specifically-covenantal naming — it ties *Elohim* to the people *Yisrael* in a relational-historical framework. // The standard English 'the God of Israel' uses the singular smoothing of *Elohim* the WoH translation explicitly rejects (see [`elohim-as-translation`](/wiki/elohim/)). The WoH translation preserves the plural class-noun rendering — *the Elohim of Israel* — matching the convention used throughout the WoH Genesis translation. // Ezekiel 10 invokes *Elohei-Yisrael* twice (10:19, 10:20) precisely at the departure moment: the *kavod Elohei-Yisrael* (the glory of the Elohim of Israel) is what leaves the Temple. The covenantal name is being invoked at the covenantal-undoing moment — the chapter is theologically loaded.

terms.standardEnglish: the valley / the plain
terms.rationale: *Biq'ah* (בִּקְעָה) is the Hebrew word for an open, flat valley or plain — distinct from *gai* (narrow ravine) or *emek* (broader valley between hills). The same noun is used in Gen 11:2 for the *biq'ah* in the land of Shinar where Babel was built, in Isa 40:4 for the valleys-to-be-raised in the wilderness highway image, and in Zech 12:11 for the *biq'at Megiddo* (the Megiddo plain). // The chapter places the bone-field in a *biq'ah* — an open, flat staging-area large enough to host *very many* bones (*rabbot me'od*, v2) and to serve as the site of a procedure that will produce an *exceedingly great army* (*chayil gadol me'od me'od*, v10). The chapter is staging the vision on a *staging-ground*, the topographic feature historically used for mass-assembly. The KJV/ASV 'valley' is acceptable; the WoH translation preserves it.

terms.standardEnglish: the dry bones
terms.rationale: *Atzamot* (עֲצָמוֹת, plural of *etzem*) is the standard Hebrew word for *bones* — the skeletal-anatomical sense. The same root (*atzam*) also covers the metaphorical *substance, essence, self* (cf. *etzem ha-yom ha-zeh* — 'on that very day'). // *Yeveshot* (יְבֵשׁוֹת) is the feminine plural adjective of *yavesh* (dry, withered, dried up) — the same root that names the *yabashah* (dry land) of Gen 1:9-10. The chapter pairs the two as a fixed phrase: *atzamot yeveshot* — *dry bones*. // The phrase becomes the chapter's keyword. *Atzamot yeveshot* recurs at 37:2, 4, 11. The Wheel of Heaven translation preserves the standard English 'dry bones' — the rendering is grammatically straightforward and the literal-physical sense is what the chapter requires.

terms.literal: son of human / son of Adam
terms.standardEnglish: son of man
terms.rationale: *Ben-adam* (בֶּן־אָדָם) — literally *son of human / son of Adam* — is the prophet Ezekiel's standard appellation throughout the book. The compound combines *ben* (son) + *adam* (human, the category-noun established in Gen 1-3). It appears **93 times** across Ezekiel — more than any other Hebrew Bible figure receives any single appellation. // The Hebrew Bible elsewhere uses *ben-adam* as a poetic-emphatic for *mortal, human-being* (Ps 8:5 — *ma enosh ki tizk'rennu u-ven-adam ki tifq'dennu* — 'what is humankind that you should remember it, and the son-of-human that you should attend to it'; cf. Ps 80:18, 144:3; Job 25:6, 35:8). The Aramaic equivalent *bar-enash* in Dan 7:13 becomes the New Testament Greek ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου (*ho huios tou anthrōpou*) — *the son of man* — Jesus' favored self-designation in the Gospels. // The WoH translation renders *ben-adam* as **'son of human'** rather than the conventional 'son of man'. Two reasons: (1) the WoH glossary has already established `ha-adam` as *the human* (rather than *the man*) — preserving the category-noun sense from Gen 1:26-27 rather than the gendered 'man'; (2) *ben-adam* is structurally about *being-in-the-human-class* (the prophet as a member of the *adam*-category that was made in the image-and-likeness, not as a male human). The rendering matches the WoH project's broader *adam*-as-human convention; 'son of human' is grammatically natural in English and preserves the category-register the Hebrew has.

terms.standardEnglish: the Lord GOD
terms.rationale: *Adonai YHWH* (אֲדֹנָי יְהוִה) — the compound divine name. *Adonai* (literally 'my lords' — plural-of-majesty of *adon*, master/lord) was the traditional Jewish liturgical substitute for the Tetragrammaton YHWH after the 3rd-century-BCE practice of not pronouncing the divine name in everyday speech. The Masoretic vocalization of the Tetragrammaton in *Adonai YHWH* compounds uses the vowel-points of *Elohim* (יְהוִה, with the *e-i* of Elohim rather than the *e-o-a* of Adonai itself) — a Masoretic device for indicating 'do not pronounce this YHWH as Adonai (because Adonai already precedes it) — pronounce it Elohim instead'. // The conventional English KJV rendering — *the Lord GOD* (with *GOD* in small caps representing YHWH) — is the historical compromise. The WoH translation preserves the actual Hebrew: *Adonai* renders as *Lord*, *YHWH* stays as the divine name *YHWH* (not 'the LORD' substitution). The result — *Lord YHWH* — surfaces both vocabulary elements directly. // The Adonai-YHWH compound is one of Ezekiel's signature divine-address registers (occurs >215 times across the book, more frequent than in any other Hebrew Bible book).

terms.standardEnglish: sinews ... flesh ... skin
terms.rationale: The chapter names a precise sequence of biological-construction stages in 37:6: *gidim* (sinews, tendons, connective tissue) → *basar* (flesh, soft tissue) → *`or* (skin, integument) → *ruach* (animating principle, inserted last). The sequence is anatomically-procedurally ordered: structural framework (bones, already in place) + connective tissue + soft tissue + outer integument + animation. // *Gidim* (גִּדִים, plural of *gid*) is the standard Hebrew noun for *sinew/tendon* — anatomically the fibrous tissue connecting muscle to bone; the noun also appears in Gen 32:33 (the *gid ha-nasheh* — sciatic-nerve sinew of Jacob's wrestling injury). // *Basar* (בָּשָׂר) is the broad-spectrum noun *flesh* — covering muscle, soft tissue, and (by extension) flesh-as-creaturely-substance (the phrase *kol basar* = 'all flesh', used throughout the Flood narrative). // *`Or* (עוֹר) is the noun for *skin* — both human skin and animal hide (Gen 3:21 *kotnot `or* — 'tunics of skin'); the chapter's use is unambiguously the integumentary covering of a body. // The Raëlian canon, in *Extra-Terrestrials Took Me To Their Planet*, reads this verse as a description of biological-creation procedure: the chapter's vocabulary (sinews-then-flesh-then-skin-then-ruach) is procedurally precise and matches the technical-sequential description of body-assembly. The WoH translation preserves the procedural language without inserting interpretive content; commentary at v6 surfaces the canon's reading and the textual data that supports it.

terms.standardEnglish: from the four winds, come, breath
terms.wohChoice: from the four ruchot, come, ruach
terms.rationale: Ezek 37:9 calls the animating-principle from four directions: *me-arba ruchot bo'i ha-ruach* — *from the four ruchot, come, ruach*. The verse uses *ruach* (wind / breath / spirit) twice in a single clause: singular *ha-ruach* (the animating principle being summoned), plural *arba ruchot* (the four winds / four cardinal directions). // The *arba ruchot* (four winds) is the Hebrew Bible's directional convention — the four corners, the four winds, the four directions (cf. Jer 49:36, Zech 2:6 / 2:10, Dan 7:2, Dan 8:8, Dan 11:4). The ruach is summoned from all four cardinal directions simultaneously to enter the assembled bodies. // The WoH translation preserves *ruach* in both occurrences as the Hebrew technical noun, consistent with the chapter's use of *ruach* throughout (cf. v 1, 5, 6, 8, 9, 10, 14). The singular-plural ambiguity in Hebrew is preserved in English by repeating the same noun form (lower-case ruach for singular, lower-case ruchot for plural — both transliterated).

terms.source: וַיִּחְיוּ וַיַּעַמְדוּ עַל־רַגְלֵיהֶם חַיִל גָּדוֹל מְאֹד־מְאֹד
terms.translit: vayichyu vaya'amdu al-raglehem chayil gadol me'od me'od
terms.literal: and they lived and stood on their feet — an exceedingly very great army
terms.standardEnglish: they lived, and stood upon their feet, an exceeding great army
terms.wohChoice: they lived, and they stood up on their feet — an exceedingly great army
terms.rationale: Ezek 37:10's closing image: the animated bodies stand up *al-raglehem* (on their feet) — *chayil gadol me'od me'od* — *an exceedingly great army*. // *Vayichyu* (qal of *chayah* — to live) names the procedural-completion: the bodies *come-to-life*. *Vaya'amdu al-raglehem* (and they stood on their feet) names the postural-functional completion: the bodies achieve standing posture, indicating muscular-neural integration is complete. // *Chayil* (חַיִל) is the standard Hebrew noun for *army, military force, host* (cf. Ex 14:4, 2 Kgs 6:14, Ps 33:16). The chapter is staging the bodies as having a military-functional organization — not a chaotic crowd but a structured force. The doubled *me'od me'od* (very very, exceedingly very) is the chapter's intensifier-of-scale: a vast force.

terms.standardEnglish: I will open your graves
terms.rationale: Ezek 37:12-13 expands the vision from valley-of-bones to grave-opening. *Hinneh ani poteach et-qivrotekhem* — *behold, I am opening your graves*. // The verb form is *poteach* — present-active participle, *currently-opening* — naming the action as already in process, not merely promised for the future. The chapter is staging the grave-opening as *underway*. // *Qivrotekhem* (קִבְרוֹתֵיכֶם) is the plural-2mp possessive of *qever* (grave, tomb, burial place). The same noun appears throughout the Hebrew Bible (Gen 23:9, 23:20 for Abraham's purchased burial cave; 2 Kgs 22:20 for Josiah's grave). The chapter is staging a *mass grave-opening* — a population-wide procedure of grave-opening and re-emergence.

terms.source: עֵץ יוֹסֵף ... עֵץ יְהוּדָה ... לְעֵץ אֶחָד
terms.translit: ets Yosef ... ets Yehudah ... l'ets echad
terms.literal: the stick of Joseph ... the stick of Judah ... into one stick
terms.standardEnglish: the stick of Joseph ... the stick of Judah ... one stick
terms.wohChoice: the stick of Joseph ... the stick of Judah ... into a single stick
terms.rationale: The two-sticks sign-act of Ezek 37:15-28. *Ets Yosef* (the stick labeled for Joseph — representing the northern kingdom, alternately called Joseph or Ephraim) and *Ets Yehudah* (the stick labeled for Judah — representing the southern kingdom centered on Jerusalem). The two are to be joined into *ets echad* (a single stick), prefiguring the political reunification of the divided Israelite kingdom. // *Ets* (עֵץ) is the standard Hebrew noun for *tree, wood, stick*. In this context the chapter is specifying *wooden sticks* / *staves* — physical objects the prophet handles. The English 'stick' is the standard rendering; some translations use 'piece of wood' (NIV) or 'staff' (Wycliffe). The WoH translation uses 'stick' for the concrete object-sense the chapter requires. // The sign-act predicts the eschatological reunification of the divided Israelite kingdom (split since 922 BCE; the northern kingdom destroyed in 722 BCE, the southern kingdom destroyed in 587 BCE; never historically reunified). The chapter is forecasting a future-state restoration that did not historically occur — making the verse a *messianic-eschatological* prediction in standard Hebrew Bible prophetic register.

terms.literal: covenant of peace ... my dwelling ... my sanctuary
terms.standardEnglish: covenant of peace ... my tabernacle ... my sanctuary
terms.wohChoice: covenant of peace ... my dwelling ... my sanctuary
terms.rationale: The closing covenant-vocabulary cluster in Ezek 37:26-28. // *Brit shalom* (בְּרִית שָׁלוֹם) — *covenant of peace* — names the post-restoration covenant explicitly as one of *shalom* (peace, wholeness, completeness). The phrase appears elsewhere at Num 25:12 (the priestly covenant with Phinehas), Isa 54:10, Mal 2:5. The chapter is naming the eschatological-restoration covenant in continuity with prior peace-covenant traditions. // *Mishkan* (מִשְׁכָּן) — *dwelling-place, tabernacle* — is the technical term for the portable wilderness sanctuary of Exodus (Ex 25-31, 35-40). The chapter promises *mishkani aleihem* (my dwelling above them) — YHWH's mishkan-presence will be over the restored people. The Christian-tradition rendering 'tabernacle' is technically correct but obscures the *dwelling* sense the Hebrew preserves; the WoH translation prefers 'dwelling' as the broader-applicable English term. // *Miqdash* (מִקְדָּשׁ) — *sanctuary, holy place* — names the architectural-cultic complex where YHWH's presence is encountered. The chapter promises a *miqdash* in the midst of the restored people, set *forever* (*l'olam*). // The cluster together names the eschatological-restoration end-state: a permanent covenant of peace, with YHWH's dwelling-presence in a permanent sanctuary among the unified people. The chapter closes the Ezekiel-triptych on this restoration-vision — the inverse of the kavod-departure of chapter 10.

terms.standardEnglish: Satan
terms.rationale: ***Ha-satan*** (הַשָּׂטָן) is the Hebrew Bible's most-contested single noun. The Hebrew preserves the **definite article** *ha* — *the satan*, *the adversary* — making this a **functional role, not a proper name**. The Christian-tradition rendering 'Satan' (capitalized, proper-name) loses the article and personifies what the Hebrew presents as a council-role. // The lexical core: *satan* (שָׂטָן) is the standard Hebrew noun for *adversary, accuser, prosecutor*. Same root as the verb *satan* (oppose, accuse). The noun appears across the Hebrew Bible: Num 22:22, 32 (YHWH's malakh standing as a *satan* — adversary — in Balaam's path); 1 Sam 29:4 (David called a potential *satan* to the Philistines); 2 Sam 19:23 (Abishai called a *satan* to David); 1 Kgs 5:18, 11:14, 11:23, 11:25 (political adversaries); Ps 109:6 (a satan standing at the right hand of the accused). In none of these is *satan* a proper name; it is a functional role. // The Hebrew Bible has only **three contexts** where *satan* appears with the definite article: Job 1-2, Zech 3:1-2 (*ha-satan* standing at the right hand of the high priest Joshua to accuse him), and the implied parallel of 1 Chr 21:1 (where *satan* notably *lacks* the article — possibly transitional to proper-name usage). In Job and Zechariah, the figure is **a council member** functioning as adversarial-prosecutor; in 1 Chronicles 21:1 the grammar is ambiguous. // The figure of *Satan-the-devil* in later Christian theology is built up over centuries from disparate sources (the serpent of Gen 3, the morning-star of Isa 14, the prince of Tyre of Ezek 28, the dragon of Rev 12, the diabolos of the NT). The Hebrew Bible's *ha-satan* of Job 1-2 is **not yet this figure**. The chapter stages ha-satan as: (1) a member of the *bnei ha-elohim* divine council (1:6, 2:1 — *gam ha-satan b'tokham*, *the satan also came among them*); (2) operating under YHWH's delegated authority (1:12, 2:6 — YHWH grants permission and sets the limits); (3) functioning as adversarial-prosecutor or tester (the role-function of the council). The figure is **adversarial-but-not-adversarial-to-YHWH** — adversarial *to Job* in the test-function, but operating *within* YHWH's authority. // The WoH translation preserves the Hebrew article — **'the adversary'** (lowercase, with definite article). This matches the modern scholarly consensus: NJPS Tanakh ('the Adversary'), Clines's Anchor Bible commentary, Habel's OTL commentary, Newsom in the New Interpreter's Bible, Greenberg, Mettinger. The translation does not commit to the proper-name reading and does not pre-empt the reader's encounter with the chapter's actual figure. // Reclassified as `inferred` because the choice between 'the adversary' (preserving the Hebrew article and role-function) and 'Satan' (the Christian-tradition retrojection) is a genuine interpretive call. The lexical evidence and the modern scholarly consensus support the role-function reading; the proper-name reading is the standard English-tradition default. The WoH translation goes with the role-function reading and surfaces the choice in commentary. // The Wheel of Heaven lens reads ha-satan as a council-internal adversarial-tester — a function within the divine governance, not an autonomous evil counterforce. This is the modern divine-council reading (Heiser's *The Unseen Realm*; the classic Mesopotamian *mukallimu* — interrogator/prosecutor — parallel) and is consistent with the WoH framing of the divine system as multi-figure-with-internal-functions.

terms.standardEnglish: to present themselves before the LORD
terms.rationale: *L'hityatzev* (לְהִתְיַצֵּב) is the hithpael infinitive of *yatzav* — *to take a stand, station oneself, present oneself*. The hithpael denotes reflexive-formal action: the bnei ha-elohim *present themselves* (place themselves) before YHWH. // The phrase appears at Job 1:6, 2:1 as a **periodic-court-assembly** formula. The same verbal form recurs in Hebrew Bible contexts of formal presentation: Num 11:16 (the seventy elders presenting themselves at the Tent of Meeting), Deut 31:14 (Moses and Joshua presenting themselves), Josh 24:1 (Israel presenting itself at Shechem), 1 Sam 10:19 (Israel presenting itself for Saul's coronation), 2 Chr 11:13 (the priests presenting themselves to Rehoboam). The verb's standard semantic field is **court-presentation** — appearing before a sovereign or authority. // The Job 1:6 and 2:1 verses are staging a **periodic divine-council assembly**: the bnei ha-elohim come to present themselves before YHWH on a recurring basis. The chapter is positioning this as standard procedure — *vay'hi ha-yom* (the day came) opens the scene as a *day-when-this-happens*, implying repetition. The WoH translation preserves the formal-court register and the YHWH name (not 'the LORD').

terms.literal: from roaming the earth and walking back and forth on it
terms.standardEnglish: from going to and fro in the earth, and from walking up and down in it
terms.wohChoice: from roaming the earth and walking back and forth on it
terms.rationale: Ha-satan's self-description at Job 1:7 and 2:2. Two verbs of locomotion: *shut* (rove, range, traverse) and *hithallekh* (hithpael of *halakh* — walk-about, walk habitually). // The hithpael *mit'hallekh* / *hithallekh* is the chapter's most pointed lexical echo. The same verbal form is used of: YHWH-Elohim *walking about* in the garden of Eden (Gen 3:8 — *mit'hallekh ba-gan*), Enoch *walking about* with the Elohim (Gen 5:22, 24 — *vayit'hallekh Chanokh et ha-Elohim*), Noah *walking about* with the Elohim (Gen 6:9 — *et ha-Elohim hit'hallekh Noach*), and divine-presence-imagery in 2 Sam 7:6-7, Lev 26:12, Deut 23:15. The verb denotes habitual, at-home, frequent movement-in-residence. // Ha-satan walks-about *on the earth* — in the same verbal form as the patriarchs walked-about *with the Elohim*. The chapter is positioning ha-satan as having the earth as its movement-domain in the same residential-habitual register as the antediluvian-favored figures had divine company as theirs. The function is **territorial-investigative** — ranging the earth, presumably gathering information for the council. The verbal echo across Gen 3:8, 5:22-24, 6:9 and Job 1:7, 2:2 is one of the Hebrew Bible's quieter cross-textual linkages. // The WoH translation preserves the standard rendering 'roaming the earth and walking back and forth on it' — the language is straightforward; the lexical echo is for the commentary to surface.

terms.literal: a man there was in the land of Uz
terms.standardEnglish: there was a man in the land of Uz
terms.wohChoice: a man there was in the land of Uz
terms.rationale: *Eretz Uz* — *the land of Uz* — is the geographic-cultural setting of the book of Job. Uz is identified in Gen 10:23 as descended from Aram (Shem's line), in Gen 22:21 as Nahor's eldest son, and in Gen 36:28 as a Horite/Edomite figure. The location is generally placed east of the Jordan, possibly in northern Arabia or Edom (Lam 4:21 locates 'the daughter of Edom dwelling in the land of Uz'). // The chapter is staging Job's story **outside Israel** — among the Aramean / Edomite peripheries, in a non-Israelite cultural sphere. This is consistent with the book's broader Wisdom-literature register (cf. the Aramean / Edomite identifications of Job's three friends at 2:11). The book is positioning itself as **international Wisdom literature** — cognate with Mesopotamian, Egyptian, and broader ANE wisdom-tradition texts. // The WoH translation preserves *Uz* as the Hebrew form (matching the project's general preservation of toponyms). The verb-noun-noun word order in the Hebrew opening (*ish hayah b'eretz Uz Iyov shmo* — 'a man there was in the land of Uz, Job by name') is the standard folktale-opening register; the WoH rendering preserves the inverted opening rather than smoothing to 'There was a man'.

terms.standardEnglish: perfect and upright
terms.rationale: The chapter's signature characterization of Job: *tam v'yashar yere Elohim v'sar me-ra* — *blameless and upright, one who feared Elohim and turned from evil*. The pair recurs at 1:1, 1:8, 2:3 — each time as the affirmation of Job's standing. // *Tam* (תָּם) — from *tom* (completeness, integrity, wholeness) — is **not 'perfect' in the moral-absolute sense**. The KJV/ASV rendering 'perfect' is misleading in modern English (which reads 'perfect' as flawless / mistake-free). The Hebrew *tam* is closer to **'blameless / whole / unblemished'** — the same root that names the ritually-unblemished sacrificial animals (*tamim*, *temimah*). Job is not asserted as morally-perfect; he is asserted as *whole / blameless / without compromising flaw*. // *Yashar* (יָשָׁר) — *upright, straight* — is the ethical-conduct twin of *tam*. The pair together names character (*tam* — internal wholeness) + conduct (*yashar* — external straightness). The Job-formula is the canonical Wisdom-literature character-affirmation. // The WoH translation goes with 'blameless and upright' (matching NJPS, NRSV, modern scholarly translations) — drops 'perfect' (KJV/ASV) which mis-modernizes.

terms.standardEnglish: my servant Job
terms.rationale: YHWH refers to Job as *avdi* — *my servant* — at Job 1:8 and 2:3. The designation is canonical Hebrew Bible high-status: *avdi* is used of Moses (Num 12:7-8, Deut 34:5, Josh 1:2, Mal 4:4), David (2 Sam 7:5, 1 Kgs 11:13, 1 Kgs 14:8, Isa 37:35), the Patriarchs (Ex 32:13 — *Abraham, Yitzchak, Yisrael avadekha*), Isaiah's Suffering Servant (Isa 52:13), Zerubbabel (Hag 2:23), and a small set of other figures of singular covenantal-relational status. // The chapter positions Job in this elite category. The chapter is staging Job as a figure of high covenantal status by YHWH's own designation — an *avdi* alongside Moses, David, Abraham. // The standard English 'my servant Job' is the canonical rendering; the WoH translation preserves it.

terms.standardEnglish: for nothing / for nought / without cause
terms.rationale: The book's keystone single word at Job 1:9 — ha-satan's accusation: *ha-chinnam yare Iyov Elohim* — *does Job fear Elohim for nothing?* The word *chinnam* (חִנָּם, from the root *chen* — favor, grace) means *gratis, for nothing, without compensation, without cause*. // The word appears in many Hebrew Bible contexts: Gen 29:15 (Laban: 'should you serve me *for nothing*?'), Ex 21:2, 11 (the freed slave goes out *chinnam*), Num 11:5 (the fish 'we ate in Egypt *chinnam*' — without cost), Ezek 6:10, 14:23 (YHWH's actions are not *chinnam*), Ps 35:7, 19, 69:5, 109:3, 119:161 (those who hate without cause), Prov 1:11, 1:17, 3:30, 23:29, 24:28, 26:2 (gratuitous violence, gratuitous beating). The semantic field clusters around *transactional vs. gratuitous* and *with-reason vs. without-reason*. // The word returns in 2:3, this time with a reversed application: YHWH says ha-satan *incited me against him to swallow him chinnam* — *without cause*. The chapter's wordplay turns on this reversal: ha-satan accused Job of being righteous *chinnam* (transactional, i.e., not really gratuitous — paid); YHWH accuses ha-satan of being adversarial *chinnam* (gratuitously, without cause). The same word reverses orientation between the two parties. // The book's broader theological argument turns on the question of unconditional vs. transactional relationship. *Chinnam* is the keyword the book uses to mark the issue.

terms.source: וּבֵרֲכוּ אֱלֹהִים בִּלְבָבָם / יְבָרֲכֶךָּ / בָּרֵךְ אֱלֹהִים וָמֻת
terms.translit: u-verakhu Elohim bilvavam / y'varakhekka / barekh Elohim va-mut
terms.literal: and they blessed Elohim in their hearts (= cursed)
terms.standardEnglish: they cursed God (using 'blessed' as euphemism)
terms.rationale: The book of Job uses the verb *barakh* (בָּרַךְ — bless) as a **euphemism for *qillel*/*arar* (curse)** in four passages: 1:5, 1:11, 2:5, 2:9. The reverential-substitution device is well-attested in the Hebrew Bible: when the text needs to put curse-language next to *Elohim* or *YHWH*, the scribal tradition substitutes *barakh* (bless) for the literal curse-verb. The substitution makes the meaning clear from context — the audience is meant to read 'bless' but understand 'curse'. // The same scribal device appears at 1 Kgs 21:10, 13 (Naboth allegedly *blessed* — i.e., cursed — Elohim and the king); Ps 10:3 (where the LXX reads *bless*, the Hebrew context implies *curse*). The Talmudic tradition (b. Berakhot 5b) explicitly identifies the device as a *tikkun soferim* (scribal correction) — a deliberate substitution to avoid placing curse-words next to divine references. // The WoH translation handles the euphemism case-by-case: where the literal *barakh* (bless) is what the chapter means (e.g., Job 1:21 *y'hi shem YHWH m'vorakh* — *may the name of YHWH be blessed*), the rendering is 'bless'. Where the chapter is using *barakh* as the curse-euphemism (1:5, 1:11, 2:5, 2:9), the rendering is 'curse' — restoring what the text actually means — with commentary surfacing the scribal-euphemistic device. // This is one of the few places in the WoH translation where the chosen English **differs from the literal Hebrew verb** — driven by the textual tradition's own evidence about the device.

terms.literal: fire of Elohim fell from the skies
terms.standardEnglish: fire of God fell from heaven
terms.wohChoice: fire of Elohim fell from the skies
terms.rationale: *Esh Elohim* (fire of Elohim) is the Hebrew Bible's designation for **divinely-sent fire** — lightning, supernatural conflagration, or fire-of-divine-origin in general. The phrase appears in 1 Kgs 18:38 (the fire that consumes Elijah's sacrifice on Mount Carmel), 2 Kgs 1:10, 12 (the fire Elijah calls down on the captains of fifties), Job 1:16 (here). The category-noun *Elohim* in the compound functions as an intensifier-genitive: 'God-fire' = fire of divine origin / divinely-sent fire. // The chapter at Job 1:16 names the second disaster as *esh Elohim nafelah min ha-shamayim* — *fire of Elohim fell from the skies*. The verb *nafal* (fall) and the source *min ha-shamayim* (from the skies) name a **descent-from-above fire phenomenon** — consistent with lightning or atmospheric-electrical discharge. The WoH translation preserves the literal compound (esh + Elohim) and the YHWH-translation convention; the commentary surfaces the lightning / meteorological reading.

terms.literal: a great wind came from beyond the desert
terms.standardEnglish: a great wind came from the wilderness
terms.wohChoice: a great wind came from beyond the desert
terms.rationale: Job 1:19 names the fourth disaster as a *ruach gedolah* — a *great wind* — coming *me-ever ha-midbar* (from beyond the desert). The compound *ruach + gedolah* uses the same root *ruach* as the Gen 1:2 cosmogonic *ruach Elohim* and the Ezekiel-vision *ruach-chayyot* — but here in the *wind*-register of the noun's three-fold lexical field (breath / wind / spirit). // The verse describes a **localized strong wind-event**: it came from across the desert, struck the four corners of the house simultaneously, brought the house down on the occupants. The combination of *coming from beyond the desert*, *striking all four corners at once*, and *causing total structural collapse* fits a downburst or strong desert windstorm (khamsin / sharqi). The chapter is naming a *meteorological* disaster — natural-causal, but described in vocabulary that surfaces the *ruach* etymological cluster the book of Job will return to in chapters 26, 27, 38. // *Me-ever ha-midbar* — *from beyond the desert* — names the wind's directional origin. The desert (*midbar*) is the southern/eastern wilderness; the wind is coming from across it (i.e., from further east — the Arabian or Mesopotamian quarter).

terms.source: יְהוָה נָתַן וַיהוָה לָקָח יְהִי שֵׁם יְהוָה מְבֹרָךְ
terms.translit: YHWH natan v'YHWH laqach — y'hi shem YHWH m'vorakh
terms.literal: YHWH gave and YHWH has taken — may the name of YHWH be blessed
terms.standardEnglish: the LORD gave and the LORD has taken away; blessed be the name of the LORD
terms.wohChoice: YHWH gave, and YHWH has taken; may the name of YHWH be blessed
terms.rationale: Job's first-test response at 1:21 — the book's most-quoted line. *YHWH natan v'YHWH laqach* — *YHWH gave and YHWH has taken*. The two clauses are perfectly balanced: the same divine subject (YHWH), the opposite actions (*natan* — gave, *laqach* — took), the same simple syntax. // The chapter's keystone theological claim: Job acknowledges that the same agent who *gave* now *takes*. The acknowledgment is unconditional — neither the giving nor the taking changes Job's orientation. The response closes with *y'hi shem YHWH m'vorakh* — *may the name of YHWH be blessed* — using *barakh* in its **strict-positive sense** (here genuinely *blessed*, not the euphemism-for-curse of v5, v11). // The verse is the precise inverse of the adversary's prediction (v11): the adversary expected Job to *curse* (using *barakh* as euphemism); Job actually *blesses* (using *barakh* in its strict sense). The verbal symmetry across vv 11 and 21 is the chapter's signature rhetorical move — the same Hebrew verb *barakh* used twice with opposite intended meanings, one euphemism, one strict-positive. // The WoH translation preserves YHWH (not 'the LORD') and the balanced parallel structure.

terms.standardEnglish: skin for skin
terms.rationale: Ha-satan's proverb at Job 2:4: *or be'ad or v'khol asher la-ish yiten be'ad nafsho* — *skin for skin — all that a person has, he will give for his life*. The Hebrew preposition *be'ad* (for, on behalf of, in exchange for) sets up an exchange-relationship. // The proverb has been read in two main ways: (1) *self-preservation reading*: skin (Job's body) is what he'll give in exchange for his life — i.e., he'll sacrifice anything *up to* his own bodily integrity to preserve his life; (2) *substitution reading*: one sheds the skin of others (children, servants) to save one's own skin — i.e., Job tolerated the first test because the losses were external; he'll break when the test reaches his own body. // Both readings converge on the same prediction: the next test must reach Job's body. The chapter's argument-structure is escalating from *what Job has* to *what Job is*. // The proverb *or be'ad or* is **not** securely attested in any other ANE source. The construction is unique in the Hebrew Bible. The chapter is staging the adversary as using folk-wisdom that the audience would recognize — but modern scholars cannot fully reconstruct the proverb's standard usage outside this context. The WoH translation preserves the literal 'skin for skin' and lets the commentary surface the two reading-tracks.

terms.source: שְׁחִין רָע מִכַּף רַגְלוֹ וְעַד קָדְקֳדוֹ
terms.translit: shechin ra mi-kaf raglo v'ad qodqodo
terms.literal: severe sores from the sole of his foot to the crown of his head
terms.standardEnglish: sore boils from the sole of his foot unto his crown
terms.wohChoice: severe sores from the sole of his foot to the crown of his head
terms.rationale: Job 2:7 names the second-test affliction as *shechin ra* — *severe sores*. *Shechin* (שְׁחִין) is the Hebrew Bible's standard term for **severe inflammatory skin afflictions**. The term appears at Ex 9:9-11 (the sixth Egyptian plague — *shechin* breaking out on humans and beasts), Lev 13:18-23 (priestly diagnosis of skin disease in the holiness code), Deut 28:27, 35 (curses of the covenant), 2 Kgs 20:7 / Isa 38:21 (Hezekiah's affliction). // The exact dermatological identification has been debated extensively. Proposals include: smallpox, severe boils, pemphigus, infected eruption, generalized impetigo, leprosy (in the old broad sense). The chapter does not specify the medical-diagnostic detail. The qualifier *ra* (bad, severe, evil) intensifies — *shechin ra* is the affliction at its worst grade. // *Mi-kaf raglo v'ad qodqodo* — *from the sole of his foot to the crown of his head* — is the standard Hebrew Bible totality-formula (cf. Deut 28:35, 2 Sam 14:25, Isa 1:6). The affliction is bodily-comprehensive: every inch of Job's skin is covered.

terms.source: גַּם אֶת־הַטּוֹב נְקַבֵּל מֵאֵת הָאֱלֹהִים וְאֶת־הָרָע לֹא נְקַבֵּל
terms.translit: gam et ha-tov n'qabel me-et ha-Elohim v'et ha-ra lo n'qabel
terms.literal: shall we accept the good from the Elohim and not accept the bad
terms.standardEnglish: shall we receive good at the hand of God, and shall we not receive evil?
terms.wohChoice: should we accept good from the Elohim and not accept bad?
terms.rationale: Job's second-test response at 2:10. The rhetorical question is the chapter's keystone theological-philosophical move: the symmetry of acceptance is required by the relationship. If you accept the good as coming from a single source, you accept the bad as coming from the same source. // The verbal pair *tov v-ra* (good and bad) is the **same vocabulary cluster** as Gen 2:9, 2:17 (the tree of *da'at tov v-ra* — *knowing good and bad*) and Gen 3:5, 3:22 (the human became like the Elohim, *yode'ei tov v-ra* — *knowing good and bad*). The chapter is invoking the Edenic-vocabulary cluster: humans *know good and bad*. The capacity to know good and bad — acquired in Gen 3 — is here applied. Job *knows* both, and accepts both. // The verb *n'qabel* (we shall accept, from *qibel* — receive, accept) is the chapter's standing-claim verb. Job is not endorsing the bad; he is accepting it **as coming from the same source as the good**. The chapter is making a sophisticated theological-philosophical move: the categorial unity of good and bad in the divine economy. // *Me-et ha-Elohim* (from the Elohim, with the definite article *ha*) — Job refers to *the Elohim* (the divine source, as a class-noun with definite article — matching the WoH project's general convention). The translation preserves *the Elohim*.

terms.standardEnglish: Job's three friends
terms.rationale: The three friends of Job — *Elifaz ha-Temani* (Eliphaz the Temanite), *Bildad ha-Shuchi* (Bildad the Shuhite), *Tzofar ha-Na'amati* (Zophar the Naamathite) — are introduced at 2:11. The Hebrew *re'a* (רֵעַ) covers *friend, companion, neighbor* — a relational-equal, not subordinate or superordinate. // The three are all **non-Israelite peripheral-Aramean Wisdom figures**. *Teman* is a region in Edom (the southern Transjordan), known for wisdom-tradition (Jer 49:7 — 'is wisdom no more in Teman?'). *Shuach* is one of Abraham's sons by Keturah (Gen 25:2), associated with the Aramean periphery. *Na'amah* is uncertain — possibly an Edomite-Aramean location. // The chapter is staging the dialogue-tradition (chs 3-31) as **international Wisdom-literature discourse** among Aramean-periphery sages — the same cultural register as Job himself (of Uz, also non-Israelite). The book is positioning itself in the ANE Wisdom-literature tradition (cognate with the Babylonian *Theodicy*, the Egyptian *Eloquent Peasant*, the broader international Wisdom-tradition). // The WoH translation prefers 'the three companions of Job' over the standard 'Job's three friends' — *companions* preserves the relational-equal register and the formal-grouping sense of *re'ei* (a group-of-companions, not loose-friends).

terms.standardEnglish: out of the whirlwind
terms.rationale: Job 38:1 opens YHWH's first speech with the source-formula *min ha-se'arah* — *from the whirlwind/storm*. The noun *se'arah* (סְעָרָה) is the same word that opens Ezekiel's vision at Ezek 1:4 — *ruach se'arah ba'ah min ha-tzafon* — *a stormy wind coming from the north*. The verbal echo across Job 38:1 and Ezek 1:4 is one of the Hebrew Bible's tighter cross-textual links — both texts stage divine manifestation as **emerging from a storm-vehicle**. // The chapter is positioning YHWH's voice in the same meteorological-vehicular register as Ezekiel's chayyot-throne apparatus. The chapter's reading of the storm as the source-vehicle of the divine voice connects Job 38 to the broader Hebrew Bible theophany-vocabulary (Ex 19:18 the smoke and trembling at Sinai; 1 Kgs 19:11-13 the wind / earthquake / fire at Horeb; Ezek 1:4 the storm-wind from the north). // *Min ha-se'arah* recurs in Job 40:6 (the start of YHWH's second speech). The two divine speeches both open with the same source-formula — the whirlwind is the chapter's standing vehicle for divine address.

terms.standardEnglish: when I laid the foundations of the earth
terms.rationale: Job 38:4 opens YHWH's cosmological challenge with *b'yosdi-aretz* — *in my founding the earth*. The verb *yasad* (יָסַד) is the architectural-construction term for **laying a foundation**. The Hebrew Bible uses this verb for: founding cities (1 Kgs 16:34 of Hiel founding Jericho), founding the Temple (Hag 2:18, Zech 4:9), founding the cosmos (Ps 24:2, 78:69, 102:25, 104:5, Isa 48:13, 51:13, 51:16, Prov 3:19). // The chapter is invoking the canonical Hebrew Bible image of the **earth as a built-structure** — sunk on foundations, having a cornerstone, with prescribed dimensions. The architectural register is sustained across Job 38:4-7 with *yasad* (found), *memaddim* (measurements), *qav* (measuring line), *adanim* (bases / pedestals), *even pinnah* (cornerstone). // The English 'when I founded the earth' preserves the architectural verb without smoothing to the generic 'created' or 'made'.

terms.literal: and all the sons of Elohim shouted
terms.standardEnglish: and all the sons of God shouted for joy
terms.wohChoice: and all the sons of the Elohim shouted for joy
terms.rationale: Job 38:7 is the **third Hebrew Bible attestation of the *bnei elohim* class** (after Gen 6:2-4 and Job 1:6, 2:1). The verse places the *bnei elohim* at the **moment of cosmic founding** — they were *present* and *active* at the foundation of the earth, the same earth Job (and humans generally) were *not* present for. The lens reading is strong: the divine-council class is being placed *at* cosmic origins, not as latecomers. // The verse forms a poetic couplet: *kokhvei voqer* (morning stars) **/** *bnei elohim* (sons of the Elohim). The parallel structure identifies them — the *morning stars sang* / *the bnei elohim shouted*. The same beings, two registers: astronomical (stars) and divine-council (bnei elohim). This is the Hebrew Bible's most explicit parallel between celestial bodies and divine-council members. // The verb *va-yariyu* (hifil of *rua*) is **military-celebratory**: 'raise a shout / sound an alarm / give a war-cry'. The same verb is used of: Israel's shout at the conquest of Jericho (Josh 6:5, 6:16, 6:20), the battle-cry of Israelite armies (Num 10:9, Judg 7:21), liturgical-worship shouts (Ps 47:1, 95:1, 100:1). The bnei-elohim shout at creation is an **organized choral acclamation** — a coordinated military-or-cultic vocalization. // The lens reads the parallel with attention: the WoH framework's broader engagement with the bnei-elohim divine-council reading (see [`b-nei-ha-elohim`](/wiki/sons-of-elohim/)) is supported in canonical Hebrew Bible textual evidence at Job 38:7. The chapter is identifying the divine-council class with celestial beings at the cosmic origin.

terms.standardEnglish: morning stars
terms.rationale: Job 38:7's *kokhvei voqer* — *morning stars* — is the canonical Hebrew Bible term for celestial bodies visible at dawn. The compound combines *kokhav* (star) + *voqer* (morning); the plural construction names the **class of dawn-visible celestial bodies**. // The verse pairs the *morning stars* with the *bnei elohim* in poetic parallelism (see [`bnei-elohim-yarinu`](/wiki/bnei-elohim-yarinu/) for the identification). The parallel identifies the two phrases: morning stars are the *bnei elohim*, in astronomical register. // The astronomical reading recurs across Hebrew Bible literature: Isa 14:13 (*kokhvei El* — *stars of El*); Dan 8:10 (*tzeva ha-shamayim* — *host of the heavens*, that were thrown down from above); the LXX of Deut 32:8 (where the Masoretic *bnei Yisrael* is replaced by *bnei elohim*). The astronomical-as-divine-council reading is well-attested in the Hebrew Bible's broader textual tradition.

terms.source: וַיָּסֶךְ בִּדְלָתַיִם יָם ... עַד־פֹּה תָבוֹא וְלֹא תֹסִיף
terms.translit: va-yasekh bidlatayim yam ... ad-po tavo v-lo tosif
terms.literal: and he shut the sea behind doors ... this far shall you come and no further
terms.standardEnglish: and he shut up the sea with doors ... hitherto shalt thou come, but no further
terms.wohChoice: shut up the sea with doors / this far you shall come and no further
terms.rationale: Job 38:8-11 stages the **sea-restraint cosmogony**. The chapter uses architectural-containment imagery: the sea is shut behind *delatayim* (doors), with a *beriach* (bar) and a *chuq* (boundary, statute). The sea is given a direct command-quotation: *ad-po tavo v-lo tosif* — *this far you shall come and no further*. // The chapter is reusing the Hebrew Bible / ANE cosmogonic image of the **primordial chaos-sea contained by divine command**. The same image-cluster appears in: Ps 104:9 (*g'vul samta bal-ya'avorun* — *a boundary you set, not to be crossed*), Jer 5:22 (*asher samti chol gevul la-yam* — *who set sand as the boundary for the sea*), Prov 8:29 (*b'sumo la-yam chuqo* — *when he set the boundary for the sea*). The chapter is invoking the canonical sea-restraint tradition. // The ANE comparative material is the **Mesopotamian Tiamat-restraint** of the *Enuma Elish* — Marduk subdues the primordial sea-goddess Tiamat and divides her body to form the cosmos. The Hebrew text demythologizes the procedure but preserves the structural pattern: chaos-sea is *contained* by divine command rather than *defeated* by combat. // The translation preserves the architectural vocabulary (doors, bar, boundary) and the direct-quotation form.

terms.literal: the springs of the sea and the searching of the deep
terms.standardEnglish: the springs of the sea / the search of the deep
terms.wohChoice: the springs of the sea / the searching of the deep
terms.rationale: Job 38:16's *nivkhei yam u-v-cheker tehom* is one of the chapter's strongest **deep-investigation challenges**. The compound names the **deep submarine sources** of the sea. // *Nevekh* (נֵבֶךְ) is a hapax-style noun, generally derived from a root *navakh* meaning *flow forth, spring*. *Nivkhei yam* — *springs of the sea* — names the **abyssal source-points** where the sea emerges. The image: the sea has *specific source-locations*, accessible only by submarine exploration. // *Cheker* (חֵקֶר) is a deliberate-exploratory verb-derived noun — *investigation, search, probing*. *Cheker tehom* — *the searching of the deep* — names the **exploratory traversal** of the abyssal domain. *Tehom* is the same primordial-deep word as Gen 1:2 — etymologically cognate with Akkadian *tiāmtu*/Tiamat (see [`tehom`](/wiki/tehom/) glossary entry). // The chapter's challenge is whether Job has *been to* the deep submarine sources, has done the *deep-sea exploration*. The lens reads this as a reference to investigative-exploratory activity in the deep-water domain — domains the chapter implies are *accessible by divine cognition* but not by human exploration. The translation preserves the exploratory-investigative register.

terms.literal: gates of death and gates of deep darkness
terms.standardEnglish: gates of death / gates of the shadow of death
terms.wohChoice: gates of death / gates of deep darkness
terms.rationale: Job 38:17's *sha'arei mavet v-sha'arei tzalmavet* — *gates of death and gates of deep darkness* — names a **bounded death-domain with entry-gates**. The chapter is staging the death-realm as a *territory with architecture*, not as a generic dissolution. // *Sha'arei mavet* (gates of death) recurs in Ps 9:14, 107:18, in the Hebrew Bible as a fixed expression for the entry-points to *She'ol*. The image is consistent with broader ANE underworld-architecture imagery (the Mesopotamian *Kur* with its seven gates in the Inanna-and-Ereshkigal myth; the Egyptian *Duat* with its gated regions). // *Tzalmavet* (צַלְמָוֶת) is variously analyzed: the traditional reading parses as *tzel* (shadow) + *mavet* (death) — giving 'shadow of death'; the modern reading takes it as a single root *tzalam* meaning 'be very dark' — giving 'deep darkness'. The modern scholarly consensus (BDB, HALOT, NJPS) reads the latter. The WoH translation goes with 'deep darkness' — preserving the etymological-modern reading while the commentary surfaces the traditional 'shadow of death' (the source of the famous Psalm 23:4 KJV phrasing).

terms.literal: storehouses of snow and storehouses of hail
terms.standardEnglish: treasures of the snow / treasures of the hail
terms.wohChoice: storehouses of snow and storehouses of hail
terms.rationale: Job 38:22-23 names the **weather-storehouses** — *otzrot sheleg* (storehouses of snow) and *otzrot barad* (storehouses of hail). The noun *otzar* (אוֹצָר) is the standard Hebrew Bible term for **storehouse, treasury, armory** — used for Joseph's grain-storehouses (Gen 41:56), the Temple-treasures (1 Kgs 7:51), royal arsenals (2 Kgs 20:13, Jer 50:25), divine wind-treasuries (Ps 135:7, Jer 51:16). // The image is striking: weather phenomena (snow, hail) are stored in **physical containers** until they are released. The chapter at v23 names the release-purpose: *chasakhti l-et tzar l-yom q'rav u-milchamah* — *I have reserved for the time of distress, for the day of battle and war*. The weather-storehouses are **military reserves** — strategic weapons deployed at specified times. // The reading is consistent with broader Hebrew Bible weather-as-divine-weapon imagery: hail at Josh 10:11 (the Amorite-coalition defeat), Ex 9:18-26 (the seventh Egyptian plague), Ezek 13:11-13, 38:22 (eschatological judgment hailstorms). The chapter is staging the atmospheric system as a **deployable arsenal**. The WoH translation prefers the term 'storehouses' (modern, technical) over KJV/ASV 'treasures' (archaic, vague).

terms.literal: the Pleiades / Orion / the Zodiac (?) / the Bear with her children
terms.standardEnglish: Pleiades / Orion / Mazzaroth / Arcturus with his sons
terms.wohChoice: Kimah / Kesil / Mazzarot / Ayish with her children
terms.rationale: Job 38:31-32 names four canonical Hebrew Bible **astronomical-constellation classes**, in the densest astronomy-vocabulary cluster in the Hebrew Bible. // **Kimah** (כִּימָה) — generally identified with the **Pleiades** (the seven sister-stars in Taurus, the famous open cluster). The same noun recurs at Job 9:9 and Amos 5:8. // **Kesil** (כְּסִיל) — generally identified with **Orion** (the canonical hunter-constellation in the equatorial sky, with the belt-and-sword pattern). Same noun in Job 9:9, Isa 13:10, Amos 5:8. The literal meaning of *kesil* is 'fool, foolish one' — the constellation may be named for the mythological hunter-figure now bound in his cosmic configuration (cf. the chapter's v31 'cords of Kesil' — Orion *bound* by his belt-and-bands). // **Mazzarot** (מַזָּרוֹת) — a hapax (only occurrence in the Hebrew Bible). The traditional identification (LXX, Vulgate) is **the zodiac constellations** — the twelve constellations of the ecliptic through which the sun, moon, and planets appear to move. The word may be cognate with Akkadian *manzaltu* (a celestial-position term) or with Hebrew *zarah* (scatter — the scattered band of zodiacal constellations). The Mishnaic Hebrew *mazzal* / *mazzaltot* (fortune-stars, astrological signs) descends from this term. The qualifier *b'ito* — *in its appointed time* — supports the **timed-cycle** reading, consistent with the zodiac. // **Ayish** (עַיִשׁ) — generally identified with **the Great Bear** (Ursa Major) constellation. The same constellation is named *Ash* (עָשׁ) at Job 9:9. *Bnei Ayish* — *the children/sons of Ayish* — names the cluster of stars forming the Bear's body. // The four objects together form the canonical Hebrew Bible **astronomical-reference catalogue**: Pleiades (cluster, north), Orion (large constellation, south), Mazzarot (the zodiac band, ecliptic), Ayish (the polar Great Bear). // The Wheel of Heaven framework engages the **precession-of-the-equinoxes and the cosmic-clock astronomy** as a load-bearing element. Job 38:31-32 is one of the Hebrew Bible's most explicit named-constellation references; the canonical Hebrew vocabulary for the Pleiades-Orion-Zodiac-Bear cluster is here. The chapter's challenge — can you bind / loose / bring forth these — acknowledges the constellations as **fixed-cycle astronomical structures** whose timing is not subject to human manipulation. // Reclassified as `inferred` because the specific identifications (Kimah = Pleiades, Kesil = Orion, Mazzarot = Zodiac, Ayish = Bear) are scholarly consensus but not lexically certain — particularly Mazzarot, which is a hapax with contested etymology. The translation preserves the Hebrew names (transliterated) with the standard scholarly identifications surfaced in commentary.

terms.standardEnglish: ordinances of heaven
terms.rationale: Job 38:33's *chuqot shamayim* — *ordinances of the skies* — names **fixed cosmic laws governing the heavens**. *Chuqot* (חֻקּוֹת, plural of *choq*) is the standard Hebrew Bible noun for *statute, ordinance, prescribed rule*. The compound asserts that the heavens operate under **prescribed law-like regularity** — *chuqot*, statutes, not random behavior. // The compound recurs in Jer 33:25 (YHWH's covenant with day and night, *chuqot shamayim va-aretz* — *the ordinances of the heavens and the earth*). The chapter's framing is consistent with the broader Hebrew Bible cosmological vocabulary: cosmic operations are **lawful, predictable, calendrically-tracked**. // The continuation *im-tasim mishtaro ba-aretz* — *can you set its dominion on the land* — invokes the **celestial-influence-on-terrestrial** framework: what happens in the heavens *governs* what happens on the land. The astrological-influence model is implicit (the chapter is not endorsing or refuting it; it is treating it as a domain Job evidently does not control). The WoH framework's engagement with precession and the cosmic clock places this verse in a position of considerable theoretical interest — it is one of the Hebrew Bible's most explicit acknowledgments that the heavens have *ordinances* that *govern* terrestrial domains.

terms.standardEnglish: the Almighty / God
terms.rationale: The book of Job uses a distinctive **patriarchal-Aramean divine-name register** rather than the standard Israelite-covenantal YHWH-Elohim cluster. // **Eloah** (אֱלוֹהַּ) is the rarer singular form of *Elohim*, appearing 52 times in the Hebrew Bible — **41 of those in Job alone**. The form is concentrated in the Job dialogues and is a marker of the book's distinctive divine-name vocabulary. The WoH translation preserves *Eloah* untranslated, matching the project's pattern of preserving Hebrew divine-name forms. // **Shaddai** (שַׁדַּי) is the patriarchal divine name (see [`qol-mayim-rabbim`](/wiki/qol-mayim-rabbim/) for full etymology) — used in the book of Job 31 times. The compound *El Shaddai* appears 7 times in Genesis (17:1, 28:3, 35:11, 43:14, 48:3, 49:25; Ex 6:3 explicitly names *El Shaddai* as the name by which YHWH was known to the patriarchs). // The combined Job-pattern (Eloah-Shaddai-El, with limited use of YHWH-Elohim except in the prose frame of chs 1-2 and 42:7-17) places the book firmly in the **international Wisdom-literature tradition** — the Aramean / Edomite cultural sphere rather than the Israelite-Sinaitic one. // The conventional English 'the Almighty' for *Shaddai* is the Vulgate-via-LXX rendering; the WoH translation preserves the Hebrew name directly, matching its broader preservation pattern (YHWH, Elohim, El).

terms.standardEnglish: once have I spoken ... yea, twice
terms.rationale: The Hebrew Bible's **X-then-X+1 climactic-number formula**. Job 40:5 uses *achat ... u-shtayim* (one ... and two) as the formula's smallest-scale instance. // The formula appears in canonical Hebrew Bible passages: Prov 6:16 (*six things YHWH hates; seven are abominations*); Prov 30:15-31 (*three then four*, sustained pattern across the chapter); Amos 1:3 onwards (*three transgressions of X, then four* — applied to eight nations); Mic 5:4 (*seven shepherds and eight princes*); Eccl 11:2 (*give a portion to seven, even to eight*). // The formula's rhetorical function is **climactic-intensifying**: the first number is the count-claim; the second number (X+1) is the actual climax-point that the rhetorical builds toward. The smaller numbers (Job's 1-then-2) compress the formula to its minimum-effective form: *I have spoken once, will not answer; twice — and I will not add*. The structural meaning: *I have spoken too much already and will speak no more*.

terms.literal: or if you have an arm like El
terms.standardEnglish: hast thou an arm like God?
terms.wohChoice: do you have an arm like El?
terms.rationale: Job 40:9's *zeroa ka-El* — *an arm like El* — invokes the canonical Hebrew Bible **divine-arm** vocabulary. *Zeroa* (זְרוֹעַ) — *arm, strength, military force, agency* — is the standard Hebrew Bible term for active divine power. // The compound *zeroa YHWH* (YHWH's arm) recurs at Ex 6:6 (*outstretched arm*), Deut 4:34 (the outstretched arm of the Exodus), Isa 51:9-10 (*Awake, awake, put on strength, O arm of YHWH*), Isa 53:1 (*to whom has the arm of YHWH been revealed*). The divine *zeroa* is the canonical Hebrew Bible **agency-of-force**. // The chapter uses the shorter form *zeroa ka-El* (an arm like El — using the broader-divine-class name *El* rather than YHWH-Elohim). The question is asked at the **divine-power-class** level: do you have agency comparable to the divine class? The implicit answer: no. // The verse's second clause — *u-v-qol kamohu tar'em* — *can you thunder with a voice like his* — matches the verbal-storm-voice register of theophany (the *qol Shaddai* of Ezek 1:24; the storm-voice of Ps 29). The chapter is testing Job against the **canonical divine-power signature** in two registers: physical (arm) and vocal (thunder).

terms.standardEnglish: Behemoth
terms.rationale: *Behemoth* (בְּהֵמוֹת) is grammatically the **plural of *behemah*** (beast, large quadruped) — used here as a **plural-of-majesty** / **plural-of-intensification**, naming a single super-class creature: *the Beast par excellence*. The same grammatical device is used for *Elohim* (plural form, intensively-singular sense). // The creature is described in Job 40:15-24 with specific anatomical and habitat detail: herbivorous (grass-eater), powerful core musculature (loins, belly), tail like a cedar, bones like channels of bronze, limbs like bars of iron, named *reshit darkhei El* (the first of the ways of El, the cosmogonic-foundational creature), inhabiting wetlands with lotus-trees and reeds, unafraid of even the Jordan river surging into his mouth, uncapturable by the standard eye-and-nose hunting methods. // **Identifications.** Three main proposals: // (1) **Hippopotamus** — the standard modern scholarly default. The herbivorous-aquatic-mass description fits hippo ecology; the wetlands habitat (lotus-trees, reeds, marsh) matches the Nile / Levantine hippo range; the Jordan-river reference places the habitat in the Israelite-knowable geography. The hippo identification has been the consensus since the 19th century. The main difficulty: the *tail-like-a-cedar* description does not fit the hippo (whose tail is short). // (2) **Mythological-cosmological beast** — the canonical land-counterpart to Leviathan-as-sea-beast, paralleling the Mesopotamian land/sea cosmic-creature pairs. The Talmudic-rabbinic tradition reads Behemoth and Leviathan as eschatological-meal creatures preserved for the messianic feast (b. Bava Batra 74b-75a). // (3) **Extinct megafauna / dinosaur** — the 19th-century creationist-tradition reading. The tail-like-a-cedar and bones-like-bronze-pipes match sauropod morphology; the chapter's first-of-the-ways-of-El status matches an *early* creature. The WoH project critiques this reading at the *taninim* glossary entry (Gen 1:21) — the Hebrew is not 'dinosaur' and the chapter does not specify. The same critique applies here. // The WoH translation preserves the Hebrew name *Behemoth* untranslated, matching the project's preservation of *Leviathan* (the parallel sea-creature) and the broader pattern of preserving canonical Hebrew names. The commentary surfaces the identification spectrum. Reclassified as `inferred` because preserving the Hebrew (vs. translating to 'hippopotamus' or another identification) is a translator's interpretive call.

terms.literal: he is the first of the ways of El
terms.standardEnglish: he is the chief of the ways of God
terms.wohChoice: he is the first of the ways of El
terms.rationale: Job 40:19's *hu reshit darkhei-El* — *he is the first of the ways of El*. The chapter places Behemoth in **cosmogonic-first-creature** position. // *Reshit* (רֵאשִׁית) is the same noun that opens Gen 1:1 (*bereshit* — *in beginning*); the lexical core is *beginning, first-fruit, first-of-its-class*. The compound *reshit darkhei* — *first of the ways/works* — recurs in Prov 8:22 of personified Wisdom: *YHWH qanani reshit darko* — *YHWH possessed me, the first of his ways*. The two passages (Job 40:19 of Behemoth; Prov 8:22 of Wisdom) use the same compound for a creature of **singular cosmic-foundational status** — the first of YHWH's projects. // The chapter is asserting Behemoth's primordial-creation status. The reading is consistent with both the **mythological-cosmological identification** (Behemoth as a cosmic-foundational beast) and the **early-extinct-megafauna identification** (Behemoth as an *early* creature in evolutionary time). The lens reads the verse for what it says: Behemoth was created at the *beginning* — first in the sequence of divine works.

terms.standardEnglish: Leviathan
terms.rationale: *Livyatan* (לִוְיָתָן) — Leviathan — the canonical Hebrew Bible sea-dragon. The name is from a root *lavah* (twist, coil, wind) — *the twisting/coiling one*. The Hebrew Bible's most extensively-described creature outside of YHWH himself. // **Hebrew Bible attestations.** Leviathan appears at: Ps 74:14 (YHWH breaks the heads of Leviathan); Ps 104:26 (Leviathan plays in the sea); Isa 27:1 (*YHWH will punish Livyatan nachash bariach v-Livyatan nachash aqallaton* — *Leviathan the fleeing serpent and Leviathan the twisting serpent — the tannin in the sea*); Job 3:8 (the Leviathan-rousers); Job 40:25 - 41:26 (the chapter-long description). // **Isaiah 27:1 is the critical cross-reference.** It **identifies Leviathan with the tannin** — the same word the WoH translation renders as *dragons* in Gen 1:21 (see [`taninim`](/wiki/taninim/) glossary entry, where the Chaoskampf demythologization at Gen 1:21 is documented). Leviathan IS the *tannin* of the prophets; Leviathan IS the canonical Hebrew Bible **sea-dragon** of the dragon-tradition. // **Ugaritic parallel.** The Ugaritic *Lotan* (ltn) is a seven-headed sea-dragon defeated by Baal in the *Baal Cycle* (KTU 1.5 I 1-3). The Hebrew *Livyatan* is the same name with the addition of the *-yatan* ending (possibly intensifier). The Hebrew Bible Leviathan is **the same creature as Ugaritic Lotan**, demythologized into Hebrew Bible canonical-divine-economy but lexically preserved. // **Job 40:25 — 41:26 — the description.** The chapter describes Leviathan with specific anatomical detail: scales as shields, mouth-doors with teeth-of-terror, sneezes-of-light, eyes-like-eyelids-of-dawn, fire-from-mouth, smoke-from-nostrils, breath-kindling-coals, iron-counted-as-straw, bronze-as-rotten-wood, stirring the deep like a boiling pot, leaving a luminous wake, made without fear, king over all the bnei shachatz (sons of pride). // **Identifications.** Three main proposals: (1) **Nile crocodile** — the standard modern scholarly default; the scale-armor, the mouth-and-teeth, the aquatic habitat all match crocodilian anatomy. The crocodile identification is consistent with most of the chapter's specific descriptors except the fire-breathing (which is mythological). (2) **Mythological sea-dragon** — Leviathan as the cosmic-sea creature, paralleling Tiamat / Lotan / Apep across ANE traditions. The fire-breathing imagery is the chapter's clearest mythological-element. (3) **Composite reading** — the chapter is using **real-crocodile anatomical baseline** with **mythological-dragon embellishment** in the fire / smoke / cosmic-king-of-pride registers. The composite reading is the standard scholarly position. // The WoH translation preserves the Hebrew name *Leviathan* and the full descriptive vocabulary including the fire-breathing material. Commentary surfaces the canonical dragon-tradition context and the cross-references to Ugaritic *Lotan*, Mesopotamian *Tiamat*, and the broader global dragon-iconography that the WoH lens engages.

terms.source: מִפִּיו לַפִּידִים יַהֲלֹכוּ ... מִנְּחִירָיו יֵצֵא עָשָׁן ... נַפְשׁוֹ גֶּחָלִים תְּלַהֵט וְלַהַב מִפִּיו יֵצֵא
terms.translit: mipiv lappidim yahalokhu ... mi-n'chirav yetze ashan ... nafsho gechalim t'lahet v-lahab mipiv yetze
terms.literal: from his mouth go torches; from his nostrils comes smoke; his breath kindles coals and flame from his mouth comes out
terms.standardEnglish: out of his mouth go burning lamps; ... out of his nostrils goeth smoke; ... his breath kindleth coals, and a flame goeth out of his mouth
terms.wohChoice: from his mouth go forth torches ... from his nostrils smoke goes out ... his breath kindles coals, and flame goes out of his mouth
terms.rationale: Job 41:11-13 stages **Leviathan's fire-breathing** in extensive detail. Three verses describe the phenomenon at three loci: mouth (v 11 *lappidim* — torches, *kidodei esh* — sparks of fire), nostrils (v 12 *ashan* — smoke), and *nefesh* (v 13 *gechalim t'lahet* — breath kindles coals; *lahab mipiv* — flame from his mouth). // The vocabulary is **direct and unambiguous**: torches go from the mouth, sparks shoot out, smoke pours from nostrils, the breath itself kindles coals, flame issues from the mouth. The chapter is describing a **fire-breathing creature** in the most explicit way the Hebrew Bible vocabulary allows. // **Naturalistic readings** propose: (1) crocodilian breath-misting in cold morning air produces visible vapor resembling smoke/fire; (2) sunlight-refraction through water droplets exhaled by the creature creates light-effects; (3) sun-glints on water-splashes resemble flames. These readings struggle with the chapter's specific vocabulary — *lappidim* (torches) and *gechalim t'lahet* (coals kindled by breath) name **actual fire**, not visible vapor. // **Mythological-poetic readings** place Leviathan in the canonical global dragon-tradition. Fire-breathing dragons appear in: Ugaritic mythology (the Tannin/Lotan tradition); Mesopotamian (Tiamat with her offspring of monsters); Egyptian (Apep the cosmic serpent); Greek (the Chimera, Typhon, Ladon); Norse (Fafnir, Jormungandr); Slavic (Zmey Gorynych); Chinese (the *long*, with their fire-pearl associations); the medieval European dragon-cycles; and Revelation 12-13 (the apocalyptic dragon making war with fire from his mouth). The Hebrew Bible's Leviathan is **a canonical instance of this global dragon-fire tradition**. // **Composite scholarly reading**: the chapter uses real-crocodile anatomical baseline with mythological-dragon embellishment in the fire/smoke/cosmic-king registers. The standard modern position. // The WoH translation preserves the explicit fire-breathing vocabulary across the three verses. Commentary surfaces the canonical dragon-tradition context. The lens reads Leviathan's fire-mouth as part of the broader Hebrew Bible **dragon-class vocabulary** that the WoH project engages from Gen 1:21 (*taninim* / great dragons) onward — Leviathan is the canonical Hebrew Bible sea-dragon, fire-breathing, in continuous tradition with the global cosmic-creature heritage.

terms.translit: atishotav tahel or v-einav k-af'apei shachar
terms.literal: his sneezes flash light, his eyes like the eyelids of dawn
terms.standardEnglish: by his sneezings a light doth shine, and his eyes are like the eyelids of the morning
terms.wohChoice: his sneezes flash light, his eyes like the eyelids of dawn
terms.rationale: Job 41:10's *atishotav tahel or* — *his sneezes flash light*. The compound names a phenomenon the chapter does not explain but describes directly: when Leviathan **sneezes**, **light flashes**. // *Atishot* (עֲטִישׁוֹת) is a hapax from a root *atash* (sneeze) — Leviathan's sneeze-events. *Tahel* (תָּהֶל) is from *halal* (shine, flash, give light) in the hifil — *causes light to flash*. The combined verb-noun pair: **sneezes that emit visible light-flashes**. // The image has been read in multiple ways: (1) **sun-reflection** — sneeze-spray catches sunlight at certain angles, producing visible refraction-flashes (the standard naturalistic reading for the crocodile identification — crocodile-sneezes spraying water in sunlight produce visible glints); (2) **bioluminescence-like phenomenon** — some kind of inherent light-emission (lens-relevant; matches the dragon-tradition's fire-mouth imagery in the next verses); (3) **mythological-poetic** (Leviathan as a cosmic-creature whose body-functions are themselves cosmic-phenomena). // The second clause *einav k-af'apei shachar* — *his eyes like the eyelids of dawn* — extends the light-emission register to the eyes. *Af'apei shachar* — *the eyelids of dawn* — invokes the **dawn-eye** iconography: Leviathan's eyes are like the first opening of the morning sky. The Babylonian Talmud (b. Berakhot 3a) takes this verse as the source-image for the morning-eye motif in Jewish liturgy. // The Egyptian iconographic parallel is the **Wedjat-eye** / **Eye of Horus** / **Sun-eye** motif, often associated with crocodilian or solar imagery. The WoH translation preserves the bare description: sneezes flash light, eyes like dawn-eyelids. The commentary surfaces the naturalistic-vs-mythological reading spectrum.

terms.literal: king over all the sons of pride
terms.standardEnglish: king over all the children of pride
terms.wohChoice: king over all the sons of pride
terms.rationale: Job 41:26's closing identification of Leviathan: *hu melekh al kol bnei shachatz* — *he is king over all the sons of pride*. // *Bnei shachatz* — *sons of pride* — is a hapax-style compound. *Shachatz* (שָׁחַץ) is rare; the same noun appears at Job 28:8 (*bnei shachatz* — *sons of the proud*, parallel to wild beasts). The compound names a class of **proud / lofty / wild** creatures. // The structural-rhetorical close is significant: the chapter's second divine speech opened (Job 40:9-14) with a **challenge for Job to humble all that is proud** (*ge'eh*, *gei'im*). The chapter then closes (41:26) with **Leviathan as king over all proud creatures** — the exact category Job was challenged to subdue. The frame closes: Job cannot subdue the pride-class because Leviathan, who is **king of that class**, is unsubdueable by Job. // The structural connection from 40:11-13 (the challenge to humble the proud) to 41:26 (Leviathan as king of the proud) is the chapter's keystone argument: **humbling the proud is divine work, not human work** — because the king of the proud is a creature only the creator can subdue. The chapter has staged Leviathan as the **proof-by-example** of the impossibility of human-cosmic-justice-administration.

terms.translit: l-shema ozen shmati-kha v-atah eyni ra'at-kha
terms.literal: to the hearing of the ear I had heard you, and now my eye has seen you
terms.standardEnglish: I have heard of thee by the hearing of the ear, but now mine eye seeth thee
terms.wohChoice: by the hearing of the ear I had heard of you, but now my eye has seen you
terms.rationale: The keystone verse of the book of Job (42:5). Job's closing-speech climax names the theological move the whirlwind speeches have produced: a shift from *hearsay-knowledge* of YHWH to *direct-experiential* knowledge. // The verse is patterned: each clause carries a sense-organ noun (*ozen* — ear, *eyin* — eye), a verb of perception in its register (*shama* — hear, *ra'ah* — see), and YHWH as the object of perception (the 2ms suffix *-kha*). The chiasm is the meaning: hearing-of-the-ear / eye-has-seen. // *Shema ozen* (שֵׁמַע אֹזֶן) — *hearing of the ear* — is the canonical Hebrew Bible idiom for **report-knowledge** / transmitted-speech-knowledge (cf. Ps 18:45, 2 Sam 22:45 in the parallel: *l-shema ozen yishame'u li* — *at the hearing of the ear they submit to me*; the idiom names knowledge through report, not through direct contact). *Eyin ra'ata-kha* — *my eye has seen you* — names the inverse: direct visual contact, in the prophetic-vision register. // The book's argument-structure turns on this contrast. The friends (chs 3–37) operate in the *shema ozen* register: their theology is the transmitted wisdom-tradition, and they urge Job to submit to that traditional knowledge. Job's position throughout is that the tradition has failed his case and that he needs YHWH's own response. The whirlwind speeches (38–41) deliver it. The keystone verse names the epistemological shift. // The translation preserves the chiastic structure (ASV-style, against NRSV's smoothing to 'I had heard of you by hearsay'). The verbal patterning of *ozen* / *eyin* and *shama* / *ra'ah* is itself the verse's argument — the parallel construction of the two sense-modalities is what makes the contrast load-bearing. Smoothing to a single noun (*hearsay*) loses the *ozen*–*eyin* pairing. Newsom (NIB 1996, 2003) explicitly defends preserving the chiastic structure on the same ground. // The WoH lens does no extra work here. The verse is the book's own theology of direct-encounter epistemology, not a project-specific reading.

terms.translit: al-ken em'as v-nikhamti al afar va-efer
terms.literal: therefore I despise/retract and am sorry/comforted upon/concerning dust and ashes
terms.standardEnglish: wherefore I abhor myself, and repent in dust and ashes
terms.wohChoice: therefore I despise myself, and repent in dust and ashes
terms.rationale: The most-disputed verse in Job and one of the most-disputed in the Hebrew Bible (42:6). The closing line of Job's submission speech. Six words; every word is contested. // **The three lexical underdeterminacies.** (1) *Em'as* (אֶמְאַס, qal imperfect 1cs of *m-'-s*) — *I despise / reject / retract / melt away*. The verb is intransitive in form here; no explicit object. Traditional translation supplies *myself* ('I despise myself'); modern philology reads intransitive — *I retract* (my previous speech), *I reject*, or *I melt away*. (2) *Nikhamti* (נִחַמְתִּי, niphal perfect 1cs of *n-ch-m*) — the verb's semantic range covers *be sorry, repent, change one's mind, be comforted, console oneself*. The verb cluster is notorious in HB philology (cf. Gen 6:6 *vayinachem YHWH*; entry *vayinnachem-yhwh*). (3) *Al afar va-efer* — the preposition *al* with *afar va-efer* (dust and ashes) admits the **locative** reading (*in dust and ashes*) and the **prepositional-object** reading (*concerning dust and ashes*). // **The three major modern readings.** (a) **Traditional submission** (ASV, KJV, medieval Jewish commentators, the dominant Christian theological reading): *Therefore I despise myself, and repent in dust and ashes*. Job is brought to recognize his presumption and submits in the canonical mourning-posture. The book vindicates submission to divine sovereignty. (b) **Newsom retract-the-lament** (Newsom NIB 1996, 2003): *Therefore I despise myself and repent of dust and ashes*. The *al* reads as *concerning*. Job retracts the ash-heap mourning-posture itself; the direct encounter has rendered the lament-stance obsolete. The book vindicates Job's complaint. (c) **Curtis 1979 / Patrick 1976 comfort-without-repentance**: *Therefore I retract, and I am comforted, concerning dust and ashes*. *Em'as* intransitive; *nikhamti* read in its *be-comforted* sense. Job declares himself comforted concerning his human-frailty (the same phrase *afar va-efer* is Abraham's self-description at Gen 18:27 — mortal-frailty). No self-rejection. // **The choice.** The WoH translation retains ASV's traditional rendering in the running English text, against the project's general preference for modern critical readings. Three grounds. First, the standing rule defaults to the most-attested PD reading where the source is genuinely underdetermined; that rule applies maximally here, since the philological dispute is unresolved among Hebrew Bible specialists. Second, the *al afar va-efer* phrase echoes 2:8 (Job sitting *b-tokh ha-efer*) and the verbal-physical-posture reading has the strongest contextual support: Job is still in the dust-and-ashes setting he entered the dialogue in. Third, the WoH lens has no stake in either reading — the book of Job is wisdom-literature, not divine-council-disclosure, and the keystone of the closing is v 5 (direct-encounter epistemology), not v 6 (the ambiguous submission/release). // **A small lens note.** Reading (c) would make the closing posture continuous with the opening posture: 1:21 (*YHWH gave, YHWH took*) and 2:10 (*shall we accept good from the Elohim and not the bad*) established Job's stance as acceptance-of-the-given without the friends' explanatory machinery. Reading (c) extends that stance through the close. Reading (a) introduces self-rejection the book has not previously demanded. The lens registers the asymmetry as a reason readings (b) and (c) deserve serious weight, but does not adjust the translation: the question is properly resolved by Hebrew Bible philologists, not by the WoH project's interpretive needs.

terms.source: לֹא דִבַּרְתֶּם אֵלַי נְכוֹנָה כְּעַבְדִּי אִיּוֹב
terms.translit: lo dibartem elay nekhonah k-avdi Iyov
terms.literal: you have not spoken to/about me what is established/right, as my servant Job (has)
terms.standardEnglish: ye have not spoken of me the thing that is right, as my servant Job hath
terms.wohChoice: you have not spoken of me what is right, as my servant Job has
terms.rationale: YHWH's verdict on the friends, given verbatim in vv 7 and 8. The keystone inversion of the book: the friends, who throughout chs 3–37 defended divine justice and accused Job of hidden sin, are condemned; Job, who throughout chs 3–37 accused YHWH of injustice and demanded an accounting, is vindicated. // **The *elay* problem.** The preposition *el* normally takes a directional sense (*to, toward*); the expected referential preposition in this context would be *al* (*about, concerning*). The clause uses *el* in what must be a referential function — *about / of me*. All major modern translations render *of me* (ASV, KJV, NRSV) or *about me* (Newsom NIB). Some commentators (Habel OTL 1985) preserve the directional sense — *you have not spoken to me what is right* — yielding *you addressed me but did not address me with rectitude*. The directional reading is grammatically tighter; the referential reading is what the verdict-context demands. The WoH translation follows the standard referential reading. The directional reading is registered in commentary as a minority but defensible alternative. // **Nekhonah.** The feminine singular adjective *nekhonah* (נְכוֹנָה) is used substantivally: *the right thing, what is established, what stands*. The underlying verb *kun* gives the sense of *what stands firm, what is established as true*. The friends' speeches were eloquent but did not *stand* as true accounts of YHWH; Job's speeches — including his accusations — did. // **The structural function of the verdict.** The book's framing chapters (1–2) staged Job's affliction as the result of a *bnei-elohim* council scene, with *ha-satan* as adversarial-prosecutor (entries *b-nei-ha-elohim*, *ha-satan*, *mit'yatzev-al-YHWH*). The dialogue cycle (3–37) and the divine speeches (38–41) never refer back to the council scene; the friends do not know about it, Job does not know about it. The verdict here closes the frame: the friends' theological-justification machinery was wrong precisely because the council scene's logic — Job's affliction as test, not as punishment for hidden sin — was something the wisdom-tradition's *retributive-justice* axiom could not see.
