The friends are not condemned for what they did not know; they are condemned for **building a theological edifice on an axiom the book has shown to be false** (suffering implies sin). Job, who held to his innocence against the axiom, is vindicated for the same reason.

terms.literal: turned the turning of / restored the restoration of
terms.standardEnglish: turned the captivity of
terms.rationale: One of the Hebrew Bible's most-recurring formulae. The phrase *shav et-shvut* (or, in ketiv form, *shav et-shvit*) appears in some 26 verses across Deuteronomy, the Major and Minor Prophets, the Psalms, Lamentations, and Job — the canonical idiom for **divine reversal of communal calamity** (restoration after exile, after defeat, after disaster). // **Key occurrences:** Deut 30:3; Jer 29:14, 30:3, 30:18, 31:23, 32:44, 33:7, 33:11, 33:26, 48:47, 49:6, 49:39; Ezek 16:53 (×3), 29:14, 39:25; Hos 6:11; Joel 3:1 (4:1 MT); Amos 9:14; Zeph 2:7, 3:20; Ps 14:7, 53:7, 85:2, 126:1, 126:4; Lam 2:14; Job 42:10. // **Two derivations.** The traditional rendering *turn the captivity* (ASV, KJV) takes the noun from the root *sh-b-h* (take captive) and reads the phrase metaphorically: *reverse the captivity-state*. The modern scholarly consensus derives the noun from *sh-w-b* (turn, return) — the same root as the governing verb *shav* — and reads it as a **cognate-accusative construction** *turn the turning* meaning *restore what has been lost / restore the fortunes of*. // **Modern scholarship has settled on the cognate-accusative reading.** Dahood (Biblica 1965), Bracke (JSOT 1985, the standard reference study), HALOT (with qualification), and the modern critical translations (NRSV, NJPS, Newsom NIB) all favor *restore the fortunes*. Bracke 1985 demonstrated philologically that the captivity-derivation is unworkable in many of the 26 occurrences. Job 42:10 is the clearest case: Job was never literally taken captive, so the captivity-reading must already be metaphorical, while the *restore the fortunes* reading is direct. // **The WoH translation departs from ASV here.** This is one of the rare cases in the WoH translation where the standing PD-default rule is overridden by modern-philological consensus. The English text renders *restored the fortunes of* (NRSV / NJPS / Newsom) instead of ASV's *turned the captivity of*. The glossary entry will govern every future occurrence of the phrase in the prophets and Psalms; the decision is made once now to avoid having to relitigate it twenty-six times. // The phrase carries no WoH-lens specific reading; the decision is purely philological-conservative — adopting the settled modern consensus where the older PD translations preserve a pre-1980s reading that subsequent Hebrew Bible scholarship has dismantled.

terms.literal: kesitah (archaic unit of currency or weight)
terms.standardEnglish: a piece of money
terms.rationale: An archaic Hebrew unit of currency or weight, occurring only three times in the Hebrew Bible: Gen 33:19 (Jacob's purchase of a parcel of land at Shechem from the sons of Hamor for 100 kesitah), Josh 24:32 (the same Shechem transaction recalled in the burial of Joseph's bones), and Job 42:11 (each of Job's relatives gives him *kesitah echat* — *one kesitah* — as restoration-gift). // **Etymology.** Uncertain. The most cited derivation is from a root meaning *apportioned* or *measured* (cognate proposals in Arabic *qasata* — distribute equitably). The unit's value is unknown; the Hebrew Bible never specifies an exchange-rate with the shekel-system that elsewhere governs HB monetary measurement. // **The LXX lamb-tradition.** The Septuagint renders *kesitah* at Gen 33:19 as *amnados* (lamb), and at Josh 24:32 with *amnoi* (lambs). The lamb-rendering may reflect an early tradition of **lamb-shaped weights** (attested in some ANE contexts) or a tradition of **lamb-equivalent value**, but the identification is speculative; modern philology does not endorse it. The translation *piece of money* (ASV, NRSV) is itself a guess at the unit's character. // **Translation policy.** Standard modern practice (Newsom NIB; NJPS 1985) preserves *kesitah* untranslated and glosses it; this respects the unit's archaism and avoids importing a guess into the running text. The WoH translation follows that practice. The entry governs all three occurrences (Gen 33:19, Josh 24:32, Job 42:11).

terms.standardEnglish: Jemimah / Keziah / Keren-happuch
terms.wohChoice: Yemimah / Ketziah / Keren-happukh (transliterated; semantic readings glossed)
terms.rationale: Job's three restoration-daughters (Job 42:14). None of the three names is attested elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible as the name of another person; all three are unique. All three are also semantically transparent in ways that cluster in a single semantic field — beauty, fragrance, adornment. // **Yemimah** (יְמִימָה). Three etymological proposals. (1) From *yom* / *yamim* (day / days) — yielding *day-bright, like-the-day*. The rabbinic tradition takes this reading: Targum Job 42:14 renders *as-the-day*; b. Bava Batra 16b reads 'as bright as the day.' (2) From *yamim* in a warm-color sense (cognate with Arabic *yam* — sea, dove, warm-day) — yielding *dove*. Pope (AB 1965), Habel (OTL 1985), and Newsom (NIB 1996) take this reading; Arabic *yamamah* — *turtle-dove* — is the closest cognate. The dove-reading is currently the leading critical option. (3) Unanalyzable proper name. // **Ketziah** (קְצִיעָה). Identical in form to *qetzi'ah* — *cassia*, a fragrant spice related to cinnamon. The spice is attested at Ps 45:9 (*qetzi'ot* — cassias, in the royal wedding-psalm's perfume-list) and at Ezek 27:19 (Tyre's spice-trade inventory). The cassia-identification is universal across modern scholarship; the name **is** the spice. // **Keren-happukh** (קֶרֶן הַפּוּךְ). Literally *horn of pukh*. *Pukh* is the dark cosmetic eye-paint / kohl / stibium-antimony used in ANE eye-makeup, attested at 2 Kgs 9:30 (Jezebel paints her eyes *ba-pukh*), Jer 4:30 (the unfaithful daughter of Zion), Isa 54:11 (the stones of restored Zion set *ba-pukh*). *Keren* is *horn* — by extension, a horn-shaped vessel, the standard cosmetic-flask shape. *Keren-happukh* = *kohl-horn*, *cosmetic-flask*, by metaphorical extension *the beautified one*. // **The shared semantic field.** All three names cluster in beauty / fragrance / adornment: *day-bright* or *dove*, *cassia-spice*, *kohl-flask*. The pattern is itself the comment v 15 will make explicit (*no women so fair were found in all the land*). The Hebrew Bible normally names women in genealogical narratives by lineage-marker; the restoration-daughters here are named by aesthetic attribute, and the choice is structural to the book's restoration-as-beauty closing. // **Translation policy.** Standard practice (ASV, NRSV, Newsom, NJPS) preserves the names untranslated. The WoH translation follows the project's transliteration conventions (Yemimah, Ketziah, Keren-happukh) rather than the older spellings (Jemimah, Keziah, Keren-happuch). The semantic readings (Dove, Cassia, Kohl-Horn) belong in the glossary, not in the running text — preserving the names as names is the responsible default. // The entry is `inferred` rather than `direct` because while the cassia-reading for Ketziah is fully secure, the dove-vs-day-brightness reading for Yemimah is a scholarly inference, and the metaphorical *beautified-one* reading for Keren-happukh extends beyond the literal *kohl-horn* sense.

terms.literal: old and full / satiated of days
terms.standardEnglish: old and full of days
terms.rationale: The Hebrew Bible's canonical formula for the **blessed completion of a long life**. The phrase or its close variants names the patriarchal-good-death — death at the natural end of a long, full life, not violent or premature, signifying divine favor at the close. // **HB occurrences:** Abraham (Gen 25:8: *vayyamot Avraham b-seyvah tovah, zaqen v-save'a* — *Abraham died in good old age, old and satisfied*); Isaac (Gen 35:29: *vayyigva Yitzchaq vayyamot... zaqen u-s'va yamim* — *Isaac expired and died... old and full of days*); David (1 Chr 29:28: *vayyamot b-seyvah tovah, s'va yamim v-osher v-khavod* — *he died in a good old age, full of days, riches, and honor*); Jehoiada the priest (2 Chr 24:15: *vayyizqan Yehoyada vayyisba yamim vayyamot* — *Jehoiada grew old and was full of days, and died*); Job (42:17: *vayyamot Iyov, zaqen u-s'va yamim*). // **The formula's structural function.** It marks the figure as a recipient of completed divine favor — the canonical patriarchal mode of departure. Its application to Job is structurally striking: Job is non-Israelite, set in Uz outside the covenantal line, framed by a divine-council scene rather than by a covenantal moment, and yet granted the patriarchal-good-death formula. The book that opened with Job placed *outside Israel* in the international Wisdom-tradition register closes by giving him the death-notice of an Israelite patriarch. // **Translation.** Uncontested. ASV, KJV, NRSV, Newsom NIB all render *old and full of days*. The literal *s'va* — *satiated, sated* — is preserved in the secondary sense of *full*; the underlying image is one of having had enough days, of being satisfied with the lifespan granted. The WoH translation follows the consensus.

terms.literal: go for yourself / go to yourself
terms.standardEnglish: Get thee out / Go forth
terms.rationale: The qal imperative of *halakh* ('go') followed by a *lamed*-prefixed second-person pronoun. The construction is the so-called **ethical dative** (or *dativus commodi* / dative of advantage / dative of interest) — the *lamed* + pronoun adds a reflexive overtone that resists single-English rendering: *go for yourself*, *go on your own account*, or in the most expansive scholarly reading, *go to your-self* (i.e., into your own becoming). // **Three readings circulate.** (a) The classical philological position treats the *lamed* as redundant intensification — Hebrew imperative idiom occasionally takes a dative pronoun without independent semantic load (cf. *qum-lakh*, *shuv-lakh*). On this reading the standard ASV 'get thee out' captures the sense. (b) Rashi, drawing on Genesis Rabbah 39:9, reads the *lamed* as *l'ha'natah'kha v'l'tovat'kha* — *for your benefit and for your good* — the journey is divinely-prescribed enrichment. (c) The modern critical consensus (Sarna, *JPS Torah Commentary*, 1989; Alter, *The Five Books of Moses*, 2004; Hamilton, NICOT, 1990) reads the *lamed* as a genuine ethical dative carrying a reflexive-advantageous nuance — the journey is *for yourself*, the new identity is *toward yourself*. // **The phrase recurs only once more in the Hebrew Bible: Gen 22:2**, the *aqedah*, where YHWH commands Abraham *lekh-lekha el eretz ha-Moriah* — *go forth to the land of Moriah* — to offer Isaac. The doubled use forms a deliberate *inclusio* across the Abraham cycle: the call that opens it (12:1, leave father's house) and the call that closes it (22:2, offer the son who is the future) are marked with the same idiom. Sarna and Alter both emphasize the rhetorical bracket. // The WoH translation reads 'Go forth' — intensified over bare 'go', neutral on the ethical-dative philological question, leaving the inclusio with 22:2 to be marked there. The claim_type is *direct* because identifying the construction as an ethical-dative imperative and noting the 12:1/22:2 inclusio are uncontested philological-literary observations.

terms.literal: shall be blessed in you / shall bless themselves by you
terms.standardEnglish: shall be blessed
terms.wohChoice: shall be blessed (at niphal-pointed verses); shall bless themselves (at hithpael-pointed verses)
terms.rationale: The Genesis universal-blessing-promise formula appears five times across the patriarchal cycle. **The MT itself preserves two different pointings of the verb:** *niphal* (passive: *shall be blessed*) at Gen 12:3, 18:18, 28:14; *hithpael* (middle / reflexive: *shall bless themselves*) at Gen 22:18, 26:4. The pointing difference is the central piece of philological evidence for one of the most-discussed translation cruxes in Genesis. // **Three positions circulate.** (a) **Traditional Christian / Pauline reading.** The LXX renders all five verses with the passive *eneulogēthēsontai*; Paul cites the verse as straightforwardly passive in **Galatians 3:8** (*eneulogēthēsontai en soi panta ta ethnē* — 'in you shall all the nations be blessed'), reading the passive as the textual foundation of the gospel-to-the-gentiles. ASV, RSV, NRSV, and most English translations in the Christian tradition follow Paul. (b) **Modern critical consensus** (Speiser, *Anchor Bible Genesis*, 1964; Sarna, JPS, 1989; Westermann, *Genesis 12–36*, Hermeneia, 1985; NJPS, JPS Tanakh). The original idiom was middle/reflexive across all five verses: *by you all the families of the ground shall bless themselves* — i.e., they shall invoke Abram's name as a paradigm of blessing (cf. Gen 48:20, *by you shall Israel bless, saying, 'Elohim make you as Ephraim and Manasseh'*). On this reading the niphal pointings at 12:3 / 18:18 / 28:14 are Masoretic harmonization toward the theologically richer passive sense, against an original consistent hithpael. (c) **MT-preserving reading** (Wenham, WBC, 1994; Hamilton, NICOT, 1990). The niphal/hithpael distinction was meaningful for the redactor and should be preserved verse-by-verse — passive at 12:3 / 18:18 / 28:14, middle at 22:18 / 26:4. // **The WoH translation takes position (c) — verse-by-verse, matching the MT pointing at each occurrence.** The choice preserves the MT's own variation rather than smoothing toward either pole; the philological dispute and the Pauline reception are surfaced in the commentary. The claim_type is *direct* because the niphal/hithpael distinction is a fact of the pointed MT, and presenting the dispute fairly across the three positions is a *direct* report of the textual evidence and scholarly state-of-the-art — not an interpretive choice. The translation choice itself follows the MT.

terms.literal: the soul(s) that they had made in Charan
terms.standardEnglish: the souls that they had gotten in Haran
terms.wohChoice: the souls that they had made in Haran
terms.rationale: The phrase *ha-nefesh asher asu b-Charan* — *the souls that they had made in Haran* — has no exact idiomatic parallel in the Hebrew Bible. *Asah* (make / do) does not standardly take *nefesh* (soul / person) as object. The verse forces a decision and **three readings circulate**. // (a) **The proselyte reading** — Rashi (following Sifrei Deuteronomy 32 and Onkelos's targum, which renders *de'sha'abidu l'oraita*, 'those whom they made subject to the Torah') reads *asu* as *converted, made into adherents*. On this reading Abram and Sarai conducted a missionary enterprise in Haran and brought converts with them. The reading has deep traditional roots but no philological support outside the rabbinic-targumic tradition. // (b) **The slaves-acquired reading** — Speiser (*Anchor Bible Genesis*, 1964), Sarna (*JPS Torah Commentary*, 1989), and Westermann (*Genesis 12–36*, Hermeneia, 1985) read *asu* as a variant of *qanah* (acquire), parallel to the *rekhush asher rakhashu* (*substance that they had gathered*) of the immediately preceding clause. The *nefesh* here are household slaves and dependents acquired by purchase in Haran. The parallelism with *rekhush rakhashu* is structurally tight. // (c) **The dependents / household reading** — a softer version of (b): the *nefesh* are the broader household — slaves, dependents, attached persons — without specifying the mode of acquisition. NJPS renders 'the persons that they had acquired in Haran'; NRSV 'the persons whom they had acquired in Haran'. // The chapter's later content (vv 16, 20 — male and female slaves listed among Abram's holdings) suggests the household-economy reading is the one the chapter assumes, but the verse-form itself declines to disambiguate. The WoH translation preserves the literal *asu* (*made*) as 'the souls that they had made' — matching ASV's 'gotten' in keeping the literal verb, leaving the proselyte/slaves question for commentary. The claim_type is *direct* because the lexical oddness and the three-way dispute are uncontested facts of the philological literature; the translation choice (preserve the literal oddness) is the most conservative possible reading.

terms.literal: oak of Moreh / oak of the Teacher
terms.standardEnglish: oak of Moreh / terebinth of Moreh
terms.rationale: Two lexically loaded words name Abram's first stop in the promised land. // **First, *elon*.** The botanical identification is contested in Hebrew lexicography. *Elon* (אֵלוֹן), *elah* (אֵלָה), and *alon* (אַלּוֹן) are three related forms that can refer to *oak*, *terebinth*, or large-tree-of-sacred-significance generally. BDB groups all three under 'oak / terebinth'; HALOT distinguishes *alon* (oak) from *elah/elon* (terebinth) with less than full confidence. The LXX renders δρῦς (oak) here. Modern scholarly translations vary: ASV 'oak', NJPS 'terebinth', NRSV 'oak'. The species precision is not load-bearing for the verse. // **Second, *Moreh*.** The proper-name form derives from the root y-r-h, 'to throw, point, teach, give oracle / *torah*'. *Moreh* is the hiphil participle: *teacher, one who instructs, oracle-giver*. The word retains its semantic transparency throughout the Hebrew Bible — *moreh* appears as a common noun meaning 'teacher / instructor' at Isa 9:14, Isa 30:20, Hab 2:18, Prov 5:13, Job 36:22. The 'oak of Moreh' is therefore plausibly read as **'oak of the Teacher / oak of the Oracle-Giver'** — a sacred-tree site associated with prophetic / divinatory consultation. // **The sacred-tree pattern across the patriarchal and pre-monarchic corpus is consistent.** *Elon Moreh* recurs at Deut 11:30 (the Gerizim/Ebal blessing-and-curse ceremony happens *etzel elonei Moreh*). Jacob buries the foreign gods *under the oak which was at Shechem* (Gen 35:4). Deborah is buried *under the oak below Bethel* called *Allon Bakhut* (Gen 35:8). Joshua sets up a stone *under the oak that was by the sanctuary of YHWH* at Shechem (Josh 24:26). Judges 6:11 places the angelic call of Gideon *under the oak which was in Ophrah*. Judges 9:6, 37 names *elon mutzav* and *elon m'on'nim* (the oak of the diviners) at Shechem. The verse here at 12:6 names the spot where the first patriarchal theophany (v 7) will immediately follow — and a sacred-tree-oracle context is exactly what the next verse delivers. // The WoH translation reads 'oak of Moreh' with ASV — the proper-name reading preserved, the teacher/oracle resonance and the sacred-tree pattern surfaced in commentary. The claim_type is *inferred* because the *Moreh*-as-teacher / sacred-tree-oracle-site reading is a scholarly inference from the cognate pattern across the corpus rather than from any single explicit statement in the text; the inference is well-attested in critical scholarship (Westermann, Speiser, Sarna all note the oracle-tree pattern) but the verse itself names a place, not a function.

terms.literal: and YHWH was seen / appeared / let himself be seen to —
terms.standardEnglish: And the LORD appeared unto —
terms.rationale: The patriarchal-theophany formula. The verb is the niphal of r-a-h (see), conjugated as *was seen / appeared / let himself be seen / made himself visible*. The niphal is mediopassive: it can be read as YHWH being passively *seen by* the patriarch (the receptive sense) or as YHWH actively *causing himself to be seen* (the reflexive-causative sense). The standard English 'appeared' smooths the niphal into a colorless intransitive — *appeared* in English suggests sudden visibility without specifying its mode. The Hebrew preserves the question of whether the seeing originates with the seer or with the seen. // **The formula recurs across the patriarchal narratives, marking covenantal-vision moments:** Gen 12:7 (first appearance to Abram at the oak of Moreh — the inaugural patriarchal theophany); Gen 17:1 (the covenant of circumcision); Gen 18:1 (Mamre — the appearance explicitly embodied as three men); Gen 26:2, 26:24 (to Isaac at Beersheba); Gen 35:9 (to Jacob on return from Paddan-Aram); Gen 48:3 (Jacob recalls the formula in his blessing of Joseph's sons). The formula bookends every major covenantal moment in the Abraham–Isaac–Jacob arc. // **The mode of seeing** is left unspecified at 12:7 and only becomes explicitly embodied at 18:1, where the three men eat, walk, and speak. The chapters between (12, 13, 15, 17) describe YHWH appearing, speaking, and giving promises, but do not editorialize on whether the appearance is visual / auditory / embodied / dreamed / visionary. The WoH translation preserves the bare 'appeared' across all instances and leaves the mode-of-seeing question for verse-level commentary; the Wheel of Heaven posture surfaces only where the embodiment is explicit (18:1 most prominently) to note continuity with the divine-descent register of chs 1-11. // The claim_type is *direct* because the theophany-formula and its patriarchal-narrative recurrences are uncontested facts of the text; the translation choice ('appeared') matches every major English version.

terms.literal: to your seed I will give this land
terms.standardEnglish: Unto thy seed will I give this land
terms.wohChoice: To your seed I will give this land
terms.rationale: **The nuclear form of the patriarchal land-promise.** Seven Hebrew words at Gen 12:7 constitute the bare covenant-nucleus that will be elaborated across the patriarchal arc. *Zera* (seed) — the agricultural-biological term for offspring / posterity / line — will be the chapter's recurring designator for the patriarch's progeny. // **The promise-formula's expansions across Genesis:** Gen 13:14-17 adds quadrant directions and the *innumerable-as-the-dust-of-the-earth* imagery; Gen 15:7-21 adds the cut-covenant ritual and the explicit Euphrates-to-the-river-of-Egypt boundaries; Gen 17:7-8 adds *brit olam* (everlasting covenant) and 'an everlasting possession'; Gen 22:17 adds the *innumerable-as-the-stars-and-as-the-sand-on-the-seashore* doubled image; Gen 26:3-4 reissues the promise to Isaac; Gen 28:13-14 reissues to Jacob at Bethel; Gen 35:11-12 reissues again to Jacob; Gen 48:4 Jacob recalls the formula to Joseph. The land-promise threads through the entire patriarchal cycle as the recurring covenantal anchor. // **The *zera* / seed image.** The Hebrew *zera* preserves the agricultural metaphor — seed-in-ground, growth, multiplication — that the patriarchal cycle's storytelling relies on (the recurring tension of barren matriarchs, the late-life births, the seed-that-shall-be-as-the-dust). Modern English translations vary: ASV 'seed' (preserves the imagery), NIV 'offspring' (middle register), RSV / NRSV 'descendants' (modern abstraction). The WoH translation preserves *seed* with ASV — the imagery is load-bearing across the corpus and resurfaces in the Psalter and the Prophets. // The claim_type is *direct* because the promise-nucleus and its recurrences are uncontested facts of the text; the translation choice (*seed* over *descendants*) preserves an image the source language carries explicitly.

terms.literal: and he called with / in the name of YHWH
terms.standardEnglish: and called upon the name of the LORD
terms.wohChoice: and called on the name of YHWH
terms.rationale: The Hebrew Bible's stable formula for **cult-site invocation**. *Qara b'shem YHWH* (literally *call with/in the name of YHWH*) covers a semantic range from worship-invocation through proclamation of the name through ritual naming. // **The formula's introduction in Genesis is at Gen 4:26**, in the closing notice of the Seth-Enosh genealogy: *az huchal liqro b'shem YHWH* — *then it was begun to call on the name of YHWH* — marking the antediluvian origin of YHWH-cult. // **The patriarchal recurrences mark altar-sites along the Abrahamic and Isaac itineraries:** Gen 12:8 (Abram in the hill country between Bethel and Ai — the chapter's first instance); Gen 13:4 (Abram returns to the same altar after the descent to Mitsrayim — the chapter is structurally framing the Mitsrayim episode between two callings-on-the-name at Bethel); Gen 21:33 (Abraham at Beersheba, after planting a tamarisk); Gen 26:25 (Isaac at Beersheba, building an altar). The verb is the patriarchal altar-formula. // **The cult-resumption claim.** The chapter is establishing Abram as the resumption of the Seth-Enosh YHWH-cult tradition — the one whose line had been broken by Babel and the dispersion. The first thing Abram does on entering the promised land (after the theophany of v 7 and the altar at Shechem) is to re-found the worship that had begun at Enosh's generation and been interrupted by the post-Flood human ambition. // The WoH translation reads 'called on the name of YHWH' matching ASV and the rendering used at 4:26 — the consistency across the formula's occurrences is the load-bearing feature. The claim_type is *direct* because the formula and its recurrences are uncontested facts of the text.

terms.standardEnglish: (translation issue spans pericope, not single phrase)
terms.rationale: **Robert Alter, *The Art of Biblical Narrative* (1981, expanded ed. 2011), is the canonical scholarly treatment of the wife-sister sequence as a *type-scene*.** Three instances appear in Genesis: **Gen 12:10–20** (Abram, Sarai, Pharaoh of Mitsrayim); **Gen 20:1–18** (Abraham, Sarah, Abimelech of Gerar); **Gen 26:1–11** (Isaac, Rebekah, Abimelech of Gerar). // **The shared narrative skeleton.** Patriarch travels to foreign land under duress (famine in 12 and 26; unspecified in 20); pre-empts the danger of foreign king seeing his wife by passing her off as his sister; wife is taken into foreign king's house (in 12 and 20; thwarted in 26 when Abimelech sees the couple together); YHWH intervenes (plagues in 12; dream-warning to Abimelech in 20; visual disclosure in 26); king discovers the deception and confronts the patriarch with rebuke (12:18, 20:9-10, 26:9-10); king returns the wife with protective decree (12:19-20, 20:14-16, 26:11). The repetition with variation is the diagnostic feature of biblical type-scene composition. // **Two readings of the parallel** circulate in critical scholarship. **(a) The classical source-critical reading** (Gunkel, *Genesis*, 1901; Speiser, *Anchor Bible Genesis*, 1964) treats the three as variant traditions preserved by different sources (12:10–20 = J; 20 = E; 26 = J again — same narrative tradition independently incorporated by different strata). **(b) The modern literary-critical reading** (Alter, 1981; Sternberg, *The Poetics of Biblical Narrative*, 1985) treats them as deliberate narrative repetition — the same scene played three times to develop character, contrast patriarchs, and trace ethical maturation across the cycle. The two readings are not mutually exclusive. // **The WoH translation's task** is to preserve the vocabulary that makes the type-scene recognizable across the three episodes: the *yefat mar'eh* / *tovat mar'eh* beauty-formula (see [eshet-yefat-mareh](#eshet-yefat-mareh)); the *imri-na achoti at* / *achoti hi* sister-claim (see [imri-na-achoti-at](#imri-na-achoti-at)); the *yarad Mitsraymah* / *bo el-... ha-eretz* descent-language; the *vayyaqach et-ha-ishah* taking-formula; the *vay'shal'chu oto* / *vay'shallechehu* expulsion-formula. The translation preserves consistent vocabulary across the three episodes so the type-scene structure is visible to the English reader. // The claim_type is *inferred* because the type-scene framing itself is a 20th-century scholarly synthesis (Alter 1981 building on Olrik and the Homeric scholarship of Walter Arend); the three episodes are *direct* facts of the text, but the *type-scene* identification is a literary-critical inference about authorial / redactional intentionality.

terms.standardEnglish: a fair woman to look upon
terms.rationale: **A stock formula in biblical narrative for the dangerously attractive female figure.** *Yefat* is the construct form of the feminine adjective *yafeh* (beautiful, fair, fine); *mar'eh* (appearance, sight, visible aspect) is the noun governed by the construct. The doubled-construct form *yefat mar'eh* — *beautiful of appearance* — is a fixed idiom across the Hebrew Bible. // **The formula's recurrences track the cases where a woman's beauty becomes consequential:** **Sarai** at Gen 12:11, 12:14 (here at the wife-sister type-scene's first instance — driving the Pharaoh-episode); **Rebekah** at Gen 26:7 (*tovat mar'eh*, 'good of appearance' — wife-sister second instance); **Rachel** at Gen 29:17 (*yefat to'ar v'yefat mar'eh*, the doubled form 'beautiful of form and beautiful of appearance' — Jacob's love at first sight); **Joseph** at Gen 39:6 (*yefeh to'ar v'yefeh mar'eh*, the same doubled form in masculine — making Joseph one of only two men in the Hebrew Bible explicitly described with the beauty-formula; the other is David at 1 Sam 16:12 *im yefeh einayim v'tov ro'i*); **Abigail** at 1 Sam 25:3 (*tovat sekhel v'yefat to'ar*, intelligence-and-beauty); **Tamar** daughter of Absalom at 2 Sam 14:27 (*ishah yefat mar'eh*); **Bathsheba** at 2 Sam 11:2 (*tovat mar'eh me'od* — the seeing-from-the-roof scene); **Abishag** the Shunammite at 1 Kgs 1:3–4 (*na'arah yafah ... yafah ad-me'od*); **Vashti** at Esther 1:11 (*tovat mar'eh*); **Esther** at Esth 2:7 (*yefat to'ar v'tovat mar'eh*). // **The formula functions in the narrative as a plot-trigger:** it signals that the woman's (or in 39:6 and 1 Sam 16:12, the man's) beauty is about to drive consequential events. Sarai's beauty here drives the Pharaoh-episode; Rebekah's at 26:7 drives the Abimelech-episode; Rachel's drives Jacob's fourteen years of labor; Joseph's drives Potiphar's wife's appropriation-attempt; David's drives his rise (and later, Bathsheba's drives his fall); Bathsheba's drives the David-Uriah crisis; Esther's drives the deliverance of the diaspora. The Hebrew Bible's storytelling reserves the beauty-formula for cases where beauty becomes a vector of narrative force. // The WoH translation preserves the formulaic structure 'a woman beautiful of appearance' — over ASV's archaic 'a fair woman to look upon' and the modern smoothing 'a beautiful woman' (which dissolves the formula). The intratextual signal is preserved. The claim_type is *direct* because the formula and its recurrences are uncontested facts of the corpus.

terms.standardEnglish: Say, I pray thee, thou art my sister
terms.wohChoice: Say, please, that you are my sister
terms.rationale: **The chapter's most ethically debated phrase, and the wife-sister type-scene's core deceit.** *Imri-na achoti at* — *say, please, my sister you (are)*. The strategy: have Sarai claim to be Abram's sister rather than his wife, so that a Pharaonic appropriation of her does not require killing the husband. The same strategy is redeployed at Gen 20:2 (Abraham to Abimelech: *achoti hi*, 'she is my sister') and Gen 26:7 (Isaac to Abimelech: *achoti hi*). // **Three readings of the strategy circulate.** // **(a) The morally-compromised reading** — the modern critical mainstream (Speiser, *Anchor Bible Genesis*, 1964; Sarna, *JPS Torah Commentary*, 1989; Westermann, *Genesis 12-36*, Hermeneia, 1985; Hamilton, NICOT, 1990; Alter, *The Five Books of Moses*, 2004). Abram is lying to save his own life by exposing Sarai to sexual appropriation. The story is included in the patriarchal cycle precisely because it is morally ambiguous — the patriarchs of Genesis are not idealized; the narrative records their lapses (Abram and Hagar in 16; Jacob's deception of Isaac in 27; Judah and Tamar in 38; Joseph's brothers' fratricidal plot in 37). The verse-form's *na* (politeness particle) and *l'ma'an yitav-li* (*so that it may go well with me*) make Abram's self-interest explicit. Pharaoh's rebuke in v 18 (*what is this you have done to me?*) delivers the chapter's implicit narrative judgment. // **(b) The apologetic-rabbinic reading** — Genesis Rabbah 41:2; the targumim; Rashi. Abram's claim is **technically true** — Sarai is Abram's half-sister (per Gen 20:12, where Abraham tells Abimelech *she is indeed my sister, the daughter of my father, but not the daughter of my mother*). The strategy is a *partial truth* deployed under duress, not a full lie. On this reading Abram's deception is mitigated. // **(c) The defensive-tactical reading** — some modern apologetic commentators; less common in critical scholarship. Abram is acting within the constraints of a foreign court system in which marriage protections did not extend to traveling foreigners. The sister-designation activates kinship-negotiation protocols that would not have applied to a foreign couple presented as married, where the husband would simply be removed. // **The Sarai-as-half-sister claim of Gen 20:12 is itself complicated** — the verse appears only in the second wife-sister episode and may be apologetic redactional material aimed at exactly the problem the chapter creates. Critical scholarship is divided on whether 20:12 should be read as authentic Abrahamic genealogy or as later harmonization. // The WoH translation preserves the polite-imperative + politeness-particle + sister-claim structure as 'Say, please, that you are my sister' — modern register, preserves *na* as politeness particle. The chapter's silence about the strategy's ethics is preserved; commentary at this verse and at 20:12 surfaces the dispute. The claim_type is *direct* because the strategy, its recurrences across the type-scene, and the three-way reading-dispute are all uncontested facts of the corpus and the scholarly literature.

terms.standardEnglish: and camels
terms.rationale: **The patriarchal narratives' most-cited anachronism.** Camels appear in the patriarchal cycle as standard pastoral inventory at Gen 12:16 (Pharaoh's gift to Abram); Gen 24:10-11, 24:64 (Abraham's servant's mission to Aram-Naharaim — ten camels prominent in the scene); Gen 30:43 (Jacob's prosperity in Laban's household); Gen 31:17 (Jacob's flight from Laban); Gen 32:8, 32:16 (the present to Esau); Gen 37:25 (the Ishmaelite caravan that buys Joseph). // **The archaeological consensus on camel domestication in the southern Levant** places the appearance of the domesticated camel as a working pack animal between roughly **1000 and 900 BCE — the early Iron Age**, well into the period of the Israelite monarchy. The decisive recent study is **Sapir-Hen and Ben-Yosef, 'The Introduction of Domestic Camels to the Southern Levant: Evidence from the Aravah Valley', *Tel Aviv* 40 (2013): 277–285** — a stratigraphic analysis of camel bones in the Aravah copper-mining sites that pins the introduction to the late tenth century BCE. Earlier reviews reached the same conclusion from different evidence: Köhler-Rollefson, 'Camels and Camel Pastoralism in Arabia', *Biblical Archaeologist* 56/4 (1993). Finkelstein and Silberman, *The Bible Unearthed* (2001), made the Iron Age dating widely known in popular scholarship. // **The patriarchal narratives are conventionally placed in the Middle Bronze Age** (ca. 2000–1500 BCE) or, on some chronologies, the Late Bronze Age (ca. 1500–1200 BCE). Both placements are centuries earlier than the documented arrival of the domesticated camel as a Levantine working animal. The camels in the patriarchal cycle are therefore widely read in critical scholarship as **redactional anachronism** — markers of the post-monarchic period in which the narratives reached final form, projected backward onto an earlier setting. // The **standard scholarly inference** is that the patriarchal narratives were composed or finalized in a period when camel pastoralism was familiar (the late monarchic or post-exilic redactor would have known camels as part of the standard pastoral inventory) and the camel was projected backward as a generic luxury-trade-animal. The four wife-sister and patriarchal-trade episodes that mention camels (12:16, 24, 30, 31, 32, 37) are concentrated in J/E material, consistent with later compositional dating. // The WoH translation renders the inventory item straight as 'camels' — the editorial question is for commentary, not for translation. The text-as-received names camels; the historical-critical question is when the text-as-received reached this form. The claim_type is *inferred* because the camel-as-anachronism reading is a *scholarly inference* from archaeological evidence external to the text; the inference is well-established in the consensus but is not a direct claim of the text itself.

terms.literal: YHWH afflicted Pharaoh with great plagues
terms.standardEnglish: the LORD plagued Pharaoh ... with great plagues
terms.wohChoice: YHWH afflicted Pharaoh ... with great plagues
terms.rationale: **The first plague-on-a-Pharaoh in the Hebrew Bible, and the verse most often cited in scholarship on Genesis 12 as proto-Exodus typology.** // *Vaynagga YHWH et-Par'oh n'ga'im g'dolim* — *YHWH afflicted Pharaoh with great plagues*. The verb *naga* (n-g-' qal/piel) means *touch, strike, afflict, smite*. The cognate noun *nega* (plague, blow, stroke, affliction-mark) is the technical Hebrew term for divinely-sent affliction across the Pentateuch. **Leviticus 13–14** uses *nega* extensively for the ritual-impurity category of skin-disease (the so-called *tzara'at* of 'leprosy'). **Deuteronomy 24:8** uses *nega ha-tzara'at* in the same sense. The root carries a specifically *cultic-juridical* connotation: the plague is not natural illness but divinely-imposed mark. // **The chapter's typological link to the Exodus is structurally precise.** The Exodus narrative uses the same root *naga / nega* for the divine plagues against Egypt: most decisively at **Exodus 11:1**, in YHWH's announcement of the tenth plague to Moses: *od nega echad avi al-Par'oh v'al-Mitsrayim* — *one more plague (nega) I will bring upon Pharaoh and upon Mitsrayim*. The same noun, the same construction with *al* (upon), the same subject (YHWH), the same object (Pharaoh and Mitsrayim). // **The Abram-Pharaoh / Moses-Pharaoh parallel structure** can be tabulated cleanly: famine drives Israel down to Mitsrayim → Pharaoh oppresses (taking Sarai here; enslavement in Exodus) → YHWH afflicts Pharaoh with plagues (*n'ga'im g'dolim* / *n'ga'im*) → Pharaoh summons the Hebrew patriarch / prophet (v 18 here; Exod 12:31 there) → Pharaoh expels the Hebrews (v 20 here; Exod 12:33 there) → the Hebrews depart with great substance (vv 16, 20 — the gift-list; Exod 12:35–36 — the spoiling of the Egyptians). The structural parallel is extensive enough that critical scholarship (**Garbini, *History and Ideology in Ancient Israel*, 1988**; **Crüsemann, *The Torah*, 1992**; **Sarna, *JPS Torah Commentary*, 1989**) treats Gen 12:10–20 as a **deliberate prefiguration** of the Exodus, composed or redacted in light of the later narrative. // **Two readings of the parallel** in critical scholarship: (a) **typological redaction** — Gen 12:10-20 was composed late, modeled on the Exodus narrative, and inserted into the Abrahamic cycle to establish Abram as proto-Moses and the patriarchal descent-and-return as proto-Exodus; (b) **convergent type-scene** — both narratives draw on a common topos of foreign-court appropriation-and-deliverance, with no necessary direction of dependence. Most modern critical scholars (Westermann, Sarna, Alter) favor (a). // The WoH translation reads 'great plagues' matching ASV — preserves the cognate link to Exodus. The claim_type is *inferred* because the proto-Exodus reading is a *scholarly inference* from the verbal parallels — well-established in the consensus but a literary-critical synthesis rather than an explicit claim of either text.

terms.literal: and they sent him away / dismissed him / expelled him
terms.standardEnglish: and they sent him away
terms.rationale: **The chapter closes with the verb that will close the Exodus.** // The verb *shalach* in the piel stem (*shillach*) is the Hebrew Bible's standard term for **expulsion / sending-out / dismissal** of persons. The piel intensifies the qal *shalach* (send) toward forced removal: not merely *send* but *send off, dismiss, expel, set free*. The same piel verb governs the keystone Exodus moments: // **Exodus 6:1** — YHWH's promise to Moses that Pharaoh will send Israel out: *b'yad chazaqah y'shalchem u-v'yad chazaqah y'gar'shem me-artzo* — *with a strong hand he will send them out (y'shalchem), and with a strong hand he will drive them out from his land*. // **Exodus 11:1** — YHWH's announcement of the tenth plague: *acharei khen y'shalach et'khem mi-zeh, k'shalcho kalah garesh y'garesh et'khem mi-zeh* — *after that he will send you out (y'shalach) from here; when he sends you out completely (k'shalcho kalah), he will utterly drive you out from here*. // **Exodus 12:33** — the immediate aftermath of the tenth plague: *vatechezaq Mitsrayim al-ha-am l'maher l'shalcham min ha-aretz* — *the Mitsrim were urgent upon the people, to send them out (l'shalcham) from the land in haste*. // **The verbal link is exact.** The Abram-expulsion of Gen 12:20 is verbally identical to the Israel-expulsion of Exod 12:33 — the same piel of *sh-l-ch*, the same context of Pharaonic dismissal under divine pressure, the same outcome of departure with great substance. The chapter verbally closes its proto-Exodus structure with the verb that will verbally open the Exodus's final scene. // The structural parallel discussed in scholarship (**Sarna, JPS, 1989**; **Garbini**, 1988; **Crüsemann**, 1992; the wider Pentateuchal-criticism literature) extends to the detail of *vay'shal'chu oto v'et-ishto v'et-kol-asher-lo* (sent him with his wife and all that he had), which parallels the Exodus departure-of-Israel-with-spoils. The chapter ends with Abram-and-household leaving Mitsrayim wealthier than they arrived, escorted out under royal command — the prefiguration of what Israel will do at the Exodus. // The WoH translation reads 'and they sent him away' matching ASV — preserves the verbal link forward to Exod 12:33. The claim_type is *inferred* because the proto-Exodus expulsion-verb reading is a *literary-critical inference* well-established in the consensus but synthetic across the two passages.

terms.literal: and the Canaanite and the Perizzite (were) then dwelling in the land
terms.standardEnglish: And the Canaanite and the Perizzite were then in the land
terms.wohChoice: And the Canaanite and the Perizzite were then dwelling in the land
terms.rationale: **The chapter's most-cited Documentary-Hypothesis flag.** The temporal-perspective parenthetical at Gen 13:7 — *v-ha-Kna'ani v-ha-Prizi az yoshev ba-aretz* — uses the participial *yoshev* (dwelling) with the temporal adverb *az* (then) to foreground a redactional vantage *later than* the events being narrated. The parenthetical only does its work if it informs the audience of a state of affairs they would not have assumed by default — i.e., if the reader is positioned in a time when the Canaanite-Perizzite presence is no longer the dominant feature of the land. // **The verse pairs with Gen 12:6** — *v-ha-Kna'ani az ba-aretz* — which uses the singular Canaanite. The expanded inventory here at 13:7 doubles it with the Perizzite, anticipating the longer ethnographic lists at Gen 15:18-21 (ten peoples), Gen 34:30 (Canaanite + Perizzite again, in Jacob's protest after Dinah), and across Deuteronomy (Deut 7:1, 20:17) and Joshua (Josh 3:10, 24:11). The doubling has been read as either a redactional expansion of the 12:6 form (so Westermann, *Genesis 12-36*, Hermeneia 1985, p. 176) or as a context-specific shift naming the two peoples Abram and Lot's herders would have been in direct competition with for grazing-rights in the hill country (so Wenham, *WBC Genesis 1-15*, 1987, p. 297). // **The Documentary Hypothesis reading** treats both notices as J-stratum parentheticals from a post-conquest perspective (Wellhausen, *Prolegomena*, 1883; Gunkel, *Genesis*, 1901; von Rad, *Genesis*, 1972 ET; Speiser, *Anchor Bible Genesis*, 1964, p. 87). The reading is mainstream in critical scholarship but not universally accepted; Cassuto (*A Commentary on the Book of Genesis*, 1944 Hebrew / 1961 ET, vol. II, p. 326) and Kitchen (*On the Reliability of the Old Testament*, 2003) argue that the parentheticals are compatible with a pre-conquest narrator describing the ethnographic situation of the patriarchs' own day. // The WoH translation preserves the participial-progressive force ('were then dwelling') matching ASV's sense; the temporal *az* is kept as 'then'. The claim_type is *inferred* because the redactional-seam reading is a well-attested critical-scholarly inference, not an explicit statement in the text; the dating question remains open in scholarship.

terms.literal: like the garden of YHWH, like the land of Mitsrayim
terms.standardEnglish: like the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt
terms.wohChoice: like the garden of YHWH, like the land of Mitsrayim
terms.rationale: **The doubled simile at Gen 13:10 is the chapter's most consequential inter-textual move.** Two of the Hebrew Bible's most freighted geographical referents are yoked to the pre-destruction plain of the Jordan. // **First, *gan YHWH*.** The phrase is a lexical back-reference to *gan b-Eden* of Gen 2:8 — the only other 'garden' associated with YHWH-Elohim in the primeval narratives. The Hebrew connects them directly: both are the *gan* of YHWH-Elohim, both are primeval-fertility prototypes, both are loci where the human / divine boundary is mediated through agricultural-spatial terms. The reading is mainstream: Westermann (*Genesis 12-36*, Hermeneia 1985, p. 178), Sarna (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 100), Hamilton (*NICOT Genesis 1-17*, 1990, p. 393), Alter (*Five Books of Moses*, 2004, p. 65), and von Rad (*Genesis*, 1972 ET, p. 173) all flag the Eden allusion. The phrase frames the Jordan plain as a second Eden and sets up the narrative engine of the next several chapters: Lot has chosen the Eden-like region precisely when YHWH is about to destroy it. The narrator's parenthetical *lifnei shachet YHWH et-Sodom v-et-Amorah* ('before YHWH destroyed Sodom and Amorah') makes the typology explicit by name. // **The Eden-loss / Sodom-destruction parallel.** Genesis stages a clear type-scene pairing across chs 2-3 and chs 18-19: a fertile divinely-favored region; a human action that violates its terms (eating from the forbidden tree / Sodom's chata'im); expulsion-and-destruction (3:23-24 / 19:24-25); a flaming sword from the divine sphere (3:24) / fire-and-sulfur from the skies (19:24). The reading is critical-consensus and pre-dates the modern literary-critical turn. // **Second, *eretz Mitsrayim*.** The Nile-delta comparison stages a different resonance: the Jordan plain is a Nile-grade agricultural prototype, irrigated and produce-rich, the model the wilderness generation will remember nostalgically (Num 11:5, *we remember the fish we ate in Mitsrayim freely, the cucumbers and the melons and the leeks and the onions and the garlic*). Lot is choosing on Mitsrayim-grade criteria — the same evaluative frame that drew Abram down to Egypt in the famine of 12:10 — and the chapter is structurally rhyming Lot's choice with Abram's earlier descent. // **The Wheel of Heaven lens** notes the back-reference without arguing the full cosmology: Gen 1's primeval-creation account and Gen 2-3's Eden-garden account are taken as describing a single project-beginning context, and the *gan-YHWH* at 13:10 invites the reader to hold that context in mind while the next chapter sequence runs the destruction-and-restart pattern through Sodom-Amorah. The translation reads literally; the typological reading is held in commentary. // The WoH translation preserves the doubled simile literally ('like the garden of YHWH, like the land of Mitsrayim'), matching ASV. The claim_type is *direct* because the lexical back-reference is grammatically explicit in the Hebrew (*gan* of YHWH is the same construction as *gan b-Eden*) and the inter-textual reading is the scholarly consensus; what the back-reference *means* theologically remains open, but that it *is* a back-reference is uncontested.

terms.partOfSpeech: verb (qal imperative) + politeness particle + noun (eyes) + 2ms suffix
terms.standardEnglish: Lift up now thine eyes
terms.rationale: **The patriarchal seeing-formula.** The construction *nasa eynayim* — literally *to lift the eyes* — is the Hebrew Bible's standard idiom for raised-gaze perception, often introducing a visionary, revelatory, or recognitional moment. The verb *nasa* (n-s-' qal: lift, carry, bear up) governs the object *eynayim* (eyes); the imperative form *sa-na* (lift, please) adds the politeness / cohortative particle *-na*. The formula's inaugural occurrence in the patriarchal corpus is here at Gen 13:14, in YHWH's direct address to Abram after Lot's separation. // **The formula recurs across the patriarchal narratives:** Gen 18:2 (Abraham at Mamre sees the three visitors approaching); Gen 22:4 (Abraham on the third day of the *aqedah* journey sees the place of the binding from afar); Gen 22:13 (Abraham sees the ram caught in the thicket — the substitute); Gen 24:63 (Isaac at evening lifts his eyes and sees the approaching camels carrying Rebekah); Gen 24:64 (Rebekah parallel-action lifts her eyes and sees Isaac); Gen 31:10 (Jacob's dream-vision of the striped flocks: *v-esa eynai v-ere ba-chalom*); Gen 31:12 (the *mal'akh ha-Elohim* in Jacob's dream cites the same formula: *sa-na eynekha u-re'eh*); Gen 33:1 (Jacob lifts his eyes and sees Esau approaching with the four hundred men); Gen 33:5 (Jacob lifts his eyes and sees the women and children); Gen 37:25 (Joseph's brothers lift their eyes and see the Ishmaelite caravan); Gen 43:29 (Joseph lifts his eyes and sees Benjamin). The formula crosses the entire patriarchal arc as the standard threshold-of-vision marker. // **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36*, Hermeneia 1985, p. 180) notes the formulaic character; **Alter** (*Five Books of Moses*, 2004) flags the consistent use; **Hamilton** (*NICOT Genesis 1-17*, 1990, p. 394) observes that in the patriarchal narratives the formula functions almost always to introduce a revelation or a recognition that changes the narrative direction. // The WoH translation preserves the verb-phrase 'lift up your eyes' across all occurrences, matching ASV; the politeness particle *-na* is rendered as 'now' (ASV convention) where it appears. The claim_type is *direct* because the formula is grammatically explicit in the Hebrew at every cited verse and the recurrence-set is an uncontested fact of the text.

terms.source: כַּעֲפַר הָאָרֶץ / כַּכּוֹכָבִים / כַּחוֹל אֲשֶׁר עַל־שְׂפַת הַיָּם
terms.translit: ka-afar ha-aretz / ka-kokhavim / ka-chol asher al-s'fat ha-yam
terms.literal: as the dust of the earth / as the stars / as the sand on the lip of the sea
terms.standardEnglish: as the dust of the earth / as the stars of heaven / as the sand on the seashore
terms.wohChoice: as the dust of the earth / as the stars of the heavens / as the sand on the seashore
terms.rationale: **Three patriarchal seed-comparisons run as a deliberate set across the Abraham cycle.** The triad is: **dust of the earth** (Gen 13:16 — the chapter's promise-expansion after Lot's separation), **stars of the heavens** (Gen 15:5 — the night-vision count-the-stars promise), and **sand on the seashore** (Gen 22:17, paired with the stars in the *aqedah* covenant-confirmation). // **The cosmographic distribution is deliberate.** Sarna (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 100) and Alter (*Five Books of Moses*, 2004, p. 66) both treat the three as a set; Hamilton (*NICOT Genesis 1-17*, 1990, p. 396) and Wenham (*WBC Genesis 1-15*, 1987, p. 298) note the same. The three comparisons cover the principal terrestrial registers: **dust** (earth, immanent, lowly — the *afar min ha-adamah* from which humanity was shaped at Gen 2:7 and to which it returns at 3:19); **stars** (sky, transcendent, height — the host of *kol-tz'va ha-shamayim* of Gen 2:1); **sand** (shore, the threshold between dry land and sea — the boundary that names the limits of the land). The triad reads as a deliberate distribution of the seed across earth, sky, and shore-margin. // **Hamilton specifically reads the dust-comparison at 13:16 as a creation-recall** — the patriarch's seed will share the substance of the original adamic forming-material. The reading sharpens the contrast with the more transcendent stars-image of 15:5: dust ties the promise to the ground and to the substance of the human; stars ties it to the heavens and to the divine perspective. // **The triad recurs in expanded form across the Hebrew Bible.** Stars-image: Gen 26:4 (to Isaac), Exod 32:13 (Moses's intercession), Deut 1:10, 10:22, 28:62, 1 Chr 27:23, Neh 9:23. Sand-image: Gen 32:13 (Jacob's prayer at the Jabbok), Josh 11:4, Judg 7:12, 1 Sam 13:5, 1 Kgs 4:20, Isa 10:22 (Romans 9:27 cites this), Jer 33:22, Hos 1:10. Dust-image: 2 Chr 1:9 (Solomon's prayer) explicitly reuses the dust comparison. The three together constitute the canonical Hebrew Bible vocabulary for the multiplied-seed promise. // The WoH translation preserves each comparison literally (*as the dust of the earth*, *as the stars*, *as the sand on the seashore*), matching ASV. The claim_type is *direct* because the three comparisons are grammatically explicit at their cited verses; calling them a triad is a scholarly observation but the observation is well-attested and uncontested.

terms.partOfSpeech: verb (qal imperative) + verb (hithpael imperative) + prep + noun
terms.literal: arise, walk-thyself-about in the land, in the length of it and in the breadth of it
terms.standardEnglish: Arise, walk through the land in the length of it and in the breadth of it
terms.wohChoice: Arise, walk through the land in the length of it and in the breadth of it
terms.rationale: **The covenantal land-walking formula at Gen 13:17 names a juridical-symbolic act.** *Qum hit'hallekh ba-aretz l-orkah ul-rochbah* — the hithpael of h-l-k (walk-about, traverse-iteratively, walk-oneself) with *ba-aretz* (in the land) plus the *l-orkah ul-rochbah* doubling (in its length and in its breadth) — has been read across the modern critical tradition as a covenantal taking-possession by traversal. The patriarch's walking the boundaries of the land *is itself* a legal act of taking title to it. // **The reading goes back to David Daube, *Studies in Biblical Law* (1947), §3** — comparing the rite to the Roman *usucapio* (taking title by uninterrupted use) and to the Anglo-Saxon *seisin*-by-perambulation (the medieval legal act of taking possession of a granted estate by walking its boundaries). Daube's reading has been adopted by **Speiser** (*Anchor Bible Genesis*, 1964, p. 100), **Sarna** (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 101), **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36*, 1985, p. 181), and **Wenham** (*WBC Genesis 1-15*, 1987, p. 299). The German biblical-law tradition discusses the same rite under the term *Begehungsritus* — boundary-walking ceremony. // **The parallel formulae make the reading load-bearing across the Hexateuch:** **Josh 1:3** — *kol-makom asher tidrokh kaf-raglekhem bo lakhem n'tatiyv* — *every place where the sole of your foot shall tread, to you have I given it* — uses the foot-treading possession-formula at the inauguration of the conquest. **Josh 18:8** — *qumu v-hit'halku ba-aretz v-kitvu otah* — *arise, walk through the land and describe it* — uses the same hithpael of h-l-k with *ba-aretz* in the seven-tribes-land-allotment context, making the walking-and-describing the explicit prerequisite for the casting of lots. **Deut 11:24** reiterates the foot-treading possession-formula in Moses's speech anticipating the conquest. // **Distinct from but related to** the broader hithpael-of-h-l-k uses: the Eden walking-formula of Gen 3:8 (*mit'hallekh b-gan*, YHWH-Elohim walking in the garden), and the patriarchal walking-with-Elohim formula of Gen 5:22 (Enoch), 6:9 (Noah), 17:1 (Abraham's *hit'hallekh l-fanai*), 24:40 (Abraham's servant's description), 48:15 (Jacob's blessing). Those uses name an ethical-relational walking-before-Elohim; the 13:17 use names a juridical-spatial walking-the-land. The two senses are related (the hithpael of iterative-reflexive walking covers both) but distinguishable. // The WoH translation preserves 'walk through the land', matching ASV; the doubled *l-orkah ul-rochbah* is kept as 'in the length of it and in the breadth of it'. The claim_type is *inferred* because the juridical-act framing is the modern scholarly synthesis (Daube 1947 and the consensus following him), not an explicit statement in the verse; what the verse explicitly commands is the walking, and the reading-as-possession-rite is the scholarly inference.

terms.standardEnglish: oaks of Mamre / terebinths of Mamre
terms.rationale: **The Mamre site near Hebron is the recurring locus of the Abraham cycle's southern hill-country residence and the geographical anchor of the patriarchal-matriarchal burial-narratives.** *Elonei Mamre* — אֵלֹנֵי מַמְרֵא — is morphologically parallel to but distinct from *elon Moreh* (Gen 12:6): the plural construct *elonei* names a *grove* of trees rather than a single tree, and *Mamre* is a different proper name from *Moreh*. The two sacred-tree sites should not be conflated. // **The proper name *Mamre*.** Gen 14:13 and 14:24 explicitly name **Mamre the Amorite** (אִישׁ הָאֱמֹרִי, *ish ha-Emori*) as one of Abram's three Amorite allies in the war of the four kings — paired with his brothers Eshkol and Aner. The grove is therefore named for its human owner / occupant; the place-name is metonymic from the man-name. Gen 23:17, 19 specify that the field of Ephron the Hittite which Abraham purchased for Sarah's burial is *liphnei Mamre* (before / opposite Mamre); Gen 25:9 places Isaac and Ishmael burying Abraham at the same site; Gen 35:27 places Jacob's reunion with Isaac at Mamre; Gen 49:30 and 50:13 anchor the Machpelah burial of Jacob at the same locus. // **The two readings of the place-history**: (a) the **classical historical reading** (Sarna, *JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 102; Wenham, *WBC Genesis 1-15*, 1987, p. 300) treats Mamre as the Amorite contemporary of Abram, with the place-name following from his presence; (b) the **alternative aetiological reading** (Westermann, *Genesis 12-36*, 1985, p. 184) treats *Mamre* as a pre-existing place-name secondarily personified into an Amorite character. The first reading is more straightforward; the second flags that the name does not have a clear Hebrew etymology. // **The recurrence-set across the Abraham cycle** makes the entry forward-loaded narratively: **Gen 13:18** (here — Abram's first residence at the site after the separation from Lot); **Gen 14:13, 14:24** (Mamre as ally in the war of the four kings; Abram dwelling *b-elonei Mamre*); **Gen 18:1** (*va-yera elav YHWH b-elonei Mamre* — the three-visitors theophany, the most embodied YHWH-appearance in the patriarchal arc); **Gen 23:17, 19** (the field of Ephron near Mamre purchased for Sarah's burial); **Gen 25:9** (Abraham buried in the cave of Machpelah, *el-sdeh Ephron... asher al-pnei Mamre*); **Gen 35:27** (Jacob returns to Isaac at *Mamre qiryat ha-arba hi Chevron*); **Gen 49:30** and **Gen 50:13** (the Machpelah-Ephron-Mamre burial-anchor invoked in the Jacob-Joseph cycle). // **The sacred-tree pattern.** Both *elon Moreh* (Shechem) and *elonei Mamre* (Hebron) fit the patriarchal-corpus sacred-tree pattern: trees at named sites that function as theophany-and-altar locations (cf. Gen 35:4 the Shechem oak, Gen 35:8 *Allon Bakhut* at Bethel, Josh 24:26 the Shechem sanctuary oak, Judg 6:11 the Ophrah oak, Judg 9:6, 37 the Shechem diviners' oak). The two sites are parallels in the pattern but distinct entries — the Moreh site names a tree by its oracle-function (*moreh* = teacher / oracle-giver), the Mamre site names a tree-grove by its human owner. // **On the botanical question** (oak vs. terebinth): BDB groups *elon* / *elah* / *alon* under 'oak / terebinth'; HALOT distinguishes them with less than full confidence; LXX renders δρῦς (oak); ASV and NRSV translate 'oaks', NJPS 'terebinths', NIV 'great trees'. The WoH translation follows ASV with 'oaks of Mamre', consistent with the 12:6 'oak of Moreh' rendering. The species precision is not load-bearing for the verse. // The claim_type is *direct* because *elonei Mamre* is a grammatically explicit proper-name phrase at every cited verse and the site-identification is uncontested in scholarship; the WoH lexical choice ('oaks') matches ASV / NRSV consensus.

terms.literal: the Refa'im ("weakened-ones / shades" or "heroes / mighty-ones") / the Zuzim / the Emim ("terrors")
terms.standardEnglish: Rephaim / Zuzim / Emim
terms.rationale: **The pre-Israelite peoples struck by Kedorlaomer at Gen 14:5 form one node in a corpus-wide giant-peoples chain.** *Refa'im* in Ashteroth-Karnaim, *Zuzim* in Ham, *Emim* in Shaveh-Kiryathaim — three Trans-Jordanian groups whose names recur with explicit giant-equation in Deuteronomy 2-3. The 14:5 list is itself bare narrative; the load-bearing material is the Deuteronomistic cross-reference. // **The Deuteronomic equations are grammatically explicit.** **Deut 2:10-11** — *ha-Emim l'fanim yashvu vah, am gadol v-rav va-ram ka-Anaqim; Refa'im yechashvu af-hem ka-Anaqim* — *the Emim formerly dwelt there, a great people and numerous and tall as the Anaqim; they too were counted as Refa'im like the Anaqim*. **Deut 2:20-21** — *eretz Refa'im techashev af-hi... v-ha-Amonim yiqr'u lahem Zamzumim; am gadol v-rav va-ram ka-Anaqim* — the Zuzim of Gen 14:5 are equated with the Ammonite-named *Zamzumim* and again *counted as Refa'im like the Anaqim*. **Deut 3:11** — *ki raq Og melekh ha-Bashan nish'ar mi-yeter ha-Refa'im; hineh arso eres barzel... tesha amot orkah v-arba amot rochbah* — *for only Og king of Bashan remained of the remnant of the Refa'im; his bedstead was an iron bedstead, nine cubits its length and four cubits its breadth* (roughly 13.5 ft x 6 ft on the standard Hebrew cubit). The Deuteronomist explicitly identifies the 14:5 peoples with the *Anaqim* and reads them as a giant-stature population. // **The *Anaqim* are themselves linked back to the antediluvian *Nephilim*.** **Num 13:33** — the spies' report on the inhabitants of Canaan: *v-sham ra'inu et-ha-Nefilim, b-nei Anaq min-ha-Nefilim; va-nehi v-eyneynu ka-chagavim* — *and there we saw the Nephilim, the sons of Anaq, from the Nephilim; and we were in our own eyes as grasshoppers*. The verse explicitly identifies the *bnei Anaq* — the Anaqim — with the *Nefilim* of Gen 6:1-4. The chain therefore runs: **Gen 6:1-4 Nefilim → Num 13:33 b-nei Anaq / Anaqim → Deut 2-3 Refa'im / Emim / Zamzumim / Og of Bashan → Gen 14:5 Refa'im / Zuzim / Emim**. The Deuteronomist's framing is that these were a pre-Israelite, giant-stature population displaced by the Trans-Jordanian peoples (Moab dispossessed the Emim, Ammon dispossessed the Zuzim/Zamzumim, Edom dispossessed the Horites), and the Israelite conquest finished the displacement with Og of Bashan as the last remnant. // **Mainstream scholarly treatment.** **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36*, 1985, pp. 195-196) treats the 14:5 peoples as a pre-Israelite-population list with the giant-traditions explicitly noted. **Sarna** (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 105) treats the same. **Wenham** (*WBC Genesis 1-15*, 1987, pp. 311-312) collates the Deut 2-3 cross-references in detail. **Hamilton** (*NICOT Genesis 1-17*, 1990, p. 403) traces the recurrence-chain through Num 13:33 to the Gen 6:4 Nefilim. The reading is not a fringe move; the giant-stature understanding of the Refa'im is the consensus of the modern critical commentaries. // **A second sense of *Refa'im* in the Hebrew Bible** designates the *shades* of the dead — the underworld inhabitants (Isa 14:9, 26:14, 26:19; Ps 88:11; Prov 2:18, 9:18, 21:16; Job 26:5). The two senses (pre-Israelite giants / shades-of-dead) are tracked as a single lexeme in BDB and HALOT, with proposals to unify them (the deceased mighty-ones; the heroes whose shades persist) or treat them as homographs from distinct roots. Greenfield (1973), L'Heureux (*Rank Among the Canaanite Gods*, 1979), and Pope (*Job*, AB, 1965) discuss the Ugaritic *rpʾum* (the divinized dead, the heroic ancestors at Ugarit) as the cognate background. The 14:5 use is the giant-peoples sense; the shades-sense is a separate philological track. // **Ashteroth-Karnaim** (the city of the Refa'im at 14:5) is a Trans-Jordanian double-named site (*Ashteroth* + *Karnaim*, the two-horned Astarte-image city), associated with the Astarte-cult; **Shaveh-Kiryathaim** is a Moabite-region site (cf. Num 32:37, Josh 13:19); **Ham** of the Zuzim is the obscure third site, possibly identical with the modern Ham of central Jordan. // **The WoH translation preserves the bare proper-name transcriptions** (*Refa'im*, *Zuzim*, *Emim*) without parenthetical 'giants' glosses, matching ASV's *Rephaim*, *Zuzim*, *Emim*. The lens-relevant material — the Deut 2-3 explicit giant-equations and the Num 13:33 Anaqim-Nefilim chain back to Gen 6:1-4 — lives in the per-verse commentary at 14:5 and in this glossary entry. The lens posture is that the chain exists and is mainstream-attested; what the chain *means* about pre-Flood populations is for downstream chapters and the wiki, not for the translated text. // The claim_type is *inferred* because the Deut 2-3 cross-references are grammatically explicit in *those* verses, but reading the 14:5 list as part of a giant-peoples chain requires the cross-reference work; the inference itself is straightforward and scholarly-consensus, but it is a synthesis across verses, not a reading directly from 14:5 alone.

terms.partOfSpeech: gentilic noun, masculine singular with article
terms.literal: the Hebrew (one) / the Eber-descendant / the one-from-beyond
terms.standardEnglish: the Hebrew
terms.rationale: **The first occurrence of the gentilic *Ivri* in the Hebrew Bible is at Gen 14:13** — *Avram ha-Ivri*, Abram the Hebrew. The etymology of *Ivri* is a long-standing scholarly dispute with three readings circulating across the modern critical literature. The dispute itself is *direct* — well-attested and uncontested as a dispute — even though no single reading is settled. // **Reading 1: the eponymous-ancestor reading (Eber).** The Genesis editor's own genealogical apparatus supports the derivation from **Eber** (עֵבֶר), the great-grandson of Shem. **Gen 10:21** singles out Shem as *avi kol-bnei Eber* — *father of all the sons of Eber* — naming Eber before the rest of the genealogy is laid out (a structural prolepsis that flags Eber's centrality). **Gen 10:24-25** and **Gen 11:14-17** trace the line from Shem → Arpachshad → Shelah → Eber → Peleg → ... → Terah → Abram. On this reading, *Ivri* is the standard gentilic from the proper name *Eber* (with the regular qatil-pattern gentilic formation: *Eber* → *Ivri*, parallel to *Yisra'el* → *Yisra'eli*). The reading is the in-text reading: it is what the Genesis editor's genealogy implies. **Sarna** (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 107) and **Cassuto** (*From Noah to Abraham*, 1964, p. 201) defend this reading. // **Reading 2: the *habiru / apiru* social-class reading.** The Akkadian *ḫabiru* (variant spellings *ʿapiru*, *ḫapiru*, *SA.GAZ* logographically) is attested across the 2nd-millennium Near Eastern documentary corpus — **Amarna letters** (especially EA 286-290, the Jerusalem letters of Abdi-Heba, which complain about *habiru* incursions); **Mari archives**; **Nuzi tablets**; **Hittite treaties** (including the standard divine-witness lists naming the *Hapiru*-gods); **Egyptian execration texts and New Kingdom administrative texts** (especially the *ʿapiru* working on the building of Pi-Ramses at the time of Ramesses II — the corpus that has drawn the heaviest Exodus-connection speculation). The *habiru* designate a marginal social class of stateless, dependent, often-displaced persons — sometimes brigands, sometimes mercenaries, sometimes hired labor, sometimes refugees. **Greenberg** (*The Hab/piru*, 1955), **Bottéro** (*Le problème des Habiru*, 1954), **Na'aman** (*Habiru and Hebrews: The Transfer of a Social Term to the Literary Sphere*, JNES 1986), **Rainey** (1995), and **Lemche** (*The Israelites in History and Tradition*, 1998) work the philological-historical case. The lexical connection between *habiru* and *Ivri* is plausible but contested; the consonants line up (ḫ-b-r ↔ ʿ-b-r, with the well-attested Northwest-Semitic ḫ → ʿ shift), and the social-class designation matches the patriarchal nomadic-stateless context. On this reading, *Avram ha-Ivri* would be a social designation (Abram the displaced-dependent / Abram the marginal-outsider) rather than an ethnic one. // **Reading 3: the *eber* (beyond / across) reading.** The third etymology derives *Ivri* from the noun *eber* (עֵבֶר, 'side / region beyond'), pointing to Abram's Trans-Euphrates origin. **Josh 24:2-3** explicitly names the patriarchal origin *b-ever ha-nahar* — *across the River* (the Euphrates) — and notes the family's pre-monotheistic Trans-Euphratine context. The **LXX** at Gen 14:13 reads περάτης (*peratēs*, 'one-from-across, one-from-the-other-side'), explicitly preserving the *eber* etymology. The Greek translators read *ha-Ivri* as *the one from across (the river)*. **Speiser** (*Anchor Bible Genesis*, 1964, pp. 102-103) and **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36*, 1985, pp. 195-196) discuss this reading. // **The three readings are not strictly incompatible.** Greenberg (1955) and Rainey (1995) propose that the Genesis editor may have inherited a social-class term (*habiru / apiru*) whose meaning shifted over time, was eponymized through the genealogical figure of *Eber*, and accommodated the *eber* / Trans-Euphrates geographical reading as a secondary etymology. The three readings stack chronologically: a 2nd-millennium social-class background → a Genesis-editor genealogical eponymization → a LXX-translator geographical reading. // **The corpus-distribution of *Ivri* is informative.** Within Genesis, the gentilic recurs in the **Joseph narrative** — Gen 39:14, 39:17 (Potiphar's wife: *ha-ish ha-Ivri*, *the Hebrew man*); Gen 40:15 (Joseph: *me-eretz ha-Ivrim*, *from the land of the Hebrews*); Gen 41:12 (the cupbearer: *na'ar Ivri*, *a Hebrew youth*); Gen 43:32 (the Egyptians' food-segregation, the only verse using *Ivrim* as a plural for the Israelite-people-in-Egypt). The distribution is striking: the term is used **by foreigners about Israelites, or by Israelites about themselves to foreigners**. The gentilic functions as an *outsider's designation* in the patriarchal corpus — which is consistent with the *habiru* social-class background. The same pattern persists into Exodus (1:15, 1:16, 1:19, 2:6, 2:7, 2:11, 2:13, 3:18, 5:3, 7:16, 9:1, 9:13, 10:3) — in nearly every instance, the term is used by Egyptians of Israelites or by Israelites to Pharaoh. // **The WoH translation renders 'the Hebrew', matching ASV / NRSV / NJPS consensus**, without preempting the etymological dispute in the translated text. The lens-relevant material (the *habiru / apiru* social-class background, the LXX *peratēs* reading, the Genesis-editor eponymization through Eber) is carried in this glossary entry and in the per-verse commentary at 14:13. // The claim_type is *direct* because the dispute itself is grammatically explicit (the three readings each have textual support — Eber in Gen 10-11, *eber* in Josh 24:2 and LXX, *habiru* in the Amarna corpus) and the scholarly state-of-the-question is well-attested; the entry does not commit to one reading.

terms.partOfSpeech: noun, masculine plural with 3ms suffix (hapax legomenon)
terms.literal: his trained-ones / his initiated-ones / his retainers
terms.standardEnglish: his trained men
terms.rationale: **The noun *chanikh* (חָנִיךְ) occurs only here in the entire Hebrew Bible** — a hapax legomenon. The verbal root **ch-n-k** (חנך) is well-attested in the broader Hebrew Bible with the basic semantic range *dedicate / inaugurate / train-up*: **Prov 22:6** *chanokh la-na'ar al-pi darko* — *train up a child in the way he should go*; **Deut 20:5** the man who has built a house *v-lo chanakho* — *and has not dedicated it*; the noun **chanukkah** (חֲנֻכָּה, 'dedication') from the same root, used of the dedication of altars (Num 7:10-11, 7:84, 7:88), the dedication of the Second Temple (Ezra 6:16), the dedication of the rebuilt walls of Jerusalem (Neh 12:27), and post-biblically the rededication of the Temple under the Hasmoneans that gives the holiday Hanukkah its name. The verbal root therefore covers *dedicate* (cultic), *inaugurate* (architectural), and *train up* (pedagogical). // **The exact sense of the noun *chanikh* at Gen 14:14 is underdetermined.** Four readings circulate: (a) **'trained men / trained retainers'** — the household members trained-up by Abram for armed service; matches the *train-up* sense of ch-n-k and the immediate context (*y-lidei beito*, *born of his house*). ASV, NRSV, KJV, RSV all read 'trained men'. (b) **'initiates / dedicated-ones'** — household members ritually dedicated to Abram's house; matches the *dedicate* sense of ch-n-k and reads the term as a juridical-status designation rather than a military one. (c) **'retainers / liegemen'** — Albright (*The Archaeology of Palestine*, 1949) and **Lambdin** (1953) adduced the **Egyptian *ḥnk*** parallel: the Middle Kingdom Egyptian execration texts and administrative documents use *ḥnk* of *retainers / dependents / liegemen* of an Asiatic chieftain — the term designating armed household-bondsmen owed to a tribal-patriarchal figure. The Egyptian parallel is the strongest comparative anchor for the verse: it gives a 2nd-millennium West-Semitic-context attestation of a closely-similar lexeme used in a closely-similar social context. **Sarna** (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 108), **Wenham** (*WBC Genesis 1-15*, 1987, p. 313), and **Hamilton** (*NICOT Genesis 1-17*, 1990, p. 404) all cite the Egyptian *ḥnk* parallel; NJPS reads *retainers* on this basis; Alter (*The Five Books of Moses*, 2004, p. 67) also reads *retainers*. (d) **'armed household-dependents'** is the synthesis reading. // **The number 318 has attracted a parallel post-biblical interpretive tradition: gematria.** The Talmudic homily at **b. Nedarim 32a** (also **b. Bava Batra 16a**) reads the 318 as the numerical value of **Eliezer** (אֱלִיעֶזֶר): aleph(1) + lamed(30) + yod(10) + ayin(70) + zayin(7) + resh(200) = 318. The reading equates the 318 with Abraham's chief servant Eliezer (named at Gen 15:2 as *Eliezer of Damascus*), reading the verse as 'he led forth Eliezer' rather than 'his 318 trained men'. The reading is post-biblical exegesis — the gematria-tradition develops in the Tannaitic and Amoraic periods — and is not the literal-historical reading. The reading is included here as **DOWNSTREAM-RECEPTION** material: the rabbinic tradition's reading of the verse, not what the Hebrew literally says. **Gen Rab 43:2** (Bereshit Rabbah) attests the same gematria-reading. // **The literal-historical reading is the plain-sense 'his trained men, born in his house, three hundred and eighteen'** — a household militia drawn from Abram's domestically-born retainers (the *y-lidei beito*, 'born-of-his-house' construction, distinguishes them from purchased slaves; cf. Gen 17:12-13, 17:23, 17:27 for the formal distinction). The number 318 is large enough to constitute a respectable raiding party but small enough to be plausibly a single patriarch's retinue; the symbolic-numerical readings (gematria-Eliezer, the 318-as-the-numerical-value-of-various-things) are secondary. // **The WoH translation renders 'his trained men, born in his house, three hundred and eighteen'**, matching ASV. The hapax status, the ch-n-k root semantics, the Egyptian *ḥnk* parallel, and the rabbinic gematria-Eliezer tradition are flagged in this glossary entry and in the per-verse commentary at 14:14. // The claim_type is *inferred* because the lexical sense of the hapax cannot be settled from internal evidence alone — the root sense of ch-n-k (dedicate / train-up) and the Egyptian *ḥnk* parallel together give a defensible synthesis (*trained armed retainers*), but the precise sense is a scholarly inference, not a grammatically explicit datum.

terms.literal: my king is Tzedek / king of righteousness
terms.standardEnglish: Melchizedek
terms.rationale: **The name *Malki-Tzedek* is morphologically a theophoric compound** — *malki* (1cs-suffixed construct: 'my king is...' / 'king of...') + *Tzedek* — and admits two principal compositional readings. The two readings are both pre-modern attested; the theophoric reading is the consensus among modern Semitists. // **Reading 1: the theophoric-name reading ('my king is Tzedek').** *Tzedek* is attested as a **West-Semitic divine name** at several anchor points: (a) **Ugaritic** — the deity-pair *Ṣdq* and *Mšr* (Tzedek and Mishor, *righteousness* and *uprightness*) attested in the divine-list KTU 1.123 and other Ugaritic texts as members of the divine assembly; (b) **Phoenician** — the historian Philo of Byblos (*Phoenician History*, preserved in Eusebius *Praeparatio Evangelica* 1.10), citing the ostensibly Phoenician priest Sanchuniathon, names *Sydyk* (Tzedek) as the father of the Dioscuri / Cabiri; (c) **Pre-Israelite Jerusalem** — the Jebusite king-name **Adoni-Tzedek** (אֲדֹנִי־צֶדֶק, 'my lord is Tzedek') at **Josh 10:1, 10:3**, ruling in Jerusalem at the time of the Israelite conquest. The pattern (theophoric Jerusalem-king-names compounded with *Tzedek*) is well-attested: Melchizedek at the patriarchal stratum, Adoni-Tzedek at the conquest stratum. The pair suggests that *Tzedek* was the local Jerusalem high-god name (or one of them) during the pre-Israelite period. **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36*, 1985, pp. 203-205), **Sarna** (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 109), **Wenham** (*WBC Genesis 1-15*, 1987, p. 316), **Hamilton** (*NICOT Genesis 1-17*, 1990, p. 409), and **Cross** (*Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic*, 1973, pp. 207-209) all read the name theophorically. // **Reading 2: the honorific-epithet reading ('king of righteousness').** **Hebrews 7:2** explicitly translates the name as βασιλεὺς δικαιοσύνης — *king of righteousness* — reading *malki-tzedek* as a construct compound 'king-of-righteousness' rather than as a theophoric. The reading is attested in the rabbinic tradition (with variations) and dominated the Christian and traditional Jewish reception. The grammatical objection is that the form *malki-* (with the *-i* suffix) is more naturally read as the 1cs-suffixed *my king* than as a construct chain (which would more typically be *melekh tzedek*, parallel to *melekh Shalem* in the same verse); but the construct-with-*-i*-ending is attested in proper names (the *-i* read as the so-called *hireq compaginis*, an archaic case-ending preserved in names). // **The figure of Melchizedek occurs only twice in the Hebrew Bible:** **Gen 14:18-20** (the present chapter) and **Ps 110:4** — *attah kohen l'olam al-divrati Malki-Tzedek* — *you are a priest forever after the order / manner of Melchizedek* — the royal-psalm verse that has carried the entire downstream priesthood-tradition.
