**Lens posture (WoH).** The Gen 15:17 theophany is high-lens-leverage for the Wheel of Heaven project — the chapter's keystone concrete-sensory divine appearance, with smoke-and-fire imagery that the project's broader lens-reading will surface in long-form articles and wiki material. The lens stays in the apparatus. The translation matches ASV at every word; the lens-relevant cross-references (Exod 19:18, Exod 13:21, Ezek 1) and the broader fire-theophany corpus are carried in this glossary entry and in the per-verse commentary at 15:17. The translation does not editorialize on what the smoke-and-fire is; what the corpus's apparatus surfaces is the lexical-philological mainstream-scholarly synthesis, not a Wheel of Heaven cosmological reading. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the four lexemes (*tanur*, *ashan*, *lappid*, *esh*) are grammatically explicit at the cited verses; (b) the recurrence-patterns (Exod 19:18 Sinai, Exod 13:21 pillar-of-fire, Isa 6:4, Ezek 1) are direct documentary evidence; (c) the modern critical scholarly synthesis (Westermann, Wenham, von Rad, Sarna, Hamilton) is mainstream and uncontested.

terms.standardEnglish: to make a covenant
terms.rationale:
**Gen 15:18 contains the first explicit *karat brit* — *cut a covenant* — of the Abraham cycle.** *Ba-yom ha-hu karat YHWH et-Avram brit* — *on that day YHWH cut a covenant with Abram*. The Hebrew idiom preserves the literal animal-bisection rite of vv 9-11, 17 in its very verb: covenants in the Hebrew Bible are *cut*, not *made*. The idiom is foundational; the WoH translation preserves the verb-literal *cut* rather than the smoothed-conventional *made*.

**The lexical evidence.** The verb *karat* (כָּרַת) in the qal has the basic sense of *cut down, cut off, cut up* — used for cutting trees (Deut 19:5; 2 Kgs 6:4), cutting flesh (1 Sam 31:9 cutting off Saul's head), cutting off persons (the *karet* legal-penalty formula: Gen 17:14 *v-nikhretah ha-nefesh ha-hi me-ameyha* — *and that soul shall be cut off from his people*). The collocation *karat brit* applies the cutting-verb to *brit* (covenant), preserving the etymological link to the animal-bisection ritual. BDB §3772 reads the idiom as *make a covenant, by cutting in two parts the victim*; HALOT concurs.

**The recurrence-distribution.** The idiom recurs approximately 80 times across the Hebrew Bible — one of the most distributed legal-religious idioms in the corpus. Selected occurrences: **Gen 21:27, 21:32** (Abimelech's covenant with Abraham at Beer-Sheba); **Gen 26:28** (Isaac with Abimelech at Beer-Sheba); **Gen 31:44** (Jacob with Laban at Mizpah); **Exod 23:32** (the commandment not to cut covenant with the Canaanite gods); **Exod 24:8** (the Sinai covenant-cutting); **Exod 34:10, 34:27** (the renewed-covenant after the golden calf); **Deut 4:23** (Moses's warning); **Deut 5:2-3** (the Horeb covenant); **Deut 7:2, 9:9, 28:69, 29:13, 31:16**; **Josh 9:6-7, 9:15-16, 24:25**; **Judg 2:2; 1 Sam 11:1, 18:3, 20:8, 22:8, 23:18; 2 Sam 3:12-13, 3:21, 5:3; 1 Kgs 5:26, 8:9, 8:21, 20:34; 2 Kgs 11:4, 11:17, 17:15, 17:35, 17:38, 23:3; Ezra 10:3; Neh 9:8, 10:1; Job 31:1, 40:28; Ps 50:5, 83:6, 89:4; Isa 28:15, 55:3, 57:8, 61:8; Jer 11:10, 31:31-33, 32:40, 34:8, 34:13, 34:15, 34:18; Ezek 17:13, 34:25, 37:26; Hos 2:20, 10:4; Zech 11:10**; etc. The full distribution covers the patriarchal narratives, the legal corpora, the Deuteronomistic history, the wisdom literature, and the prophetic books. The idiom is one of the most cross-corpus-reachable Hebrew Bible expressions.

**The variant idiom *karat amanah*** (Neh 9:38) and *karat eshet* (Isa 28:15 *karatnu brit et-mavet* — *we have cut a covenant with death*) extend the same verb to other compact-formation contexts.

**The ANE comparative context.** The idiom is uniquely Hebrew; the ANE corpus uses different vocabulary for covenant-ratification (Akkadian *riksu rakāsu* — to bind a binding; Hittite *iškiya- linkiya-* — to bind by oath; Aramaic *gzr ʿdyʾ* — to cut a treaty, the Sefire-treaty idiom — which is the *closest* parallel to the Hebrew *karat brit*). The Aramaic *gzr* (cut) + *ʿdyʾ* (treaty/pact) at Sefire KAI 222-224 is the precise cognate idiom — the same cutting-verb applied to the same legal-religious noun. The Hebrew-Aramaic shared idiom suggests a West-Semitic legal background for the rite-and-idiom together. **Weinfeld 1970 *JAOS* 90** discusses the comparative material; **Hillers** (*Covenant*, 1969) and **McCarthy** (*Treaty and Covenant*, 1978) collate the parallels.

**The verb-literal *cut* vs. the smoothed *make*.** The conventional English Bibles overwhelmingly smooth *karat brit* to *make a covenant* (KJV, ASV, NRSV, NIV). The smoothing loses the etymological link to the animal-bisection ritual. The WoH translation preserves the literal *cut*: *YHWH cut a covenant with Abram*. NJPS reads *made a covenant*; modern critical scholarly translations (Alter, *The Five Books of Moses*, 2004; Friedman, *Commentary on the Torah*, 2001) vary between *made* and *cut*; Alter at Gen 15:18 reads *Yahweh sealed a covenant with Abram*, preserving the formal register without the etymological link.

**The WoH choice.** The translation preserves *cut a covenant* throughout the Hebrew Bible wherever *karat brit* appears. The choice is justified by (a) the etymological-literal preservation of the verb's link to the ritual; (b) the parallel to the Aramaic *gzr ʿdyʾ* West-Semitic legal idiom; (c) the consistency with the Gen 15:9-11, 17 ritual described in the same chapter; (d) the recovery of a lexical dimension that the conventional smoothing erases. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the idiom is grammatically explicit at the cited verses; (b) the cross-corpus distribution (80+ occurrences) is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the Aramaic-cognate evidence from Sefire is direct documentary evidence.

terms.partOfSpeech: list of ten gentilic-nouns with definite article
terms.literal: the Qeini, the Qenizi, the Qadmoni, the Chitti, the Perizi, the Refa'im, the Emori, the Kena'ani, the Girgashi, the Yevusi
terms.standardEnglish: the Kenites, Kenizzites, Kadmonites, Hittites, Perizzites, Rephaim, Amorites, Canaanites, Girgashites, Jebusites
terms.wohChoice: the Qeini, the Qenizi, the Qadmoni, the Chitti, the Perizi, the Refa'im, the Emori, the Kena'ani, the Girgashi, the Yevusi
terms.rationale:
**The ten-fold list of vv 19-21 is unique in the Hebrew Bible.** Every other Pentateuchal enumeration of the land's pre-Israelite peoples has six, seven, or fewer. The Gen 15:19-21 list is the *maximal* form: ten peoples, deliberately matched to the *maximal* land-claim of v 18 (from the river of Mitsrayim to the Euphrates).

**The Hebrew Bible's enumeration-variations.** **Six-fold lists**: Exod 3:8, 3:17 (*Kena'ani, Chitti, Emori, Perizi, Chivi, Yevusi*); Exod 13:5 (same six); Exod 23:23 (*Emori, Chitti, Perizi, Kena'ani, Chivi, Yevusi*); Exod 33:2 (same six in varied order); Exod 34:11 (same six); Deut 20:17 (same six). **Seven-fold lists**: Deut 7:1 (adds *Girgashi*: *Chitti, Girgashi, Emori, Kena'ani, Perizi, Chivi, Yevusi*); Josh 3:10 (same seven); Josh 24:11 (same seven). **Variations**: Gen 10:15-18's Table-of-Nations enumeration of Kena'an's descendants overlaps but is structured differently. **Gen 15:19-21's ten-fold list** is unique: it adds the *Qeini, Qenizi, Qadmoni* triad to the standard land-peoples and substitutes *Refa'im* for *Chivi*. The maximization is structural and editorial.

**The Qeini / Qenizi / Qadmoni triad.** The three peoples added uniquely at vv 19-20 appear in no other Pentateuchal land-peoples list. (a) **Qeini** (קֵינִי) — the Kenites. The gentilic recurs at Num 24:21-22 (Balaam's oracle); Judg 1:16 (the Kenites with Moses's affines descending from Jethro); Judg 4:11, 4:17, 5:24 (Heber the Kenite, Jael); 1 Sam 15:6 (the Kenites explicitly spared by Saul in the Amaleq campaign); 1 Sam 27:10, 30:29; 1 Chr 2:55. The Kenites are typically allied or in-married with Israel rather than displaced by it. The name's etymology is debated (Halpern, *The Emergence of Israel in Canaan*, 1983; Knauf, *Midian*, 1988); some readings derive from *qayin* (smith) and link to metalworking, others to a tribal name. (b) **Qenizi** (קְנִזִּי) — the Kenizzites. The gentilic recurs at Num 32:12 (Caleb the Kenizzite); Josh 14:6, 14:14 (Caleb's gentilic). Caleb's lineage is Kenizzite; the Kenizzites are absorbed into Judah. The name is genealogically connected to the Edomite Kenaz (Gen 36:11, 15, 42). (c) **Qadmoni** (קַדְמֹנִי) — *eastern ones*. The lexeme is a hapax in this gentilic sense. The etymology is direct: from *qedem* (east), *qadmoni* names *the eastern people*. The most likely referent is the Trans-Jordanian / Syrian-desert peoples east of the Jordan and the Salt Sea — *b'nei qedem*, *children of the east*, the standard Hebrew Bible term for the eastern desert peoples (cf. Gen 29:1; Judg 6:3, 6:33, 7:12, 8:10; 1 Kgs 5:10; Job 1:3 *gadol mi-kol b'nei qedem*; Isa 11:14; Jer 49:28; Ezek 25:4, 25:10). The Qadmoni would then be the *b'nei qedem* in gentilic-singular form.

**The substitution of *Refa'im* for *Chivi*.** The standard six-fold list has *Chivi* (Hivites) where Gen 15:19-21 has *Refa'im*. The substitution is editorially significant: it links the Gen 15 list to the giant-peoples chain of Gen 14:5 (the existing *refaim-giant-peoples* entry) and to the broader Deut 2-3 giant-peoples corpus. The maximalist character of the Gen 15 list extends to its inclusion of the giant-stature population among the land-peoples to be displaced.

**The geographical scope.** The ten-fold list matches the v 18 maximal land-claim. The reduced six-fold list of Exod 3:8 etc. covers the Wadi-of-Egypt-to-entrance-of-Hamath standard land; the Gen 15 ten-fold list covers the Euphrates-to-Wadi-of-Egypt maximal land. Westermann (*Genesis 12-36*, 1985, p. 230), Sarna (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 117), and Wenham (*WBC Genesis 1-15*, 1987, p. 334) read the ten-fold list as a deliberate editorial maximization matched to the maximal land-claim of v 18.

**Modern critical reading.** The standard documentary-hypothesis reading attributes the ten-fold list to a late editorial expansion (J or a post-J redactor); the standard six-fold list is read as the earlier core, with later editors maximizing the enumeration. **Westermann** treats the list as a redactional development; **Sarna** treats it as a deliberate stylistic feature; **Wenham** treats it as consistent with the chapter's maximal-claim register. The redactional reading is not contested; the question is whether the maximization is early or late.

**Land-claim geography.** *Min'har Mitsrayim ad ha-nahar ha-gadol n'har P'rat* — *from the river of Mitsrayim unto the great river, the river Perat* — covers the territory from the Wadi el-Arish (or, on the maximal reading, an arm of the Nile) in the southwest to the Euphrates in the northeast. The reduced *me-Naḥal Mitsrayim ad l'vo Chamat* of Num 34:5 and 1 Kgs 8:65 covers a much smaller territory. The Solomonic kingdom briefly approached the Euphrates land-claim (1 Kgs 4:21 *u-Sh'lomo hayah moshel b-khol ha-mamlakhot min ha-nahar* — *Solomon was ruling over all the kingdoms from the River*; 1 Kgs 5:1).

**The WoH translation.** The transliterations preserve the Hebrew gentilic forms (*Qeini, Qenizi, Qadmoni, Chitti, Perizi, Refa'im, Emori, Kena'ani, Girgashi, Yevusi*) rather than the Anglicized forms (Kenites, Kenizzites, etc.), matching the chapter's pattern (the *refaim-giant-peoples* entry handles *Refa'im*). The transliterations are documented in the per-verse commentary. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the ten-fold list is grammatically explicit at vv 19-21; (b) the variations across Exod 3:8, Deut 7:1, Josh 3:10, etc. are direct documentary evidence of the enumeration-variability; (c) the *qedem* / *b'nei qedem* etymological link for *Qadmoni* is grammatically explicit; (d) the documentary-hypothesis redactional reading is scholarly synthesis but mainstream and uncontested.

terms.literal: maidservant, female slave (of a specific legal class)
terms.standardEnglish: handmaid (ASV/KJV); slave-girl (NRSV/NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: handmaid (with ANE substitute-wife legal background carried in apparatus)
terms.rationale:
**The *shifchah* of Gen 16:1-3 is a specific legal-social institution, not merely a generic female slave.** The Hebrew lexeme *shifchah* (שִׁפְחָה) denotes a class of female slave whose offspring by the master, at the mistress's initiative and on her body, could legally count as the mistress's children. The narrative of vv 1-3 (Sarai gives Hagar to Abram *l-ishah* — *for a wife*) is the canonical Hebrew Bible enactment of the institution; the parallel narratives at Gen 30:3-13 (Bilhah and Zilpah given to Jacob by Rachel and Leah) follow the same legal-narrative pattern. The English *handmaid* (ASV/KJV) is conventional but does not carry the cultural-legal specificity; the modern *slave-girl* (NRSV/NJPS) carries the slave-status but loses the substitute-wife arrangement.

**The ANE legal parallels.** The institution is directly paralleled in two distinct second-millennium-BCE legal corpora. (a) **Code of Hammurabi §146** (Old Babylonian, c. 1750 BCE): if a man marries a hierodule (*nadītu*) who is barred from bearing children, and she gives a slave-girl (*šugītum*) to her husband as substitute, and the slave-girl bears children — and the slave-girl then 'makes herself equal with her mistress' — the mistress may not sell her but may reduce her again to slave-status. The Hammurabi parallel is direct lexical-social-institutional comparison: the legal scenario of Gen 16:4-6 (the *shifchah* who *vatekal gevirtah b-eyneha* — 'lightly esteemed her mistress in her eyes' — and is then dealt-with-harshly by the mistress) replays the Hammurabi scenario almost verbatim. Pritchard, *ANET* (1969), pp. 172-173 prints the text. (b) **Nuzi tablets, HSS V 67** (Middle Babylonian, c. 1500 BCE, Hurrian-Mesopotamian milieu): the Hurrian marriage-contract corpus from Nuzi contains explicit provisions in which a wife who fails to bear children is obligated to provide a slave-girl as substitute-wife for the husband; the contracts further specify that the substitute-wife's children belong to the legal wife, not to the slave-girl. **Speiser** (*Anchor Bible Genesis*, 1964, pp. 119-121) reads the Nuzi parallels as *closer* in detail to the patriarchal narratives than the Hammurabi parallel, and connects the patriarchal Aramean-Hurrian milieu (Harran, Padan-Aram) to the Nuzi legal tradition.

**The modern critical consensus.** **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36*, 1985, pp. 238-241), **Sarna** (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 119 and Excursus 12), **Wenham** (*WBC Genesis 16-50*, 1994, pp. 7-9), and **Hamilton** (*NICOT Genesis 1-17*, 1990, pp. 444-447) all read the *shifchah* arrangement against the ANE legal background, with the Hammurabi and Nuzi parallels as the standard comparative material. The cross-corpus reach is direct: the Gen 30 narratives (Bilhah given by Rachel, Zilpah given by Leah) replay the same institution, and the *shifchah* lexeme governs both narratives.

**The *shifchah* / *amah* distinction.** The Hebrew Bible distinguishes *shifchah* (the substitute-wife class) from *amah* (the standard maidservant class). The distinction is not absolute — the lexemes overlap — but the *shifchah* designation is preferred where the substitute-wife arrangement is in view. Hagar is *shifchah* at Gen 16:1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 8 and *amah* nowhere in the chapter; Bilhah and Zilpah are *shifchah* at Gen 30:3-13 and the narrative-conclusion of 30:18. The lexical distribution is a direct philological flag of the institutional reading.

**The WoH translation choice.** The translation preserves ASV's *handmaid* in the running English at vv 1-8 (the conventional English does not exclude the substitute-wife reading; the cultural-legal specificity is carried in commentary at vv 1-3 and in this glossary entry). The alternative *slave-girl* (NRSV/NJPS) is direct and would be defensible; the choice is editorial preservation of the ASV-baseline. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the lexeme *shifchah* is grammatically explicit at the cited verses; (b) the *shifchah*/*amah* distinction is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the Hammurabi §146 and Nuzi HSS V 67 ANE parallels are direct documentary evidence; (d) the Westermann/Sarna/Wenham/Hamilton/Speiser reading is the modern critical consensus.

terms.source: חֲמָסִ֣י עָלֶיךָ֒ ... יִשְׁפֹּ֥ט יְהוָ֖ה בֵּינִ֥י וּבֵינֶֽיךָ
terms.translit: chamasi alekha ... yishpot YHWH beyni u-veynekha
terms.partOfSpeech: noun + prep phrase (curse-formula) + verbal jussive (litigation formula)
terms.literal: my violence (be) upon you ... YHWH judge between me and between you
terms.standardEnglish: my wrong be upon you ... YHWH judge between me and you (ASV)
terms.wohChoice: my wrong be upon you ... YHWH judge between me and you (ASV, with *chamas* register and forensic-curse pairing carried in apparatus)
terms.rationale:
**Sarai's declaration at v 5 deploys two paired legal-religious formulae: a *chamas*-curse and a YHWH-litigation invocation.** *Chamasi alekha* — *my violence (be) upon you* — names the wrong as *chamas*, the strong Hebrew term for criminal violence / wrongful injury, and rhetorically transfers the wrong onto the addressee. The second clause *yishpot YHWH beyni u-veynekha* — *YHWH judge between me and you* — invokes YHWH as covenant-litigation arbiter between the two parties. The pairing is a known forensic-religious formula in the patriarchal narratives and the wider Hebrew Bible.

**The *chamas* register.** The noun *chamas* (חָמָס) is the strongest Hebrew Bible term for criminal violence, wrongful injury, or unjust wrong. The pre-existing *shachat-chamas* glossary entry covers the antediluvian usage at Gen 6:11, 13 (the earth filled with *chamas*) as the formal-legal ground for the flood. Cross-corpus distribution outside Genesis: Judg 9:24 (the *chamas* of Abimelech's seventy-brother massacre, framed as the verse's keystone legal-religious indictment); Hab 1:2-3 (the prophet's complaint that he cries *chamas* and is not answered); Hos 12:1 (Ephraim filled with *chamas*); Mic 6:12 (the rich men of Jerusalem full of *chamas*); Jonah 3:8 (the Ninevites' *chamas* to be repented of); Ps 11:5, 27:12, 35:11, 55:10, 58:3, 73:6, 140:2, 140:5, 140:12; Prov 4:17, 10:6, 10:11, 13:2, 16:29, 26:6; Isa 53:9 (the Suffering Servant has done no *chamas*); Isa 59:6, 60:18; Jer 6:7, 20:8, 22:3, 22:17, 51:35, 51:46; Ezek 7:11, 7:23, 8:17, 12:19, 28:16, 45:9; Joel 4:19; Amos 3:10, 6:3; Obad 1:10; Mal 2:16; Zeph 1:9. The lexical-distribution is dense in the prophetic-indictment corpus, where *chamas* names the social-criminal injustice that triggers YHWH's judgment.

**The *yishpot YHWH* litigation idiom.** *Yishpot YHWH beyni u-veynekha* (יִשְׁפֹּט יְהוָה בֵּינִי וּבֵינֶךָ) — *YHWH judge between me and you* — is the standard Hebrew Bible covenant-litigation invocation. The idiom recurs at: **Gen 31:53** (Laban and Jacob at the Mizpah covenant: *Elohei Avraham v-Elohei Nachor yishp'tu beyneinu* — *the God of Abraham and the God of Nachor shall judge between us*); **1 Sam 24:13, 24:16** (David before Saul: *yishpot YHWH beyni u-veynekha v-nakam YHWH mim'kha* — *YHWH judge between me and you and YHWH avenge me from you*); **Judg 11:27** (Jephthah's messenger to the Ammonites: *yishpot YHWH ha-shofet ha-yom* — *let YHWH the judge judge today*). The idiom names YHWH as the third party in a two-party dispute, invoking divine arbitration.

**The forensic-curse pairing.** The pairing of a *chamas*-declaration with a *yishpot YHWH* litigation-invocation is editorially deliberate. The wrong is named (*chamas*), the wrong is transferred to the addressee (*alekha*), the divine arbiter is invoked (*yishpot YHWH*), and the dispute is formally opened (*beyni u-veynekha*). The compound formula is a *legal speech-act* in the strict sense: Sarai is not merely complaining, she is initiating a religious-juridical proceeding. **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36*, 1985, p. 240), **Sarna** (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 120), and **Wenham** (*WBC Genesis 16-50*, 1994, p. 10) read the v 5 declaration as a formal speech-act in this register.

**The English translation.** The conventional English Bibles render *chamasi alekha* with various degrees of softening: ASV/KJV *my wrong be upon you*; NRSV *may the wrong done to me be on you*; NJPS *the wrong done me is your fault*; Alter (2004) *my outrage is upon you*. The ASV-baseline *my wrong be upon you* is preserved in the running English; the *chamas*-register and the forensic-curse pairing are carried in commentary at v 5 and in this glossary entry. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the lexemes *chamas* and *yishpot* are grammatically explicit; (b) the cross-corpus distribution of *chamas* and *yishpot YHWH beyni u-veynekha* is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the forensic-speech-act reading is the Westermann/Sarna/Wenham consensus.

terms.standardEnglish: angel of the LORD (KJV/ASV/RSV); messenger of the LORD (less common)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 16:7 is the first occurrence of *mal'akh YHWH* — the messenger of YHWH — in the Hebrew Bible.** מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה. The figure recurs four times in this single scene (vv 7, 9, 10, 11) and across the patriarchal cycle and the wider Hebrew Bible (full distribution below). The WoH translation renders the figure as *messenger of YHWH* throughout, preserving the Hebrew etymology and the messenger-identity scholarly question. The conventional English *angel of the LORD* imports later Greek-and-Latin theology (*angelos* / *angelus* as a class of created spiritual being); the Hebrew *mal'akh* is the standard ordinary-language word for a human messenger (e.g. Gen 32:4 Jacob sends *mal'akhim* to Esau; 1 Sam 6:21 the people of Beth-Shemesh send *mal'akhim*; 1 Kgs 19:2 Jezebel sends a *mal'akh* to Elijah) and only secondarily denotes a divine messenger.

**The lexeme.** *Mal'akh* (מַלְאָךְ) derives from a root l-ʾ-k (cognate to Ugaritic *lʾk*, *to send*); the noun denotes *one who is sent, a messenger*. The lexeme is morphologically neutral on the messenger's nature: a *mal'akh* may be a human envoy (the diplomatic and military uses, e.g. Num 20:14 Moses sends *mal'akhim* to Edom; 2 Sam 5:11 Hiram sends *mal'akhim* to David), a prophet (Hag 1:13 the prophet Haggai called *mal'akh YHWH*; Mal 3:1; the name of the book Mal'akhi itself), or a divine messenger. BDB §4397 reads the lexeme as *messenger, representative*; HALOT concurs. The LXX standardly translates *mal'akh* by *angelos* (ἄγγελος), itself simply the Greek word for *messenger*; the theological-class sense of *angel* is a downstream development in Hellenistic-Jewish and Christian thought.

**The messenger-identity question.** The single load-bearing scholarly issue of the *mal'akh YHWH* scenes is the *identity* of the messenger figure relative to YHWH. The Genesis 16 narrative itself foregrounds the question: the *mal'akh YHWH* speaks (vv 8-12), and Hagar then *va-tiqra shem-YHWH ha-dover eleha* — *calls the name of YHWH who spoke to her* (v 13) — identifying the speaker not as a separate envoy but as YHWH himself. The same dynamic recurs across the corpus: Exod 3:2 the *mal'akh YHWH* appears in the burning bush, and v 4 has *YHWH himself* speaking from the bush; Judg 6:11-22 the *mal'akh YHWH* appears to Gideon and is addressed by Gideon as YHWH at v 22, prompting Gideon's seeing-God-and-living anxiety. Modern critical scholarship recognizes three main positions on this material — none uniquely consensus, all defended in print by serious philological scholars.

**Position (a): the *mal'akh* is a distinct created envoy.** The classic Christian-tradition reading, exemplified in the patristic and medieval angelology corpus, treats the *mal'akh YHWH* as a distinct created spiritual being who speaks on YHWH's behalf and is sometimes mistaken for YHWH by the human recipient. The reading is supported by passages where the *mal'akh* is clearly distinguished from YHWH (Zech 1:11-12 the *mal'akh YHWH* intercedes *to* YHWH; 1 Kgs 19:7 the *mal'akh* of YHWH speaks to Elijah and is distinct from YHWH's later self-revelation in the *qol demamah daqah*). **Heidt** (*Angelology of the Old Testament*, 1949) is the standard mid-twentieth-century Catholic-scholarly defense of the distinct-envoy reading. The position survives in conservative-evangelical and Catholic-traditionalist commentary.

**Position (b): the *mal'akh* IS YHWH (the identity reading).** Modern critical scholarship since the mid-twentieth century has increasingly read the *mal'akh YHWH* as a *mode of YHWH's own appearance* rather than as a distinct envoy. The reading is supported by passages where the *mal'akh* speaks in the first-person AS YHWH (Gen 16:10 *I will multiply your seed*; Gen 22:12 *now I know that you fear God*; Exod 3:6 *I am the God of your father*), where the human recipient identifies the *mal'akh* AS YHWH (Gen 16:13; Judg 6:22-23; Judg 13:22), and where YHWH and the *mal'akh YHWH* are interchanged in narration (Gen 22:11, 15 the *mal'akh YHWH* speaks; Gen 22:16 *says YHWH*; Exod 3:2 the *mal'akh YHWH* appears; Exod 3:4 *YHWH saw ... and Elohim called*). **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36*, 1985, pp. 242-244) reads the *mal'akh YHWH* in the patriarchal narratives as a *theophanic mode*, not a distinct figure; **von Rad** (*Genesis*, OTL, 1972, pp. 192-193) reads the figure as YHWH himself appearing in a non-overwhelming form. The identity-reading is the modern critical default in continental scholarship.

**Position (c): the *mal'akh* is a literary-theological device.** A third position, developed in twenty-first-century scholarship, reads the *mal'akh YHWH* as a *literary device* that allows YHWH to appear without theophanic risk — a way of preserving divine transcendence while permitting personal encounter. The reading takes the alternation between *mal'akh YHWH* and YHWH not as a doctrinal claim about a distinct figure but as a narrative-rhetorical strategy. **Hamori** (*When Gods Were Men: Biblical Theophany and Anthropomorphic Realism*, 2008) develops the position with reference to the wider corpus of *anthropomorphic realism* in the patriarchal narratives. **Sommer** (*The Bodies of God and the World of Ancient Israel*, 2009) develops a related but distinct *fluidity model* in which the divine self can have multiple bodies or manifestations, with the *mal'akh YHWH* as one such manifestation. **MacDonald** (2009 *The Origin of 'Diversity' in the Israelite Concept of God*) addresses the diversity of divine manifestations in the Hebrew Bible and the *mal'akh YHWH* as a case.

**The WoH translation commits to none of the three positions.** *Messenger of YHWH* preserves the Hebrew lexical etymology and lets the identity-question be carried at the apparatus level (commentary and glossary), not at the translation level. The conventional *angel of the LORD* both imports later angelology and tilts toward position (a) by suggesting a distinct created being. The translation choice is editorially neutral on the messenger-identity question. Project-specific synthesis is *not* in this entry; the entry is a faithful presentation of the modern critical landscape.

**The cross-corpus distribution.** The figure recurs across the Hebrew Bible. **Patriarchal narratives**: Gen 16:7, 9, 10, 11 (Hagar in the wilderness); Gen 21:17 (Hagar at the well at Beer-Lachai-Roi, second encounter); Gen 22:11, 15 (the Aqedah, twice); Gen 31:11 (Jacob's dream at Padan-Aram); Gen 48:16 (Jacob's blessing of Joseph's sons, the *mal'akh ha-go'el oti* — *the messenger who redeemed me from all evil*). **Exodus and wilderness**: Exod 3:2 (the burning bush); Exod 14:19 (the *mal'akh ha-Elohim* repositions between Israel and the Egyptians); Exod 23:20-23 (the *mal'akh* who will guide Israel, the editorially significant *shemi b-qirbo* — *my name is in him*); Exod 32:34, 33:2 (post-golden-calf, the *mal'akh* sent in place of YHWH's direct presence); Num 20:16; Num 22:22-35 (Balaam's *mal'akh YHWH* with drawn sword). **Joshua and Judges**: Josh 5:13-15 (the *sar tsva YHWH* — *commander of YHWH's army* — at Jericho, identified by Westermann and many with the *mal'akh YHWH* figure); Judg 2:1-5 (the *mal'akh YHWH* at Bochim); Judg 5:23 (the curse of Meroz by the *mal'akh YHWH* in the Song of Deborah); Judg 6:11-22 (Gideon at Ophrah); Judg 13:3-21 (Manoah and the birth of Samson). **Samuel-Kings**: 2 Sam 24:16-17 (the plague-angel at Jerusalem's threshing floor); 1 Kgs 19:5-7 (Elijah at Horeb); 2 Kgs 1:3-15 (Elijah and Ahaziah's messengers); 2 Kgs 19:35 (the destruction of Sennacherib's army, paralleled at Isa 37:36); 1 Chr 21:12-30; 2 Chr 32:21. **Prophets**: Isa 37:36; Isa 63:9 (the *mal'akh panav* — *messenger of his face* — saved them, a unique compound); Hos 12:5 (Jacob and the *mal'akh*); Zech 1:11-12 (the *mal'akh YHWH* who intercedes); Zech 3:1-6 (the *mal'akh YHWH* in the Joshua-the-high-priest vision); Mal 3:1 (the future *mal'akh ha-brit*). **Psalms and writings**: Ps 34:8, 35:5-6; Job 33:23 (the *mal'akh melitz* — *interpreter-messenger*).

**Note on the post-exilic developed angelology.** The post-exilic period sees the development of a class-of-angels framework (Daniel's Gabriel and Michael; the named-angels of 1 Enoch, Tobit's Raphael, the Qumran corpus); the patriarchal *mal'akh YHWH* belongs to the pre-developed-angelology stratum and should not be read forward through it. The transition is itself a major topic in Second Temple Jewish scholarship (Olyan 1993 *A Thousand Thousands Served Him*; Sullivan 2004 *Wrestling with Angels*; Bautch 2003). The *messenger of YHWH* translation preserves the philological distinctness of the pre-developed-angelology stratum from the post-exilic developed class. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the lexeme *mal'akh* and the construct phrase *mal'akh YHWH* are grammatically explicit at every cited verse; (b) the cross-corpus distribution is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the three modern critical positions on messenger-identity are documented in the cited literature; (d) the translation choice *messenger of YHWH* is etymologically literal and does not commit the project to any of the three positions.

terms.standardEnglish: I will greatly multiply (ASV/KJV); I will greatly increase (NRSV)
terms.wohChoice: I will greatly multiply (ASV-baseline; doubled-infinitive intensive carried in apparatus)
terms.rationale:
**The Hebrew construction at Gen 16:10 — *harbah arbeh et-zarekh* — uses the *doubled infinitive absolute + finite verb* intensifying construction, one of the Hebrew Bible's most distinctive syntactic features.** The infinitive absolute *harbah* (from rbh, to be many, to multiply) prefixed before the finite hiphil yiqtol *arbeh* (I will multiply) does not duplicate vocabulary at random; the construction conveys *emphasis, certainty, or intensification* of the verbal action. The conventional English rendering *I will greatly multiply* (ASV) captures the intensification with the adverb *greatly*; modern translations vary between *surely multiply*, *exceedingly multiply*, and *I will indeed multiply*.

**The grammar.** The doubled-infinitive intensive is treated at length in **Gesenius-Kautzsch-Cowley §113l-r** (the infinitive absolute with the finite verb of the same root) and in **Joüon-Muraoka §123** (the *figura etymologica*). The construction's basic semantic effect is *strengthening or emphasizing the verbal idea*; the precise pragmatic force varies with context (asseverative, intensive, durative, or emphatic). Williams' *Hebrew Syntax* §205 and Waltke-O'Connor §35.3.1 treat the construction systematically. The infinitive can precede the finite verb (the dominant pattern, as here) or follow it.

**The recurrence-distribution.** The doubled-infinitive intensive recurs hundreds of times across the Hebrew Bible. The first occurrence is Gen 2:16 *mi-kol etz ha-gan akhol tokhel* — *of every tree of the garden eating you may eat / you may freely eat* — the construction's inaugural use, in the Eden permission-formula. The Gen 2:17 negative parallel *mot tamut* — *dying you shall die / you shall surely die* — applies the same construction to the death-threat. In the patriarchal narratives the construction is dense: Gen 22:17 (*barekh avarekhkha* — *blessing I will bless you*, paired with *harbah arbeh*); Gen 31:30 (*nikhsof nikhsafta* — *longing you have longed*); Gen 43:7 (*ha-yado'a neda* — *knowing could we know*); Gen 50:24 (*paqod yifqod* — *visiting will visit*). In the legal corpora the construction is the standard formulation of legal-emphatic statements: Exod 8:24 *harchek lo tarchiqu* — *far off you shall not go far off*; Exod 19:13 *saqol yisaqel* — *stoning he shall be stoned*; Exod 21:5 *amor yomar* — *saying says*; the *mot yumat* — *dying he shall be put to death* — formula across Exod 21:12, 15, 16, 17 and the wider legal corpus. In the prophetic literature the construction is intensified emphatic-asseveration: Isa 6:9 *shimu shamoa* — *hearing hear*; Isa 24:19 *roa hitroa'a ha-aretz / por hitporerah eretz / mot hitmotetah eretz* — the triple-doubled cataclysm formula. The construction's reach is corpus-wide.

**The *harbah arbeh* specific subset.** The specific verb *rbh* in doubled-infinitive intensive form recurs at: Gen 3:16 (*harbah arbeh itzvonekh v-heronekh* — *multiplying I will multiply your sorrow and your conception* — the curse on the woman); Gen 16:10 (here, to Hagar); Gen 17:2, 17:20 (the latter specifically of Ishmael at the Abrahamic covenant, paralleling this verse); Gen 22:17 (paired with the comparison-triad to Abraham post-Aqedah); Exod 32:13; Deut 1:10, 13:18, 28:63, 30:5, 30:16. The *harbah arbeh* form is concentrated in the patriarchal seed-multiplication promises but is not exclusive to them. The verb *rbh* doubled in this form is one of the most theologically loaded doubled-infinitive subsets in the corpus.

**Distinction from the patriarchal-seed-comparison-triad.** The pre-existing glossary entry *patriarchal-seed-comparison-triad* (v2.10.0) covers the dust/stars/sand comparison formulae (Gen 13:16, 15:5, 22:17). The *harbah arbeh* construction is grammatically distinct from the comparison-triad: it is an intensifying syntactic construction, not a comparison. At Gen 22:17 the two are paired (*barekh avarekhkha v-harbah arbeh et-zarekh ka-kokhavei ha-shamayim v-cha-chol asher al-sfat ha-yam* — *blessing I will bless you and multiplying I will multiply your seed as the stars of the heavens and as the sand on the shore of the sea*); at Gen 16:10 the doubled-infinitive intensive appears without the comparison-triad, marking it as syntactically separable from the comparison. The two entries cover distinct features and do not collide.

**Hagar's parallel position.** The Gen 16:10 promise *harbah arbeh et-zarekh v-lo yisafer me-rov* — *multiplying I will multiply your seed, and it shall not be numbered for multitude* — is the *only* seed-multiplication formula in Genesis addressed to a woman, and addressed to a non-Israelite woman (an Egyptian *shifchah*). The formula's address-recipient is structurally significant: Hagar receives in her own person a promise that elsewhere in Genesis is given to the patriarchs (Abram at 13:16, 15:5, 17:2; Isaac at 26:24; Jacob at 28:14, 32:13). The closing clause *v-lo yisafer me-rov* (*it shall not be numbered for multitude*) echoes the *cannot be numbered* phrasing of Gen 13:16 (Abraham) and 32:13 (Jacob), formally aligning the Hagar promise with the patriarchal-promise corpus.

**The WoH translation choice.** The translation preserves ASV's *I will greatly multiply*; the doubled-infinitive intensive is carried in commentary at v 10 and in this glossary entry. The alternative *surely / exceedingly multiply* or *multiplying I will multiply* (Alter 2004's general practice for the construction) would be defensible; the ASV-baseline is preserved for readability. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the doubled-infinitive intensive construction is grammatically explicit; (b) the cross-corpus distribution is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the Gesenius-Kautzsch-Cowley / Joüon-Muraoka / Waltke-O'Connor treatments are the standard grammatical references.

terms.literal: and you shall call his name Yishma-El, because YHWH has heard
terms.standardEnglish: and you shall call his name Ishmael, because the LORD hath heard (ASV)
terms.wohChoice: and you shall call his name Yishma'el, because YHWH has heard (transliterated theophoric)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 16:11 is the first in-text patriarchal name-etymology of the Hebrew Bible, and the first to deploy the *El*-theophoric pattern.** *V-qarat sh'mo Yishma'el ki-shama YHWH el-onyekh* — *and you shall call his name Yishma-El, because YHWH has heard your affliction*. The name *Yishma'el* (יִשְׁמָעֵאל) is a theophoric construction: a finite-verb element (*yishma* — *he hears*, from *shama*) + the divine name element *El* (אֵל). The etymology is in-text: the narrator explains the name by direct reference to the verb (*shama*) whose subject is YHWH (*shama YHWH*). The etymological formula *X / ki Y* — *(named) X / because Y* — is the standard Hebrew Bible name-etymology pattern; the verb *qara* (call) + the *ki* clause is the formal marker.

**The cross-corpus patriarchal name-etymology pattern.** The Hebrew Bible has a developed convention of patriarchal name-etymologies: a name is given, the narrator explains the name by reference to a related verb or noun, and the explanation is marked with *ki* (because) or its functional equivalent. The pattern recurs across the patriarchal corpus: **Yitzchaq (Isaac)** at Gen 17:17, 17:19, 18:12-15, 21:6 — from *tzachaq* (laugh; Abraham's laugh, Sarah's laugh, the playful Sarah's *tzachaqti* / *m'tzacheq*); **Esav (Esau)** at Gen 25:25 — from *eshu* / red-hairy (the etymology is contested; the explicit verbal connection is to *admoni* and *adeer* / *se'ar*); **Ya'aqov (Jacob)** at Gen 25:26 — from *aqev* (heel; he grasped his brother's heel), with the secondary etymology at Gen 27:36 from *aqav* (supplant); **Yosef (Joseph)** at Gen 30:24 — from *asaf* (gather) and *yosef* (add); **Re'uven (Reuben)** at Gen 29:32 — from *ra'ah b'onyi* (he has seen my affliction) and *ye'ehavani* (will love); **Shim'on (Simeon)** at Gen 29:33 — from *shama* (he hears, ki shama YHWH — directly parallel to Yishma'el's etymology); **Yehuda (Judah)** at Gen 29:35 — from *odeh* (I will praise). The pattern extends through the rest of Jacob's sons (Dan, Naphtali, Gad, Asher, Issachar, Zevulun, Dinah) at Gen 30:4-21. The pattern's load-bearing function is to fix the name in the narrative; the names are not arbitrary, they are story-compressions.

**The Yishma'el / Shim'on parallel.** Among the patriarchal name-etymologies, Yishma'el and Shim'on share the same root verb *shama* (hear): *Yishma'el* — *El will hear*; *Shim'on* — *YHWH has heard*. The pairing is structurally significant: the *shama* root names the relation between a divine subject and a human affliction (vv 11, 29:33 both have *shama* of YHWH; vv 11, 29:32, 29:33 cluster three sister-and-handmaid affliction-etymologies in close textual proximity, with Gen 16 the inaugural instance). **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36*, 1985, pp. 244-245), **Sarna** (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 121), and **Wenham** (*WBC Genesis 16-50*, 1994, pp. 11-12) all treat the Yishma'el etymology as the first of the developed patriarchal name-etymology pattern.

**The El / YHWH co-occurrence.** A small but theologically interesting feature of v 11 is the Hebrew juxtaposition: the *name* is *Yishma-El* (theophoric in *El*); the *subject of the verb* explaining the name is *YHWH* (*ki shama YHWH*). The construction effectively predicates the action of YHWH onto a name compounded with El. **Cross-reference**: the pre-existing glossary entries *el-elyon* and *yhwh-el-elyon-apposition* (Gen 14) cover the broader El-and-YHWH co-occurrence pattern. The Yishma'el v 11 instance is a small further data-point in that pattern: the Genesis editor permits the El-theophoric child-naming alongside YHWH-narrative-action without harmonization. The pattern is direct documentary evidence for a pre-monotheistic-harmonization stratum of the patriarchal corpus.

**The transliteration.** The translation preserves *Yishma'el* over the Anglicized *Ishmael*, matching the chapter convention (*Mitsrayim*, *Kena'an*, etc.). The etymological explanation is preserved literally (*because YHWH has heard your affliction*).

**Knauf 1989 and the Ishmaelite ethnographic background.** **Knauf** (*Ismael: Untersuchungen zur Geschichte Palästinas und Nordarabiens im 1. Jahrtausend v. Chr.*, 1989) reconstructs the Ishmaelite tribal confederation as a North-Arabian / Trans-Jordanian camel-pastoralist league active in the late Iron Age (8th-6th centuries BCE); the *b'nei Yishma'el* of Gen 25:13-16 (the twelve-tribe Ishmaelite list) overlaps with attested North-Arabian tribal names from the Neo-Assyrian and Neo-Babylonian record (Tema, Dumah, Adbeel, Kedar, Nebaioth). The ethnographic background is *direct* (documentary-attested tribal names) and supports reading the Yishma'el-genealogy material as preserving real Late-Iron-Age tribal data. The reading is mainstream in modern ANE-historical scholarship (Knauf 1989; Eph'al 1982 *The Ancient Arabs*; Retsö 2003 *The Arabs in Antiquity*). // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the etymological-explanation formula *qara sh'mo X ki Y* is grammatically explicit; (b) the cross-corpus patriarchal name-etymology pattern is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the Westermann/Sarna/Wenham treatments are the modern critical consensus; (d) the Knauf 1989 North-Arabian-tribal ethnographic background is documentarily attested.

terms.literal: wild-ass of a man / a man wild-ass
terms.standardEnglish: a wild-ass of a man (ASV); a wild ass of a man (KJV); a wild ass of a man (NRSV); like a wild ass (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: a wild-ass of a man (ASV-baseline; pere-pastoralist register and downstream-receptions carried in apparatus)
terms.rationale:
**The phrase *pere adam* at Gen 16:12 is a hapax compound — a unique construct phrase in the Hebrew Bible.** *V-hu yihyeh pere adam* — *and he shall be a wild-ass of a man* — combines the noun *pere* (wild-ass, Equus hemionus, the Asiatic onager) with *adam* (man) in an apparently appositional construction (the precise grammatical relation is debated; see below). The phrase introduces the Ishmael oracle (v 12 in full: *and he shall be a wild-ass of a man; his hand shall be against every man, and every man's hand against him; and he shall dwell over against all his brothers*). The translation choice is editorially significant: the receptions of the phrase (Islamic, Orientalist, racializing) are downstream of the Hebrew text and must be carefully distinguished from the philological-level reading.

**The *pere* lexeme.** *Pere* (פֶּרֶא) is the standard biblical designation of the wild ass — Equus hemionus, the Asiatic onager, the standard biblical symbol of untameable wilderness-freedom outside human domestication. The longest *pere* passage is **Job 39:5-8** (the *pere* who *laughs at the multitude of the city / hears not the shouting of the driver / the range of the mountains is his pasture*), where the wild-ass is the YHWH-speech's exemplar of created creature beyond human management. Cross-corpus *pere* distribution: **Gen 16:12** (here); **Jer 2:24** (Israel as a *pere limudah midbar* — *a wild-ass accustomed to the wilderness*, in heat); **Jer 14:6** (the wild-asses snuffing wind in drought); **Hos 8:9** (Ephraim as a *pere boded lo* — *a wild ass alone for itself* — in the Assyrian-tribute context); **Job 6:5, 11:12, 24:5, 39:5**; **Ps 104:11**; **Isa 32:14**. The lexeme's semantic core is *wild, free, untamed, of the wilderness, outside human ownership and management*. The wild-ass is not the donkey (*chamor*) — the domesticated species — but its undomesticated cousin.

**Two readings of the phrase.** The compound *pere adam* admits at least two readings, both defensible from the Hebrew. (a) **Descriptive of mode-of-life**: Ishmael will live as a wild, free, untamed pastoralist of the wilderness, not as a settled agriculturalist. The reading takes the *pere* as positive desert-freedom imagery: the wild-ass is *not* a negative symbol of belligerence but the standard biblical positive symbol of wilderness-pastoralist autonomy. The reading is supported by the v 12 closing clause *al-pnei kol echav yishkon* — *over against all his brothers shall he dwell* — which on the geographical reading places Ishmael in the wilderness east/south of the settled patriarchal land, the location of the historical Ishmaelite tribal confederation (see *yishmael-name-etymology* entry on Knauf 1989). The reading is mainstream in modern critical scholarship: **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36*, 1985, pp. 245-246), **Sarna** (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 121), **Wenham** (*WBC Genesis 16-50*, 1994, p. 12), and **Hamilton** (*NICOT Genesis 1-17*, 1990, pp. 454-455) all read (a) as primary. The wild-ass is the *vehicle of the metaphor*, not its negative valence.

(b) **Descriptive of conflict-disposition**: the second clause of v 12 (*his hand shall be against every man and every man's hand against him*) frames the *pere adam* as figure of hostility — Ishmael will live in perpetual conflict with his neighbors. This reading takes the *pere* as a negative symbol of belligerence rather than as a positive symbol of wilderness-freedom. The reading is supported by the structure of the verse (the wild-ass image is *followed by* the hand-against-hand formula, suggesting elaboration rather than contrast). However, the reading is *not* the modern critical consensus, and was historically associated with Orientalist racializing receptions (see below) that read the verse as a divine prophecy of Arab belligerence. Modern critical scholarship reads (b) as the elaboration of the wilderness-pastoralist mode-of-life of (a) — the hand-against-hand language is the standard description of nomadic-pastoralist relations with settled neighbors, not a moral condemnation.

**The *al-pnei kol echav yishkon* crux.** The verse's closing clause is grammatically ambiguous. *Al-pnei* (עַל־פְּנֵי) can mean either *before / east of* (geographical, locating Ishmael east or alongside his brothers) or *in defiance of / in the face of* (relational, locating Ishmael in conflict with his brothers). The ASV *before the face of all his brethren* preserves the ambiguity (the English *before* is itself spatial-ambiguous between *in front of* and *east of*); some modern translations resolve to one or the other (NRSV *over against*; NJPS *alongside of all his kinsmen*; Alter 2004 *in despite of all his kin*; JPS 1917 *in the presence of all his brethren*). The grammatical evidence allows both readings; the geographical *east of* reading is supported by the Ishmaelite tribal-geography of Gen 25:18 (the Ishmaelites *dwell from Havilah unto Shur ... over against all his brethren shall he fall* — using the same *al-pnei* idiom in a clearly geographical sense). The translation preserves the ASV-style ambiguity (*over against*) and flags the crux in commentary.

**Downstream Islamic reception.** The verse has a major reception in Islamic tradition. The Quran identifies Ishmael (*Isma'il*) as the ancestor of the Arabs and as the firstborn-prophet-son of Ibrahim (Abraham); the *pere adam* phrasing is received in the Islamic tradition not as a negative prophecy but as a *positive* image of the desert-freedom of the Arab pastoralist heritage. **Sura 14:39** (Abraham's prayer of thanks for Ishmael and Isaac); **Sura 19:54-55** (Ishmael as a prophet); **Sura 2:125-129** (Abraham and Ishmael at the Ka'ba). The early-Islamic tafsir tradition reads Ishmael's wilderness-mode-of-life as the dignified ancestry of the Arab tribes. The reception is *flagged but not preempted*: it is downstream of the Hebrew text and belongs to Islamic interpretive tradition, not to the philological reading of Gen 16:12. **Genesis Rabbah** on Ishmael (Gen Rab 45:9-11, 53:11, 71:9) collects a range of Jewish-midrashic readings, some valorizing and some critical; the rabbinic tradition is itself not monolithic.

**Downstream Orientalist reception, flagged for editorial caution.** The 19th- and 20th-century Orientalist scholarly tradition (Renan, Wellhausen at his worst moments, the colonial-era ethnographic literature) read *pere adam* as a divine prophecy of Arab belligerence and used the reading to underwrite racializing characterizations of Arab peoples. The reading is *not* in the Hebrew text — the Hebrew text presents the wilderness-pastoralist mode-of-life of an ancestral figure, not a racial prophecy — and modern critical scholarship explicitly rejects the racializing receptions (Said *Orientalism*, 1978; Knauf 1989; Hamori 2008). **This glossary entry must not ship implicit racialization.** The Ishmael oracle is the ancestor-oracle of a pastoralist tribal confederation, not a divine sanction of any modern racial-political claim. The downstream Orientalist receptions are named here only to flag them for the editorial record and to clarify that the *direct* reading of the Hebrew does not support them.

**Modern scholarly literature.** **Knauf** (*Ismael*, 1989) reconstructs the historical Ishmaelite confederation. **Sarna** (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, Excursus 13 *Ishmael*) provides a balanced philological-and-historical treatment. **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36*, 1985, pp. 245-247) reads the verse as the ancestor-oracle of the wilderness-tribal-pastoralist mode-of-life. **Hamori** (*When Gods Were Men*, 2008) addresses the wider anthropomorphic-divine-encounter context. **Said** (*Orientalism*, 1978) is the foundational critique of the Orientalist receptions and is the standard reference for the editorial caution.

**The WoH translation choice.** The translation preserves ASV's *a wild-ass of a man* and *and he shall dwell over against all his brothers* — both ambiguities preserved. The receptions are flagged at the apparatus level. Project-specific synthesis is *not* in this entry: the entry presents the philological-historical material and explicitly distances the WoH project from any racializing reception of the verse. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the lexemes *pere* and *adam* are grammatically explicit; (b) the cross-corpus distribution of *pere* is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the *al-pnei* dual-sense is grammatically established; (d) Knauf's historical reconstruction is documentary-evidence-based; (e) the modern critical reading is Westermann/Sarna/Wenham/Hamilton consensus; (f) the editorial-caution flag on Orientalist receptions is the modern post-Said scholarly standard.

terms.source: אַתָּ֖ה אֵ֣ל רֳאִ֑י ... הֲגַ֥ם הֲלֹ֛ם רָאִ֖יתִי אַחֲרֵ֥י רֹאִֽי
terms.translit: atah El Ro'i ... ha-gam halom ra'iti acharei ro'i
terms.strongs: H410 (El), H7210 (ro'i / ra'ah), H1988 (halom), H430 (Elohim, variant)
terms.literal: You are El of seeing / God who sees ... have I even here seen after the one who sees me?
terms.standardEnglish: Thou art a God that seeth ... Have I even here looked after him that seeth me? (ASV)
terms.wohChoice: You are El Ro'i ... Have I even here looked after him that sees me? (transliterated divine name; ASV-rendering of the opaque second clause; MT *halom* preserved over the *Elohim* emendation)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 16:13 contains two distinct philological items of editorial significance.** First, the divine name *El Ro'i* — אֵל רֳאִי — which Hagar bestows on the speaker of the preceding *mal'akh YHWH* oracle. Second, the textual crux at the second clause: the Masoretic Text reads *halom* (here / in this place), while the LXX and some scholars emend to *Elohim* (God). The translation choice on each item is editorially significant and the two items are entangled in scholarly treatment.

**Part 1: El Ro'i, the first divine-name-naming by a woman in the Hebrew Bible.** *Va-tiqra shem-YHWH ha-dover eleha atah El Ro'i* — *and she called the name of YHWH who spoke to her: You are El Ro'i*. The verse is the first occurrence in the Hebrew Bible of a divine-name-naming by a *woman*, and by a *non-Israelite* (Egyptian) woman at that. The convention of divine-name-bestowal by patriarchs is well-developed in Genesis: Abram names *YHWH El Olam* at Gen 21:33; Hagar's act at 16:13 anticipates this pattern and *precedes* it in the narrative-chronological order. **Trible** (*Texts of Terror*, 1984, pp. 9-35) and **Reis** (*Reading the Lines*, 2002) read the Hagar narrative as theologically load-bearing exactly because of this first-divine-name-naming feature. The name *El Ro'i* combines the divine name *El* (אֵל) with the participial-or-nominal form *Ro'i* (רֳאִי) from r-ʾ-h (to see). The two main grammatical readings are: (i) *El who sees / God of seeing* (the participial reading, with *Ro'i* as participle of r-ʾ-h with first-person suffix — *the one who sees me*); (ii) *El of my seeing / God whom I have seen* (the nominal reading, with *ro'i* as a verbal noun + suffix). BDB and HALOT cite both possibilities; the surrounding context (the second clause's *acharei ro'i*) is consistent with either reading. **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36*, 1985, p. 247) and **Sarna** (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 122) read primarily as *El who sees / God who sees me*; **Speiser** (*Anchor Bible Genesis*, 1964, pp. 117-118) reads as *El of vision / El of the vision*.

**Part 2: the *halom* / *Elohim* textual variant.** The second clause of v 13 — *ha-gam halom ra'iti acharei ro'i* — is one of the most opaque clauses in the Pentateuch and the modern translations diverge sharply. The MT reads *halom* (הֲלֹם — *here, hither, in this place*), giving on a literal reading *have I even here (or: even still) seen after the one who sees me?* The ASV reads *Have I even here looked after him that seeth me?*; NJPS *Have I not gone on seeing after He saw me!*; Alter 2004 *Did I indeed go on seeing here after He had seen me?* preserving the MT. The emended reading replaces *halom* with *Elohim* (אֱלֹהִים — God), giving *have I really seen God and remained alive after seeing him?* — a reading that parallels the seeing-God-and-living anxiety formula at **Exod 33:20** (*lo yir'ani ha-adam va-chai* — *no man shall see me and live*), **Judg 6:22-23** (Gideon's anxiety after seeing the *mal'akh YHWH*: *oy adonai YHWH ki al-ken ra'iti mal'akh YHWH panim el-panim*), and **Judg 13:22** (Manoah's anxiety: *mot namut ki Elohim ra'inu* — *we shall surely die for we have seen Elohim*). The emendation is supported by some LXX witnesses (the LXX reading *enopion eidon ophthenta moi* preserves an *Elohim*-or-*panim*-style underlying text), the Vulgate, and Speiser AB, NRSV, and Westermann. The MT reading is preserved by ASV, NJPS, Alter 2004, JPS 1985, NIV, and the modern critical defenders of the MT-priority position.

**The scholarly distribution.** Modern critical scholarship is genuinely divided on the *halom* / *Elohim* question. (a) **MT-priority position**: Sarna (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 122) reads the MT *halom* and explicitly rejects the emendation; the MT is the longest-attested reading and the LXX evidence is itself ambiguous (the LXX may have read a paraphrastic Hebrew or may have misread *halom*). Alter (2004) preserves the MT. (b) **Emendation position**: Westermann (*Genesis 12-36*, 1985, pp. 247-248) prefers the *Elohim* emendation on the grounds of (i) the parallels at Exod 33:20, Judg 6:22-23, Judg 13:22; (ii) the LXX/Vulgate underlying text; (iii) the difficulty of the MT *halom* in context. Speiser AB and NRSV follow Westermann. (c) **Agnostic position**: Wenham (*WBC Genesis 16-50*, 1994, pp. 12-13) presents both possibilities and declines to commit.

**The WoH choice: MT preserved.** Consistent with the established practice across the WoH Genesis translation (cf. the chapter-15 Tzeboim ketiv/qere resolution), the WoH translation preserves the Masoretic Text reading. The *halom* is rendered with ASV's *Have I even here looked after him that sees me?* — preserving the opacity of the MT in the English. The emendation is flagged in commentary at v 13 and in this glossary entry but not adopted. The choice is editorially conservative: the MT is the canonical text of Jewish-and-Christian scriptural tradition, and the *Elohim*-emendation, while defensible, is itself a scholarly reconstruction. The WoH preserves the MT and lets the dispute be carried in apparatus.

**Part 3: implications for the messenger-identity question (cross-link to *malakh-yhwh-messenger* entry).** Verse 13 is the chapter's climactic theological moment, regardless of the *halom*/*Elohim* choice. Hagar identifies the speaker — who has been narrated as *mal'akh YHWH* throughout vv 7-12 — as YHWH himself (*va-tiqra shem-YHWH ha-dover eleha*). The identification supports position (b) of the messenger-identity question (the *mal'akh* IS YHWH), but does not preempt positions (a) or (c). On position (a) Hagar is *mistaking* the messenger for YHWH; on position (b) Hagar is *correctly identifying* the figure; on position (c) the alternation between *mal'akh YHWH* and YHWH is the literary-theological device's signal that Hagar's identification is part of the device. The chapter does not adjudicate; the *malakh-yhwh-messenger* entry presents the three positions and lets the apparatus carry them.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Westermann 1985, Sarna 1989, Wenham 1994, Speiser 1964 (all on the textual crux); Trible 1984, Reis 2002 (on the gendered-naming feature); Hamori 2008 (on the wider anthropomorphic-divine-encounter context, including the seeing-God-and-living formulae); MacDonald 2009 (on the diversity of divine-encounter conventions). // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the divine name *El Ro'i* is grammatically explicit in the MT; (b) the *halom*/*Elohim* textual variant is documentary-attested in the MT vs LXX/Vulgate witnesses; (c) the seeing-God-and-living formula corpus is verifiable by cross-corpus lexical search; (d) the MT-priority editorial choice is consistent with the WoH project's established practice and is one of the two mainstream modern critical positions.

terms.literal: well of the Living One who sees me / well of the living one of my seeing
terms.standardEnglish: Beer-lahai-roi (ASV/KJV); the well Beer-lahai-roi (NRSV); Beer-lahai-roi (NJPS)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 16:14 names the well at which the *mal'akh YHWH* encountered Hagar.** *Al-ken qara la-b'er be'er Lachai Ro'i hineh vein-Qadesh u-vein Bared* — *therefore the well was called Be'er Lachai Ro'i; behold, it is between Qadesh and Bered*. The well-name is a tripartite construct chain: *Be'er* (well) + *Lachai* (of the Living One / of the Living) + *Ro'i* (of seeing me / who sees me). The name commemorates the v 13 divine-encounter and the v 13 divine-name *El Ro'i* (see *el-roi-and-halom-elohim-variant* entry).

**The grammatical analysis.** The middle element *Lachai* (לַחַי) combines the preposition *l-* (to, for, of) with *Chai* (alive, living, Living One). The combination *Lachai* is morphologically straightforward (*l-* + *chai*) but the divine-epithet *El Chai* (Living God) recurs across the Hebrew Bible — Josh 3:10, 1 Sam 17:26, 17:36, 2 Kgs 19:4, 19:16, Ps 42:3, 84:3, Hos 2:1 — and the *Lachai* element of the well-name is naturally read as preserving a Living-God epithet linked to *El Ro'i* of v 13. The third element *Ro'i* (רֹאִי) is the participle-or-verbal-noun of r-ʾ-h with first-person suffix, the same form that appears in v 13's divine name. The full reading is *well of the Living One who sees me*, a compact commemoration of the Hagar-encounter (the YHWH who saw Hagar; Hagar who saw YHWH; the well at the site of mutual seeing).

**Cross-corpus recurrence.** The well recurs at two later patriarchal verses: **Gen 24:62** (*v-Yitzchaq ba mi-bo Be'er Lachai Ro'i* — *and Isaac came from going to Beer-Lachai-Roi*); **Gen 25:11** (*v-yeshev Yitzchaq im Be'er Lachai Ro'i* — *and Isaac dwelt by Beer-Lachai-Roi* after Abraham's death). The well is a fixed point in the patriarchal geography and a recurring residence-site for Isaac. The recurrence at 25:11 is editorially significant: Isaac settles at the well of the Hagar-encounter, an inter-narrative connection that the Genesis editor leaves implicit rather than commenting on. **Sarna** (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 122) notes the recurrence; **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36*, 1985, p. 248) reads the recurrence as part of the Hagar-Isaac structural pairing across the Abraham cycle.

**The patriarchal well-naming pattern.** The Hebrew Bible's patriarchal narratives contain a developed convention of well-naming. Cross-corpus pattern: **Gen 21:31** *Be'er-Sheva* — *well of the seven / well of the oath* — named after Abraham's covenant with Abimelech; **Gen 26:20-22** *Eseq / Sitnah / Rechovot* — three wells named by Isaac after the disputes with the Philistines (contention, enmity, broad places); **Gen 26:33** *Shiv'ah / Be'er-Sheva again* — the well at which Isaac and Abimelech swore the second covenant; **Exod 15:23** *Marah* — bitter water; **Num 21:16-18** *Be'er* — the well of the wilderness song. The pattern is consistent: a well is named after an event, a person, an act, or a property, and the name fixes the site in narrative memory. *Be'er Lachai Ro'i* is the first well-naming of the patriarchal cycle (preceding *Be'er-Sheva*) and is the only one named after a divine-encounter (the others are named after human events).

**The geography.** The text locates the well *between Qadesh and Bered* (בֵּין־קָדֵשׁ וּבֵין־בָּרֶד). *Qadesh* is identifiable with Kadesh-Barnea (modern Ain Qudeirat in the north-eastern Sinai), the well-attested wilderness oasis on the southern boundary of the patriarchal land (Num 13:26, 20:1, Deut 1:46, etc.). *Bered* is unidentified — the name occurs nowhere else in the Hebrew Bible — and various proposals have been made (Wadi Umm el-Bared, various sites in the Negev). The geographical placement in the Sinai/Negev wilderness, on the route to Egypt (consistent with Hagar's flight back toward her homeland), is direct documentary evidence for a wilderness-oasis tradition.

**The WoH translation choice.** The well-name is transliterated as *Be'er Lachai Ro'i*, matching the chapter's transliteration convention (Yishma'el, Qadesh, El Ro'i). The Anglicized *Beer-lahai-roi* (ASV) is the conventional English but loses the construct-chain structure visible in the transliteration. The toponyms are likewise transliterated (*Qadesh*, *Bered*).

**Foreign-woman-cultic-site register.** A small but theologically interesting feature: *Be'er Lachai Ro'i* is the first patriarchal cultic-site named for a divine-encounter, and it is named by an *Egyptian foreign woman* in the narrative. The pattern of foreign-woman-cultic-site-naming is rare in the Hebrew Bible (Ruth at Bethlehem is a non-cultic case; Tamar at Enaim is geographical, not cultic; Zipporah's *chatan damim* at Exod 4:24-26 is a personal-naming, not a site-naming). The Hagar/Be'er-Lachai-Roi case stands as nearly unique. The lens-relevant material — that a foreign woman's encounter establishes the first patriarchal divine-encounter cultic-site — is flagged here but not editorialized. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the well-name is grammatically explicit; (b) the cross-corpus recurrence at Gen 24:62 and 25:11 is verifiable; (c) the patriarchal well-naming pattern is documentarily attested across Genesis and Exodus; (d) the Qadesh-Bered geographical placement is direct biblical-geography material.

terms.literal: El of Shaddai / El the Shaddai
terms.standardEnglish: God Almighty (ASV / KJV / NIV; LXX *pantokrator*; Vulgate *omnipotens*)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 17:1 is the first occurrence of *El Shaddai* in the Hebrew Bible.** The verse stages a formal divine self-introduction (*ani El Shaddai* — *I am El Shaddai*), the first such *ani*-formula in the patriarchal narratives, and the divine name behind the P-document's programmatic patriarchal-name claim at Exod 6:3. The compound joins the West-Semitic high-god name *El* (אֵל) with the epithet *Shaddai* (שַׁדַּי) of disputed etymology. The translation preserves the transliterated form, matching the WoH practice with *El Elyon* at Gen 14:22 and *El Ro'i* at Gen 16:13, and rejects the ASV/Vulgate *God Almighty* rendering — which silently encodes the *shadad* etymology and collapses the West-Semitic *El*-compound into a generic divine title.

**Three modern critical etymologies stand in parallel and none is consensus.**

(i) **From *shadad* — *destroy, be powerful, overpower*.** The verbal root *shadad* (שָׁדַד) yields a noun *shadday* read as *the Mighty One / the Destroyer*. This is the traditional reading, the etymology behind LXX *pantokrator* (Almighty), Vulgate *omnipotens*, and the ASV / KJV / NIV / NRSV *Almighty*. The reading is grammatically defensible from the root but is not the modern critical preference. The *shadad* lexeme is well-attested in the prophetic-violence corpus (Isa 13:6, 15:1, 21:2; Jer 4:13, 6:26; Joel 1:15; etc.) but its connection to the divine epithet *Shaddai* is morphologically strained — the standard formation would yield *shoded* (active participle) or *shadud* (passive), not *shadday*.

(ii) **From Akkadian *šadû* — *mountain*.** The Akkadian noun *šadû* (mountain) yields *šaddāyu* (mountain-dweller / the one of the mountain), giving *El Shaddai* as *El of the mountain* — a West-Semitic high-god title placing the deity at the cosmic mountain. The reading is developed by **Albright** (*JBL* 54, 1935, pp. 173-204, 'The Names Shaddai and Abram') and **Cross** (*Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic*, 1973, pp. 52-60), and is supported by the wider West-Semitic mountain-god type (Baal of Mount Zaphon, El at his cosmic mountain in the Ugaritic corpus). The geographic-cultic distribution of the name in the patriarchal corpus (the patriarchs cross between Bethel, Mamre, and Beersheba — all high places) is consistent with the mountain-god reading. The Akkadian etymology is the most widely adopted in modern critical scholarship, though not without challenge.

(iii) **From a West-Semitic divine epithet *Šaddai*.** The reading takes *Shaddai* as an independent West-Semitic divine name or class-epithet, attested independently of the Hebrew Bible. The strongest documentary evidence is the **Deir ʿAlla inscription** (Jordan, c. 800 BCE), which preserves *shdyn* — *the shaddayin*, a class of divine beings paralleling the *elohim*. The Deir ʿAlla *shdyn* is direct documentary evidence for an independent West-Semitic *Šaddai* tradition outside the Hebrew Bible. **Ouellette** (*RB* 76, 1969, 'Le pluriel d'EL et de Shadday') develops the class-epithet reading; **Hess** (*Israelite Religions*, 2007, pp. 142-145) summarizes the evidence and presents the Deir ʿAlla parallel. On this reading, *El Shaddai* is the merging of the West-Semitic high god *El* with a separately attested divine-class title *Šaddai*, giving *El (the one) of the Shaddai-class*. The class-epithet reading is the strongest of the three on direct epigraphic evidence.

**The P-document harmonization at Exod 6:3.** *Va-era el-Avraham el-Yitzchaq v-el-Ya'aqov b-El Shaddai u-shemi YHWH lo nodati lahem* — *I appeared to Avraham, to Yitzchaq, and to Ya'aqov as El Shaddai, but by my name YHWH I was not known to them*. This is the P-document's programmatic source-critical claim: the patriarchs knew the deity only as *El Shaddai*; the disclosure of the YHWH name belongs to the Moses-revelation at Exod 3 and 6. The claim is editorially deliberate and is the canonical source-critical justification for the P-document's patriarchal-narrative divine-name register. The Gen 17 narrative is the canonical occurrence of the *El Shaddai* self-disclosure.

**Cross-corpus distribution.** *El Shaddai* recurs at six patriarchal verses, all in P or P-adjacent material: **Gen 17:1** (the covenant of circumcision, the canonical inaugural occurrence); **Gen 28:3** (Isaac's blessing of Jacob: *v-El Shaddai yevarekh otkha*); **Gen 35:11** (the Bethel renaming: *ani El Shaddai pere u-rveh*); **Gen 43:14** (Jacob's send-off of Benjamin to Egypt: *v-El Shaddai yiten lakhem rachamim*); **Gen 48:3** (Jacob's blessing of Joseph's sons: *El Shaddai nira elay b-Luz*); **Gen 49:25** (Jacob's blessing of Joseph: *El Shaddai vi-yvarekhekha*). The standalone *Shaddai* (without *El*) recurs heavily in Job (31 occurrences — see the *shaddai-eloah* glossary entry which covers the Job register specifically), at Num 24:4, 24:16 (Balaam oracles), Ruth 1:20-21 (Naomi's complaint), Ps 68:15, 91:1, Isa 13:6, Joel 1:15, Ezek 1:24, 10:5.

**Cross-reference with the existing *shaddai-eloah* entry.** The earlier glossary entry *shaddai-eloah* (v2.6.0) covers the Job-register occurrences of *Shaddai* as part of the book of Job's distinctive patriarchal-Aramean divine-name vocabulary (Eloah + Shaddai + El, with limited YHWH-Elohim use). The present *el-shaddai* entry covers the patriarchal-narrative occurrences of the *El Shaddai* compound proper. The two entries together govern the full Hebrew Bible distribution of the name.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Albright 1935 *JBL* 54; Cross 1973 *Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic*; Ouellette 1969 *RB* 76; Hess 2007 *Israelite Religions*; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989, pp. 122-124; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 256-260; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, pp. 19-21; Friedman *Who Wrote the Bible?* 1987, pp. 169-178 (on the P-document framework); Knohl *The Sanctuary of Silence* 1995 (on the P/H stratification).

**The WoH translation choice.** *El Shaddai* is preserved in transliteration. The conventional English *God Almighty* (ASV / KJV / NIV) collapses the West-Semitic *El*-compound into a generic divine title and silently encodes the *shadad* etymology that the modern critical scholarship has largely abandoned. The transliteration preserves the source-critical question (the etymology is genuinely unresolved) and matches the WoH chapter convention for *El*-compounds. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the divine name is grammatically explicit in the Masoretic Text and is a documentary fact of the Hebrew Bible; (b) the P-document harmonization at Exod 6:3 is documentary-attested; (c) the cross-corpus distribution across the six patriarchal occurrences is verifiable by lexical search; (d) the three modern critical etymologies are mainstream scholarly positions, none of which is project-specific; (e) the Deir ʿAlla *shdyn* epigraphic evidence is documentary.

terms.literal: walk-yourself before me and be whole/blameless/complete
terms.standardEnglish: walk before me, and be thou perfect (ASV / KJV); be wholehearted (NJPS); be blameless (NRSV / NIV)
terms.wohChoice: walk before me, and be you blameless
terms.rationale:
**The covenant-register stock formula at Gen 17:1.** The imperative pair *hithallekh lefanai veheyeh tamim* — *walk before me and be blameless* — opens the *El Shaddai* covenant-disclosure with a programmatic statement of expected covenantal conduct. The phrase combines two distinct but interrelated lexical-grammatical items: (i) the hithpael of *halakh* (walk) with the preposition *lefanai* (before me / in my presence); (ii) the nominal predicate *tamim* (whole / complete / unblemished / blameless). The combination is a covenant-ethical stock formula and recurs across the Hebrew Bible.

**The *hithallekh lefanai* / walking-before-God lexeme.** The hithpael of *halakh* (הָלַךְ) carries iterative-reflexive sense: not a single act of walking but the habitual conduct of one's life. The construction *hithallekh* + *lefanai* (or *et*) + divine name is the canonical Hebrew Bible figure for habitual conduct of life in the divine presence. Cross-corpus distribution: **Gen 5:22, 5:24** of Enoch (*va-yithallekh Chanokh et ha-Elohim* — *and Enoch walked with the Elohim*); **Gen 6:9** of Noah (*et-ha-Elohim hithallekh Noach* — *Noah walked with the Elohim*); **Gen 17:1** (here, of Avraham); **Gen 24:40** (Avraham's retrospective self-description: *YHWH asher hithallakhti lefanav*); **1 Sam 2:30** (the priestly-house promise: *beit-avikha yithalkhu lefanai ad-olam*); **1 Sam 2:35** (the faithful-priest replacement: *yithallekh lifne meshichi*); **1 Kgs 2:4, 3:6, 8:23, 8:25, 9:4** (the Davidic covenantal conduct formula); **2 Kgs 20:3 / Isa 38:3** (Hezekiah's appeal: *hithallakhti lefanekha*); **Ps 56:14, 116:9** (the worshipper's conduct in the land of the living). The formula's load-bearing function is to name a class of figures whose habitual life-conduct is *before* / *with* the deity — Enoch, Noah, Avraham, David, the faithful priests.

**The *tamim* lexeme.** *Tamim* (תָּמִים) is the standard Hebrew Bible adjective for *whole, complete, unblemished, blameless*. Its primary register is sacrificial: the unblemished animal of Exod 12:5 (the Passover lamb *seh tamim*), Lev 1:3 (the *olah*: *zakhar tamim*), and across the Leviticus sacrificial code. The covenant-ethical extension applies the same term to the human covenantal subject: **Gen 6:9** of Noah (*ish tzadik tamim hayah b-dorotav* — *a righteous man, blameless in his generations*); **Gen 17:1** (here); **Deut 18:13** (*tamim tihye im YHWH Elohekha* — *blameless you shall be with YHWH your God*, the legal-formula closing of the Deuteronomic divination prohibition); **Josh 24:14** (Joshua's covenant-renewal: *iv'du oto b-tamim u-v-emet*); **2 Sam 22:24 / Ps 18:24** (David's psalm: *va-ehiye tamim imo*); **Ps 15:2** (the gate-liturgy: *holekh tamim u-fo'el tzedek*); **Ps 18:33** (David: *ha-El ha-m-azreni chayil va-yiten tamim darki*); **Ps 101:2, 101:6** (David's house-conduct formula); **Ps 119:1** (the Torah psalm: *ashrei t'mimei darekh*); **Prov 11:5, 11:20, 20:7, 28:18** (the wisdom corpus). The lexeme's semantic core is *integrity, wholeness, completeness in conduct* — covenantal integrity, not moral perfectionism in the later Christian-theological sense.

**The ASV *perfect* and the moral-perfectionism mis-reading.** ASV's *be thou perfect* preserves the King James lineage but invites the modern English connotation of *moral perfection*. The Hebrew *tamim* does not carry the moral-perfectionism sense; it carries the *integrity / wholeness / unblemished-completeness* sense. NJPS *be wholehearted* is closer to the Hebrew but loses the unblemished-completeness register; NRSV / NIV *be blameless* is the modern critical consensus rendering and preserves the legal-ethical register without the moral-perfectionism overtones. The translation adopts *blameless*.

**The covenantal speech-act structure.** The Gen 17:1 formula is grammatically two imperatives in series (*hithallekh* + *heyeh*), structurally framing the El-Shaddai self-disclosure with an immediate covenantal demand. The pattern recurs in Hebrew-Bible covenant-disclosure passages: the disclosure of the divine party is paired with the conduct-demand on the human party. The chapter-17 instance is the canonical patriarchal example.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989, p. 124; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 257-258; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, p. 20; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004, pp. 80-81. The covenantal-conduct formula treatment in Levenson *Sinai and Zion* 1985 and Mendenhall 1955 on the broader ANE covenant-formulary background. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the lexemes *hithallekh* and *tamim* are grammatically explicit; (b) the cross-corpus distribution of both lexemes is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the covenantal-conduct formula is documented in the standard commentaries; (d) the *tamim* / *blameless* over *perfect* rendering matches the modern critical consensus (Sarna, Westermann, NRSV, NIV).

terms.literal: and let me give / set / put my-brit
terms.standardEnglish: and I will make my covenant (ASV / ESV / NRSV); I will set my covenant
terms.wohChoice: and I will make my covenant
terms.rationale:
**Gen 17:2's *v-etnah briti* uses the *natan brit* covenant-verb idiom — distinct from the more common *karat brit* and *heqim brit*.** The Hebrew Bible deploys at least four distinct verbs in idiomatic combination with *brit* (covenant), and the chapter-17 cluster uses three of them in close textual proximity: *natan brit* (v 2), *heqim brit* (vv 7, 19, 21; see *meqim-brit* entry), and the implicit covenant-relation of *zot briti* (v 10). The verb-selection is precise and the chapter is using the cluster as a deliberate technical-vocabulary range.

**The four covenant-verb idioms.**

(i) **Karat brit** (כָּרַת בְּרִית — *cut a covenant*). The most common Hebrew covenant-verb idiom, ~80 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible; the inaugural patriarchal occurrence at Gen 15:18 stages the cut-covenant ritual with the divided animals. See the existing *karat-brit-cut-covenant* glossary entry. The idiom carries the ritual-inaugural register: a covenant is *cut* at its institution.

(ii) **Heqim brit** (הֵקִים בְּרִית — *establish / raise up a covenant*). The hiphil-causative of *qum*; ~13 occurrences, concentrated in P/H material. The idiom carries the *raise-up / confirm / make-stand* register and is used in P for both the Noachic (Gen 6:18, 9:9, 9:11, 9:17) and Abrahamic (Gen 17:7, 17:19, 17:21; Exod 6:4) covenants. See the *meqim-brit* glossary entry. Some scholars (Weinfeld, Anchor Bible Dictionary 'Covenant') read *heqim brit* as *confirm / ratify an existing arrangement* rather than as *inaugurate*, on the grounds that the verb is used when a covenant is being re-established or carried forward generationally; others (Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989) read the verb as a P-document stylistic variant of *karat brit*.

(iii) **Natan brit** (נָתַן בְּרִית — *give / set / put a covenant*). The verb is *natan* (give, set, place); the cohortative *etnah* at Gen 17:2 is the inaugural patriarchal occurrence. Cross-corpus distribution: **Gen 17:2** (here, *v-etnah briti*); **Num 25:12** (*hineni noten lo et briti shalom* — *behold I am giving him my covenant of peace*, to Phineas after the Baal-Peor zeal); **Isa 42:6** (*v-etenkha li-vrit am* — *I will give you for a covenant of the people*, the servant); **Isa 49:8** (*v-etenkha li-vrit am* — same construction, the servant again); **Jer 31:33** (*v-natati et torati b-qirbam* — though here *Torah* not *brit*). The *natan brit* idiom is rarer than *karat* or *heqim* and tends to occur where the covenant is being *granted as a gift* (Phineas, the servant) rather than *cut at inauguration* (the patriarchs at Gen 15) or *raised up generationally* (the patriarchs at Gen 17:7, 17:19).

(iv) **Zakhar brit** (זָכַר בְּרִית — *remember a covenant*). The verb is *zakhar* (remember). See the existing *vezakharti-briti* glossary entry. The idiom is the covenant-memory-trigger register: at Gen 9:15 the rainbow triggers divine memory; at Exod 2:24 the exodus is triggered by divine memory of the covenant; at Lev 26:42 the future-restoration is staged through divine memory.

**The Gen 17 verb-cluster pattern.** The chapter deploys three of the four covenant-verbs in deliberate succession: v 2 uses *natan* (cohortative *etnah*) for the initial covenant-grant; vv 7, 19, 21 use *heqim* (perfect *vahaqimoti* / imperfect *aqim*) for the covenantal establishment with the patriarchal line; the implicit *zot briti* at v 10 stages the institutional content.
