The reading is supported by (a) the lexeme's load — *za'aqah* is the cry of *victims*, not the cry of perpetrators; (b) the Ezek 16:49 explicit characterization of Sodom's sin: *hineh ze hayah avon Sodom achotekh ga'on sivat-lechem v-shalvat hashqet hayah lah v-livnoteiha v-yad ani v-evyon lo hechziqah* — *behold, this was the iniquity of your sister Sodom: pride, fullness of bread, and prosperous ease was hers and her daughters', but she did not strengthen the hand of the poor and needy*. Ezekiel reads Sodom's sin as the oppression of the poor — exactly the context that generates the *za'aqah* of Gen 18:20. Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 131-132 and Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 287-288 both endorse the *outcry against Sodom* reading.

**The translation.** ASV's *cry* is colorless and loses the forensic edge. NRSV's *outcry* and NJPS's *outcry* preserve the forensic register. The WoH translation reads *outcry* — one of the WoH translation's intentional non-ASV-baseline choices, justified by the lexeme's forensic-judicial load.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989, pp. 131-132; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 287-288; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, pp. 51-52; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, pp. 18-20; Brueggemann *Genesis* (Interpretation) 1982 on the intercession-pericope; HALOT s.v. *zaaq*, *tsaaq*; BDB §2201, §6818. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the lexemes *za'aqah* and *tza'aqah* are grammatically explicit and their lexical core is documentary; (b) the cross-corpus distribution at Gen 4:10, Exod 2-3, Exod 22:22-24, 1 Sam 9:16, etc. is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the *outcry against Sodom* reading is the modern scholarly consensus, corroborated by Ezek 16:49.

terms.partOfSpeech: verb qal cohortative 1cs + particle of entreaty + verb qal cohortative 1cs
terms.standardEnglish: I will go down now and see (ASV / KJV); I will go down to see (NJPS / NRSV / NIV)
terms.wohChoice: I will go down now, and see
terms.rationale:
**Gen 18:21 uses the divine-descent-to-investigate formula.** *Erdah-na v-er'eh ha-k-tza'aqatah ha-ba'ah elai asu kalah v-im-lo eda'ah* — *I will go down now and see whether they have done altogether according to the outcry of it that has come to me; and if not, I will know*. The verb *yarad* (יָרַד — *go down, descend*) in cohortative or imperfect, paired with a verb of seeing (*ra'ah*) or acting, recurs at a defined set of moments in the primeval and patriarchal narratives. The pattern is editorially deliberate and scholarly-recognized: the divine subject is *above*; the human or earthly object of investigation is *below*; the descent is *deliberate*, *spatial*, and motivated by the need to *see for oneself* — to verify the *za'aqah* (cf. the *zaaqah-outcry-of-the-oppressed* entry).

**The canonical instances.**

- **Gen 11:5** at Babel — *va-yered YHWH lir'ot et-ha-ir v-et-ha-migdal asher banu bnei ha-adam* — *and YHWH went down to see the city and the tower which the children of man had built*. The first occurrence of the formula in the Hebrew Bible, framing the Babel narrative as a divine-investigation episode.
- **Gen 11:7** at Babel — *havah neredah v-navlah sham sefatam* — *come, let us go down and confound their language there*. The cohortative plural form (note the plural *neredah* and *navlah*, with the divine-council overtone shared with the Gen 1:26 *na'aseh adam* and the Gen 3:22 *hen ha-adam hayah k-achad mimennu*).
- **Gen 18:21** here — *erdah-na v-er'eh* — *I will go down now and see*. The cohortative singular form; YHWH speaks as the singular speaker after the v 20 explicit naming.
- **Exod 3:8** at the burning bush — *va-ered l-hatzilo m-yad Mitzrayim u-l-ha'aloto min-ha-aretz ha-hi el-eretz tovah u-rchavah* — *and I have come down to deliver them from the hand of Mitzrayim, and to bring them up out of that land to a good and broad land*. The descent here is not investigative but rescue-and-deliverance; the same verb (*yarad*) is used, but the motivating *za'aqah*-investigation is already complete (Exod 3:7 *I have seen the affliction, I have heard the cry*).
- **Exod 19:11, 19:18, 19:20** of the Sinai-descent — *ki ba-yom ha-shlishi yered YHWH l-eynei kol-ha-am al-Har Sinai* (v 11); *va-yered YHWH al-Har Sinai el-rosh ha-har* (v 20). The descent here is for theophanic encounter and law-giving, not investigation.

**The prophetic continuation.**

- **Mic 1:3** — *ki hineh YHWH yotze mim-meqomo v-yarad v-darakh al-bamotei aretz* — *for behold, YHWH comes out of his place, and will come down and tread upon the high places of the earth*. The Micah prophecy of judgment uses the descent-formula directly. The Mic 1:3 verse is one of the canonical prophetic-judgment-descent passages.
- **Isa 64:1 (Heb 63:19)** — *lu qarata shamayim yaradta mi-panekha harim nazolu* — *oh, that you would rend the heavens and come down, that the mountains might flow down at your presence*. The eschatological-descent plea, applied as the prophetic-petition form of the formula.

**Why the pattern.** The formula presupposes a cosmographic vertical axis: the divine subject occupies an *upper* location (*shamayim*, *meromim*, the heavenly council); the earthly affairs to be investigated, judged, or rescued occupy a *lower* location. The descent is spatial — the divine subject moves from above to below — and is presented in concretely-physical terms. The pattern is part of the J-source's *anthropomorphic realism* (cf. the *divine-eating-anthropomorphism* entry and Hamori 2008): YHWH moves through space the way a person moves through space, descending to verify by direct observation.

**Modern scholarly treatment.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989, pp. 132 (Gen 18:21) and 81 (Gen 11:5); Westermann *Genesis 1-11* 1984, pp. 547-548 (the Babel-Sodom parallel); Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 287-288 (Sodom); Niditch *Chaos to Cosmos* 1985 (the cosmographic vertical-axis framework); Hamori *When Gods Were Men* 2008 (the J-source's spatial-anthropomorphism); Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, pp. 51-52; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, pp. 18-20.

**Babel-Sodom parallel.** The verbal parallels between Gen 11:5 and Gen 18:21 are editorially deliberate. Both: (a) a human population on the earth acts in a way that triggers divine attention; (b) YHWH *descends* (*yarad*) to *see* (*ra'ah*); (c) judgment follows. The Babel-Sodom pairing is one of the canonical J-source structural parallelisms (Westermann 1984 / 1985; Sarna 1989). The two episodes structurally bracket the patriarchal narratives' first major prehistory — the Tower-of-Babel divine-investigation of Gen 11 and the Sodom-divine-investigation of Gen 18-19 — and the verbal echo signals the structural connection.

**Anthropomorphic realism — entry-level scholarly register only.** The Gen 18:21 formula is one of the patriarchal narratives' clearest examples of the J-source's *spatial anthropomorphism*: YHWH is not omniscient-from-above but learns by *going down to see*. The reading is preserved in mainstream HB scholarship; the WoH translation makes no further interpretive move beyond the established scholarly register. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the verb *yarad* with the cohortative or imperfect form is grammatically explicit at every cited verse; (b) the verbal parallels between Gen 11:5, Gen 18:21, Exod 3:8 are verbatim or near-verbatim; (c) the divine-descent pattern as a recurring J-source formula is documented in Westermann 1984/1985, Sarna 1989, Niditch 1985, and Hamori 2008.

terms.translit: v-Avraham odenu omed lifnei YHWH (MT) / v-YHWH odenu omed lifnei Avraham (tiqun-attested original)
terms.partOfSpeech: proper noun + adverb + qal participle ms + prepositional phrase + divine name
terms.literal: and-Avraham still standing before YHWH (MT) / and-YHWH still standing before Avraham (tiqun-original)
terms.standardEnglish: Abraham still stood before the LORD (ASV / KJV — MT-following)
terms.wohChoice: Avraham still stood before YHWH (MT preserved)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 18:22 is one of the canonical *tiqune soferim* (scribal-emendation tradition) verses of the Hebrew Bible.** The MT reads *va-yifnu mi-sham ha-anashim va-yelkhu sdomah v-Avraham odenu omed lifnei YHWH* — *and the men turned from there and went toward Sodom; but Avraham still stood before YHWH*. The Masoretic tradition — recorded explicitly in the Massorah Magna's marginal lists, in **b. Megillah 25b**, in **Bereshit Rabbah 49:7**, in **Tanchuma Yashan Beshallach 16**, and in the **Mekhilta de-Rabbi Yishmael** on Exod 15:7 — holds that the *original* text read *v-YHWH odenu omed lifnei Avraham* — *and YHWH still stood before Avraham*. The original was deliberately emended (*kinah ha-katuv* — *Scripture used a euphemism*; the technical formula introducing the *tiqune soferim*) for reverence, because the original depicts YHWH standing in attendance before a human being, which the scribes found theologically uncomfortable. The MT preserves the emended form; the tradition explicitly records the original.

**The standard list of eighteen *tiqune soferim*.** The exact count and contents vary across rabbinic sources — some lists have eight, some eleven, some thirteen, some seventeen, some eighteen — but the eighteen-verse list (the most commonly cited) includes:

- **Gen 18:22** (here)
- **Num 11:15** (*b-ra'ati*  → *b-ra'atam*)
- **Num 12:12** (*imenu* → *imo*; *bsareinu* → *bsaro*)
- **1 Sam 3:13** (*lahem* → *lahem* — vowel/reading variant)
- **2 Sam 16:12** (*b-eyni* → *b-onyi*)
- **2 Sam 20:1** (*l-ohalav* → *l-ohalav*)
- **1 Kgs 12:16** (parallel at 2 Chr 10:16)
- **2 Chr 10:16**
- **Jer 2:11** (*k-vodi* → *k-vodam*)
- **Ezek 8:17**
- **Hos 4:7** (*k-vodi* → *k-vodam*)
- **Hab 1:12** (*lo tamut* → *lo namut*)
- **Zech 2:12 (Heb 2:8)** (*eyni* → *eyno*)
- **Mal 1:13**
- **Ps 106:20** (*k-vodi* → *k-vodam*)
- **Job 7:20** (*alai* → *alai*)
- **Job 32:3**
- **Lam 3:20** (*v-tashiach alai nafshi* → *v-tashoach alai nafshi*)

Gen 18:22 appears in every standard list and is one of the three or four most frequently cited examples. The shared theological logic across the eighteen is the avoidance of either anthropomorphism that would impugn divine dignity, or speech that would associate YHWH with profanity or weakness — the formal scribal-Halakhah of theological reverence applied at specific verses.

**The modern textual-critical position.** Modern scholarship is divided on the *tiqun*-tradition's historical status.

*The conservative-traditional reading.* **McCarthy** (*The Tiqqune Sopherim and Other Theological Corrections in the Masoretic Text of the Old Testament*, OBO 36, 1981) — the canonical monograph on the tradition — treats the tradition as recording *actual* scribal emendations preserved in the Masoretic apparatus. The model is parallel to the *qere/ketiv* (written/read) and the *itture soferim* (scribal omissions): a recognized form of textual transmission with traceable history. The *tiqune soferim* on this reading are part of the formal Masoretic apparatus and reflect a real pre-Masoretic textual situation. McCarthy 1981 is the standard reference work on the topic.

*The skeptical reading.* **Tov** (*Textual Criticism of the Hebrew Bible*, 3rd ed. 2012, §6.B.3, pp. 64-65) and **Würthwein** (*The Text of the Old Testament*, 2nd ed. 1995, pp. 18-19) treat the tradition as a *later rabbinic interpretive flag* retrospectively justifying the MT reading. The tradition records what the rabbis believed the original *should* have been — what they reconstructed from theological-rhetorical considerations — not necessarily what an earlier text *did* read. Tov's argument: no LXX, Samaritan, Qumran, or Targumic witness preserves the *YHWH still stood before Avraham* reading at Gen 18:22; the alternative reading is preserved only in the Masoretic marginal apparatus and the rabbinic *baraitot*. The textual evidence for an actual variant is weak.

*The mediating reading.* The mediating modern position (most contemporary commentaries) holds that whether or not an *actual* variant ever existed in a manuscript, the *tradition's witness* to a theologically-motivated emendation is itself a Masoretic-tradition fact: the scribes (or the rabbis preserving the scribal lore) recognized the original reading as theologically uncomfortable and recorded an alternative. The witness is preserved independent of the question of physical manuscript transmission.

**Yeivin's framework.** **Yeivin** (*Introduction to the Tiberian Masorah*, SBL, 1980) is the standard introduction to the Masoretic apparatus as a whole. Yeivin treats the *tiqune soferim* alongside the other Masoretic apparatus features (the *qere/ketiv*, the *puncta extraordinaria*, the *itture soferim*, the *sevirin*) as components of the Masoretic textual tradition — each carrying distinct scholarly weight but all forming part of the integrated Masoretic apparatus.

**The theological force of the *tiqun*-attested original.** The original reading *v-YHWH odenu omed lifnei Avraham* (*and YHWH still stood before Avraham*) is theologically substantial. The phrase *omed lifnei* (*standing before*) carries the specific connotation of *standing in attendance, standing in service, standing as the petitioner* — the posture of the subordinate before the superior. Examples: 1 Kgs 17:1 (Elijah *omed lifnei* YHWH); 1 Kgs 18:15; Jer 15:19; Zech 3:1 (Joshua the high priest *omed lifnei mal'akh YHWH*). The MT reading places Avraham in this subordinate posture before YHWH (the conventional patriarch-before-deity posture, theologically unproblematic). The *tiqun*-attested original reverses the relation — YHWH in the subordinate-standing posture before Avraham, the human as the petitioned-judge and YHWH as the figure-being-petitioned. The theological force is the inversion of the standard divine-human hierarchy: in the chapter's intercession scene, Avraham becomes (briefly, momentarily) the addressee and YHWH the one-who-stands-in-attendance.

**The verse's narrative logic.** The MT and the *tiqun*-attested original both make narrative sense, but with different theological accents. The MT logic: the men depart for Sodom; Avraham, the petitioner, remains in YHWH's presence to plead for the city. The *tiqun*-attested-original logic: the men depart for Sodom; YHWH remains *with* Avraham (in attendance) while the intercession is conducted. The intercession-pericope (vv 23-33) reads slightly differently in the two cases — in the MT Avraham approaches and pleads with YHWH; in the original YHWH is already in the standing-in-attendance posture, dignifying the intercession with a kind of patience the MT does not articulate.

**The translation.** The WoH translation preserves the MT (*Avraham still stood before YHWH*) per the established WoH textual-conservative practice of following the MT consonantal text. The *tiqun*-attested original is documented in the apparatus.

**Modern scholarly literature.** McCarthy *The Tiqqune Sopherim* (OBO 36) 1981 (the canonical monograph); Tov *Textual Criticism of the Hebrew Bible* 3rd ed. 2012, §6.B.3, pp. 64-65; Würthwein *The Text of the Old Testament* 2nd ed. 1995, pp. 18-19; Yeivin *Introduction to the Tiberian Masorah* 1980; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989, p. 132; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 288-289; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, p. 52; b. Megillah 25b; Bereshit Rabbah 49:7; Tanchuma Yashan Beshallach 16; Mekhilta de-Rabbi Yishmael on Exod 15:7. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the *tiqune soferim* tradition is documentary in the Masoretic apparatus and the rabbinic *baraitot*; (b) the standard list of eighteen *tiqune soferim* including Gen 18:22 is documented across the McCarthy 1981 monograph and the Tov 2012 textual-criticism handbook; (c) the alternative reading and its theological motivation are explicitly preserved in the rabbinic sources cited; (d) the modern critical positions on the historical status of the tradition (McCarthy's recording-actual-emendations reading vs. Tov's retrospective-interpretive-flag reading) are documented in the literature.

terms.source: וַיִּגַּשׁ אַבְרָהָם וַיֹּאמַר ... הֲשֹׁפֵט כָּל־הָאָרֶץ לֹא יַעֲשֶׂה מִשְׁפָּט
terms.translit: va-yigash Avraham va-yomar ... ha-shofet kol ha-aretz lo ya'aseh mishpat
terms.partOfSpeech: verb qal imperfect (waw-consec) + proper noun + interrogative + qal participle (judge) + noun phrase + negation + qal imperfect + noun
terms.literal: and Avraham drew near and said ... shall the judge of all the earth not do justice?
terms.standardEnglish: Abraham drew near, and said ... shall not the Judge of all the earth do justice? (ASV)
terms.wohChoice: Avraham drew near, and said ... shall not the Judge of all the earth do justice?
terms.rationale:
**Gen 18:22-33 is the foundational HB intercession-pericope.** Avraham's *descending-bargaining sequence* — fifty → forty-five → forty → thirty → twenty → ten righteous — is the Hebrew Bible's first and most structurally distinctive intercession scene. The pericope contains two structurally distinct moves: (a) the *foundational-justice-question* at v 25 — *ha-shofet kol ha-aretz lo ya'aseh mishpat* (*shall not the Judge of all the earth do justice?*) — establishing the theological grounds; (b) the *descending-numeric-bargaining* across vv 23-32, working out the principle in casuistic detail. The entry consolidates both elements as a single intercession-pericope; see the related entries *tzedakah-u-mishpat-justice-pair* (v 19), *afar-va-efer-creatural-humility* (v 27), and *zaaqah-outcry-of-the-oppressed* (vv 20-21) for adjacent material.

**The v 25 foundational-justice-question.** *Chalilah l'kha me-asot ka-davar ha-ze l-hamit tzadiq im-rasha v-hayah ka-tzadiq ka-rasha chalilah lakh ha-shofet kol ha-aretz lo ya'aseh mishpat* — *far be it from you to do after this manner, to slay the righteous with the wicked, so that the righteous should be as the wicked; far be it from you: shall not the Judge of all the earth do justice?* The verse establishes three things in one sentence: (1) **universal-judicial-competence** — *shofet kol ha-aretz* (*judge of the whole earth*, not merely of Avraham's lineage, the covenant-people, or any local domain); (2) **moral-coherence requirement** — *lo ya'aseh mishpat?* — a true judge must enact justice consistent with the moral order; (3) **inadmissibility of indiscriminate destruction** — destroying *tzadiq im-rasha* (righteous along with wicked) would violate the moral-coherence requirement.

The rhetorical force of the verse is the *binding* of YHWH to the principle of judicial justice. Avraham does not entertain the possibility that the Judge of all the earth might fail to do *mishpat*; he invokes the principle to *commit* YHWH to act in accordance with it. The rhetorical structure is *binding-by-affirmation*: Avraham affirms that YHWH cannot do other than justice, and then proceeds to test the affirmation by progressively lowering the threshold of righteousness required to spare the city.

**The descending-bargaining sequence.** Verses 23-32 stage the casuistic working-out: 50 (vv 23-26) → 45 (vv 27-28) → 40 (v 29) → 30 (v 30) → 20 (v 31) → 10 (v 32). The structural features are editorially deliberate.

- **The intervals.** The descent is irregular — by 5, then 5, then 10, then 10, then 10. The first two descents (50→45→40) are by 5; the next three (40→30→20→10) are by 10. The acceleration is editorially marked.
- **The threshold endpoint.** The bargaining stops at 10. The chapter does not stage a 5 → 1 continuation. The endpoint is editorially marked as the *minimum* below which the principle is not tested. The traditional rabbinic interpretation (*Bereshit Rabbah* 49:13; cf. *Avoth d-Rabbi Nathan* and the *minyan* tradition) treats 10 as the minyan-threshold — the minimum community required to constitute a *tzibur* (congregation) and thus the minimum collective whose merit can save the surrounding context. The interpretation is post-biblical but illuminates the editorial choice of 10 as the stopping point.
- **The reverence-formulae.** The bargaining is interspersed with formulae of self-deprecation that themselves escalate: v 27 *anokhi afar va-efer* (I am but dust and ashes — see *afar-va-efer-creatural-humility* entry); v 30 *al-na yichar la-Adonai va-adabrah* (let not the Lord be angry, and I will speak); v 31 *hineh-na ho'alti l-daber el-Adonai* (behold, I have taken upon myself to speak to my Lord); v 32 *al-na yichar la-Adonai va-adabrah akh-ha-pa'am* (let not the Lord be angry, and I will speak just this once). The escalation in self-deprecation mirrors the descent in numeric threshold — Avraham increases his rhetorical hedging as the threshold lowers.
- **YHWH's responses.** YHWH's answers are uniformly affirmative and use parallel constructions: *lo eschit / lo asheh ba'avur (number)* — *I will not destroy / I will not do it for the sake of (number)*. The repetition emphasizes that the principle holds at every threshold; YHWH does not refuse a single descent. The bargaining is *successful* at every step.

**Cross-corpus intercession-tradition.** The pericope is the foundational instance of the HB intercession-tradition; the subsequent canonical instances:

- **Exod 32:11-14** — Moses intercedes for Israel after the golden calf. Moses appeals to YHWH's reputation (*lamah yo'mru Mitzrayim* — *why should the Mitzrayim say*), to the patriarchal promises (*zachor l-Avraham l-Yitzchaq u-l-Yisrael avadeykha* — *remember Avraham, Yitzchaq, and Yisrael your servants*); the intercession is successful (*va-yinacheh YHWH al-ha-ra'ah* — *and YHWH repented of the evil*).
- **Num 14:13-20** — Moses intercedes again after the spies' report. The structural parallel to Gen 18: a city or people under threat of destruction; a single human intercessor appealing to divine justice and reputation; a partial mercy granted.
- **1 Sam 12:23** — Samuel's standing-intercession: *gam anokhi chalilah li me-chato la-YHWH me-chadol l-hitpalel ba-adkhem* — *as for me, far be it from me that I should sin against YHWH by ceasing to pray for you*. The *chalilah li* formula directly echoes Gen 18:25's *chalilah l'kha* — the *far-be-it-from* construction.
- **1 Kgs 8:30, 33-50** — Solomon's intercession-prayer at the dedication of the Temple.
- **Jer 7:16, 11:14, 14:11** — YHWH's *forbidding* Jeremiah to intercede (the inverse case: intercession is the prophet's standing role, but is here suspended).
- **Amos 7:1-9** — Amos's intercession against the locust-and-fire visions of judgment (successful in the first two; ceased in the third).
- **Ezek 9:8, 11:13** — Ezekiel's intercession during the visions of judgment: *aha Adonai YHWH ha-kalah atah oseh et-she'erit Yisrael* — *Lord YHWH, will you destroy all the remnant of Yisrael?* Direct verbal echo of Gen 18:23-24.
- **Job 42:8-10** — Job's intercession for the three friends. The structural parallel: a single intercessor pleads for those condemned; the intercession is successful.

The Avraham pericope inaugurates the tradition and provides its theological grounds. The pattern is then taken up by Moses, Samuel, Jeremiah (paradoxically — by being forbidden it), Amos, Ezekiel, and Job; the tradition becomes the canonical Hebrew-Bible prophet-as-intercessor framework.

**Mainstream scholarly treatment.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989, pp. 132-134; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 290-296; Brueggemann *Genesis* (Interpretation) 1982 on the intercession-pericope (the load-bearing modern treatment of the *moral-theological* significance — the verse establishes the principle that the divine itself is subject to moral law); Brueggemann *Theology of the Old Testament* 1997, pp. 384-389 (the *anti-divine-injustice* prophetic register); Childs *Old Testament Theology in a Canonical Context* 1985 on intercession as a theological category; Alter *Genesis* 1996, pp. 81-84 (the literary-rhetorical climax-of-mercy device); Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, pp. 52-56; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, pp. 21-30.

**The theological-rhetorical significance.** The Gen 18 intercession is the Hebrew Bible's foundational articulation of the principle that the divine is bound by justice — that *mishpat* applies to YHWH as well as to human judges. The principle reverses the standard divine-arbitrariness model of much ANE religion: the Hebrew Bible's God is not free to do anything; the Hebrew Bible's God is bound by the *mishpat* that the God himself enacts as judge of all the earth. The principle is then taken up by the prophetic tradition as the *anti-divine-injustice* register and becomes one of the foundations of HB theological ethics. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the pericope's structure (the descending numeric sequence; the v 25 question; the reverence-formulae) is grammatically and rhetorically explicit; (b) the cross-corpus intercession-tradition is documentary; (c) the foundational-justice-formula reading is mainstream HB scholarship (Westermann, Sarna, Brueggemann, Childs).

terms.standardEnglish: dust and ashes (ASV / NJPS / NRSV / NIV — consensus)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 18:27 introduces the *afar va-efer* (dust and ashes) self-deprecation formula in Avraham's intercession.** *V-anokhi afar va-efer* — *who am but dust and ashes*. The compound pairs two Hebrew lexemes: *afar* (עָפָר — *dust, dry earth, soil, particulate matter*; the substance of human creation at Gen 2:7 and human dissolution at Gen 3:19) and *efer* (אֵפֶר — *ashes, the residue of burning*; the substance of mourning and repentance across the prophetic and writings register). The compound is *rare* in the Hebrew Bible: it occurs only three times, all in keystone humility-and-repentance contexts.

**The three Hebrew Bible occurrences.**

- **Gen 18:27** here. Avraham's intercession-prelude. The function is *creatural-humility-as-grounds-for-intercession*: Avraham frames his right-to-petition not on covenant-status (which he holds) but on the ontological fact of being made of dust and destined for ashes. The argumentative logic: *because I am dust and ashes, and yet I take it upon myself to speak — therefore hear me*. The formula is a hedge that paradoxically authorizes the speech: by acknowledging utter creatural inadequacy, the speaker establishes the seriousness of the address.
- **Job 30:19** — *horani la-chomer va-etmashel k-afar va-efer* — *he has cast me into the mire, and I have become like dust and ashes*. The Job lament-register. The compound here is comparative (*k-afar va-efer* — *like dust and ashes*) rather than declarative; Job is *becoming* dust and ashes in the affliction, having been thrown into the mire (*chomer* — the same lexeme as the *adam from adamah* creation material). The function is *lament*: the speaker complains of being reduced to creatural-zero.
- **Job 42:6** — *al-ken em'as v-nichamti al-afar va-efer* — *therefore I despise (myself) and repent (or: take comfort) in dust and ashes*. The Job repentance-register. The compound here is locative (*al-afar va-efer* — *on/in dust and ashes*) — the standard mourning-and-repentance posture (sitting in or on ashes; cf. 2 Sam 13:19; Jonah 3:6; Esth 4:1, 4:3). The function is *repentance-or-comfort* (the verb *em'as v-nichamti* admits multiple readings; see the existing glossary entry *em-as-v-nikhamti-al-afar-va-efer* on the three-way philological dispute about Job 42:6).

The three occurrences thus form a structural triad: intercession-hedge (Gen 18:27) → lament-complaint (Job 30:19) → repentance-or-comfort (Job 42:6). The compound's three appearances are all keystone-context, suggesting the phrase functions as a marked-formula reserved for moments of ontological-creatural-acknowledgment.

**The two component motifs.**

*The creation-from-dust motif.* The *afar* lexeme is the substance of human creation: *va-yitzer YHWH Elohim et-ha-adam afar min-ha-adamah* (Gen 2:7 — *and YHWH Elohim formed the man, dust from the ground*). The lexeme is then the substance of human dissolution: *ki afar atah v-el-afar tashuv* (Gen 3:19 — *for you are dust, and to dust you shall return*). The *afar*-motif bookends the human creature: created from dust, returning to dust. The motif is recapitulated in the wisdom literature (Eccl 3:20, 12:7; Ps 103:14 *ki hu yada yitzreinu zakhur ki afar anachnu* — *for he knows our frame; he remembers that we are dust*; Ps 104:29).

*The mourning-and-ashes motif.* The *efer* lexeme is the substance of mourning and repentance. The canonical *sackcloth-and-ashes* register: 2 Sam 13:19 (Tamar after the assault); 2 Kgs 13:2-3, Esth 4:1-3 (Mordecai's response to Haman's edict); Isa 58:5 (the fast-day register); Jer 6:26 (the threat of judgment); Lam 2:10 (the elders of the daughter of Zion); Dan 9:3 (Daniel's penitential prayer); Jonah 3:6 (the king of Nineveh covered with sackcloth and sitting in ashes); Job 2:8 (Job sitting in ashes). The *efer*-motif marks moments of self-humiliation, mourning, and penitential approach to the deity.

The compound *afar va-efer* combines the two motifs: the creature *made of* dust and *acknowledging itself as* destined for ashes. The compound is thus a *minimal-creatural-statement*: this is what I am ontologically; this is what I will become.

**Second-Temple deuterocanonical reception.** The *afar va-efer* compound is taken up in the Second-Temple wisdom corpus:

- **Sir 10:9** (LXX *spodos kai gē*) — *why are dust and ashes proud?* Applied as a moral commonplace against pride.
- **Sir 17:32** — applies the formula to humans collectively as *spodos kai gē* (ashes and earth).
- **Sir 40:3** — applies the formula in a list of universal human conditions.

The Second-Temple register treats the compound as a *moral commonplace* rather than as a keystone-context marker. The Hebrew Bible's three occurrences are at keystone moments; the Sira occurrences are general-pedagogical.

**The Christian penitential reception — Ash Wednesday.** The Latin Christian liturgical tradition develops the *afar va-efer* (LXX *gē kai spodos* → Vulgate *pulvis et cinis*) motif into the Ash Wednesday formula *memento homo quia pulvis es et in pulverem reverteris* — *remember, man, that you are dust and to dust you shall return* — applied to penitents at the imposition of ashes. The formula combines Gen 3:19 (*ki afar atah v-el-afar tashuv*) with the *efer* motif. The Catholic and Anglican liturgical traditions preserve the formula; some Reformed traditions reject the rite while preserving the underlying penitential-creatural framework. The reception is flagged as downstream Greek and Latin and is not preempted in the WoH translation.

**The translation.** Consensus across major English versions: ASV, NJPS, NRSV, NIV all read *dust and ashes*. The WoH translation reads *dust and ashes* per consensus.

**Cross-reference.** See the existing glossary entry *em-as-v-nikhamti-al-afar-va-efer* on Job 42:6 — the three-way philological dispute about the verb *em'as* (despise / loathe / reject) and *nichamti* (repent / take comfort / be consoled) and the appropriate preposition (*al* — on / in / concerning); the *afar va-efer* compound there functions as the locative-or-instrumental phrase. The Gen 18:27 instance is structurally simpler (the compound functions predicatively: *I am dust and ashes*) and does not raise the same philological questions as Job 42:6.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989, pp. 132-133; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 291-292; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, p. 53; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, pp. 25-26; Clines *Job 21-37* (WBC) 2006 on Job 30:19; Clines *Job 38-42* (WBC) 2011 on Job 42:6; Newsom *The Book of Job: A Contest of Moral Imaginations* 2003. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the compound *afar va-efer* is lexically explicit; (b) the three Hebrew Bible occurrences (Gen 18:27, Job 30:19, 42:6) are verbatim; (c) the component motifs (creation-from-dust at Gen 2:7 / 3:19; mourning-ashes across the prophets) are documentary; (d) the Second-Temple Sira and the Ash Wednesday liturgical receptions are documentary continuations.

terms.partOfSpeech: preposition with 3ms suffix, marked with four supralinear dots
terms.literal: to-him (with the four letters marked by supralinear dots)
terms.standardEnglish: to him (ASV / consensus — dots not represented in translation)
terms.wohChoice: to him (MT preserved, dots noted in apparatus)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 18:9 preserves four supralinear dots over the letters of *elav* (אֵׄלָׄיׄוׄ — *to him*).** The dots appear over each of the four letters of the word: *aleph*, *lamed*, *yod*, *vav*. The notation is one of the canonical *puncta extraordinaria* (*nequdot* — the *extraordinary dots*) of the Hebrew Bible, a Masoretic scribal feature distinct from but functionally analogous to the *tiqun-soferim* tradition (see the *tiqun-soferim-yhwh-stood-before-abraham* entry on Gen 18:22).

**The standard list of fifteen *puncta extraordinaria*.** The canonical list (recorded in *Sofrim* 6:3, in the *Massorah Magna*'s marginal lists, and reproduced in modern critical editions including BHS / BHQ):

- **Gen 16:5** — *beynekha* (between you) marked with a single dot on the yod
- **Gen 18:9** here — *elav* with four dots
- **Gen 19:33** — *u-v-qumah* (and when she arose) with a dot on the *vav* of *uvqumah* (the Lot-and-the-elder-daughter scene)
- **Gen 33:4** — *va-yishaqehu* (and he kissed him) with dots over the four letters (the Esau-Jacob reunion)
- **Gen 37:12** — *et* with dots over both letters (the Joseph-narrative)
- **Num 3:39** — *v-Aharon* with a dot on the *vav* — *and Aaron*
- **Num 9:10** — *r-choqah* (distant) with a dot on the *he*
- **Num 21:30** — *asher* with a dot on the *resh*
- **Num 29:15** — *isaron* (a tenth) with a dot on the *vav*
- **Deut 29:28 (Heb 29:27 or 29:28)** — *lanu u-l-vaneinu* with dots over eleven letters (the longest dotted sequence)
- **2 Sam 19:20 (Heb 19:21)** — *yatza* with a dot on the *tzade*
- **Isa 44:9** — *hemah* with dots
- **Ezek 41:20** — *ha-heychal*
- **Ezek 46:22** — *m-haqtza'ot*
- **Ps 27:13** — *lulei* (the word as a whole is dotted)

The list of fifteen is standard; some sources count differently (the *Sofrim*-tradition lists ten in the Pentateuch and five in the Prophets and Writings). The Gen 18:9 instance is one of the most frequently cited examples and one of the few where multiple letters of a single word are dotted simultaneously.

**The two readings.** Modern scholarship on the *puncta extraordinaria* parallels the divided position on the *tiqun soferim*.

*The traditional rabbinic reading.* The rabbinic interpretation of the Gen 18:9 dots (recorded in **b. Bava Metzia 87a**, **Bereshit Rabbah 48:15**, and the *Avot d-Rabbi Nathan*) treats the dots as marking a *textual reservation*. Two main rabbinic interpretations: (a) **the visitors-knew-Sarah's-name reading**: the dots mark that the visitors did not actually need to ask *where is Sarah your wife?* — they already knew she was inside; the question was asked *for Avraham's sake* (or, in another midrash, *to honor Sarah by mentioning her*); the dots flag the technical-inaccuracy of *they said to him* (as if to him alone) since the speech was knowingly indirected; (b) **the asked-each-other reading**: in another rabbinic interpretation, the visitors asked one *another* rather than asking Avraham — *to him* is dotted because it is not strictly accurate; the dots mark this textual reservation. The rabbinic readings treat the dots as carrying *interpretive meaning* — each dotted passage is read for its hermeneutic significance.

*The modern text-critical reading.* The modern scholarly tradition (**Tov** *Textual Criticism of the Hebrew Bible* 3rd ed. 2012, §6.B.5; **McCarthy** *The Tiqqune Sopherim* 1981, pp. 207-264 on related dot-and-mark notations; **Yeivin** *Introduction to the Tiberian Masorah* 1980 §80-81; **Würthwein** *The Text of the Old Testament* 2nd ed. 1995, pp. 17-18) treats the *puncta extraordinaria* as the Masoretes' preservation of *ancient editorial flags*. The most common modern interpretation: the dots mark words whose presence in the text was *uncertain* to early scribes — i.e., words that early manuscripts varied on, with some witnesses including them and others omitting them. The Masoretes, preserving the consonantal text, marked the doubtful words with supralinear dots rather than omitting them — letting the consonants stand while flagging the textual uncertainty for later readers. On this reading, the dots are *editorial doubt notation* — the ancient equivalent of square brackets or a footnote indicating *variant attested*.

The two readings are not mutually exclusive: the modern reading explains the dots' origin (ancient textual uncertainty); the rabbinic reading explains their *received* hermeneutic significance (the dotted passages becoming sites of interpretive interest precisely because they were marked). Mainstream modern scholarship endorses the editorial-flag origin with the recognition that the rabbinic interpretations form a substantial layer of Jewish hermeneutic tradition built on top of the original notation.

**The Gen 18:9 case specifically.** The four-letter dotting of *elav* is unusually thorough — the entire word is marked, suggesting either (a) the entire word's presence was textually doubted (some early manuscripts omitted *elav* entirely, reading simply *va-yomru ayyeh Sarah ishtekha* — *they said: where is Sarah your wife?* without the *to him*); or (b) the rabbinic reading is correct and the dots mark a specific hermeneutic point requiring all four letters to be flagged. The LXX preserves the word (*eipan de pros auton* — *they said to him*), as do the Samaritan Pentateuch, the Vulgate (*dixerunt ad eum*), and the Targumim. The textual witness for omission is weak; the dots' origin is therefore plausibly hermeneutic rather than text-critical, though Tov 2012 cautions that the editorial-flag origin remains the simpler explanation for the *puncta extraordinaria* phenomenon as a class.

**Function in the chapter.** The Gen 18:9 dots fall at a structurally significant moment: the verse is the first in the chapter where the visitors initiate a speech-act (in vv 4-5 Avraham speaks first; in v 9 the visitors speak), and the speech-act introduces Sarah, the chapter's promised-conception subject. The position is editorially loaded. Whether the dots originated as editorial-flag or as hermeneutic-marker, their function in the received text is to draw attention to the verse's pivot from hospitality-scene to theophany-scene.

**The translation.** The dots are not represented in the English translation (the ASV-baseline reads *and they said to him*); they are recorded in the Hebrew apparatus where the consonantal text preserves them and in the per-verse commentary. The WoH translation follows the consensus practice.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Tov *Textual Criticism of the Hebrew Bible* 3rd ed. 2012, §6.B.5; McCarthy *The Tiqqune Sopherim* (OBO 36) 1981; Yeivin *Introduction to the Tiberian Masorah* 1980, §80-81; Würthwein *The Text of the Old Testament* 2nd ed. 1995, pp. 17-18; Ginsburg *Introduction to the Massoretico-Critical Edition of the Hebrew Bible* 1897, pp. 318-334 (the classical treatment); Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989, p. 129; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985, p. 279; b. Bava Metzia 87a; Bereshit Rabbah 48:15; *Sofrim* 6:3. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the *puncta extraordinaria* are documentary in the MT — the dots are physically preserved in the manuscript tradition; (b) the standard list of fifteen verses including Gen 18:9 is documented across the Sofrim tradition and the modern critical editions; (c) the two modern critical positions on the dots' origin (Tov's editorial-flag reading; the rabbinic hermeneutic reading) are documented in the cited literature; (d) the specific rabbinic interpretations of Gen 18:9 are documentary in b. Bava Metzia 87a and Bereshit Rabbah 48:15.

terms.partOfSpeech: hiphil imperative 2mp + qal cohortative 1cp + direct object
terms.standardEnglish: bring them out to us, that we may know them (ASV / KJV); bring them out to us so that we may know them (NRSV); have intercourse with them (NJPS); rape them (NLT / CEB)
terms.wohChoice: bring them out unto us, that we may know them (literal ASV-baseline, preserves the *yada* lexeme)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 19:5 is the reception-history flashpoint of the entire Sodom narrative.** The men of Sodom surround Lot's house and demand the visitors: *hotzi'em eleinu v-neda otam* — *bring them out to us, that we may know them*. The verb *yada* (to know) carries a documented sexual sense in the HB at Gen 4:1 (*v-ha-adam yada et-Chavvah ishto* — *and the man knew Eve his wife*), Gen 4:17, Gen 4:25, Gen 19:8 (in the same chapter, of Lot's daughters: *asher lo yad'u ish* — *who have not known a man*), Gen 24:16 (Rebekah *v-ish lo yeda'ah* — *and no man had known her*), Judg 11:39, 1 Sam 1:19, and especially Judg 19:22-25, the verbatim parallel-scene at Gibeah where the same demand-formula recurs and the gang-rape is in fact carried out. The sexual sense at Gen 19:5 is the modern scholarly consensus.

**The bifurcation in reception history.** The chapter's reception then bifurcates sharply between the patristic-medieval *sodomy* reading and the modern critical *gang-rape-violence-and-inhospitality* reading. Both readings are documented here in parallel; neither is preempted by the WoH translation.

**The patristic-medieval *sodomy* reading.** The Christian Western tradition from late antiquity through the early modern period read the Sodom narrative as the textual ground of *sodomia* — same-sex sexual contact as such, irrespective of consent or violence. Augustine treats the Sodom destruction in *De Civitate Dei* XVI.30 and in the *Confessions* III.8 as punishment for *contra naturam* sexual contact. Aquinas (*Summa Theologiae* II-II q. 154 a. 11) formalizes the category *vitium sodomiticum* as one of the four *species* of *luxuria contra naturam*. The medieval Latin penitentials elaborate the category; the English *sodomy* and the cognates *sodomite*, *sodomitical* in the major European languages all derive from this exegetical tradition via the Latin place-name. The reading was the standard Western Christian reception for roughly fifteen centuries and remains the dominant popular-cultural association of the chapter today.

**The modern critical *inhospitality-and-violence* reading.** Modern critical scholarship from the late nineteenth century onward has progressively re-read the chapter against the patristic-medieval reception. The reading rests on four converging arguments.

*(a) The HB's own characterization of Sodom's sin elsewhere.* The most decisive evidence is Ezek 16:48-50: *chai-ani ne'um adonai YHWH im-asetah Sodom achotekh hi u-vnoteha ka-asher asit at u-vnotayikh hineh ze hayah avon Sodom achotekh ga'on sivat-lechem v-shalvat hashqet hayah lah v-livnoteiha v-yad ani v-evyon lo hechziqah va-tigbehenah va-ta'asenah to'evah l-fanai va-asir et-hen ka-asher ra'iti* — *as I live, says the Lord YHWH, Sodom your sister and her daughters did not do as you and your daughters did; behold, this was the iniquity of Sodom your sister: pride, fullness of bread, and prosperous ease was hers and her daughters', but she did not strengthen the hand of the poor and needy; they were haughty and committed abomination before me, so I removed them when I saw it*. The enumeration is decisive: pride (*ga'on*), surfeit of food (*sivat-lechem*), prosperous ease (*shalvat hashqet*), and failure to aid the poor (*v-yad ani v-evyon lo hechziqah*). Same-sex contact is not in the list. The *to'evah* (abomination) of v 50 is theologically generic, not specifically sexual.

*(b) The cross-corpus pattern of HB Sodom-references.* The wider HB references to Sodom characterize the city as paradigmatic of violence, oppression, and arrogance: Isa 1:9-10 (Sodom and Amorah as figures of pervasive injustice; *qetzinei Sodom* — *rulers of Sodom* — addressed to the corrupt Jerusalem leadership), Isa 3:9 (*Sodom higidu lo kichedu* — *like Sodom they declare their sin openly*, in context of social oppression), Jer 23:14 (Jerusalem's prophets compared to Sodom in moral terms — adultery, falsehood, strengthening the hands of evildoers), Lam 4:6 (Jerusalem's punishment greater than Sodom's *mahapekhah*), Amos 4:11, Zeph 2:9, Wis 19:13-17 (Hellenistic Jewish text, Wisdom of Solomon, explicitly *inhospitality* — *misetheian xenon*). The non-sexual register is dominant.

*(c) The *za'aqah* mechanism.* The Sodom destruction is triggered at Gen 18:20-21 by the *za'aqat Sodom va-Amorah* — the *outcry of Sodom*. The lexeme is the HB's standard term for the cry of the *oppressed* — see the *zaaqah-outcry-of-the-oppressed* entry for the full cross-corpus distribution (Gen 4:10 Abel; Exod 2:23-25 and 3:7-9 Israel in Egypt; Exod 22:22-24 widow-and-orphan; 1 Sam 9:16; Job 34:28; James 5:4). The *za'aqah* mechanism presupposes ongoing oppression-of-victims, not a single sexual episode. The structural-narrative logic of Gen 18-19 is therefore: the cry of Sodom's victims has reached YHWH (18:20-21) → YHWH descends to investigate (18:21) → the gang-rape attempt at 19:5 is the *confirming exhibit* of the pattern, not the precipitating cause.

*(d) The Gibeah parallel at Judg 19.* The Judg 19 scene is verbatim-parallel: the men of the city surround the house, demand to *know* the male guest, the host offers his daughter (and the concubine) in place of the guest, and the concubine is in fact handed over and gang-raped to death. The Judges scene is unambiguously violence-against-a-female-outsider once the trade is accepted; the Gen 19 scene is structurally identical at the demand-stage. The parallel makes the *gang-rape-violence-against-outsiders* reading editorially explicit in the canon.

The modern critical reading is documented in Sarna *JPS Genesis* (1989) pp. 134-136; Brueggemann *Genesis* (Interpretation, 1982); Westermann *Genesis 12-36* (1985) pp. 297-305; Letellier *Day in Mamre, Night in Sodom* (1995) chs 4-5; Loader *A Tale of Two Cities: Sodom and Gomorrah* (1990); Tornkvist *Eros Defiled?* (1998); Carden *Sodomy: A History of a Christian Biblical Myth* (2004 — full-length monograph on the receptional history). Carden 2004 documents the receptional pathway by which the *sodomy* category was constructed across the patristic-medieval tradition and re-examines the Hebrew Bible material against it. The modern critical consensus is mainstream in Hebrew-Bible scholarship.

**The translation.** The WoH translation preserves the literal *that we may know them*, ASV-baseline. This commits the English to neither reading at the lexical level: the *yada* lexeme is preserved; the sexual sense is documented in apparatus and is the modern scholarly consensus; the further bifurcation (orientation vs. violence) is documented in apparatus and not preempted. The translation choice is editorially neutral on the receptional question. The alternative NJPS *that we may have intercourse with them* commits cleanly to the sexual reading and is defensible; the NLT *that we may rape them* commits to the gang-rape-violence reading and is also defensible; the WoH translation declines both modernizations in favor of preserving the *yada* lexeme literally, letting the reader engage the bifurcation through the apparatus rather than through the English.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989, pp. 134-136; Brueggemann *Genesis* (Interpretation) 1982; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* (Hermeneia) 1985, pp. 297-305; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, pp. 51-58; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, pp. 33-39; Letellier *Day in Mamre, Night in Sodom* 1995, chs 4-5; Loader *A Tale of Two Cities: Sodom and Gomorrah* 1990; Tornkvist *Eros Defiled?* 1998; Carden *Sodomy: A History of a Christian Biblical Myth* 2004; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004, pp. 86-90. For the patristic-medieval reception: Augustine *De Civitate Dei* XVI.30; Aquinas *Summa Theologiae* II-II q. 154 a. 11; the standard treatments of medieval sexual-ethics history in Brundage *Law, Sex, and Christian Society in Medieval Europe* 1987 and Boswell *Christianity, Social Tolerance, and Homosexuality* 1980. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the *yada* lexeme and its sexual-sense distribution are grammatically and lexically explicit; (b) the Ezek 16:48-50 enumeration of Sodom's sin is documentary; (c) the wider HB Sodom-reference pattern is verifiable by lexical search; (d) the patristic-medieval *sodomia* receptional history is documentary (Augustine, Aquinas, the penitentials, the etymological pathway of *sodomy* into the European languages); (e) the modern critical literature documenting the bifurcation is the cited scholarly corpus.

terms.source: הִנֵּה־נָא לִי שְׁתֵּי בָנוֹת ... וַעֲשׂוּ לָהֶן כַּטּוֹב בְּעֵינֵיכֶם
terms.translit: hineh-na li shtei vanot ... v-asu lahen ka-tov b-eyneykhem
terms.partOfSpeech: demonstrative + preposition + numeric + noun feminine plural + qal imperative + preposition + adjective
terms.literal: behold-now to-me two daughters ... and-do to-them as-the-good in-your-eyes
terms.standardEnglish: do to them as is good in your eyes (ASV / KJV); do to them whatever you please (NJPS / NIV); do to them what seems right to you (smoothing)
terms.wohChoice: do to them as is good in your eyes (ASV-baseline, preserves the *ka-tov b-eyneykhem* idiom literally)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 19:8 records Lot's offer of his two virgin daughters to the mob in place of the messengers.** The Hebrew is unambiguous: *hineh-na li shtei vanot asher lo yad'u ish otzi'ah-na et-hen aleikhem v-asu lahen ka-tov b-eyneykhem raq la-anashim ha-el al-ta'asu davar ki al-ken ba'u b-tzel qorati* — *behold, I have two daughters who have not known a man; let me bring them out to you, and do to them as is good in your eyes; only to these men do nothing, since they have come under the shadow of my roof*. The narrator records the offer without editorial comment. The mob refuses (vv 9-11) and the daughters are not in fact handed over, but the offer is made and is structurally definitive of Lot's character.

**The ancient hospitality-and-honor reading.** Westermann (*Genesis 12-36*, Hermeneia 1985, pp. 301-303), Sarna (*JPS Genesis* 1989, p. 136), Wenham (*WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994), Hamilton (*NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, pp. 36-39), and the patristic and medieval Christian commentary tradition all read the offer as an extreme expression of the ANE host-protects-his-guests obligation. In the ANE legal and ethical context, the host's *honor* was bound to the protection of those who had come *under the shadow of his roof* (the Hebrew *ki al-ken ba'u b-tzel qorati* is the explicit articulation of the obligation). The honor-code reading takes Lot's offer as a recognized social institution under extreme pressure: the host owes inviolability to his male guests; the daughters are the only resource Lot has to offer to deflect the mob. The ancient reading does not exculpate Lot — it explains the offer's place in the ANE honor-system without endorsing the system.

**The modern feminist-critical reading.** Beginning in the 1980s a body of modern feminist-critical scholarship has developed a fundamentally different reading of the verse. The reading begins with the structural parallel at Judg 19:22-25 (the Gibeah outrage), where the verbatim demand-formula recurs and the host's daughter-or-concubine-trade is *in fact accepted*: the Levite's concubine is handed over to the mob and gang-raped to death. The Judg 19 scene is the structurally explicit working-out of the Lot-offer logic: when the trade is accepted, the consequence is the woman's death.

**Trible** (*Texts of Terror: Literary-Feminist Readings of Biblical Narratives*, 1984, ch. 3) is the foundational treatment of the Judg 19 concubine as a *text of terror*, with extended reference to the Gen 19 parallel. Trible reads the narrator's silence in both texts — the absence of editorial comment on the offers and the consequences — as the narratives' participation in the patriarchal structure that makes such trades thinkable. **Fewell and Gunn** (*Gender, Power, and Promise: The Subject of the Bible's First Story*, 1993) develop the reading across the Genesis-Joshua arc, treating the patriarchal narratives as systematically encoding the *trade of female bodies for male hospitality-honor*. **Bach** (*Women, Seduction, and Betrayal in Biblical Narrative*, 1997, with the earlier 1999 collection *Women in the Hebrew Bible*) extends the reading to the wider narrative corpus. **Exum** (*Fragmented Women: Feminist (Sub)versions of Biblical Narratives*, 1993, and *Plotted, Shot, and Painted: Cultural Representations of Biblical Women*, 1996) treats the visual-cultural reception of the Lot-daughters offer alongside the textual material. The reading is mainstream in modern feminist Hebrew-Bible scholarship and is taught as such in standard graduate Hebrew-Bible curricula.

**The convergence and the divergence.** The two readings converge on the descriptive facts: the offer is made; the daughters are virgin; the verb-phrase *ka-tov b-eyneykhem* (*as is good in your eyes*) places the daughters' fate at the mob's discretion; the daughters are not in fact handed over; the messengers' intervention at v 10 forestalls the consequence. The two readings diverge on the ethical-evaluative question: is the offer to be read with the ANE honor-code as exculpatory or partially-exculpatory, or is the narrator's silence to be read as the narrative's participation in the structure that makes the offer thinkable? Both readings are documented here in parallel; neither is preempted by the WoH translation.

**The narrator's silence.** Both readings agree that the narrator does not editorially condemn Lot for the offer. The modern reading takes this as the load-bearing fact: the *absence* of condemnation in the text is itself a textual feature of the patriarchal-honor structure. The ancient reading takes the absence as evidence that within the ANE honor-system the offer was a recognized response to an extreme situation. Both readings agree that the text is morally complex and that the offer is the chapter's most ethically pressured moment.

**The chapter's coda and the daughters.** Verses 30-38 — the Lot-and-the-daughters incest at the cave — return the daughters to narrative focus and are the chapter's other ethically-pressured moment. The two episodes are structurally paired: at v 8 Lot offers his daughters to the mob; at vv 33-35 the daughters take initiative with their drunken father; the chapter brackets Lot's family around a double pattern of patriarchal-sexual rupture. Bauer (*Reading Lot's Daughters*, 1999) is the standard modern treatment of the bracket-structure.

**The translation.** The ASV-baseline *do to them as is good in your eyes* preserves the Hebrew *ka-tov b-eyneykhem* idiom literally. The NJPS *do to them whatever you please* and NIV *do to them what seems right to you* modernize and soften; both are defensible. The WoH translation declines the smoothing in favor of preserving the *eye*-idiom (cf. the wider HB *b-eynei* construction's distribution).

**Modern scholarly literature.** Trible *Texts of Terror* 1984; Bach *Women, Seduction, and Betrayal in Biblical Narrative* 1997 / *Women in the Hebrew Bible* 1999; Fewell and Gunn *Gender, Power, and Promise* 1993; Exum *Fragmented Women* 1993 and *Plotted, Shot, and Painted* 1996; Bauer *Reading Lot's Daughters* 1999; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989, p. 136; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 301-303; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, pp. 53-55; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, pp. 36-39; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004, p. 88. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the offer is grammatically explicit in the Hebrew; (b) the parallel-scene at Judg 19:22-25 is verbatim at the demand-formula level; (c) the modern feminist-critical reception is documented in the cited literature; (d) the ancient ANE host-protects-guests honor-code is documented in the ANE literature and modern scholarly treatments.

terms.partOfSpeech: noun masculine plural with prepositional prefix
terms.standardEnglish: with blindness (ASV / KJV / NJPS / NRSV consensus)
terms.wohChoice: with blindness (ASV-baseline; dazzling-light nuance in apparatus)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 19:11 uses the rare lexeme *sanverim* — one of only two HB occurrences.** The messengers strike the men at the entrance of Lot's house *ba-sanverim* (בַּסַּנְוֵרִים) — *with the blindnesses*. The lexeme is a plural-of-effect or plural-of-abstraction; the ordinary HB term for blindness is the singular *ivvaron* (עִוָּרוֹן, cf. Deut 28:28, Zech 12:4) or *iver* (עִוֵּר — blind person). The *sanverim* form is morphologically distinctive and is restricted to two attestations across the entire HB.

**The two attestations.**

*(i) Gen 19:11* — here. The messengers strike the men of Sodom *ba-sanverim mi-qaton v-ad-gadol* — *with blindness, from small to great* — so that they cannot find the door of Lot's house.

*(ii) 2 Kgs 6:18* — Elisha's prayer when the Aramean army surrounds him at Dotan. Elisha prays *hakeh-na et ha-goy ha-zeh ba-sanverim va-yakem ba-sanverim ki-dvar Elisha* — *strike this people with blindness, and (YHWH) struck them with blindness according to the word of Elisha*. The structural parallel to Gen 19:11 is striking and editorially deliberate: a divine-or-prophetic agent strikes a hostile group surrounding a protected house or person with *sanverim*, rendering them unable to find their target.

**The etymology.** The lexeme's etymology is contested. Three positions in modern scholarship.

*(a) The Akkadian loanword reading.* HALOT s.v. *sanverim* and Tigay (*JPS Excursus on the Sanverim*) note a possible Akkadian loanword from *šunwurum* (CAD Š/III pp. 297-298) — a verbal-noun form from the root *nwr* (light, brightness) with the *š-* causative prefix and the *-um* nominal suffix. The Akkadian form denotes *dazzling, blinding-by-light, illumination-to-the-point-of-loss-of-sight*. On this etymology *sanverim* denotes not ordinary loss of sight but a *flash of blinding light* — a sensory-overload form of blindness. The reading is supported by (i) the plural-of-effect form (*sanverim* as the multiple flashes / multiple effects of the dazzling); (ii) the parallel at 2 Kgs 6 where the Aramean army is later led un-blinded into Samaria, suggesting the *sanverim* is a temporary disorienting state rather than permanent blindness; (iii) the wider ANE pattern of divine-light theophany associated with destruction (cf. the *kavod* radiance, the Sinai theophany *barak* lightning, the wider Mesopotamian *melammu* divine-radiance tradition). The reading is mainstream in HALOT, Tigay, and the modern Akkadian-comparative lexicographic tradition.

*(b) The West-Semitic native formation reading.* BDB §5575 derives the form from a postulated West-Semitic root *snwr* (to dazzle, blind), without committing to an Akkadian loanword origin. The root would be otherwise unattested in the HB. The reading is conservative-lexicographic and less explanatory of the morphology.

*(c) The Aramaic-influence reading.* Some commentators (Gesenius; cf. the older lexicographic tradition) treat the form as Aramaism, related to the Aramaic *sumya* (blind), with the morphology reflecting late-stratum influence. The reading is less common in modern scholarship.

The Akkadian *šunwurum* reading is the modern scholarly default. The structural-narrative point is the same on all three etymologies: the *sanverim* is the agent-of-the-divine-or-prophetic-protection's tool for disabling a hostile group at close quarters without killing them.

**The 2 Kgs 6 cross-reference.** The structural parallel between Gen 19:11 and 2 Kgs 6:18 is one of the canonical Genesis-Kings typological links. Both scenes stage: a divine-or-prophetic agent inside a protected location, a hostile group outside, the *sanverim* strike, and the hostile group's inability to find its target. The Elisha cycle's deliberate Genesis-Exodus typology (Brueggemann *2 Kings* OTL; the wider Elisha-as-second-Moses scholarship) makes the verbal echo editorially significant.

**The translation.** ASV's *blindness* preserves the consensus reading without committing to the etymology question. The WoH translation preserves ASV-baseline; the dazzling-light nuance is carried in this glossary entry and in the v 11 commentary. The alternative *dazzling blindness* or *blinding light* would commit the translation to the Akkadian-etymology reading; the WoH translation declines the commitment in favor of the conservative lexical rendering.

**Modern scholarly literature.** HALOT s.v. *sanverim*; CAD Š/III pp. 297-298 s.v. *šunwurum*; BDB §5575; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989, p. 136; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985, p. 304; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, pp. 55-56; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, pp. 40-41; Tigay *JPS Excursus* on the lexeme; Brueggemann *2 Kings* (OTL) on the Elisha-cycle Genesis-typology; Cogan and Tadmor *Anchor 2 Kings* 1988 on 2 Kgs 6:18. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the lexeme *sanverim* is grammatically explicit and the two HB attestations are documentary; (b) the structural parallel between Gen 19:11 and 2 Kgs 6:18 is verifiable at the lexical level; (c) the Akkadian *šunwurum* etymology is documented in CAD and HALOT.

terms.partOfSpeech: prepositional phrase + noun feminine construct + divine name
terms.standardEnglish: YHWH being merciful unto him (ASV); in YHWH's mercy on him (NRSV); because YHWH spared him (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: YHWH being merciful unto him (ASV-baseline; sparing-compassion nuance in apparatus)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 19:16 uses the lexeme *chemlah* (חֶמְלָה) — a marked HB compassion-term distinct from the more common *chesed* (covenant loving-kindness) and *rachamim* (womb-compassion).** The verse: *va-yitmahmah va-yachaziqu ha-anashim b-yado u-v-yad-ishto u-v-yad shtei v-notav b-chemlat YHWH alav va-yotzi'uhu va-yanichuhu mi-chutz la-ir* — *but he lingered; and the men laid hold upon his hand, and upon the hand of his wife, and upon the hand of his two daughters, YHWH being merciful unto him; and they brought him forth, and set him without the city*. The construct *b-chemlat YHWH* with the *be*-preposition is a rare grammatical form; the Gen 19:16 occurrence is the lexeme's first attestation in the HB and the only patriarchal-narrative instance.

**The lexeme.** BDB §2551 and HALOT s.v. *chemlah*: the lexeme denotes *sparing, having pity, refraining from destroying or harming when one has the power and right to do so*. The verbal root *chamal* (חמל) is more common (~40 HB occurrences) and carries the same sparing-sense. The noun is rare (~3 attestations). The semantic core distinguishes *chemlah* from *chesed* (relational covenant-fidelity) and *rachamim* (the visceral compassion-from-the-womb, *rechem* the cognate). *Chemlah* is judicial-volitional sparing: the agent has the right to destroy or take; chooses not to. The judicial register is what makes the lexeme structurally apt at Gen 19:16 — Lot is being spared from a destruction-event that has been declared and is in process.

**Cross-corpus distribution.** The cognate verbal forms recur at structurally similar HB moments.

*Sparing-vs-cherem cluster.* **1 Sam 15:3, 15:9, 15:15** — Saul's *chemlah* on Agag and the best of the spoil, explicitly contrasted with the *cherem* command. The *chemlah* here is *failed-sparing-discipline*: Saul spares what should not be spared, and the consequence is the loss of the kingdom. The lexeme's judicial register is foregrounded.

*Sparing-from-deserved-judgment cluster.* **2 Sam 12:4** — Nathan's parable to David: the rich man *va-yachmol* (*spared*) from his own flock, taking the poor man's ewe instead. The verb's failure-mode: failure to spare from one's own resources, taking from another's. The lexeme's relational register: sparing-and-not-sparing as the structure of justice.

*Divine-sparing cluster.* **Mal 3:17** — *v-chamalti aleihem ka-asher yachmol ish al-b'no ha-oved oto* — *and I will spare them, as a man spares his own son who serves him*. The divine *chemlah* explicitly paralleled to the father-and-son sparing-relation. The lexeme's familial-judicial register. **Joel 2:18** — *va-y-qane YHWH l-artzo va-yachmol al-amo* — *YHWH was jealous for his land and spared his people*, in the post-locust restoration. **Jon 4:10-11** — *atah chasta al ha-qiqayon ... va-ani lo achus al-Nineveh* — Jonah's *chus* on the gourd-vine contrasted with YHWH's *chus* on Nineveh, in the gourd-parable closing the book; the verb *chus* is a near-synonym of *chamal* and the two cluster across the prophetic literature.

*Negative-formula cluster.* **Ezek 9:5** — *al-tachos eynkhem v-al-tachmolu* — *let not your eye spare, do not have pity* — the explicit non-sparing command of the destroying-angels-with-marking-iron pericope. The negated form is the *chemlah* lexeme's reverse: the destruction proceeds without pity. The Gen 19 destruction is *with* *chemlah* on Lot; the Ezek 9 destruction is *without* *chemlah* on the marked-unmarked. The two pericopes structurally mirror each other.

**The Gen 19:16 placement.** The verse falls between two narrative moments: the messengers' urging of Lot to flee (v 15 — *qum qach et-ishtekha v-et-shtei v-notekha ha-nimtza'ot pen-tisapeh ba-avon ha-ir* — *arise, take your wife, and your two daughters that are here, lest you be consumed in the iniquity of the city*) and Lot's actual extraction (vv 17-22 — the journey to Tzo'ar). The verse marks the inflection-point at which the sparing-mechanism is named. The chapter's structural pairing is then editorially explicit: at v 16 *b-chemlat YHWH alav* (YHWH's sparing-of-Lot) → at v 29 *va-yizkor Elohim et-Avraham* (Elohim's remembering-of-Avraham). The two formulae together constitute the mechanism of Lot's deliverance: sparing-by-YHWH at the moment of extraction, paired with remembering-of-Avraham at the moment of the destruction. See the *vayyizkor-elohim* entry for the *zakhar*-with-divine-subject cross-corpus pattern that pairs with this entry.

**The translation.** ASV's *YHWH being merciful unto him* preserves the construct's force; NJPS's *because YHWH spared him* commits cleanly to the sparing-sense but loses the *chemlah*-lexeme-nuance; NRSV's *in YHWH's mercy on him* is closer to ASV. The WoH translation preserves ASV-baseline; the sparing-from-deserved-destruction nuance is carried in apparatus.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989, p. 137; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 303-304; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, p. 56; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, pp. 41-42; HALOT s.v. *chemlah, chamal*; BDB §2551; the cross-corpus treatments in McKane *ICC Jeremiah* on the *chamal* / *chus* prophetic pair; the standard Mal-Joel-Jonah commentary literature. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the lexeme *chemlah* is grammatically explicit and rare; (b) the sparing-from-deserved-destruction semantic core is documentary in BDB and HALOT; (c) the cross-corpus distribution at 1 Sam 15, 2 Sam 12:4, Mal 3:17, Joel 2:18, Jon 4:10-11, Ezek 9:5 is verifiable by lexical search; (d) the pairing with the v 29 *zakhar*-mechanism is grammatically and structurally explicit.

terms.partOfSpeech: negative particle + hiphil imperfect 2ms jussive + adverb with 2ms suffix
terms.standardEnglish: do not look behind you (ASV); do not look back (NJPS / NRSV); do not look back at the city (NLT smoothing)
terms.wohChoice: do not look behind you (ASV-baseline, preserves the *acharekha* spatial)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 19:17 deploys the *do-not-look-back* prohibition formula.** The messenger's instruction to Lot's family on the way out of the city: *himmalet al-nafshekha al-tabit acharekha v-al-ta'amod b-khol-ha-kikkar ha-harah himmalet pen-tisapeh* — *escape for your life; do not look behind you, nor stay anywhere in the Plain; escape to the mountain, lest you be consumed*. The verb is *navat* (נבט) in the hiphil — *to look intently, gaze at, behold*. The lexeme is distinct from the more general *ra'ah* (to see); *navat* carries a sense of *fixed, deliberate, intent looking* rather than ordinary sight. The fulfillment-or-violation at v 26 — *va-tabet ishto me-acharav va-t'hi netziv melach* — *but his wife looked back from behind him, and she became a pillar of salt* — uses the same root in the qal.

**The HB lexical pattern of the prohibition.** The full HB do-not-look-back distribution is sparse: the Gen 19:17 / 19:26 pair is the foundational instance. Forward applications include Judg 19:30 (the metaphorical post-Gibeah *look back to the days from when the children of Israel came up*) and the wider *negev* (south-direction-not-look-back) prophetic register, but no verbatim recurrence of *al-tabit acharekha* in the same construction. The formula's force is in its concrete-physical specificity: *do not turn the head while fleeing*; *do not gaze back at the destruction*.

**The NT recall at Luke 17:32.** *Mnemoneuete tes gynaikos Lot* — *remember Lot's wife*. The Lukan saying (in the Olivet-discourse / coming-of-the-Son-of-Man material at Luke 17:20-37) uses Lot's wife's looking-back as the typological warning about the day of judgment: *whoever seeks to preserve his life will lose it, but whoever loses his life will preserve it* (v 33, immediately following the Lot's-wife mention). The Lukan author quotes the Gen 19 narrative as eschatological-typological material; the wider Luke 17 pericope cites Sodom-and-Lot at vv 28-29 (the *gophrit va-esh* destruction, see the *gophrit-va-esh-destruction-formula* entry). The Luke 17:32 recall is one of the clearest NT-OT verbal echoes in the synoptic corpus and is the source-text for the proverbial English *remember Lot's wife* warning.

**The comparative ANE / cross-cultural pattern.** The do-not-look-back / do-not-touch / do-not-eat threshold prohibition is a widespread narrative pattern across ANE and broader cross-cultural mythologies. Three canonical comparative instances are usually cited.

*(a) The Orpheus-and-Eurydice myth.* In the Greek (Virgil *Georgics* IV; Ovid *Metamorphoses* X), Orpheus is permitted to lead Eurydice out of Hades on condition that he not look back at her until they reach the upper world; he looks back; she is lost. The structural parallel to Lot's wife is editorially explicit: a do-not-look-back prohibition on the journey out of the realm of death; violation; immediate loss.

*(b) The *Descent of Inanna* (Sumerian).* The myth (ETCSL 1.4.1) stages Inanna's descent to the underworld and her return; the return is conditional on substitutes and on threshold-rules. The wider Sumerian-Akkadian *return-from-the-underworld* corpus includes do-not-touch and do-not-eat formulas that are structurally analogous (see also the Akkadian *Descent of Ishtar*; ANET pp. 106-109).

*(c) The Adapa myth (Akkadian).* The Adapa fragments (ANET pp. 101-103; Izre'el *Adapa and the South Wind*, 2001) stage a *do-not-eat* warning that turns out to be the inverted condition for Adapa's gaining immortality: Adapa refuses the food of life because Ea has warned him it is the food of death, and so loses immortality. The structural parallel to the Eden narrative (Gen 3) is widely noted; the wider pattern of threshold-prohibitions linking divine-encounter to consequence is the comparative-mythological frame.

The comparative pattern is documented in the standard ANE-comparative literature (Pritchard *ANET* 1969; Foster *Before the Muses* 2005; the wider folklore-motif index Thompson C331 *Tabu: looking back*). The Gen 19:17 / 19:26 instance is the HB representative of the wider type-scene. The WoH lens does not extend further into project-distinctive synthesis from this verse — the comparative pattern is observational, not interpretively load-bearing.

**The translation.** ASV's *do not look behind you* preserves the spatial *acharekha* (*behind you*) directionally; NJPS's *do not look back* simplifies the spatial-relative; the WoH translation preserves ASV-baseline. The *navat* gaze-intensive sense is carried in apparatus.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989, pp. 137-138; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 305-307; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, p. 57; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, pp. 42-43; Hamilton *Anchor Bible Genesis 11-50* on the formula; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004, p. 90; for the NT reception: Marshall *NIGTC Luke* 1978; Fitzmyer *Anchor Luke* 1981, vol. II on Luke 17:32; for the comparative mythological frame: Pritchard *ANET* 1969; Foster *Before the Muses* 2005; Izre'el *Adapa and the South Wind* 2001; the Aarne-Thompson motif index. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the verb *navat* (hiphil) and the construction *al-tabit acharekha* are grammatically explicit; (b) the v 17 prohibition and v 26 fulfillment use the same root; (c) the Luke 17:32 recall is documentary; (d) the comparative ANE / cross-cultural pattern is documented in the standard comparative literature and is presented here as observational, not as direct literary dependence.

terms.standardEnglish: brimstone and fire (KJV / ASV-archaic); sulfur and fire (NRSV / NJPS / modern consensus)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 19:24 contains the Hebrew Bible's canonical destruction-formula *gophrit va-esh* — *sulfur and fire*.** *Va-YHWH himtir al-Sodom v-al-Amorah gophrit va-esh me-et YHWH min ha-shamayim* — *Then YHWH rained upon Sodom and upon Amorah sulfur and fire from YHWH out of the heavens*. The formula is the source-text for one of the most distributed destruction-formulae in the HB and is the source-text for the NT and post-biblical *lake of fire and brimstone* eschatological tradition.

**The lexemes.** *Gophrit* (גָּפְרִית) is sulfur — a yellow, combustible mineral that burns with a blue flame and produces sulfur-dioxide fumes. The lexeme is rare in the HB (~7 attestations, all in destruction-or-judgment contexts) and is technically specific. *Esh* (אֵשׁ) is the ordinary noun for fire (~380 HB attestations). The pair is the standard combustion-substance + combustion-act formula: the substance that burns + the act of burning. The pair has hendiadys-character: *sulfur-and-fire* functions as a single destructive-event-image, not as two separable substances.

**The cross-corpus chain.** The *gophrit va-esh* formula recurs across the HB destruction-corpus with editorial deliberation. The cross-corpus reach is among the most distributed of any HB destruction-formula.

*Pentateuchal.* **Deut 29:22 (Heb 29:21)** — *gophrit va-melach srefah kol-artzah lo tizara v-lo tatzmiach v-lo ya'aleh vah kol-esev k-mahapekhat Sodom va-Amorah Admah u-Tzvoyim* — *sulfur and salt, the whole land a burning, it shall not be sown, nor bear, nor any grass grow in it, like the overthrow of Sodom and Amorah, Admah and Tzvoyim*. The covenant-curse list explicitly cites the Sodom-overthrow as the destruction-pattern; the *gophrit* lexeme is here paired with *melach* (salt) rather than *esh* (fire), but the destruction-context and the explicit Sodom-citation make the verbal-chain link to Gen 19:24 editorially definite. **Deut 32:22** — the *eish qadchah b-api* / *toq'da ad-sh'ol* destruction in the Song of Moses, structurally parallel.

*Prophetic.* **Isa 30:33** — *nishmat YHWH k-nachal gophrit bo'erah-bah* — *the breath of YHWH like a torrent of sulfur kindles it*, in the Tophet / topheth oracle. **Isa 34:9** — the Edom-oracle: *v-nehefkhu nechaleha l-zefet v-afarah l-gophrit v-hayetah artzah l-zefet bo'erah* — *her streams shall be turned to pitch, and her dust to sulfur, and her land shall become burning pitch*. The Edom-destruction explicitly typed to the Sodom-destruction. **Ezek 38:22** — Gog-Magog oracle: *v-nishpateti ito ba-dever u-v-dam v-geshem shotef v-avnei elgavish esh v-gophrit amtir alav v-al-agapav v-al-amim rabim asher ito* — *I will enter into judgment with him with pestilence and blood; and an overflowing rain, and great hailstones, fire, and sulfur, I will rain upon him, and upon his hordes, and upon the many peoples that are with him*. The Ezek 38 Gog-Magog destruction adopts the Gen 19:24 *amtir ... esh v-gophrit* (*I will rain ... fire and sulfur*) construction verbatim. The verbal echo is editorial citation.

*Wisdom and Writings.* **Job 18:15** — *y-zoreh al-navehu gophrit* — *sulfur is scattered on his habitation*, the destruction of the wicked. **Ps 11:6** — *yamter al-rasha'im pachim esh v-gophrit v-ruach zil'afot m'nat kosam* — *upon the wicked he will rain snares, fire and sulfur, and a scorching wind shall be the portion of their cup*. The Ps 11:6 echo of Gen 19:24 is one of the clearest Psalter-to-Genesis verbal citations.

**The NT and post-biblical reception.** The formula becomes one of the most distributed in the NT eschatological corpus.

*Lukan reception.* **Luke 17:29** — *he hemera de exelthen Lot apo Sodomon ebrexen pyr kai theion ap' ouranou kai apolesen pantas* — *the day Lot went out from Sodom, it rained fire and sulfur from heaven, and destroyed all of them*. The Lukan saying in the Olivet-discourse-parallel (Luke 17:20-37, the coming-of-the-Son-of-Man material) uses the Sodom-destruction as eschatological-typological warning. The Greek *ebrexen pyr kai theion* is a verbatim Septuagintal echo of LXX Gen 19:24 (*kyrios ebrexen epi Sodoma kai Gomorra theion kai pyr*).

*Revelation reception.* The Apocalypse develops the formula into the eschatological *lake of fire and sulfur* — *limne tou pyros kai theiou* — across four major passages. **Rev 14:10** — the worshipper of the beast *kai basanisthesetai en pyri kai theio* (*and shall be tormented with fire and sulfur*); **Rev 19:20** — the beast and the false prophet *ezosan eis ten limnen tou pyros ten kaiomenen en theio* (*were cast alive into the lake of fire burning with sulfur*); **Rev 20:10** — the devil *eblethē eis ten limnen tou pyros kai theiou* (*was cast into the lake of fire and sulfur*); **Rev 21:8** — the unbelievers' portion is *en te limne te kaiomene pyri kai theio* (*in the lake burning with fire and sulfur*). The *lake of fire and sulfur* image is the Apocalypse's standard eschatological-destruction-image and is one of the most influential single-image NT eschatological motifs. The chain from Gen 19:24 → LXX → Luke 17:29 → Revelation is verbally continuous and editorially explicit.

**The destruction-image.** The HB sulfur-and-fire image is concretely physical — sulfur is a combustible mineral that produces a distinctive blue flame and acrid sulfur-dioxide smoke; raining from the heavens describes (in the Hebrew narrator's frame) a fall of burning material from above. The image's later receptional path (the *fire and brimstone* of the King James English; the *hellfire and brimstone* of the early modern Christian preaching tradition; the broader Western cultural association of sulfur with hell and eternal punishment) all derive from this verbal-chain. The Hebrew text's own register is destruction-event-description, not eschatological-place-description; the eschatological-place reading is a downstream development.

**The translation.** KJV / ASV-archaic *brimstone and fire* preserves the older English. *Brimstone* (Middle English *bren-stoon* — burning stone) is the older English term for sulfur and is etymologically transparent; modern English *sulfur* is the chemical-element name. The WoH translation reads *sulfur and fire* (NRSV / NJPS / modern consensus); the *brimstone* register is documented here for the receptional context.
