The cited cross-corpus loci are documented in this entry's *rationale* but not added to *appliesTo* in this chapter (the WoH translation has not yet reached those texts; *appliesTo* will be expanded by additive amendments as those chapters are translated).

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) Gen 20:17 is textually the first occurrence of *hithpallel* and of the *p-l-l* root in any prayer-related sense in the canonical Hebrew Bible; (b) the function-definition as intercessory is documented in the v 7 pairing; (c) the cross-corpus distribution is documentary; (d) the etymological dispute is documented in the cited scholarly literature, with both the mainstream lexical position and the Greenberg refinement preserved. Project-specific synthesis (the prayer-as-disclosure-channel reading; the deeper Wheel of Heaven framing) is reserved for the wiki and is not preempted by this entry.

terms.partOfSpeech: infinitive absolute + finite verb (intensifying construction) + noun phrase
terms.literal: restraining he restrained (i.e., he fast closed up) ... every womb
terms.standardEnglish: (YHWH) had fast closed up every womb
terms.wohChoice: (YHWH) had fast closed up every womb
terms.rationale:
**Gen 20:18 is the chapter's etiological coda.** The narrative's structural movement — patriarch endangered → foreign king afflicted → patriarch intercedes (v 17) → wombs reopen — is reconstructed retrospectively at this closing verse: *ki-atzor atzar YHWH be-ad kol-rechem le-veit Avimelekh al-d'var Sarah eshet Avraham* — *for YHWH had fast closed up every womb of the house of Avimelekh, because of Sarah, Abraham's wife*. The doubled construction *atzor atzar* (infinitive absolute + finite verb of *atzar* — *restrain, hold back, close up*) is the Hebrew Bible's standard intensification pattern for emphatic verb-action; ASV's *fast closed up* preserves the doubling visibly in English. The closed-wombs are the divine sanction against Avimelekh's house for the unwitting taking-of-Sarah, and v 17's healing (*va-yirpa Elohim et-Avimelekh v-et-ishto v-amhotav va-yeledu* — *and the Elohim healed Avimelekh and his wife and his maidservants, and they bore children*) is the sanction's lifting.

**The closed-wombs-as-divine-sanction motif.** The verse instantiates one of the Hebrew Bible's most consistent narrative motifs: the womb as the locus of divine action, with closing and opening of wombs as direct divine intervention. The cross-corpus distribution across the matriarchal-barrenness pattern is extensive and structurally load-bearing:

*Sarah / Sarai.* **Gen 11:30** — *va-t'hi Sarai aqarah ein lah valad* — *and Sarai was barren; she had no child* — the unresolved-barrenness datum that hangs over the entire Abraham cycle and resolves only at Gen 21:1-2 *va-YHWH paqad et-Sarah ka-asher amar va-ya'as YHWH l-Sarah ka-asher diber va-tahar va-teled Sarah l-Avraham ben* — *YHWH visited Sarah as he had said, and YHWH did to Sarah as he had spoken; Sarah conceived and bore Avraham a son*. The Sarai-and-Sarah barrenness is the foundational HB matriarchal-barrenness instance, and the *paqad* visiting-verb that resolves it (a deliberate verbal-recall of the closed-wombs / opened-wombs pattern) is the cross-corpus standard for divine intervention in womb-status.

*Rebekah.* **Gen 25:21** — *va-ye'tar Yitzchaq la-YHWH l-nochach ishto ki aqarah hi va-ye'ater lo YHWH va-tahar Rivkah ishto* — *Yitzchaq entreated YHWH for his wife, because she was barren; YHWH was entreated of him, and Rivkah his wife conceived*. The intercession-resolves-barrenness pattern is verbally near-identical to the Gen 20 pattern, with *atar* (entreat) replacing the *hithpallel* of Gen 20:17 (the lexical cluster overlaps).

*Rachel and Leah.* **Gen 29:31** — *va-yare YHWH ki s'nu'ah Leah va-yiftach et-rachmah v-Rachel aqarah* — *YHWH saw that Leah was hated, and he opened her womb; but Rachel was barren* — the load-bearing Leah / Rachel inversion, with *yiftach et-rachmah* (the open-the-womb verb in piel of *p-t-ch*) directly parallel to the *atzar-rechem* close-the-womb verb of Gen 20:18; **Gen 30:22** — *va-yizkor Elohim et-Rachel va-yishma eleha Elohim va-yiftach et-rachmah* — *Elohim remembered Rachel and Elohim hearkened to her, and he opened her womb* — the canonical *vayyizkor-Elohim* remembering-formula combined with the open-the-womb verb (see [vayyizkor-elohim](#vayyizkor-elohim)).

*Manoah's wife / Samson's mother.* **Judg 13:2-3** — *v-ishto aqarah v-lo yaladah va-yera malakh-YHWH el-ha-ishah va-yomer eleha hineh-na at aqarah v-lo yaladt v-harit v-yaladt ben* — *his wife was barren and had not borne; the messenger of YHWH appeared to the woman and said, 'Behold now, you are barren and have not borne; but you shall conceive and bear a son'* — the annunciation-to-the-barren pattern, with the messenger-of-YHWH framing (see [malakh-yhwh-messenger](#malakh-yhwh-messenger)) and the foundational Samson-cycle barrenness-resolution.

*Hannah.* **1 Sam 1:5-6** — *u-l-Channah yiten manah achat apayim ki et-Channah ahev va-YHWH sagar rachmah; v-khi'isathah tzaratah gam-ka'as ba-avur har'imah ki-sagar YHWH be-ad rachmah* — *to Hannah he gave one portion of grace, for he loved Hannah; but YHWH had closed her womb; and her rival also provoked her sorely, to make her fret, because YHWH had closed her womb* — the **closest verbal parallel** to Gen 20:18 in the entire corpus. The Hannah barrenness uses *sagar rachmah* / *sagar be-ad rachmah* (the *sagar*-the-womb / *sagar-be-ad-the-womb* verbal collocation) where Gen 20:18 uses *atzar be-ad rechem*; the two verbal patterns are interchangeable in the cross-corpus distribution. The 1 Sam 1:11 *va-y'hi ki-zakhar YHWH et-Channah va-tahar va-teled ben* — *YHWH remembered Hannah; she conceived and bore a son* — resolves the barrenness with the same *zakhar* remembering-formula as Gen 30:22 (Rachel) and as Gen 8:1 (Noach).

**The matriarchal-barrenness pattern as canonical structure.** The cross-corpus pattern produces the canonical HB matriarchal-and-foundational-women structure: Sarah → Rebekah → Rachel → Manoah's wife → Hannah → (deutero-canonically Elizabeth, NT). Every major foundational figure in the Hebrew Bible is preceded by a barrenness that the deity directly resolves. The Gen 20:18 verse fits this pattern by inversion: where the matriarchal-barrenness instances see the deity closing the wombs of the future-matriarchs to be resolved later by divine action, Gen 20:18 sees the deity closing the wombs of the foreign-king's household as sanction for the unwitting taking-of-Sarah, with the resolution coming at v 17 by the prophet's intercession. The Gen 20 chapter thus deploys the closed-wombs motif in the structurally-inverted register: the wombs that are closed are the foreign-king's, not the matriarch's; the resolution is by prophetic intercession, not by direct divine remembering.

**Chapter-internal irony.** The wife-sister deception undertaken to protect the patriarchal couple has produced precisely the barrenness-on-the-foreign-household that the cycle ordinarily reserves for the matriarchs themselves. The chapter's chiastic ironies (foreign-king-as-moral-voice at v 4; Avraham's fear-of-the-Elohim assumption falsified at v 11; closed-wombs sanction at v 18) are tightly integrated: the deception that arose from Avraham's a-priori judgment that Gerar lacks the fear-of-the-Elohim has fallen on the very household that turned out to fear the Elohim. The chapter's structural critique of Avraham's reasoning is sharpest at the v 18 coda.

**Scholarly literature.** **H. C. Brichto *Kin, Cult, Land and Afterlife — A Biblical Complex* 1973 *HUCA* 44, pp. 1-54** (the foundational treatment of the womb-theology of the matriarchal cycle); **Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 326-329**; **Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 74-75**; **Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 71-74**; **Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 144-145**; **D. Steinmetz *From Father to Son: Kinship, Conflict, and Continuity in Genesis* 1991** on the cross-corpus matriarchal-barrenness pattern; **C. Meyers *Discovering Eve: Ancient Israelite Women in Context* 1988** and *Rediscovering Eve* 2013 on the cultural-historical reading of HB barrenness narratives; **E. Fuchs *Sexual Politics in the Biblical Narrative* 2000** on the gender-political reading; the **annunciation-type-scene** treatment in **Alter *The Art of Biblical Narrative* 1981 pp. 47-62** (one of the founding chapter-cases of Alter's type-scene methodology, with Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel, Manoah's wife, and the Shunammite as the canonical annunciation-type-scene set).

**Translation.** The ASV-baseline *had fast closed up every womb* is preserved, with the doubled infinitive-absolute construction visible in the *fast* intensifier. The verb *atzar* (restrain, hold back, close up) is preserved as *closed up* (rather than smoothed to *prevented from bearing* or similar); the doubling is preserved as *fast closed up* (rather than smoothed to *had completely / utterly closed*). The closed-wombs are preserved as *every womb* rather than the smoothed *all the women* — the body-part metonymy is the Hebrew's own and is preserved.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the doubled infinitive-absolute construction is grammatically explicit; (b) the closed-wombs motif and the cross-corpus matriarchal-barrenness pattern are documentary; (c) the *sagar / atzar / paqad / zakhar* verbal cluster for divine womb-action is documented in HALOT and BDB and in the cited scholarly literature; (d) the chapter's structural irony is mainstream-scholarly. Project-specific synthesis (any Wheel of Heaven reading of the matriarchal-barrenness motif beyond the mainstream-scholarly treatment) is reserved for the wiki and is not preempted by this entry.
